topics.title: Fundamentals and Basic Principles: Solar Radiation Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT631 - RENEWABLE ENERGY AND GREEN TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fundamentals and Basic Principles: Solar Radiation Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Solar Radiation?

**Solar Radiation** is the electromagnetic energy emitted by the Sun, produced by continuous thermonuclear fusion reactions (primarily the proton–proton chain) in its core, where hydrogen is converted into helium at temperatures of approximately **15 million Kelvin**. The Sun behaves almost like a **blackbody radiator** at an effective surface temperature of about **5778 K**, radiating energy across a wide spectrum — from ultraviolet (UV) through visible light to infrared (IR).

At the mean Earth–Sun distance of **1 AU (≈ 1.496 × 10⁸ km)**, the total radiant flux density incident on a surface held perpendicular to the solar beam, **outside the Earth's atmosphere**, is known as the **Solar Constant (I_sc)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition — Solar Constant**
> The solar constant is defined as the rate at which solar energy is received per unit area on a surface placed **perpendicular to the Sun's rays**, at the **mean distance** of the Earth from the Sun, **outside the Earth's atmosphere**.
>
> **Standard accepted value: I_sc = 1367 W/m²** (older WMO value)
> **Updated value: I_sc = 1361 W/m²** (per latest NASA SORCE satellite data)

> [!NOTE]
> The term *constant* is historical; in reality, the extraterrestrial irradiance varies by about **±3.3 %** throughout the year due to the **ellipticity of the Earth's orbit** (perihelion in early January, aphelion in early July).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Sun as a Giant Lamp

Imagine the Sun as an enormous, perfectly round, very hot light bulb suspended in space. Just like the brightness (illuminance) you measure from a lamp depends on **how far you stand from it**, the energy we receive on Earth depends on:

- **Distance from the Sun** (Earth's elliptical orbit)
- **Atmospheric thickness** the rays must pass through (air mass)
- **Orientation of the receiving surface** (tilt and azimuth)
- **Cloud cover and pollution** (diffuse vs. direct component)

> A solar collector oriented perpendicular to the beam at noon on a clear, dry day at the equator near equinox will receive almost the full **I_sc**, while a horizontal collector in the same location near sunrise will receive only a tiny fraction because the rays have to traverse a much longer slant path through the atmosphere.

## 1.3 Why is Solar Radiation Measurement Important?

Solar radiation data is the **primary input** for the design, sizing, performance simulation, and economic feasibility analysis of every solar energy system:

- **Photovoltaic (PV) systems** — energy yield estimation
- **Solar thermal collectors** — efficiency and heat delivery
- **Concentrated Solar Power (CSP)** — direct normal irradiance (DNI) is the critical parameter
- **Building integrated solar** — passive and active design
- **Agricultural and climate studies** — evapotranspiration models

## 1.4 Solar Spectrum & Atmospheric Effects

The Sun emits energy roughly as a **blackbody at 5778 K**, peaking near **0.5 μm (green light)**. By the time this radiation reaches the Earth's surface, the spectrum has been modified by three main atmospheric processes:

| Process | Effect | Engineering Impact |
|---|---|---|
| **Selective Absorption** | O₃ absorbs UV; H₂O & CO₂ absorb IR | Spectral mismatch losses in PV |
| **Scattering** (Rayleigh, Mie) | Redirects short-wavelength light | Creates the *diffuse* sky radiation |
| **Reflection** | Clouds, aerosols, ground | Reduces the *global* irradiance |

The fraction that survives the atmosphere without being scattered or absorbed is called **Direct (or Beam) Normal Irradiance (DNI)**, while the scattered portion arriving from the entire sky dome is the **Diffuse Horizontal Irradiance (DHI)**. Their sum (with the appropriate geometric projection) is the **Global Horizontal Irradiance (GHI)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **GHI = DNI × cos(θz) + DHI**
> where θz is the **solar zenith angle**. This relation is the fundamental energy-balance equation used in every KTU numerical problem on tilted surfaces.

## 1.5 The Solar Constant — Numerical Anchors

| Parameter | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Solar Constant (WMO) | I_sc | 1367 | W/m² |
| Solar Constant (NASA) | I_sc | 1361 | W/m² |
| Stefan–Boltzmann constant | σ | 5.670 × 10⁻⁸ | W/m²·K⁴ |
| Sun's surface temperature | T_sun | 5778 | K |
| Sun's radius | R_s | 6.96 × 10⁵ | km |
| Mean Earth–Sun distance | 1 AU | 1.496 × 10⁸ | km |
| Eccentricity of Earth's orbit | e | 0.0167 | — |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of Solar Radiation at the Ground

When solar radiation penetrates the atmosphere and reaches a receiver on the ground, it is conventionally classified into three components:

1. **Direct (Beam) Radiation (I_b)** — the radiation received from the Sun's disc *without* being scattered by the atmosphere. For a surface held normal to the beam, it is called **Direct Normal Irradiance (DNI)**.

2. **Diffuse Radiation (I_d)** — the radiation received from the entire *sky dome* after the solar beam has been scattered by molecules, aerosols, and clouds. It is essentially isotropic under overcast skies.

3. **Reflected (or Albedo) Radiation (I_r)** — the radiation reflected from the ground or surrounding surfaces onto a tilted receiver. Governed by the **ground albedo (ρ_g)**, typically 0.2 for grass, 0.3 for dry soil, up to 0.9 for fresh snow.

> **For a tilted surface at tilt angle β:**
> $$I_T = I_b \cos\theta + I_d \left(\frac{1 + \cos\beta}{2}\right) + I \rho_g \left(\frac{1 - \cos\beta}{2}\right)$$
> where θ is the angle of incidence on the tilted surface, and I is the global horizontal irradiance. This is the famous **Liu & Jordan isotropic sky model**, used universally in KTU problems.

## 2.2 Extraterrestrial Radiation on a Horizontal Surface (I_o)

The extraterrestrial irradiance on a surface **perpendicular** to the solar beam, on day *n* of the year, varies with the Earth–Sun distance:

$$I_{on} = I_{sc} \left(1 + 0.033 \cos\frac{360 n}{365}\right)$$

where the bracketed term is the **Eccentricity Correction Factor (E_o)**. This is essential for KTU problems on **monthly average daily radiation**.

For a horizontal surface *outside* the atmosphere, the extraterrestrial irradiance is:

$$I_{oh} = I_{on} \cos\theta_z$$

## 2.3 Solar Geometry — The Master Set of Angles

KTU problems heavily test the following **six fundamental angles**. Memorize them as a single logical set.

| # | Angle | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Latitude | φ | Angular position N (+) or S (−) of the site from the equator |
| 2 | Declination | δ | Angular position of the Sun N/S of the equator at solar noon |
| 3 | Hour Angle | ω | Angular displacement of the Sun east (−) or west (+) of the local meridian |
| 4 | Solar Altitude | α_s | Angle of the Sun above the horizontal |
| 5 | Solar Zenith | θ_z | Angle of the Sun measured from the vertical (zenith = 90° − α_s) |
| 6 | Solar Azimuth | γ_s | Horizontal angle measured from North, clockwise, to the Sun's projection |

### 2.3.1 Cooper's Equation for Declination Angle

The declination angle, δ, on day *n* of the year:

$$\delta = 23.45 \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + n)\right] \quad \text{[degrees]}$$

> δ varies between **−23.45°** (winter solstice, Dec 21) and **+23.45°** (summer solstice, June 21), and is **0°** at the equinoxes (Mar 21, Sep 23).

### 2.3.2 Hour Angle

The hour angle ω, in degrees, is related to the solar time (t_s) in hours by:

$$\omega = 15(t_s - 12) \quad \text{[degrees]}$$

> The Sun moves **15° per hour** across the sky (360°/24 h). Negative in morning, positive in afternoon, 0° at solar noon.

### 2.3.3 Solar Altitude, Zenith & Azimuth

The **cosine of the zenith angle** (equivalently, the sine of the altitude) is given by the **fundamental spherical trigonometry relation**:

$$\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$$

$$\alpha_s = 90° - \theta_z$$

The **solar azimuth angle** (measured from North, clockwise):

$$\cos\gamma_s = \frac{\sin\delta \cos\phi - \cos\delta \sin\phi \cos\omega}{\cos\alpha_s}$$

### 2.3.4 Sunrise / Sunset Hour Angle (ω_s)

The hour angle at sunrise (or sunset) is found by setting **θ_z = 90°** (Sun on the horizon), giving:

$$\cos\omega_s = -\tan\phi \tan\delta$$

> The **day length** is: $N = \frac{2}{15} \omega_s$ hours.

## 2.4 Air Mass (AM)

The **air mass** is the ratio of the slant path length of solar rays through the atmosphere to the path length when the Sun is at the zenith:

$$AM = \frac{1}{\cos\theta_z} = \sec\theta_z$$

| Air Mass | Sun Position | Application |
|---|---|---|
| AM 0 | Outside atmosphere | Satellite, extraterrestrial |
| AM 1 | Sun at zenith, sea level | Tropical peak |
| **AM 1.5** | Sun at 48.2° from zenith | **STC for PV module rating** |
| AM 2 | Sun at 60° from zenith | Standard for some thermal collectors |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight**: STC (Standard Test Conditions) for PV modules is defined as **1000 W/m²**, AM 1.5 spectrum, and cell temperature of **25 °C**. This is the rating stamped on every commercial PV module.

## 2.5 Instruments for Solar Radiation Measurement

| Instrument | Parameter Measured | Principle | WMO Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pyrheliometer** | Direct Normal Irradiance (DNI) | Narrow-angle (~5°) thermopile pointed at Sun | First Class |
| **Pyranometer** | Global / Diffuse (with shade ring) | Thermopile under glass dome (180° hemisphere) | First Class |
| **Sunshine Recorder (Campbell-Stokes)** | Sunshine duration | Focused Sun burns a trace on paper card | — |
| **Pyranometer with shadow band** | Diffuse Horizontal (DHI) | Pyranometer + occulting band | First Class |
| **Sun Photometer** | Aerosol Optical Depth | Direct Sun intensity at specific wavelengths | — |

### 2.5.1 Pyranometer — Construction

A pyranometer uses a **thermoelectric detector** (a circular multi-junction thermopile) coated with a broadband **black absorber**. The hot junctions are in thermal contact with the absorbing surface; the cold junctions are referenced to the instrument body. The temperature difference produces a small EMF (typically μV to mV range) proportional to the incident irradiance. The dome is double-glassed optical glass to shield the detector from convective heat loss and to filter out long-wave IR.

### 2.5.2 Pyrheliometer — Construction

A pyrheliometer has a narrow acceptance angle of about **5° to 5.7°** (full opening angle) and is mounted on a **sun tracker**. It directly measures the **DNI** without diffuse contamination. The most common type is the **Eppley NIP** (Normal Incidence Pyrheliometer).

## 2.6 Empirical Correlations for Estimating Solar Radiation

When measured data is unavailable, KTU problems typically use these correlations:

### 2.6.1 Angstrom-Type Correlation (Monthly Average Daily)

$$\frac{\bar{H}}{\bar{H_o}} = a + b \left(\frac{\bar{n}}{\bar{N}}\right)$$

where:
- $\bar{H}$ = monthly average daily global radiation on a horizontal surface (MJ/m²/day)
- $\bar{H_o}$ = monthly average daily extraterrestrial radiation
- $\bar{n}$ = monthly average daily sunshine hours
- $\bar{N}$ = monthly average maximum possible sunshine hours (theoretical day length)
- $a$, $b$ = empirical regression coefficients

### 2.6.2 $\bar{H_o}$ Calculation (Cooper's Method)

$$\bar{H_o} = \frac{24 \times 3600}{\pi} \, I_{sc} \, E_o \left(\cos\phi \cos\delta \sin\omega_s + \frac{\pi \omega_s}{180} \sin\phi \sin\delta\right)$$

where:
- $\delta$ = mean declination for the month
- $\omega_s$ = mean sunrise hour angle for the month
- $E_o$ = eccentricity correction factor

### 2.6.3 Hargreaves–Samani Equation (Using Temperature Data)

$$H = 0.16 \cdot (T_{max} - T_{min})^{0.5} \cdot R_a$$

where $R_a$ is the extraterrestrial radiation (in mm of water equivalent / day), used when only temperature data is available.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Quick Revision)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Memorisation List for KTU Board Exam**
> The following table consolidates every equation tested in this module. Note the use of `\vert` instead of `|` to keep the markdown table intact.

| # | Quantity | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Extraterrestrial normal irradiance | $I_{on} = I_{sc} \left(1 + 0.033 \cos\frac{360n}{365}\right)$ | W/m², n = day of year |
| 2 | Declination angle | $\delta = 23.45 \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284+n)\right]$ | degrees |
| 3 | Hour angle | $\omega = 15(t_s - 12)$ | degrees, t_s in hours |
| 4 | Cosine of zenith angle | $\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Sunrise/sunset hour angle | $\cos\omega_s = -\tan\phi \tan\delta$ | degrees |
| 6 | Day length | $N = \frac{2}{15}\omega_s$ | hours |
| 7 | Air Mass | $AM = \sec\theta_z$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Tilted surface incidence | $\cos\theta = \sin\delta\sin(\phi-\beta) + \cos\delta\cos(\phi-\beta)\cos\gamma$ (south-facing fixed) | depends on orientation |
| 9 | GHI relation | $GHI = DNI \cos\theta_z + DHI$ | W/m² |
| 10 | Tilted global | $I_T = I_b\cos\theta + I_d\left(\frac{1+\cos\beta}{2}\right) + I\rho_g\left(\frac{1-\cos\beta}{2}\right)$ | Liu & Jordan |
| 11 | Angstrom correlation | $\bar{H}/\bar{H_o} = a + b(\bar{n}/\bar{N})$ | monthly average |
| 12 | $\bar{H_o}$ (daily) | $\bar{H_o} = \frac{86400}{\pi} I_{sc} E_o \left(\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + \frac{\pi\omega_s}{180}\sin\phi\sin\delta\right)$ | J/m²/day |

> **Engineering Insight:** The Liu & Jordan tilted-surface model is the *first-pass* design tool used by every solar engineer worldwide. More accurate models (Perez, Hay–Davies, Reindl) account for the *anisotropy* of the diffuse sky, but the isotropic model is **universally required at KTU level** as the foundation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Extraterrestrial Radiation on a Horizontal Surface (I_{oh})

We start from the **inverse square law** of radiation and apply the Earth's orbital eccentricity correction.

**Step 1 — Inverse Square Law**

If the Sun emits total power $L_\odot$ (luminosity ≈ 3.846 × 10²⁶ W), and is at a distance *r* from the Earth, then the flux density on a sphere of radius *r* is:

$$I = \frac{L_\odot}{4\pi r^2}$$

**Step 2 — Definition of the Solar Constant**

By definition, at $r = 1 \text{ AU}$:
$$I_{sc} = \frac{L_\odot}{4\pi (1\,\text{AU})^2} = 1367 \; \text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Eccentricity Correction**

Because Earth's orbit is an ellipse, the instantaneous Earth–Sun distance varies. The actual flux on day *n* is:

$$I_{on} = I_{sc} \left(\frac{r_{mean}}{r}\right)^2$$

**Step 4 — Approximate Closed Form**

A trigonometric approximation of $(r_{mean}/r)^2$ accurate to within 0.1 % is:

$$I_{on} = I_{sc} \left[1 + 0.033 \cos\left(\frac{360 n}{365}\right)\right]$$

**Step 5 — Projecting onto a Horizontal Surface**

The horizontal component of $I_{on}$ is obtained by multiplying by $\cos\theta_z$ (where $\theta_z$ is the zenith angle):

$$I_{oh} = I_{on} \cos\theta_z = I_{sc} \left[1 + 0.033 \cos\left(\frac{360 n}{365}\right)\right] \cos\theta_z$$

This is the **instantaneous extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface** outside the atmosphere.

> **Conversion Logic:** From Step 4 to Step 5, the only change is multiplying by the cosine of the incidence angle on the horizontal surface. This is the same geometric projection you would use for a vector of magnitude $I_{on}$ pointing from the Sun onto a horizontal plane.

## 3.2 Derivation: Sunrise / Sunset Hour Angle (ω_s)

The hour angle at sunrise is found when the Sun is *exactly on the horizon*, i.e., when its altitude $\alpha_s = 0°$ and zenith $\theta_z = 90°$.

**Step 1 — Start from the zenith angle relation**

$$\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$$

**Step 2 — Set $\theta_z = 90°$, so $\cos\theta_z = 0$**

$$0 = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega_s$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\cos\omega_s$**

$$\cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega_s = -\sin\phi \sin\delta$$

$$\cos\omega_s = -\frac{\sin\phi \sin\delta}{\cos\phi \cos\delta}$$

**Step 4 — Use the identity $\frac{\sin x}{\cos x} = \tan x$**

$$\boxed{\cos\omega_s = -\tan\phi \tan\delta}$$

**Step 5 — Day Length N**

The Sun takes $\omega_s$ degrees of angular travel from sunrise to solar noon (and the same to sunset), i.e., $2\omega_s$ degrees total. At 15° per hour:

$$N = \frac{2\omega_s}{15} = \frac{2}{15}\cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi \tan\delta)$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The negative sign is geometric — at sunrise, the hour angle is *negative* in our convention (Sun in the east). We take the *positive* magnitude $\omega_s$ in degrees and convert to hours by dividing by 15°/h.

## 3.3 Derivation: Angle of Incidence on a Tilted Surface (θ)

Consider a flat-plate collector facing the equator (azimuth γ = 0) at a tilt β from horizontal, located at latitude φ. The angle of incidence θ between the Sun's beam and the *normal* to the collector is given by the dot product of the Sun's direction vector and the collector's normal vector. Expanding in spherical trigonometry:

**Step 1 — Sun direction in the local coordinate system**

The Sun's beam makes zenith angle θz with the vertical, and azimuth γs from North. Its direction cosines are:
- Vertical: $\cos\theta_z = \sin\alpha_s$
- East–West: $-\cos\alpha_s \sin\gamma_s$ (or +ve depending on convention)
- North–South: $\cos\alpha_s \cos\gamma_s$

**Step 2 — Collector normal direction**

A south-facing collector at tilt β from horizontal has a normal vector pointing with vertical component $\cos\beta$ and a south-pointing horizontal component of $\sin\beta$.

**Step 3 — Dot Product**

$$\cos\theta = \cos\beta \cos\theta_z + \sin\beta \sin\theta_z \cos(\gamma_s - \gamma)$$

For a south-facing collector, γ = 0, and using $\sin\theta_z = \cos\alpha_s$, $\cos\theta_z = \sin\alpha_s$:

$$\boxed{\cos\theta = \sin\delta \sin(\phi - \beta) + \cos\delta \cos(\phi - \beta) \cos\omega}$$

**Step 4 — Special Cases Verification**

| Case | Result | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| β = 0 (horizontal) | $\cos\theta = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega = \cos\theta_z$ | Reduces to zenith |
| φ = β (collector latitude) | $\cos\theta = \sin\delta \sin(0) + \cos\delta \cos(0) \cos\omega = \cos\delta \cos\omega$ | At equinox (δ=0), θ = ω |
| β = φ, δ = 0 | $\cos\theta = \cos\omega$ | Noon incidence = 0 (Sun normal) |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Examples (Analytical Matrix)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example 1 — Instantaneous Extraterrestrial Radiation**
>
> *Compute the extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface at solar noon on day n = 100 (April 10) at latitude φ = 13°N (Trivandrum).*
>
> **Step A — Declination angle**
> $$\delta = 23.45 \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + 100)\right] = 23.45 \sin(379.07°)$$
> $$= 23.45 \sin(19.07°) = 23.45 \times 0.3266 = 7.66°$$
>
> **Step B — Eccentricity correction factor**
> $$E_o = 1 + 0.033 \cos\left(\frac{360 \times 100}{365}\right) = 1 + 0.033 \cos(98.63°)$$
> $$= 1 + 0.033 \times (-0.1472) = 0.9951$$
>
> **Step C — Zenith angle at solar noon (ω = 0)**
> $$\cos\theta_z = \sin(13°)\sin(7.66°) + \cos(13°)\cos(7.66°)\cos(0°)$$
> $$= (0.225)(0.133) + (0.974)(0.991)(1) = 0.030 + 0.965 = 0.995$$
> $$\theta_z = 5.75°$$
>
> **Step D — Extraterrestrial horizontal irradiance**
> $$I_{oh} = 1367 \times 0.9951 \times 0.995 = 1353 \; \text{W/m}^2$$
>
> The value is close to 1367 W/m² because Trivandrum (near equator) at noon in April receives the Sun almost directly overhead.

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example 2 — Day Length and Sunrise/Sunset Hour Angle**
>
> *Find the day length on the equinox (δ = 0) at a location in Kerala with latitude φ = 10°N.*
>
> **Step A — Apply sunrise hour angle formula**
> $$\cos\omega_s = -\tan(10°)\tan(0°) = 0$$
> $$\omega_s = 90°$$
>
> **Step B — Day length**
> $$N = \frac{2 \times 90°}{15°/\text{h}} = 12 \text{ hours}$$
>
> **Conversion Logic:** At the equinox, the Sun is exactly over the equator. So every location on Earth experiences *exactly* 12 hours of daylight — this is the KTU "trick" answer when δ = 0.

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example 3 — Air Mass at Solar Noon in Winter**
>
> *Compute the air mass at solar noon on December 21 at latitude φ = 30°N.*
>
> **Step A — Declination on Dec 21**
> $$\delta = 23.45 \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + 355)\right] = 23.45 \sin(629.26°)$$
> Reduce modulo 360°: $629.26° - 360° = 269.26°$
> $$\sin(269.26°) = \sin(269.26° - 270°) = \sin(-0.74°) = -0.0129$$
> $$\delta = 23.45 \times (-0.0129) = -23.30° \approx -23.45°$$
>
> **Step B — Zenith at solar noon**
> $$\cos\theta_z = \sin(30°)\sin(-23.45°) + \cos(30°)\cos(-23.45°)\cos(0°)$$
> $$= (0.5)(-0.3979) + (0.866)(0.9174)(1) = -0.199 + 0.794 = 0.595$$
> $$\theta_z = 53.5°$$
>
> **Step C — Air Mass**
> $$AM = \sec(53.5°) = \frac{1}{0.595} = 1.68$$
>
> Air mass is low (near 1) — winter noon has a *higher Sun path* at this latitude. Summer noon would have AM ≈ 1.03, slightly higher Sun.

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example 4 — Angstrom's Equation Application**
>
> *At a site, the monthly mean daily global radiation is measured as $\bar{H} = 18 \, \text{MJ/m}^2\text{/day}$ for June. The mean daily extraterrestrial radiation $\bar{H_o} = 32.5 \, \text{MJ/m}^2\text{/day}$. The mean daily sunshine hours $\bar{n} = 8 \, \text{h}$ and the theoretical day length $\bar{N} = 12.5 \, \text{h}$. Empirical constants are a = 0.25, b = 0.55. Verify.*
>
> **Step A — Compute the ratio $\bar{H}/\bar{H_o}$**
> $$\frac{\bar{H}}{\bar{H_o}} = \frac{18}{32.5} = 0.554$$
>
> **Step B — Compute $\bar{n}/\bar{N}$**
> $$\frac{\bar{n}}{\bar{N}} = \frac{8}{12.5} = 0.64$$
>
> **Step C — Check the Angstrom equation**
> $$a + b\frac{\bar{n}}{\bar{N}} = 0.25 + 0.55 \times 0.64 = 0.25 + 0.352 = 0.602$$
>
> **Step D — Compare**
> The Angstrom prediction is 0.602, but the measured ratio is 0.554. The discrepancy of 0.048 indicates either an overcast day correction or the empirical constants need site-specific calibration. This is the standard form of an Angstrom problem on the KTU exam.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture: Solar Radiation Measurement System

The following **Mermaid block diagram** maps the physical flow of radiation from the Sun through the atmosphere into the instrumentation chain, ending in the data acquisition unit. This is the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** that a student should be able to reproduce on the KTU exam drawing sheet.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Sun Source 5778K Blackbody] --> B[Extraterrestrial Radiation I_on]
    B --> C[Earth Atmosphere]
    C --> D[Direct Beam Radiation DNI]
    C --> E[Diffuse Sky Radiation DHI]
    C --> F[Albedo Reflected Radiation]
    D --> G[Pyrheliometer on Sun Tracker]
    E --> H[Pyranometer with Shade Ring]
    F --> I[Pyranometer Inverted]
    G --> J[Thermopile Signal Microvolts]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Signal Conditioner Amplifier]
    K --> L[ADC Converter]
    L --> M[Data Logger Storage]
    M --> N[PC Workstation Analysis]
    N --> O[Monthly Average Daily H MJm2]
    O --> P[Solar System Design Input]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- The **Sun (A)** emits radiation in all directions; only the fraction that intercepts the Earth is relevant.
- **Atmospheric attenuation (C)** splits the radiation into three components: direct (D), diffuse (E), and a small reflected component (F).
- Three **instruments** (G, H, I) are used — each measures one of these three components.
- Their analog **thermopile signals** are conditioned, digitised, stored, and processed to yield the **monthly average daily global radiation** used by the solar system designer (P).

## 4.2 Functional Flow: Tilted-Surface Radiation Geometry

The following diagram shows the geometric decomposition of the **Liu & Jordan tilted-surface model**, which is the central engineering formula for collector orientation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Global Horizontal GHI] --> B[Beam Component on Horizontal]
    A --> C[Diffuse Component DHI]
    A --> D[Reflected Component]
    B --> E[Multiply by cos theta over cos theta z]
    E --> F[Beam on Tilted I_b cos theta]
    C --> G[Isotropic Sky Factor 1 plus cos beta over 2]
    G --> H[Diffuse on Tilted]
    D --> I[Ground Factor 1 minus cos beta over 2]
    I --> J[Reflected on Tilted]
    F --> K[Total Tilted Radiation I_T]
    H --> K
    J --> K
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- The total radiation on a horizontal surface (A) is decomposed into three physical components.
- The **beam component (B)** is geometrically projected from horizontal to tilted via the ratio of cosines.
- The **diffuse component (C)** is multiplied by the *sky view factor* $(1 + \cos\beta)/2$ — the fraction of the sky dome "seen" by the tilted surface.
- The **reflected component (D)** is multiplied by the *ground view factor* $(1 - \cos\beta)/2$.
- The three are summed to obtain the total tilted radiation (K).

## 4.3 Logical Sequence: Declination Angle Variation Through the Year

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Day n of Year] --> B[Compute 360/365 times 284 plus n]
    B --> C[Take Sine Function]
    C --> D[Multiply by 23.45 degrees]
    D --> E[Declination delta]
    E --> F{Month Check}
    F -->|n around 172| G[June 21 Max delta 23.45]
    F -->|n around 355| H[Dec 21 Min delta minus 23.45]
    F -->|n around 80 or 266| I[Equinoxes delta equals 0]
    E --> J[Feed to Hour Angle and Zenith Equations]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- The declination δ is purely a function of the day number *n*.
- It is a sinusoidal function peaking at the **summer solstice (June 21, n ≈ 172)** and bottoming at the **winter solstice (Dec 21, n ≈ 355)**.
- It is **0°** at both equinoxes (around March 21 and September 23).
- This single output δ is then used as a building block for every other solar geometry equation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]  
> **Define the term "Solar Constant" and state its standard value. Why is it called a "constant" even though the extraterrestrial irradiance varies through the year?**

**Model Answer:**

The solar constant is defined as the rate at which solar energy is received per unit area on a surface held **perpendicular** to the Sun's rays, at the **mean distance** of the Earth from the Sun, **outside the Earth's atmosphere**. Its standard accepted value is **I_sc = 1367 W/m²** (WMO) or **1361 W/m²** (NASA).

It is called a "constant" because, by definition, it is referenced to a *fixed* mean Earth–Sun distance. In reality, the actual extraterrestrial irradiance varies by about **±3.3 %** through the year because of the **ellipticity of the Earth's orbit**. The actual instantaneous value is given by the eccentricity-corrected form:

$$I_{on} = I_{sc}\left[1 + 0.033 \cos\left(\frac{360 n}{365}\right)\right]$$

The maximum (~1415 W/m²) occurs near perihelion in early January, and the minimum (~1321 W/m²) near aphelion in early July.

---

> **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]  
> **What is meant by "Air Mass" (AM)? Why is AM 1.5 chosen as the standard for testing photovoltaic modules?**

**Model Answer:**

**Air Mass (AM)** is defined as the ratio of the actual path length traversed by solar radiation through the Earth's atmosphere to the path length when the Sun is directly overhead (at the zenith). Mathematically:

$$AM = \frac{1}{\cos\theta_z} = \sec\theta_z$$

where θz is the solar zenith angle.

**AM 1.5** corresponds to a zenith angle of approximately **48.2°**, which represents the average air mass experienced in mid-latitude locations (e.g., continental USA, Europe, much of India during typical operating hours). It is the internationally adopted **Standard Test Condition (STC)** for PV modules by IEC 60904, paired with an irradiance of **1000 W/m²** and a cell temperature of **25 °C**, because:

1. It is representative of *real-world average* operating conditions for terrestrial PV.
2. It allows **standardised comparison** between modules from different manufacturers.
3. It defines a *spectral distribution* (AM 1.5G) used to design the simulator flash spectrum.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read This Carefully**
> For solar geometry derivations, students **commonly lose marks** for:
> 1. Forgetting to **convert hour angle from time to degrees** (factor of 15).
> 2. Confusing **declination angle** (astronomical, on day n) with **latitude** (site parameter).
> 3. Using the **wrong sign convention** in sunrise hour angle formula (forgetting the negative sign gives $\cos\omega_s = -\tan\phi\tan\delta$, not positive).
> 4. Mixing up **"air mass" and "declination"** definitions.
> 5. Failing to state the **assumptions** in the Liu & Jordan model (isotropic sky, ground reflection as Lambertian).
> 6. Skipping intermediate steps in $\bar{H_o}$ derivations. **Show every trigonometric substitution** — KTU examiners explicitly look for it.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Solar Geometry & Extraterrestrial Radiation

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
>
> (a) *Derive the equations for the declination angle and the hour angle. A solar collector is located at latitude φ = 28.6°N, longitude 77.2°E. The local clock time is 14:00 (IST, UTC+5:30). Find the solar declination, hour angle, and the extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface for day n = 172 (June 21) at the site. Assume the equation of time correction E_t = 0 for this day.* (7 Marks)
>
> (b) *What is Air Mass? Calculate the air mass and the sunrise hour angle for the same location and day. Hence determine the day length.* (7 Marks)

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 — Declination angle (δ) on day n = 172**

Using Cooper's equation:
$$\delta = 23.45 \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + 172)\right] = 23.45 \sin\left[\frac{360 \times 456}{365}\right]$$
$$= 23.45 \sin(449.75°)$$

Reduce modulo 360°: $449.75° - 360° = 89.75°$
$$\sin(89.75°) = 0.99999 \approx 1$$
$$\delta = 23.45 \times 1 = 23.45°$$

**Conversion Logic:** This is the **summer solstice**, so the declination reaches its astronomical maximum of +23.45°.

**Step 2 — Hour angle (ω) at 14:00 IST**

Local standard time meridian for IST (UTC+5:30): $L_{st} = 82.5°E$

Site longitude: $L_{loc} = 77.2°E$

**Step 2a — Solar time (t_s)**

$$t_s = LST + 4(L_{st} - L_{loc})/60 + E_t/60$$

With $LST = 14:00$, $E_t = 0$:
$$t_s = 14 + 4(82.5 - 77.2)/60 + 0 = 14 + 4(5.3)/60 = 14 + 0.353 = 14.353 \text{ h}$$

**Step 2b — Hour angle**

$$\omega = 15(t_s - 12) = 15(14.353 - 12) = 15 \times 2.353 = 35.3°$$

**Conversion Logic:** Afternoon hour angles are positive. 14:00 IST is **2 h 21 min** after solar noon, corresponding to 35.3° west of the meridian.

**Step 3 — Extraterrestrial radiation on horizontal surface**

**Step 3a — Eccentricity correction factor**
$$E_o = 1 + 0.033\cos\left(\frac{360 \times 172}{365}\right) = 1 + 0.033\cos(169.6°)$$
$$= 1 + 0.033 \times (-0.983) = 0.968$$

**Step 3b — Zenith angle**
$$\cos\theta_z = \sin(28.6°)\sin(23.45°) + \cos(28.6°)\cos(23.45°)\cos(35.3°)$$
$$= (0.479)(0.398) + (0.878)(0.917)(0.816)$$
$$= 0.1906 + 0.6574 = 0.8480$$
$$\theta_z = 32.05°$$

**Step 3c — Extraterrestrial normal irradiance**
$$I_{on} = 1367 \times 0.968 = 1323.3 \; \text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 3d — Extraterrestrial on horizontal surface**
$$I_{oh} = I_{on} \cos\theta_z = 1323.3 \times 0.8480 = 1122.2 \; \text{W/m}^2$$

**Answer:** δ = 23.45°, ω = 35.3°, $I_{oh}$ ≈ **1122 W/m²**.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Definition of Air Mass (AM)**

Air mass is the ratio of the slant path length of solar rays through the atmosphere to the path length at the zenith:

$$AM = \frac{1}{\cos\theta_z} = \sec\theta_z$$

**Step 2 — Air mass at 14:00 IST on day 172**

From the previous solution, $\cos\theta_z = 0.8480$:
$$AM = \frac{1}{0.8480} = 1.179$$

**Step 3 — Sunrise hour angle (ω_s)**

Apply the horizon condition:
$$\cos\omega_s = -\tan\phi \tan\delta = -\tan(28.6°)\tan(23.45°)$$
$$= -(0.5459)(0.4335) = -0.2367$$
$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-0.2367) = 103.7°$$

**Step 4 — Day length N**

$$N = \frac{2\omega_s}{15} = \frac{2 \times 103.7°}{15°/\text{h}} = 13.83 \; \text{h} \approx 13\text{h }50\text{ min}$$

**Answer:** AM ≈ **1.18**, ω_s = **103.7°**, Day length ≈ **13 h 50 min**.

**Conversion Logic:** At the summer solstice at 28.6°N, the day is much longer than 12 h — about 13 h 50 min of sunshine. The hour angle (103.7°) corresponds to sunrise at solar time $t_s = 12 - \omega_s/15 = 12 - 6.91 = 5:05$ AM (solar time).

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Empirical Correlations & Tilted-Surface Radiation

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
>
> (a) *What is the Angstrom-type empirical correlation? At a site, the monthly mean daily extraterrestrial radiation $\bar{H_o} = 30 \, \text{MJ/m}^2\text{/day}$. The measured mean daily global radiation is $\bar{H} = 16.5 \, \text{MJ/m}^2\text{/day}$. The mean daily actual sunshine duration is $\bar{n} = 7 \, \text{h}$, and the theoretical maximum day length is $\bar{N} = 12 \, \text{h}$. Using empirical constants a = 0.30 and b = 0.50, verify the Angstrom relation and find the clearness index.* (7 Marks)
>
> (b) *Explain the Liu & Jordan tilted-surface model. A horizontal surface receives a global irradiance of 800 W/m², consisting of 500 W/m² direct and 300 W/m² diffuse. Compute the total radiation on a south-facing surface tilted at 30° from horizontal. The angle of incidence on the tilted surface is 25°, the ground albedo is 0.2, and the zenith angle is 40°.* (7 Marks)

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Step 1 — The Angstrom-Type Correlation**

The Angstrom correlation relates the *clearness index* (ratio of global to extraterrestrial radiation on the horizontal surface) to the *sunshine fraction*:

$$\frac{\bar{H}}{\bar{H_o}} = a + b\left(\frac{\bar{n}}{\bar{N}}\right)$$

where:
- $\bar{H}, \bar{H_o}$ = monthly mean daily global and extraterrestrial radiation (MJ/m²/day)
- $\bar{n}$ = mean daily actual sunshine hours
- $\bar{N}$ = maximum possible sunshine hours (theoretical day length)
- $a, b$ = empirical regression coefficients (site-specific)

**Step 2 — Calculate the clearness index**

$$K_T = \frac{\bar{H}}{\bar{H_o}} = \frac{16.5}{30} = 0.55$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the sunshine fraction**

$$\frac{\bar{n}}{\bar{N}} = \frac{7}{12} = 0.5833$$

**Step 4 — Verify the Angstrom equation**

$$a + b\left(\frac{\bar{n}}{\bar{N}}\right) = 0.30 + 0.50 \times 0.5833 = 0.30 + 0.2917 = 0.5917$$

**Step 5 — Compare with measured clearness index**

Measured $K_T = 0.550$, predicted $K_T = 0.592$.

The difference is $\Delta = 0.042$ (about 7.1 % relative). This small discrepancy is typical — empirical coefficients $a, b$ are usually fitted to a multi-year dataset and have an uncertainty of ±5–10 %.

**Answer:** The Angstrom relation is *approximately verified*. Clearness index $K_T$ = **0.55** (overcast/medium).

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 — The Liu & Jordan Tilted-Surface Model**

Liu and Jordan (1963) proposed an *isotropic sky* model in which the diffuse sky radiation is uniform across the entire celestial hemisphere. The total radiation on a tilted surface is the sum of three components:

$$I_T = I_b \cos\theta + I_d \left(\frac{1 + \cos\beta}{2}\right) + I \rho_g \left(\frac{1 - \cos\beta}{2}\right)$$

where:
- $I_b \cos\theta$ = beam radiation on the tilted surface
- $I_d \left(\frac{1 + \cos\beta}{2}\right)$ = diffuse radiation on the tilted surface, with $\frac{1 + \cos\beta}{2}$ being the **sky view factor**
- $I \rho_g \left(\frac{1 - \cos\beta}{2}\right)$ = ground-reflected radiation, with $\frac{1 - \cos\beta}{2}$ being the **ground view factor**
- $\beta$ = tilt angle, $\rho_g$ = ground albedo, $I$ = total global horizontal irradiance

**Assumptions:** (i) the sky is *isotropic* — diffuse radiation is uniform over the hemisphere; (ii) the ground is a *Lambertian reflector* — reflected radiation is uniform over the lower hemisphere; (iii) the surface is a *flat plate*.

**Step 2 — Compute the beam component on the tilted surface**

$$I_b \cos\theta = 500 \times \cos(25°) = 500 \times 0.9063 = 453.2 \; \text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the diffuse component on the tilted surface**

$$I_d \left(\frac{1 + \cos\beta}{2}\right) = 300 \times \left(\frac{1 + \cos(30°)}{2}\right) = 300 \times \left(\frac{1 + 0.866}{2}\right) = 300 \times 0.933 = 279.9 \; \text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute the ground-reflected component**

$$I \rho_g \left(\frac{1 - \cos\beta}{2}\right) = 800 \times 0.2 \times \left(\frac{1 - \cos(30°)}{2}\right) = 160 \times \left(\frac{1 - 0.866}{2}\right) = 160 \times 0.067 = 10.7 \; \text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Total tilted radiation**

$$I_T = 453.2 + 279.9 + 10.7 = 743.8 \; \text{W/m}^2$$

**Conversion Logic:** The total tilted radiation (744 W/m²) is *less* than the horizontal (800 W/m²) in this case. This is because the Sun is **closer to the zenith (40°) than to the collector normal at 30° tilt** — i.e., the tilt is *suboptimal* for this particular zenith angle. If β were closer to θz, $I_T$ would be higher than 800 W/m². (This is the geometric proof that a tilt of (φ − δ) for a south-facing collector is optimal on an annual basis at low latitudes.)

**Answer:** $I_T$ ≈ **744 W/m²**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Section Before the Exam)**

### ★ Core Definitions (1-line each)
- **Solar Constant (I_sc):** 1367 W/m² — flux at 1 AU, perpendicular to beam, outside atmosphere.
- **Air Mass (AM):** Path-length ratio = $\sec\theta_z$. STC uses AM 1.5.
- **Declination (δ):** Sun's angular position N/S of equator; varies ±23.45° via Cooper's equation.
- **Hour Angle (ω):** Sun's east-west position; ω = 15(t_s − 12)°; 15°/h.
- **Clearness Index (K_T):** $\bar{H}/\bar{H_o}$ — measure of atmospheric clarity.
- **Sunshine Fraction:** $\bar{n}/\bar{N}$ — measure of cloud cover.

### ★ Master Formula Set (all 12 in Section 2.7)
- Carry this table to the exam hall mentally. Every KTU problem in this module reduces to a *combination* of these 12 equations.

### ★ Numerical Anchors to Memorize
- I_sc = 1367 W/m²
- Earth's orbital eccentricity = 0.0167
- Earth's axial tilt = 23.45°
- STC irradiance = 1000 W/m² at AM 1.5, 25 °C
- Typical ground albedo: 0.2 (grass), 0.3 (dry soil), 0.6 (snow)

### ★ Top 5 Exam Pitfalls
1. **Sign of δ** — positive in northern summer, negative in northern winter.
2. **Hour angle sign** — negative morning, zero at noon, positive afternoon.
3. **Sunrise/sunset ω_s** — formula has a NEGATIVE sign: $\cos\omega_s = -\tan\phi\tan\delta$.
4. **Trigonometric mode** — set your calculator to **DEG** (not RAD) unless specified otherwise.
5. **R² vs. r² in correlations** — Angstrom-type uses a *linear* form; don't confuse with quadratic.

### ★ KTU Frequently Asked Sub-Topics
1. Numericals on $\bar{H_o}$ calculation (very common — 7-mark questions).
2. Numerical on tilted-surface radiation using Liu & Jordan (very common — 7-mark questions).
3. Derivation of sunrise hour angle (frequently asked as 3-mark derivation).
4. Pyrheliometer vs. pyranometer (definition + diagram — 3-mark short answer).
5. AM 1.5 G spectrum reasoning (definition + application — 3-mark short answer).
6. Angstrom correlation numericals (frequently appears in problems on estimation when measured data is sparse).

### ★ Engineering Wisdom to Quote
- *Liu & Jordan (1963) isotropic sky model* is the workhorse of PV yield simulation software like PVsyst and SAM.
- *AM 1.5G spectrum* (ASTM G173) is the **de facto** worldwide standard for PV module rating.
- *K_T* values: 0.0–0.35 overcast, 0.35–0.55 partly cloudy, 0.55–0.75 mostly clear, 0.75–1.0 clear sky.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Principles of Solar Thermal Energy Conversion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT631 - RENEWABLE ENERGY AND GREEN TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Principles of Solar Thermal Energy Conversion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Basic Principles of Solar Thermal Energy Conversion

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Solar Thermal Energy Conversion (STEC)** is the direct thermodynamic process of harnessing the electromagnetic radiant energy emitted by the Sun and transforming it into usable thermal energy (heat) by means of specialized collector surfaces, working fluids, and heat-transfer mechanisms. Unlike photovoltaic conversion (which converts sunlight directly into electricity via the photoelectric effect), solar thermal conversion preserves the energy in the form of sensible or latent heat, which can subsequently be used for space heating, water heating, industrial process heat, refrigeration, or electricity generation through thermodynamic cycles (e.g., Rankine, Brayton).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) explicitly mandates that students must clearly differentiate between **Solar Thermal** and **Solar Photovoltaic** pathways. Solar thermal = heat → optional secondary mechanical/electrical conversion. Solar PV = direct quantum → electrical conversion.

The fundamental governing principle relies on **radiative heat transfer** governed by the Stefan–Boltzmann Law and **spectral absorption–emission selectivity** (Kirchhoff's Law), where a surface engineered to have high absorptance ($\alpha$) in the solar spectrum ($\lambda \approx 0.25 \text{ to } 2.5 \, \mu m$) and low thermal emittance ($\varepsilon$) in the infrared re-emission spectrum ($\lambda > 2.5 \, \mu m$) maximizes net energy capture.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine two identical black garden hoses lying on a sunlit lawn at noon. One hose is left open and transparent; the other is wrapped with a thin sheet of glossy black polyethylene. After two hours, the transparent hose is barely lukewarm, but the wrapped hose delivers scalding water.

**Why the difference?**
- The transparent hose allows most short-wave solar photons to pass straight through, heating only a small volume of water inefficiently.
- The black-wrapped hose absorbs nearly all incident photons ($\alpha \approx 0.95$), converts them to molecular kinetic energy at the inner wall, and conducts that heat into the water.
- The glossy outer layer of the wrapping reflects the long-wave infrared re-radiation back inward (low $\varepsilon$), preventing the heat from escaping.

This is, in essence, the principle behind every solar flat-plate collector, parabolic trough, and solar cooker on Earth.

### 1.3 Core Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Solar Constant | $I_{sc}$ | **1367** (old) / **1361** (new SI) | $W/m^2$ |
| Extraterrestrial Normal Irradiance | $I_{on}$ | 1367 | $W/m^2$ |
| Earth's Orbital Eccentricity | $e$ | 0.0167 | dimensionless |
| Solar Spectral Peak Wavelength | $\lambda_{max}$ | **0.48** (visible green) | $\mu m$ |
| Sun's Surface Temperature | $T_{sun}$ | **5778** | K |
| Earth's Mean Orbital Radius | $r_m$ | $1.496 \times 10^{11}$ | m |
| Stefan–Boltzmann Constant | $\sigma$ | $5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ | $W/m^2 K^4$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Solar Constant** represents the radiant flux density received on a surface oriented perpendicular to the Sun's rays at the **mean Earth–Sun distance** of 1 AU, outside any atmospheric attenuation. It is the foundational boundary condition for all KTU numerical problems on extraterrestrial radiation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Conversion Chain: From Photon to Useful Work

The journey of solar energy from space to a useful heat output involves **five sequential physical stages**:

1. **Extraterrestrial Emission (E1):** The Sun, behaving as a near-blackbody at ~5778 K, emits photons isotropically according to Planck's distribution. The radiant exitance at its surface is governed by the Stefan–Boltzmann Law:
$$M_{sun} = \sigma \, T_{sun}^{4}$$

2. **Geometric Attenuation (E2):** As photons travel the 1 AU distance to Earth, the spherical spreading loss reduces the flux by an inverse-square geometric factor. The resulting flux on a surface normal to the rays is the **Solar Constant** $I_{sc}$.

3. **Atmospheric Interaction (E3):** Passing through the atmosphere, photons undergo **scattering** (Rayleigh, Mie), **absorption** (by $O_3, H_2O, CO_2$), and **reflection** (by clouds). This produces three surface components:
   - **Beam (Direct) Radiation $I_b$** — collimated rays from the solar disk.
   - **Diffuse Radiation $I_d$** — scattered sky radiation.
   - **Ground-Reflected Radiation $I_r$** — albedo contribution.

4. **Selective Absorption (E4):** The collector surface absorbs a fraction $\alpha$ of incident radiation. The absorbed flux becomes sensible heat at the absorber plate.

5. **Thermal Transfer & Losses (E5):** Heat is transferred to the working fluid via convection. Simultaneously, the absorber loses heat through **conduction**, **convection**, and **re-radiation**. The **net useful heat gain** $Q_u$ is:
$$Q_u = A_c \, S - Q_{losses}$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every high-yield formula that appears in KTU ESE questions for PEAGT631. Memorize the symbol definitions and unit conventions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Solar Constant on day *n* | $I_{on} = I_{sc}\left(1 + 0.033\cos\!\left(\dfrac{360\,n}{365}\right)\right)$ | $I_{sc} = 1367\,W/m^2$ |
| 2 | Air Mass (for $\beta > 0$) | $AM = \dfrac{1}{\sin \beta}$ | $\beta$ = solar altitude angle |
| 3 | Declination Angle | $\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left(\dfrac{360(284+n)}{365}\right)$ | $n$ = day of year (Jan 1 = 1) |
| 4 | Hour Angle | $\omega = 15^\circ (t_{solar} - 12)$ | Negative in morning, positive in afternoon |
| 5 | Equation of Time | $EoT = 9.87\sin(2B) - 7.53\cos(B) - 1.5\sin(B)$ | $B = 360(n-81)/365$ |
| 6 | Solar Altitude | $\sin\beta = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$ | $\phi$ = latitude |
| 7 | Solar Azimuth (approx.) | $\sin\gamma_s = \dfrac{\cos\delta\sin\omega}{\cos\beta}$ | Measured from South |
| 8 | Angle of Incidence (south-tilted) | $\cos\theta = \sin\delta\sin(\phi-\beta) + \cos\delta\cos(\phi-\beta)\cos\omega$ | $\beta$ = tilt angle |
| 9 | Beam Radiation on Tilted Surface | $I_{b,t} = I_b \, R_b$ | $R_b = \dfrac{\cos\theta}{\cos\theta_z}$ |
| 10 | Total Radiation on Tilted | $I_T = I_b R_b + I_d \!\left(\dfrac{1+\cos\beta}{2}\right) + I \rho_g \!\left(\dfrac{1-\cos\beta}{2}\right)$ | $\rho_g$ = ground albedo |
| 11 | Useful Heat Gain (F-chart base) | $Q_u = A_c F_R\!\left[S - U_L (T_{fi} - T_a)\right]^{+}$ | $F_R$ = heat removal factor |
| 12 | Stefan–Boltzmann Re-radiation | $Q_{rad} = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, A \, (T_s^{4} - T_{sky}^{4})$ | $T_{sky} \approx T_a - 6$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** For 3-mark short questions, students are expected to write the formula **with** the symbol definition in one sentence. Forgetting units in the final numerical answer is the #1 cause of mark deductions in Module 1 problems.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The mastery of solar thermal conversion principles directly underpins the design of:
- **Domestic solar water heaters** (flat-plate and evacuated tube)
- **Concentrated Solar Power (CSP)** plants (parabolic trough, solar tower, dish-Stirling)
- **Solar dryers** for agricultural produce (spices, fish, tea)
- **Solar desalination** units in coastal Kerala
- **Building-integrated solar thermal facades** (BIST)
- **Solar cooling** absorption chillers for tropical climates

In the Indian and KTU context, the **MNRE (Ministry of New and Renewable Energy)** mandates solar thermal installations as part of the **ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code) 2017** for commercial complexes above 100 kW thermal demand.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Earth–Sun Distance Correction Factor $E_0$

**Problem Context:** The Earth orbits the Sun in an ellipse with the Sun at one focus. The Earth–Sun distance varies by $\pm 1.67\%$ over the year, causing the extraterrestrial irradiance to vary accordingly.

**Starting Point — Kepler's First Law (elliptical geometry):**

Let $r$ be the instantaneous Earth–Sun distance, $r_m$ the mean distance (1 AU), and $e$ the orbital eccentricity. By definition of an ellipse:
$$\frac{r_m}{r} = \frac{(1+e)^2}{(1+e^2 - 2e\cos\nu)^{1/2}}$$

where $\nu$ is the true anomaly (angular position measured from perihelion).

However, in solar engineering, a simpler empirical relation is universally adopted. The instantaneous extraterrestrial normal irradiance on day $n$ is:
$$I_{on} = I_{sc}\left[1 + e\cos\!\left(\frac{360\,n}{360.25}\right)\right]^{2} \times \frac{r_m^{2}}{r^{2}}$$

Substituting the mean Earth–Sun relation and linearizing (Spencer, 1971):
$$I_{on} \approx I_{sc}\left(1 + 0.033\cos\!\left(\frac{360\,n}{365}\right)\right)$$

This is the **standard KTU formula** that students must reproduce in the ESE.

### Step-by-Step Numerical Walkthrough

**Given:** $n = 172$ (June 21, summer solstice), $I_{sc} = 1367\,W/m^2$.

**Step 1:** Compute the argument of the cosine.
$$\theta = \frac{360 \times 172}{365} = 169.6438^\circ$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the cosine.
$$\cos(169.6438^\circ) = -0.9877$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the eccentricity correction.
$$I_{on} = 1367 \times \left[1 + 0.033 \times (-0.9877)\right]$$

**Step 4:** Compute the bracket.
$$1 - 0.0326 = 0.9674$$

**Step 5:** Final multiplication.
$$I_{on} = 1367 \times 0.9674 = 1322.43 \, W/m^2$$

**Interpretation:** On the summer solstice, Earth is near aphelion (farthest from the Sun), so $I_{on}$ is at its annual minimum, even though the Northern Hemisphere receives its longest insolation day.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Solar Declination Angle $\delta$

The Earth's rotational axis is tilted at $23.45^\circ$ relative to the orbital plane (ecliptic). This tilt, combined with the orbital motion, causes the Sun's apparent declination (the celestial latitude of the Sun) to vary sinusoidally throughout the year.

**Geometric Foundation:**
The projection of the Earth's axial tilt onto the ecliptic plane produces the declination as a sinusoidal function of the day angle $\Gamma$:
$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left(\frac{360(284+n)}{365}\right)$$

**Derivation Logic:**

Let the Earth–Sun vector lie in the ecliptic plane. The Sun's apparent position projected onto the equatorial coordinate system gives the declination. The argument $\left(\frac{360(284+n)}{365}\right)$ accounts for the fact that the **maximum positive declination** ($+23.45^\circ$) occurs at the **summer solstice**, which falls around $n = 172$ (June 21). Plugging $n = 172$:
$$284 + 172 = 456 \implies \frac{360 \times 456}{365} = 449.75^\circ$$

Reducing modulo $360^\circ$: $449.75 - 360 = 89.75^\circ$. The sine of $89.75^\circ \approx 0.9999 \approx 1$, confirming $\delta \approx +23.45^\circ$. ✓

### Numerical Walkthrough for Kerala ($\phi = 10.0^\circ$ N)

**Day:** $n = 80$ (around March 21, vernal equinox).
**Step 1:** Argument.
$$X = \frac{360(284+80)}{365} = \frac{360 \times 364}{365} = 358.85^\circ$$

**Step 2:** Cosine? We need sine, since the formula has sine.
$$\sin(358.85^\circ) = -0.0199$$

**Step 3:** Compute declination.
$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \times (-0.0199) = -0.466^\circ$$

**Interpretation:** Near the equinoxes, declination is approximately zero — the Sun is directly overhead at the equator. Kerala, at $10^\circ$ N, is therefore relatively close to sub-solar point at solar noon.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Beam Radiation on a Tilted Surface — The Tilt Factor $R_b$

A common KTU numerical problem asks: "Given the horizontal beam irradiance, latitude, declination, and tilt angle, find the beam irradiance on a tilted surface."

**Starting Principle:**
The beam irradiance on any surface is given by:
$$I_{b,\text{surface}} = I_b \cos\theta$$

where $\theta$ is the angle of incidence between the incoming beam and the surface normal.

**Step 1 — Cosine of zenith angle on horizontal:**
For a horizontal surface, the normal is vertical, and the zenith angle $\theta_z = 90^\circ - \beta$. Thus:
$$\cos\theta_z = \sin\beta$$

Using the solar altitude formula:
$$\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$$

**Step 2 — Cosine of incidence on tilted surface (south-facing, tilt $\beta_t$):**

The geometric identity (Iqbal, 1983; Duffie & Beckman) for a south-facing tilted surface in the Northern Hemisphere is:
$$\cos\theta_t = \sin\delta\sin(\phi - \beta_t) + \cos\delta\cos(\phi - \beta_t)\cos\omega$$

**Step 3 — Define the Tilt Ratio:**
$$\boxed{R_b = \frac{\cos\theta_t}{\cos\theta_z}}$$

This ratio, when multiplied by the horizontal beam irradiance, gives the beam component on the tilted surface.

### Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Given:** Latitude $\phi = 13^\circ$ N (Thiruvananthapuram), declination $\delta = 0^\circ$ (equinox), tilt $\beta_t = 13^\circ$, hour angle $\omega = 0^\circ$ (solar noon), horizontal beam $I_b = 800\,W/m^2$.

**Step 1:** Compute $\cos\theta_z$.
$$\cos\theta_z = \sin(13^\circ)\sin(0^\circ) + \cos(13^\circ)\cos(0^\circ)\cos(0^\circ)$$
$$\cos\theta_z = 0 + 0.9744 \times 1 = 0.9744$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\cos\theta_t$.
$$\cos\theta_t = \sin(0^\circ)\sin(13^\circ - 13^\circ) + \cos(0^\circ)\cos(13^\circ - 13^\circ)\cos(0^\circ)$$
$$\cos\theta_t = 0 + 1 \times 1 \times 1 = 1.0$$

**Step 3:** Compute $R_b$.
$$R_b = \frac{1.0}{0.9744} = 1.0262$$

**Step 4:** Compute tilted beam.
$$I_{b,t} = 800 \times 1.0262 = 821.0 \, W/m^2$$

**Interpretation:** Because the tilt angle equals the latitude at the equinox, the surface is oriented exactly perpendicular to the noon Sun, yielding a 2.6% gain over the horizontal beam.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Loss Mechanisms in a Solar Flat-Plate Collector

| Loss Type | Symbol | Physical Origin | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|---|
| Top Convection | $U_t$ | Wind-driven convection from glass cover | $2.8 - 4.5 \, W/m^2K$ |
| Bottom Conduction | $U_b$ | Insulation (typically fiberglass) | $0.5 - 1.0 \, W/m^2K$ |
| Side Conduction | $U_e$ | Frame edge effects | $0.3 - 0.6 \, W/m^2K$ |
| Radiation from Absorber | $U_r$ | Stefan–Boltzmann re-emission | $3.5 - 5.5 \, W/m^2K$ |
| Total Loss Coefficient | $U_L$ | Sum of above | $4.0 - 8.0 \, W/m^2K$ |

The useful heat gain of a flat-plate collector is then:
$$Q_u = A_c F_R \left[I_T(\tau\alpha) - U_L (T_{fi} - T_a)\right]$$

where $F_R$ is the **heat removal factor** (ratio of actual useful gain to the gain if the entire absorber were at fluid inlet temperature), $\tau$ is the cover transmittance, and $\alpha$ is the absorber absorptance.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Solar Thermal Conversion Pathway

The following Mermaid block diagram traces the full transformation from incident photons to useful thermal output, highlighting the major subsystem blocks and energy-flow nodes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["SUN_CORE_FUSION"] --> B["PHOTON_EMISSION_5778K"]
    B --> C["GEOMETRIC_SPREADING_LOSS"]
    C --> D["ATMOSPHERIC_FILTER"]
    D --> E["BEAM_COMPONENT_Ib"]
    D --> F["DIFFUSE_COMPONENT_Id"]
    D --> G["GROUND_REFLECTED_Ir"]
    E --> H["TILTED_COLLECTOR_SURFACE"]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I["SELECTIVE_ABSORBER_PLATE"]
    I --> J["CONDUCTION_TO_TUBE"]
    J --> K["CONVECTION_TO_FLUID"]
    K --> L["WORKING_FLUID_OUTLET"]
    H --> M["LOSSES_BLOCK"]
    M --> N["TOP_LOSS_Ut"]
    M --> O["BOTTOM_LOSS_Ub"]
    M --> P["RERADIATION_Ur"]
    M --> Q["WIND_LOSS_Uwind"]
    L --> R["USEFUL_THERMAL_OUTPUT_Qu"]
```

**Engineering Interpretation of Each Node:**
- `SUN_CORE_FUSION`: Proton-proton chain reaction at ~15 million K core temperature.
- `GEOMETRIC_SPREADING_LOSS`: Inverse-square attenuation reduces $3.846 \times 10^{26}\,W$ of total luminosity to $1367\,W/m^2$ at 1 AU.
- `ATMOSPHERIC_FILTER`: Produces the three components by Beer–Lambert extinction and Rayleigh scattering.
- `SELECTIVE_ABSORBER_PLATE`: Engineered surface (e.g., black chrome, cermet) with $\alpha > 0.95$ and $\varepsilon < 0.10$.
- `LOSSES_BLOCK`: All irreversible parasitic thermal channels that degrade collector efficiency.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a Flat-Plate Collector System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S["INCOMING_SOLAR_RADIATION"] --> G["GLASS_COVER_TRANSMITTANCE_tau"]
    G --> AB["ABSORBER_PLATE_ALPHA"]
    AB --> TU["RIBBED_TUBE_NETWORK"]
    AB -.-> R["RERADIATION_LOSS"]
    AB -.-> CV["CONVECTION_LOSS"]
    AB -.-> CD["CONDUCTION_TO_BACK_INSULATION"]
    TU --> IN["FLUID_INLET_Tfi"]
    TU --> OUT["FLUID_OUTLET_Tfo"]
    OUT --> P["CIRCULATION_PUMP_OR_THERMOSYPHON"]
    P --> TK["STORAGE_TANK"]
    TK --> LD["DOMESTIC_OR_PROCESS_LOAD"]
```

**Subsystem Roles:**
- `GLASS_COVER_TRANSMITTANCE_tau`: Provides greenhouse enclosure; reduces convective losses by ~40%.
- `RIBBED_TUBE_NETWORK`: Enhances conductive coupling between absorber and fluid.
- `THERMOSYPHON`: Passive buoyancy-driven flow loop (no pump, used in Kerala rooftop installations).
- `STORAGE_TANK`: Insulated reservoir for time-shifting solar gain to off-sunshine demand.

## 4.3 Solar Geometry Reference Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["OBSERVER_LATITUDE_PHI"] --> H["HORIZON_PLANE"]
    P --> Z["ZENITH_DIRECTION"]
    Z --> SZ["SOLAR_ZENITH_ANGLE_theta_z"]
    H --> SA["SOLAR_ALTITUDE_ANGLE_beta"]
    SZ --- SA
    P --> SZ
    P --> N["NORTH_AXIS"]
    SZ --> AS["SOLAR_AZIMUTH_ANGLE_gamma"]
    N --- AS
    P --> TP["TILTED_PLANE_NORMAL"]
    TP --> TI["TILT_ANGLE_beta_t"]
    H --> TI
    SZ --> AI["ANGLE_OF_INCIDENT_THETA_i"]
    TP --> AI
```

**Geometric Reading Guide:**
- $\theta_z$ + $\beta$ = $90^\circ$ (always).
- $\theta_i$ governs how perpendicular the beam strikes the tilted surface.
- $\gamma$ (azimuth) determines whether the surface is N, S, E, or W-facing.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Solar Thermal Energy Conversion.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (Expected KTU Pattern):**

> Solar Thermal Energy Conversion (STEC) is the process of converting the electromagnetic radiant energy emitted by the Sun into usable thermal energy (heat) by absorbing the radiation on a darkened surface and transferring the resulting heat to a working fluid.
>
> (1 mark) Conversion pathway: Sun → radiation → absorber → heat → working fluid.
>
> (1 mark) Distinguishing feature: Energy is retained as **heat** (not converted directly to electricity as in PV).
>
> (1 mark) Application linkage: Used for water heating, space heating, drying, desalination, and indirectly for power generation via heat engines.

### Question 2: State and explain the Stefan–Boltzmann Law as applied to solar re-radiation losses.
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

> (1 mark) Statement: The radiant exitance of a blackbody is proportional to the fourth power of its absolute temperature: $M = \sigma T^{4}$.
>
> (1 mark) Re-radiation context: An absorber plate at temperature $T_p$ re-emits energy to the sky at $T_{sky} \approx T_a - 6\,K$. The net radiative heat loss is:
> $$Q_{rad} = \varepsilon \sigma A (T_p^{4} - T_{sky}^{4})$$
>
> (1 mark) Engineering implication: This is why **selective coatings** (high $\alpha$, low $\varepsilon$) are critical — they suppress the fourth-power temperature penalty for the absorber.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU ESE 2024 Scheme mandates an **internal choice** in every Part B question. Two independent alternatives are provided below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Solar Geometry and Tilted-Surface Irradiance

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
Derive the expression for the **declination angle** $\delta$ as a function of the day of the year $n$. Explain its physical significance and compute $\delta$ for $n = 172$, $n = 80$, and $n = 355$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Physical meaning:** (1 mark)
The declination angle is the angular position of the Sun's rays relative to the equatorial plane. It arises from the $23.45^\circ$ tilt of Earth's rotational axis.

**Step 2 — Sinusoidal model:** (2 marks)
Since the Earth's orbit is approximately circular and the axial tilt is constant, $\delta$ varies sinusoidally with the day angle:
$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left(\frac{360(284+n)}{365}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Sign convention:** (1 mark)
$\delta > 0$ in the Northern Hemisphere summer, $\delta < 0$ in winter, and $\delta \approx 0$ at the equinoxes.

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluations:** (3 marks)

| Day ($n$) | Argument $\frac{360(284+n)}{365}$ | $\sin(\cdot)$ | $\delta$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 172 | $449.75^\circ \rightarrow 89.75^\circ$ | $+0.9999$ | $+23.45^\circ$ (Summer Solstice) |
| 80  | $358.85^\circ$ | $-0.0199$ | $-0.47^\circ$ (Vernal Equinox) |
| 355 | $630.85^\circ \rightarrow 270.85^\circ$ | $-0.9999$ | $-23.45^\circ$ (Winter Solstice) |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
For a location at latitude $\phi = 20^\circ$ N on a day when $\delta = 0^\circ$, calculate the **hour angle at sunset** and the **day length**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Sunset hour angle condition:** (2 marks)
Sunset occurs when the solar altitude $\beta = 0$, i.e., $\cos\theta_z = 0$. Substituting into the zenith formula:
$$\sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega_s = 0$$

**Step 2 — Solving for $\omega_s$:** (2 marks)
With $\delta = 0$, this simplifies to:
$$\cos\phi \cos\omega_s = 0 \implies \cos\omega_s = 0 \implies \omega_s = 90^\circ$$

**Step 3 — Day length:** (1 mark)
$$N = \frac{2\omega_s}{15^\circ/\text{hr}} = \frac{2 \times 90^\circ}{15^\circ/\text{hr}} = 12 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 4 — Verification and interpretation:** (2 marks)
At equinox, the Sun is on the celestial equator, and every latitude experiences a **12-hour day** (ignoring atmospheric refraction). This result is independent of latitude — a beautiful symmetry of solar geometry.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Solar Radiation on Tilted Surfaces and Collector Performance

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
A flat-plate solar collector is installed at $\phi = 15^\circ$ N, tilted at $\beta_t = 15^\circ$, facing due south, on a day with $\delta = 10^\circ$. At solar noon ($\omega = 0$), the horizontal beam irradiance is $I_b = 900\,W/m^2$ and diffuse irradiance is $I_d = 150\,W/m^2$. Ground albedo $\rho_g = 0.2$.
Calculate the **total irradiance on the tilted surface**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute horizontal beam on tilted surface (tilt factor).** (2 marks)

For $\omega = 0$, the zenith angle formula reduces to $\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta$.

$$\cos\theta_z = \sin(15^\circ)\sin(10^\circ) + \cos(15^\circ)\cos(10^\circ)$$
$$= (0.2588)(0.1736) + (0.9659)(0.9848)$$
$$= 0.0449 + 0.9513 = 0.9962$$

For tilted surface: $\cos\theta_t = \sin\delta\sin(\phi - \beta_t) + \cos\delta\cos(\phi - \beta_t)$
$$= \sin(10^\circ)\sin(0^\circ) + \cos(10^\circ)\cos(0^\circ)$$
$$= 0 + 0.9848 = 0.9848$$

**Step 2 — Tilt factor $R_b$.** (1 mark)
$$R_b = \frac{0.9848}{0.9962} = 0.9886$$

**Step 3 — Beam on tilted surface.** (1 mark)
$$I_{b,t} = 900 \times 0.9886 = 889.7 \, W/m^2$$

**Step 4 — Diffuse on tilted (isotropic sky model).** (1 mark)
$$I_{d,t} = I_d \times \frac{1+\cos\beta_t}{2} = 150 \times \frac{1+0.9659}{2} = 150 \times 0.9830 = 147.4 \, W/m^2$$

**Step 5 — Reflected ground component.** (1 mark)
$$I_{r,t} = (I_b + I_d) \times \rho_g \times \frac{1-\cos\beta_t}{2}$$
$$= 1050 \times 0.2 \times \frac{1-0.9659}{2} = 210 \times 0.01705 = 3.58 \, W/m^2$$

**Step 6 — Total tilted irradiance.** (1 mark)
$$I_T = 889.7 + 147.4 + 3.58 = 1040.7 \, W/m^2$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
For the same collector, the following performance parameters are given:
- $A_c = 2.5\,m^2$
- $(\tau\alpha) = 0.85$
- $U_L = 5.5\,W/m^2K$
- $F_R = 0.85$
- Ambient temperature $T_a = 30^\circ C = 303\,K$
- Fluid inlet temperature $T_{fi} = 50^\circ C = 323\,K$

Calculate the **useful heat gain** and the **instantaneous efficiency**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Use the Hottel–Whillier–Bliss equation:** (2 marks)
$$Q_u = A_c F_R \left[I_T(\tau\alpha) - U_L(T_{fi} - T_a)\right]^{+}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the absorbed flux term:** (1 mark)
$$I_T(\tau\alpha) = 1040.7 \times 0.85 = 884.6 \, W/m^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the loss term:** (1 mark)
$$U_L(T_{fi} - T_a) = 5.5 \times (50 - 30) = 110 \, W/m^2$$

**Step 4 — Net flux and useful heat:** (2 marks)
$$\text{Net flux} = 884.6 - 110 = 774.6 \, W/m^2$$
$$Q_u = 2.5 \times 0.85 \times 774.6 = 1646.0 \, W = 1.646 \, kW$$

**Step 5 — Instantaneous thermal efficiency:** (1 mark)
$$\eta = \frac{Q_u}{A_c \cdot I_T} = \frac{1646.0}{2.5 \times 1040.7} = 0.6328 = 63.28\%$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in Declination:** Students frequently forget that $\delta$ is **negative** from autumn to spring equinox. KTU examiners specifically award 0.5 marks for the correct sign in numerical answers.
>
> 2. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing hour angles in degrees with day lengths in hours without dividing by $15^\circ/\text{hr}$ is a recurring error. Always convert angles to radians when invoking trig functions, but keep degrees for time conversions.
>
> 3. **Forgetting $(\tau\alpha)$ Product:** The Hottel–Whillier–Bliss equation requires the **product** of transmittance and absorptance, not their sum. Approximately 1 in 4 students commits this error.
>
> 4. **Albedo Direction:** Ground-reflected radiation should be computed using the **global horizontal** irradiance $(I_b + I_d)$, not just $I_b$. A common mistake.
>
> 5. **Day Numbering:** KTU uses **January 1 = 1** (not zero). Confusing this shifts all derived angles by one full day.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Module 1**

- ☐ **Solar Constant** = $1367\,W/m^2$ at 1 AU, outside the atmosphere, on a surface normal to the rays.
- ☐ **Eccentricity correction** applies the $\pm 3.3\%$ seasonal modulation using $n$ (day of year).
- ☐ **Three radiation components:** Beam $I_b$, Diffuse $I_d$, Reflected $I_r$.
- ☐ **Air Mass (AM)** = $\dfrac{1}{\sin\beta}$ for $\beta > 0^\circ$; AM 1.5 is the standard PV test spectrum.
- ☐ **Declination** follows a **sinusoid** with amplitude $23.45^\circ$ and zero crossings at the equinoxes.
- ☐ **Hour Angle** = $15^\circ \times (\text{solar hour} - 12)$; negative morning, positive afternoon.
- ☐ **Solar Altitude + Zenith = $90^\circ$**; this identity simplifies many derivations.
- ☐ **Angle of Incidence** uses **3D solar geometry** involving $\phi$, $\delta$, $\omega$, and $\beta_t$.
- ☐ **Tilt Factor $R_b$** = ratio of beam on tilted to beam on horizontal; never exceeds ~1.15 for daily averages.
- ☐ **Isotropic Sky Diffuse Model** assumes uniform sky radiance; simple but overestimates early/late hours.
- ☐ **Hottel–Whillier–Bliss** is the **workhorse efficiency equation** for flat-plate collectors.
- ☐ **Selective surfaces** are characterized by **$\alpha/\varepsilon$ ratio** — high values (≥ 15) are preferred.
- ☐ **Stefan–Boltzmann** governs **re-radiation loss** and scales with **$T^{4}$** — temperature control is critical.
- ☐ For Kerala ($\phi \approx 8\text{–}13^\circ$ N), the **optimal year-round tilt** is approximately equal to latitude.
- ☐ **Kerala-specific MNRE data:** Average daily insolation = $4.5 \text{–} 5.5\,kWh/m^2/day$ — among India's highest.
- ☐ Always state **boundary conditions** and **assumptions** explicitly before solving any KTU numerical problem.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flat Plate and Concentrating Collectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT631 - RENEWABLE ENERGY AND GREEN TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Flat Plate and Concentrating Collectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Solar Thermal Collectors — The Big Picture

A **solar collector** is a device engineered to absorb incident solar radiation, convert it into thermal energy (heat), and transfer that heat to a working fluid (water, air, oil, or molten salt). In the KTU 2024 syllabus for *Renewable Energy and Green Technology*, solar collectors are classified into two principal families:

| Family | Operating Temperature | Tracking | Concentration |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Flat Plate Collector (FPC)** | Low–Medium (30 °C – 100 °C) | Stationary | 1× (no concentration) |
| **Concentrating Collector (CC)** | Medium–High (100 °C – 1000 °C+) | Single or dual-axis tracking | 2× – 10,000× |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 — Defining Scope**
> Module 1 strictly tests: classification, construction, orientation, performance (Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation), heat loss coefficients of FPCs, and a comparative study with focusing-type collectors.

## 1.2 Flat Plate Collector (FPC) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** A **Flat Plate Collector (FPC)** is a stationary, rectangular solar thermal device that captures *both beam and diffuse* solar radiation on a flat absorber surface, converting it into useful heat at temperatures up to approximately **100 °C** for domestic and commercial low-grade thermal applications.

### Intuitive Analogy — The Parked Car

Imagine leaving a black dashboard car in the Kerala summer sun with the windows closed. The car's interior becomes unbearably hot — even though the glass (windshield) is *transparent*. This is exactly the **greenhouse effect** that powers an FPC. Sunlight enters through a glass cover, hits a blackened absorber plate, gets converted into long-wave infrared heat, and *cannot escape* back through the glass. A network of tubes welded to the absorber plate carries water, which absorbs this trapped heat — just like the metal seatbelt buckles burning your hand on a hot afternoon.

## 1.3 Concentrating Collector — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** A **Concentrating (or Focusing) Solar Collector** uses optical elements — reflectors (mirrors) or refractors (lenses) — to focus a large area of sunlight onto a small absorber area, thereby achieving high temperatures (**>150 °C**) suitable for industrial process heat, power generation, and solar thermal fuels.

### Intuitive Analogy — The Magnifying Glass

Remember using a magnifying glass as a child to focus sunlight onto a dry leaf and burn a hole through it? The leaf temperature rose to over **400 °C** at the focal spot — even though your hand holding the lens only felt mild warmth. The concentrating collector is the industrial adult version of that magnifying glass, where a parabolic mirror replaces the lens and a metal tube replaces the leaf.

## 1.4 Classification Hierarchy (Syllabus Map)

```
Solar Collectors
├── Non-Concentrating (Flat Plate)
│   ├── Liquid-type FPC
│   └── Air-type FPC
└── Concentrating
    ├── Line-Focusing (1-D tracking)
    │   ├── Parabolic Trough
    │   ├── Fresnel Lens
    │   └── Compound Parabolic Concentrator (CPC)
    └── Point-Focusing (2-D tracking)
        ├── Parabolic Dish (Dish-Stirling)
        └── Power Tower (Heliostat Field)
```

## 1.5 Physical Constants Used Throughout

- **Solar Constant (Iₛc) = 1367 W/m²**
- **Stefan-Boltzmann constant (σ) = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴**
- **Air mass (AM) 1.5 reference spectrum** for terrestrial collector testing
- **Standard test condition:** Incident radiation **I = 1000 W/m²**, ambient **Tₐ = 30 °C**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Flat Plate Collector — Constructional Anatomy

An FPC is a sandwich of five engineered layers. From top to bottom:

### Layer 1 — Transparent Cover (Glazing)
- **Material:** Low-iron tempered glass (transmissivity τ ≈ 0.91)
- **Function:** Allows short-wave solar radiation; blocks long-wave re-radiation
- **Thickness:** 3 mm – 4 mm
- **Single / double / triple glazing** progressively raises stagnation temperature but reduces transmittance

### Layer 2 — Air Gap (Trickle Space)
- **Thickness:** 25 mm – 40 mm
- **Function:** Suppresses convective heat loss from absorber to glass

### Layer 3 — Absorber Plate
- **Material:** Copper (best) / Aluminum / Steel
- **Surface coating:** Selective black coating (e.g., black chrome, black nickel)
  - **High absorptivity (α ≈ 0.95)** for short-wave radiation
  - **Low emissivity (ε ≈ 0.10)** for long-wave re-radiation
- **Geometry:** Tube-and-fin (most common) or serpentine

### Layer 4 — Insulation (Back + Side)
- **Material:** Rock wool / Glass wool / Polyurethane foam
- **Thickness:** 50 mm – 100 mm (back); 25 mm (sides)
- **Function:** Minimizes conductive losses to ambient

### Layer 5 — Casing / Frame
- **Material:** Galvanized iron / Aluminum
- **Function:** Structural rigidity, weatherproofing

## 2.2 Working Principle — Step by Step

1. **Solar radiation (I)** strikes the glass cover at incidence angle θ.
2. A fraction **(τα)** — transmittance–absorptance product — penetrates to the absorber.
3. Absorber converts radiation into **sensible heat** in the metal plate.
4. Heat conducts along the plate fins to the riser tubes.
5. The fluid (water/air) flowing inside the tubes absorbs heat (gain **Qᵤ**).
6. Residual heat is lost through **top (Uₜ), bottom (U_b), and edge (U_e)** paths.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why tilted, not horizontal?** In Kerala (latitude ≈ 10° N), the optimum tilt angle β is approximately **(latitude ± 15°)** — giving ≈ 10° in summer and 25° in winter for maximum annual yield.

## 2.3 Heat Loss Analysis — The Three Escapes

The single most exam-critical sub-topic. Every KTU question bank asks students to derive or apply these coefficients.

### (a) Top Loss Coefficient (Uₜ)

Conduction + convection + radiation from **absorber plate (Tₚ)** upward through the air gap to the **glass cover (T_g)** and from the glass to the **ambient (Tₐ)**.

$$
U_t = \left[\frac{N}{\frac{C}{T_p}\left[\frac{T_p - T_a}{N+f}\right]^{e}} + \frac{1}{h_w}\right]^{-1} + \frac{\sigma(T_p^2 + T_a^2)(T_p + T_a)}{\varepsilon_p^{-1} + \varepsilon_g^{-1} - 1}
$$

Where:

- **N** = number of glass covers (typically 1, 2, or 3)
- **f** = (1 + 0.089 h_w − 0.1166 h_w εₚ)(1 + 0.07866 N)
- **e** = 0.43 (1 − 100/Tₚ) — empirical exponent
- **C** = 365.9 (1 − 0.03883 N + 0.0001588 N²) — empirical constant (vertical plate)
- **h_w** = wind-convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K), typically **5 – 15 W/m²·K**

### (b) Bottom Loss Coefficient (U_b)

Conduction through insulation of thickness **L_b** and thermal conductivity **k_b**:

$$
U_b = \frac{k_b}{L_b}
$$

### (c) Side / Edge Loss Coefficient (U_e)

Heat loss from the perimeter where the absorber meets the frame:

$$
U_e = \frac{k_e \cdot L_e \cdot P}{A_c}
$$

Where:

- **Lₑ** = thickness of edge insulation
- **kₑ** = thermal conductivity of edge insulation
- **P** = perimeter of the collector
- **A_c** = gross collector area

### (d) Overall Heat Loss Coefficient (U_L)

The single composite metric used in every efficiency calculation:

$$
U_L = U_t + U_b + U_e
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Typical values:** U_L ≈ **4 – 7 W/m²·K** for well-designed FPCs.

## 2.4 The Hottel–Whillier–Bliss Equation — The Crown Jewel

This is the **single most important equation** in KTU Module 1. It quantifies the useful heat gain of an FPC.

$$
Q_u = A_c \cdot F_R \left[(\tau \alpha) I - U_L (T_{f,i} - T_a)\right]
$$

And the instantaneous thermal efficiency:

$$
\eta_i = \frac{Q_u}{A_c I} = F_R (\tau \alpha) - F_R U_L \frac{(T_{f,i} - T_a)}{I}
$$

Where:

- **F_R** = Heat Removal Factor (typically **0.7 – 0.95**)
- **F_R (τα)** = Optical efficiency intercept on η-axis
- **F_R U_L** = Slope of the efficiency curve (losses)
- **T_{f,i}** = Fluid inlet temperature
- **T_a** = Ambient temperature
- **I** = Incident solar radiation on tilted surface

## 2.5 Heat Removal Factor (F_R) — Klein's Derivation

The heat removal factor accounts for the fact that the fluid leaving the collector is *cooler* than the absorber plate. Klein (1978) gave:

$$
F_R = \frac{\dot{m} C_p}{A_c U_L} \left[1 - \exp\left(-\frac{A_c U_L F'}{\dot{m} C_p}\right)\right]
$$

Where **F'** is the **collector efficiency factor** (depends on fin efficiency F and tube–plate geometry).

## 2.6 Concentrating Collectors — Classification & Mathematics

### Concentration Ratio (C)

$$
C = \frac{A_a}{A_r} = \frac{\text{Aperture (collector) area}}{\text{Receiver (absorber) area}}
$$

For tracking types:

| Collector Type | Concentration Ratio | Tracking |
|---|---|---|
| Parabolic Trough | 10 – 80 | Single-axis (N–S) |
| Linear Fresnel | 10 – 40 | Single-axis |
| Parabolic Dish | 500 – 5000 | Dual-axis |
| Solar Power Tower | 300 – 1500 | Dual-axis (heliostats) |

### Maximum Achievable Concentration (Thermodynamic Limit)

From the second law and black-body radiation (Ries & Wolf, limiting case):

$$
C_{max} = \frac{1}{\sin^2 \theta_s}
$$

Where **θ_s** is the half acceptance angle. For θ_s = 0 (perfect optics, no diffuse capture):

$$
C_{max} = \frac{T_{receiver}^4}{T_{sun}^4} \quad (\text{ideal point focus})
$$

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table — Memorize for ESE**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_u = A_c F_R [(\tau\alpha)I - U_L(T_{f,i}-T_a)]$ | Useful heat gain | W |
| 2 | $\eta_i = F_R(\tau\alpha) - F_R U_L \frac{(T_{f,i}-T_a)}{I}$ | Instantaneous efficiency | dimensionless |
| 3 | $U_L = U_t + U_b + U_e$ | Total heat loss coefficient | W/m²·K |
| 4 | $U_b = k_b / L_b$ | Bottom loss coefficient | W/m²·K |
| 5 | $U_e = (k_e L_e P)/A_c$ | Edge loss coefficient | W/m²·K |
| 6 | $C = A_a / A_r$ | Concentration ratio | dimensionless |
| 7 | $F_R = \frac{\dot m C_p}{A_c U_L}[1-\exp(-A_c U_L F'/(\dot m C_p))]$ | Heat removal factor | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\theta_z$ at noon = $\vert \phi - \delta \vert$ | Solar zenith angle | degrees |
| 9 | $\beta_{opt} = \phi - \delta$ | Optimum tilt for max daily insolation | degrees |
| 10 | $\delta = 23.45 \sin(360(284+n)/365)$ | Solar declination | degrees |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **FPCs** dominate the rooftop solar water heater market (>70% globally) — Indian MNRE mandates BIS-standards (IS 12933) tested at IS 12976.
- **Parabolic Troughs** power plants like *NOOR III* (Morocco, 150 MW) and *SEGS* (USA, 354 MW).
- **Solar Towers** like *Ivanpah* (USA, 392 MW) and *Gemasolar* (Spain, 24-h storage) demonstrate grid-scale solar thermal with molten-salt thermal storage.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation — Overall Heat Loss Coefficient U_L
