# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the General Formula for monthly average daily total solar radiation on a tilted surface. List its three physical components.**

**Model Answer:**

The General Formula is a fundamental energy balance equation used to estimate the total solar radiation incident on a tilted (inclined) collector surface, expressed as:

$$\bar{H}_T = \bar{H}_b \bar{R}_b + \bar{H}_d \left( \frac{1 + \cos\beta}{2} \right) + \bar{H} \rho_g \left( \frac{1 - \cos\beta}{2} \right)$$

The **three physical components** are:
1. **Beam (Direct) Radiation Term:** $\bar{H}_b \bar{R}_b$ — direct solar rays projected onto the tilted plane.
2. **Diffuse (Sky) Radiation Term:** $\bar{H}_d (1+\cos\beta)/2$ — scattered sky radiation based on the isotropic sky assumption.
3. **Ground-Reflected Radiation Term:** $\bar{H} \rho_g (1-\cos\beta)/2$ — radiation reflected from the terrain (albedo effect).

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the geometric tilt factor $R_b$? Write its simplified expression for a south-facing surface in the Northern Hemisphere.**

**Model Answer:**

The geometric tilt factor $\bar{R}_b$ is the ratio of the **monthly average daily beam radiation on a tilted surface** to that on a **horizontal surface**. It accounts for the geometric projection of the solar beam onto the inclined collector.

$$\bar{R}_b = \frac{\cos(\phi - \beta) \cos\delta \sin\omega_s' + (\pi/180)\,\omega_s' \sin(\phi - \beta) \sin\delta}{\cos\phi \cos\delta \sin\omega_s + (\pi/180)\,\omega_s \sin\phi \sin\delta}$$

Where:
- $\phi$ = latitude, $\beta$ = tilt angle, $\delta$ = declination
- $\omega_s$ = sunset hour angle on horizontal surface
- $\omega_s'$ = sunset hour angle on the tilted surface

---

## 📕 Part B: 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

---

### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the General Formula for monthly average daily total solar radiation on a tilted surface, starting from the instantaneous (hourly) form. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a location at latitude $\phi = 12°\text{N}$ (Kochi, Kerala), calculate the total monthly average daily radiation on a south-facing surface tilted at $\beta = 12°$, given:
- Monthly average daily global radiation on horizontal: $\bar{H} = 18.5 \text{ MJ/m}^2\cdot\text{day}$
- Monthly average daily diffuse radiation on horizontal: $\bar{H}_d = 7.2 \text{ MJ/m}^2\cdot\text{day}$
- Declination for the average day of the month: $\delta = +10°$
- Ground reflectivity: $\rho_g = 0.2$ **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Instantaneous Form Assumption:**
We start with the **isotropic sky model** (Liu & Jordan, 1963), which assumes diffuse sky radiation is uniformly distributed across the entire sky dome. The instantaneous total radiation on a tilted surface $I_T$ is the sum of three components:

$$I_T = I_{b,T} + I_{d,T} + I_{r,T}$$

**Step 2 — Beam Component Derivation:**
The direct normal irradiance is $I_{bn}$. On a horizontal surface:

$$I_b = I_{bn} \cos\theta_z$$

On a tilted surface, the angle of incidence $\theta$ differs from the zenith angle $\theta_z$:

$$I_{b,T} = I_{bn} \cos\theta$$

Taking the ratio:

$$R_b = \frac{I_{b,T}}{I_b} = \frac{\cos\theta}{\cos\theta_z}$$

Therefore:

$$I_{b,T} = I_b R_b$$

**Step 3 — Diffuse Component (Sky View Factor):**
A surface tilted at angle $\beta$ from horizontal has a sky view factor of $(1+\cos\beta)/2$ (this is the standard result from view-factor integration of a flat plate to a hemisphere). Hence:

$$I_{d,T} = I_d \cdot \frac{1 + \cos\beta}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Reflected Component (Ground View Factor):**
Similarly, the ground view factor is $(1-\cos\beta)/2$. The reflected intensity is:

$$I_{r,T} = I \cdot \rho_g \cdot \frac{1 - \cos\beta}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Time Integration to Daily:**
The daily total is the time integral from sunrise to sunset (sunrise hour angle $\omega_1$ to sunset hour angle $\omega_2$ on the tilted surface):

$$H_T = \int_{\omega_1}^{\omega_2} I_T \, d\omega$$

**Step 6 — Monthly Averaging:**
Using the representative day of the month (declination $\delta_{\text{rep}}$) and averaging over $N$ days:

$$\bar{H}_T = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} H_{T,i}$$

**Step 7 — Final General Formula:**

$$\boxed{\bar{H}_T = \bar{H}_b \bar{R}_b + \bar{H}_d \left( \frac{1 + \cos\beta}{2} \right) + \bar{H} \rho_g \left( \frac{1 - \cos\beta}{2} \right)}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Isotropic diffuse sky radiation (Liu-Jordan model)
2. Uniform ground albedo (single $\rho_g$ value)
3. No shadowing from surrounding terrain
4. Clear-sky during the integration period
5. Single representative day per month (climatological averaging)

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $\phi = 12°$, $\beta = 12°$, $\delta = 10°$, $\rho_g = 0.2$
- $\bar{H} = 18.5 \text{ MJ/m}^2\cdot\text{day}$, $\bar{H}_d = 7.2 \text{ MJ/m}^2\cdot\text{day}$

**Step 1 — Compute $\bar{H}_b$:**

$$\bar{H}_b = \bar{H} - \bar{H}_d = 18.5 - 7.2 = 11.3 \text{ MJ/m}^2\cdot\text{day}$$

**Step 2 — Sunset Hour Angle on Horizontal Surface:**

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi \tan\delta) = \cos^{-1}(-\tan 12° \cdot \tan 10°)$$

$$\tan 12° = 0.2126, \quad \tan 10° = 0.1763$$

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-0.2126 \times 0.1763) = \cos^{-1}(-0.03748) = 92.15°$$

**Step 3 — Sunset Hour Angle on Tilted Surface:**

$$\omega_s' = \cos^{-1}[-\tan(\phi - \beta)\tan\delta] = \cos^{-1}[-\tan(0°)\tan 10°]$$

$$\phi - \beta = 0° \Rightarrow \tan(0°) = 0$$

$$\omega_s' = \cos^{-1}(0) = 90°$$

**Step 4 — Take Minimum:**
$$\omega_s' = \min\{92.15°, 90°\} = 90°$$

**Step 5 — Compute $R_b$ Numerator:**
$$\text{Num} = \cos(0°)\cos(10°)\sin(90°) + (\pi/180)(90°)\sin(0°)\sin(10°)$$

$$= 1 \times 0.9848 \times 1 + (0.01745)(90)(0)(0.1736)$$

$$= 0.9848 + 0 = 0.9848$$

**Step 6 — Compute $R_b$ Denominator:**
$$\text{Den} = \cos(12°)\cos(10°)\sin(92.15°) + (\pi/180)(92.15°)\sin(12°)\sin(10°)$$

$$= (0.9781)(0.9848)(\sin 92.15°) + (0.01745)(92.15)(0.2079)(0.1736)$$

$$= (0.9781)(0.9848)(0.9992) + 0.0579$$

$$= 0.9627 + 0.0579 = 1.0206$$

**Step 7 — Compute $\bar{R}_b$:**

$$\bar{R}_b = \frac{0.9848}{1.0206} = 0.9649$$

**Step 8 — Beam Term:**

$$\bar{H}_{b,T} = \bar{H}_b \bar{R}_b = 11.3 \times 0.9649 = 10.903 \text{ MJ/m}^2\cdot\text{day}$$

**Step 9 — Diffuse Term:**

$$\bar{H}_{d,T} = 7.2 \times \frac{1 + \cos 12°}{2} = 7.2 \times \frac{1 + 0.9781}{2} = 7.2 \times 0.9891 = 7.121 \text{ MJ/m}^2\cdot\text{day}$$

**Step 10 — Reflected Term:**

$$\bar{H}_{r,T} = 18.5 \times 0.2 \times \frac{1 - \cos 12°}{2} = 3.7 \times \frac{1 - 0.9781}{2} = 3.7 \times 0.01095 = 0.0405 \text{ MJ/m}^2\cdot\text{day}$$

**Step 11 — Total Tilted Radiation:**

$$\bar{H}_T = 10.903 + 7.121 + 0.0405 = \mathbf{18.07 \text{ MJ/m}^2\cdot\text{day}}$$

---

### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the physical significance of the three terms in the General Formula. Describe the isotropic sky model and the sky view factor concept. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PV installation is planned at Thiruvananthapuram ($\phi = 8.5°\text{N}$). The horizontal global radiation for June is $\bar{H} = 19.8 \text{ MJ/m}^2\cdot\text{day}$ and the diffuse radiation is $\bar{H}_d = 9.0 \text{ MJ/m}^2\cdot\text{day}$. The declination on June 21 is $\delta = +23.45°$. Find the optimum tilt angle $\beta$ that maximizes beam radiation (using $R_b$) for the given month, and calculate the corresponding total tilted radiation. Use $\rho_g = 0.2$. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Physical Significance (7 marks):**

**Term 1 — Beam Term ($\bar{H}_b \bar{R}_b$):**
This represents the **direct solar radiation** that travels in straight lines from the sun to the collector. The factor $\bar{R}_b$ is a **purely geometric projection ratio** that converts the horizontal beam to the tilted plane. Physically, when the sun is high in the sky (small zenith angle) and the collector is tilted toward the sun, $\bar{R}_b > 1$, meaning the tilted surface receives **more** beam radiation than the horizontal — a key reason PV panels are tilted.

**Term 2 — Diffuse Term ($\bar{H}_d (1+\cos\beta)/2$):**
This represents the **scattered sky radiation** from clouds, aerosols, and air molecules. The **isotropic sky model** (Liu & Jordan, 1963) assumes this scattered radiation is uniformly distributed over the entire celestial hemisphere. The factor $(1+\cos\beta)/2$ is the **view factor** of a tilted flat surface to a hemispherical sky — geometrically equal to the average of $\cos\beta$ weighted over the sky dome.

- When $\beta = 0°$ (horizontal): view factor = 1 (sees full sky)
- When $\beta = 90°$ (vertical): view factor = 0.5 (sees half sky)
- This is the reason **diffuse contribution decreases** as tilt angle increases.

**Term 3 — Reflected Term ($\bar{H} \rho_g (1-\cos\beta)/2$):**
This represents radiation **reflected from the ground** (or snow, water, sand). The factor $\rho_g$ is the **albedo** (0 for perfectly absorbing ground, 1 for perfect mirror). The view factor $(1-\cos\beta)/2$ is the geometric complement of the sky view factor — a tilted surface sees **less ground** when tilted, but the contribution increases with $\rho_g$ and $\beta$.

- For snow: $\rho_g \approx 0.7$ (highly reflective)
- For dry grass: $\rho_g \approx 0.2$
- For dark asphalt: $\rho_g \approx 0.05$

**Isotropic Sky Model — Why It Works:**
- Computationally simple and **accurate within ±5%** for **overcast sky** conditions.
- Underestimates contribution near the **horizon** (where sky is brighter) and the **circumsolar region** (where sky is bright near the sun).
- More advanced models (Hay-Davies, Perez, Klucher) add anisotropy corrections.

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $\phi = 8.5°$, $\delta = 23.45°$, $\rho_g = 0.2$
- $\bar{H} = 19.8$, $\bar{H}_d = 9.0$ MJ/m²·day
- $\bar{H}_b = 19.8 - 9.0 = 10.8$ MJ/m²·day

**Step 1 — To Maximize Beam Radiation, Set $\beta = \phi$ on the equinox-adjusted date, OR specifically for June (summer in N. Hemisphere, sun is north of zenith in Kerala):**

For Thiruvananthapuram ($\phi = 8.5°$ N), the solar declination on June 21 is $\delta = +23.45°$. Since $\delta > \phi$, the sun is **north of the zenith** (passes slightly to the north of overhead). For a fixed south-facing surface, the optimal tilt that maximizes beam radiation is:

$$\beta_{\text{opt}} = |\phi - \delta| = |8.5° - 23.45°| = 14.95° \approx 15°$$

(For a fixed monthly operation in summer, the sun's average position is high; tilting slightly backward toward the south maximizes the integrated beam.)

**Step 2 — Compute Sunset Hour Angle on Horizontal Surface:**

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi \tan\delta) = \cos^{-1}(-\tan 8.5° \times \tan 23.45°)$$

$$\tan 8.5° = 0.1493, \quad \tan 23.45° = 0.4335$$

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-0.1493 \times 0.4335) = \cos^{-1}(-0.0647) = 93.71°$$

**Step 3 — Sunset Hour Angle on Tilted Surface (with $\beta = 15°$):**

$$\phi - \beta = 8.5° - 15° = -6.5°$$

$$\omega_s' = \cos^{-1}[-\tan(-6.5°)\tan 23.45°] = \cos^{-1}[0.1139 \times 0.4335]$$

$$= \cos^{-1}(0.0494) = 87.17°$$

**Step 4 — Take Minimum:**
$$\omega_s' = \min\{93.71°, 87.17°\} = 87.17°$$

**Step 5 — Compute $R_b$ Numerator:**
$$\text{Num} = \cos(-6.5°)\cos(23.45°)\sin(87.17°) + (\pi/180)(87.17°)\sin(-6.5°)\sin(23.45°)$$

$$= (0.9936)(0.9178)(0.9987) + (0.01745)(87.17)(-0.1132)(0.3977)$$

$$= 0.9108 + (-0.0698) = 0.8410$$

**Step 6 — Compute $R_b$ Denominator:**
$$\text{Den} = \cos(8.5°)\cos(23.45°)\sin(93.71°) + (\pi/180)(93.71°)\sin(8.5°)\sin(23.45°)$$

$$= (0.9890)(0.9178)(0.9981) + (0.01745)(93.71)(0.1478)(0.3977)$$

$$= 0.9058 + 0.1021 = 1.0079$$

**Step 7 — Compute $\bar{R}_b$:**

$$\bar{R}_b = \frac{0.8410}{1.0079} = 0.8345$$

**Step 8 — Beam Term:**

$$\bar{H}_{b,T} = 10.8 \times 0.8345 = 9.013 \text{ MJ/m}^2\cdot\text{day}$$

**Step 9 — Diffuse Term:**

$$\bar{H}_{d,T} = 9.0 \times \frac{1 + \cos 15°}{2} = 9.0 \times \frac{1 + 0.9659}{2} = 9.0 \times 0.9830 = 8.847 \text{ MJ/m}^2\cdot\text{day}$$

**Step 10 — Reflected Term:**

$$\bar{H}_{r,T} = 19.8 \times 0.2 \times \frac{1 - \cos 15°}{2} = 3.96 \times 0.01705 = 0.0675 \text{ MJ/m}^2\cdot\text{day}$$

**Step 11 — Total Tilted Radiation at $\beta = 15°$:**

$$\bar{H}_T = 9.013 + 8.847 + 0.0675 = \mathbf{17.93 \text{ MJ/m}^2\cdot\text{day}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Note that $\bar{H}_T < \bar{H}$ for this case (17.93 vs 19.8). This is because in summer at low latitudes (Kerala), the sun is **near zenith** at noon, and tilting the panel **reduces** the integrated beam radiation because the sun spends time both north and south of zenith. The horizontal surface actually captures more total energy in this scenario — a classic **low-latitude summer anomaly**. The tilt may be optimized for **annual** yield rather than monthly peak.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Degree vs. Radian Error:** The $\pi/180$ factor in the $R_b$ formula is often **forgotten** by students, leading to a **1.5–2 mark deduction**. Always include it explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing $\bar{H}_b$ and $\bar{H}$:** Students frequently use $\bar{H}$ (global) instead of $\bar{H}_b$ (beam only) in the beam term. The beam term **must use beam radiation only** — 2 marks lost if $\bar{H}$ is used.
> 3. **Forgetting the $\min$ in $\omega_s'$:** The sunset hour angle on a tilted surface must be the **minimum** of the two possible values, not always the tilted one. This is a 1-mark subtle deduction.
> 4. **View Factor Boundary Check:** Always check that the diffuse term is $0.5 \bar{H}_d$ at $\beta = 90°$ and equals $\bar{H}_d$ at $\beta = 0°$. If your answer violates this, you've made an error.
> 5. **Units Consistency:** Keep all radiation values in $\text{MJ/m}^2\cdot\text{day}$ consistently. Mixing with $\text{kWh/m}^2\cdot\text{day}$ loses 1 mark unless conversion is shown.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> ### 📌 Rapid Revision Checklist — The General Formula

- ✅ The **General Formula** estimates **monthly average daily total radiation** on a **tilted surface** $\bar{H}_T$.

- ✅ It has **three components**: **Beam**, **Diffuse**, and **Ground-Reflected** — all linear additions.

- ✅ The **Beam Term** uses $\bar{R}_b$, a **geometric projection ratio** that depends on **latitude**, **tilt**, and **declination**.

- ✅ The **Diffuse Term** uses the **isotropic sky assumption** with view factor $(1+\cos\beta)/2$.

- ✅ The **Reflected Term** uses the **albedo** $\rho_g$ with view factor $(1-\cos\beta)/2$.

- ✅ The **sunset hour angle** on a horizontal surface: $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi \tan\delta)$.

- ✅ The **sunset hour angle on a tilted surface** is the **minimum** of the horizontal and tilted values.

- ✅ The $\pi/180$ factor is required in $R_b$ because $\omega_s$ is in **degrees** and the $\sin\omega_s$ term is **dimensionless** — the factor unifies units.

- ✅ **Standard values to memorize:**
  - $G_{sc} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^2$ (solar constant)
  - $\rho_g = 0.2$ (typical ground albedo)
  - **Albedo values:** snow ≈ 0.7, grass ≈ 0.2, asphalt ≈ 0.05, sand ≈ 0.4

- ✅ **Limitations of the formula:**
  - Ignores **circumsolar brightening** (use Hay-Davies for tropical sun)
  - Ignores **horizon brightening** (use Klucher model for mountainous regions)
  - Less accurate under **partly cloudy** tropical conditions (Kerala monsoon)

- ✅ **Engineering Application in Kerala (KTU context):**
  - For **annual** maximum yield: $\beta_{\text{opt}} \approx \phi$ (latitude)
  - For **summer** (April–Aug): $\beta_{\text{opt}} \approx \phi - 15°$ (Kochi → 0°; flat)
  - For **winter** (Nov–Feb): $\beta_{\text{opt}} \approx \phi + 15°$ (Kochi → 25–30°)

- ✅ **KTU Pattern:** In exams, always state the **three components**, the **assumptions**, and **show all steps** in the numerical problem — partial marking is generous only when the formula and units are correctly identified.

- ✅ The formula is the **gateway equation** to PV system design — mastering it is essential for PEAGT631 Module 2 and subsequent design-based questions in Part B.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lift and Drag. Basics of Wind Energy Conversion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT631 - RENEWABLE ENERGY AND GREEN TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Lift and Drag. Basics of Wind Energy Conversion # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Aerodynamic Lift **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):** **Lift** is the aerodynamic force component generated perpendicular to the direction of the relative wind (oncoming airflow) acting on a body — typically an airfoil-shaped wind turbine blade. Lift is the *useful force* that rotates a wind turbine rotor. **Mathematical Expression:** $$L = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,A\,V^{2}\,C_{L}$$ Where: - $\rho$ = Air density (kg/m³), standard value **1.225 kg/m³** at sea level - $A$ = Reference area (m²) — usually the blade planform area - $V$ = Relative wind velocity (m/s) - $C_{L}$ = **Lift coefficient** (dimensionless) > [!IMPORTANT] > Lift is **NOT** produced because air moves faster over the top of the blade (the "longer path" myth). Lift is produced due to the **pressure differential** caused by circulation around the airfoil and the deflection of air (Newton's Third Law). ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine holding your hand flat out of a moving car window. When you tilt your hand slightly upward (angle of attack), the air pushes your hand **up** — that upward push is lift. The harder you tilt (up to a point), the greater the lift. A wind turbine blade is just a highly engineered version of your hand, shaped like an airplane wing to maximize this upward (tangential) force. ## 1.2 Aerodynamic Drag **Formal Definition:** **Drag** is the aerodynamic force component acting **parallel** to the direction of the relative wind. It represents the resistance offered by the air to the motion of the body. **Mathematical Expression:** $$D = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,A\,V^{2}\,C_{D}$$ Where $C_{D}$ = **Drag coefficient** (dimensionless). ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Using the same hand-out-of-car-window experiment: you feel a strong force trying to **push your hand backward** against the direction of travel. That backward force is drag. On a wind turbine, drag is a *parasitic* force — it does not produce useful rotation and instead creates unwanted thrust on the tower. > [!NOTE] > Both Lift and Drag are proportional to the **square of velocity**. This is why doubling wind speed quadruples the aerodynamic forces, and the available wind power increases by a factor of 8 (cubic relationship — covered in §3). ## 1.3 Angle of Attack & Lift-to-Drag Ratio - **Angle of Attack (α):** The angle between the **chord line** of the airfoil and the **relative wind direction**. - **Stall:** When α exceeds a critical value (~15°–20°), the airflow separates from the upper surface, $C_{L}$ drops sharply, and $C_{D}$ rises — called *aerodynamic stall*. - **Lift-to-Drag Ratio (L/D):** The figure of merit of an airfoil. Wind turbine blades are designed for the **highest possible L/D** at the operating angle of attack. ## 1.4 Basics of Wind Energy Conversion **Formal Definition:** Wind Energy Conversion is the process of converting the **kinetic energy** of moving air into **mechanical energy** (via rotor rotation) and then into **electrical energy** (via a generator). The complete conversion chain is: > **Wind Kinetic Energy → Rotor Mechanical Energy → Generator Electrical Energy → Grid/Load** > [!IMPORTANT] > The maximum fraction of kinetic energy that an *ideal* wind turbine can extract from the wind is **59.3%**, known as the **Betz Limit**. No turbine — however perfectly designed — can exceed this limit because the air must retain some kinetic energy to leave the rotor disk. ### Conceptual Analogy Think of wind like water flowing through a horizontal waterwheel. You cannot slow the water to zero (else no more water flows through), and you cannot let it pass at full speed (else you extract nothing). Betz showed mathematically that the *sweet spot* where power extraction is maximum is when the wind slows to **1/3 of its original speed** behind the rotor. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Force Decomposition on a Wind Turbine Blade The relative wind hitting a rotating blade has two components: 1. **Axial (normal) component** $V_{a}$ — along the wind direction 2. **Tangential component** $V_{t} = \omega r$ — perpendicular, due to blade rotation **Resultant relative velocity:** $$V_{rel} = \sqrt{V_{a}^{2} + V_{t}^{2}}$$ **Flow angle (φ):** $$\tan\phi = \frac{V_{a}}{V_{t}} = \frac{V_{a}}{\omega r}$$ **The lift force $L$ (perpendicular to $V_{rel}$) is decomposed into:** - **Tangential force** $F_{t} = L \sin\phi - D \cos\phi$ → produces **Torque** - **Axial (thrust) force** $F_{a} = L \cos\phi + D \sin\phi$ → produces **Tower Thrust** ## 2.2 Power Available in the Wind Consider a wind stream of cross-sectional area $A$ moving at velocity $V$. **Mass flow rate of air:** $$\dot{m} = \rho A V \quad [\text{kg/s}]$$ **Kinetic energy per second = Power in the wind:** $$P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\dot{m}\,V^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho A V^{3} \quad [\text{Watts}]$$ > [!NOTE] > The cubic relationship $P \propto V^{3}$ is the single most important fact in wind energy. A small increase in wind speed produces a dramatic increase in available power. This is why wind farm siting is critically dependent on accurate wind speed measurement. ## 2.3 Power Extracted and Power Coefficient $$P_{extracted} = C_{p} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho A V^{3}$$ Where $C_{p}$ = **Power coefficient** (fraction of wind power converted to useful mechanical power). ## 2.4 Betz Coefficient (Betz Limit) The **maximum theoretical** $C_{p}$ for any wind turbine: $$C_{p,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \quad (59.3\%)$$ ## 2.5 Tip Speed Ratio (TSR, λ) $$\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{V} = \frac{2\pi R N}{60\,V}$$ Where: - $\omega$ = Angular velocity (rad/s) - $R$ = Rotor radius (m) - $N$ = Rotor speed (rpm) - $V$ = Free wind speed (m/s) **TSR regimes:** - **λ ≈ 1**: Savonius / drag-type rotors (slow, low efficiency) - **λ ≈ 6–8**: Modern horizontal-axis three-blade turbines (high efficiency) - **λ > 10**: Multi-blade high-speed turbines ## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V^{2} C_{L}$ | Lift force | N | | 2 | $D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V^{2} C_{D}$ | Drag force | N | | 3 | $V_{rel} = \sqrt{V_{a}^{2} + V_{t}^{2}}$ | Relative wind speed | m/s | | 4 | $\tan\phi = V_{a} / (\omega r)$ | Flow angle relation | — | | 5 | $P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V^{3}$ | Power in wind | W | | 6 | $P_{ext} = C_{p} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V^{3}$ | Power extracted | W | | 7 | $C_{p,\max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$ | Betz limit | — | | 8 | $\lambda = \omega R / V$ | Tip speed ratio | — | | 9 | $E = P \cdot t$ | Energy produced (Wh) | Wh | | 10 | $F_{a} = L \cos\phi + D \sin\phi$ | Thrust on tower | N | | 11 | $F_{t} = L \sin\phi - D \cos\phi$ | Tangential force | N | | 12 | $A = \pi R^{2}$ | Rotor swept area | m² | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** These equations are used by wind turbine manufacturers (Vestas, Siemens-Gamesa, Suzlon) during blade aerodynamic design, control system tuning, and Annual Energy Production (AEP) estimation. The Betz limit is used as a benchmark — real machines achieve $C_{p} \approx 0.35$–$0.45$. ## 2.7 Classification of Wind Turbines - **By Axis:** Horizontal Axis (HAWT) vs Vertical Axis (VAWT) - **By Power:** Micro (<1 kW), Small (1–100 kW), Medium (100 kW–1 MW), Large (>1 MW) - **By Speed:** Constant Speed vs Variable Speed - **By Blade Design:** Lift-type (high speed) vs Drag-type (low speed) # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Power Available in the Wind **Step 1 — Define the control volume.** Consider a tube of air of cross-section $A$ moving at velocity $V$. In time $\Delta t$, the length of air column passing a fixed point is $V \cdot \Delta t$. **Step 2 — Compute the mass of air.** $$m = \rho \cdot \text{Volume} = \rho \cdot A \cdot V \cdot \Delta t$$ **Step 3 — Write the kinetic energy of this mass.** $$KE = \tfrac{1}{2} m V^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} (\rho A V \Delta t) V^{2}$$ **Step 4 — Power = Energy per unit time.** $$P = \frac{KE}{\Delta t} = \frac{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho A V \Delta t \cdot V^{2}}{\Delta t}$$ **Step 5 — Simplify.** $$\boxed{P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho A V^{3} \quad [\text{Watts}]}$$ > **Conversion logic:** The $\Delta t$ cancels because kinetic energy is proportional to mass (which contains time) divided by time. This leaves velocity cubed — the famous cubic relationship. --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: The Betz Limit (Betz Coefficient) **Setup (Actuator Disk Model by Albert Betz, 1919):** Let: - $V_{1}$ = Wind velocity far upstream - $V$ = Wind velocity at the rotor disk - $V_{2}$ = Wind velocity far downstream - $A_{1}, A, A_{2}$ = Corresponding cross-sectional areas **Step 1 — Continuity (mass conservation).** Air mass flow rate is constant: $$\rho A_{1} V_{1} = \rho A V = \rho A_{2} V_{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad A V = \text{constant}$$ **Step 2 — Define induction factors.** Let $a$ = **axial induction factor** (fraction by which wind is slowed at disk): $$V = V_{1}(1 - a) \quad \text{and} \quad V_{2} = V_{1}(1 - 2a)$$ **Step 3 — Apply momentum theorem (Newton's 2nd Law to a control volume).** Force on the rotor = Rate of change of momentum: $$F = \dot{m}(V_{1} - V_{2}) = \rho A V (V_{1} - V_{2})$$ Substituting $V$ and $V_{2}$: $$F = \rho A V_{1}(1 - a)\bigl[V_{1} - V_{1}(1 - 2a)\bigr] = \rho A V_{1}^{2}(1 - a)(2a)$$ **Step 4 — Power extracted by rotor.** $$P = F \cdot V = \rho A V_{1}(1 - a) \cdot V_{1}^{2}(1 - a)(2a) \cdot V_{1}(1 - a)$$ Wait — careful: $F \cdot V$ where $V$ is the velocity at the disk. Rewriting cleanly: $$P = F \cdot V_{disk} = \rho A V_{1}^{2}(1 - a)(2a) \cdot V_{1}(1 - a)$$ $$P = 2\rho A V_{1}^{3} a (1 - a)^{2}$$ **Step 5 — Power in upstream wind.** $$P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A_{1} V_{1}^{3}$$ **Step 6 — Power coefficient.** $$C_{p} = \frac{P}{P_{wind}} = \frac{2\rho A V_{1}^{3} a(1 - a)^{2}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho A_{1} V_{1}^{3}} = \frac{4a(1-a)^{2}}{(1 - a \cdot \text{area factor})}$$ Using continuity: $A_{1} = A(1 - a)$ (approximately), giving: $$C_{p} = 4a(1 - a)^{2}$$ **Step 7 — Maximize $C_{p}$.** Differentiate with respect to $a$ and set to zero: $$\frac{dC_{p}}{da} = 4(1-a)^{2} + 4a \cdot 2(1-a)(-1) = 4(1-a)\bigl[(1-a) - 2a\bigr]$$ $$\frac{dC_{p}}{da} = 4(1-a)(1 - 3a) = 0$$ Non-trivial solution: $1 - 3a = 0 \Rightarrow a = 1/3$. **Step 8 — Substitute $a = 1/3$.** $$C_{p,\max} = 4 \cdot \tfrac{1}{3} \cdot \left(1 - \tfrac{1}{3}\right)^{2} = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{9} = \frac{16}{27}$$ **Step 9 — Final result.** $$\boxed{C_{p,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 = 59.3\%}$$ **Step 10 — Optimal downstream velocity.** At $a = 1/3$: $V_{2} = V_{1}(1 - 2a) = V_{1}/3$. > **Conversion logic:** This proves the air must leave the rotor at **1/3 of its incoming speed** to extract maximum energy. If it leaves slower ($V_{2} \to 0$), the air piles up and blocks incoming flow. If it leaves at full speed, no energy is extracted. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example **Problem:** A wind turbine has rotor diameter 80 m, wind speed 12 m/s, air density 1.225 kg/m³. Calculate (a) power in the wind, (b) maximum extractable power (Betz), (c) power at actual $C_{p} = 0.40$. **Solution:** (a) Swept area: $A = \pi R^{2} = \pi (40)^{2} = 5026.5 \text{ m}^{2}$ $$P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(5026.5)(12)^{3} = 5.31 \text{ MW}$$ (b) $P_{Betz} = 0.593 \times 5.31 = 3.15 \text{ MW}$ (c) $P_{actual} = 0.40 \times 5.31 = 2.12 \text{ MW}$ --- ## 3.4 Lift-to-Drag Decomposition Derivation From the force triangle on a blade element: ``` Lift (L) ⟂ V_rel ↗ F_t ─────●─────→ V_rel ↘ Drag (D) ∥ V_rel ↓ F_a ``` Using trigonometry on the relative-velocity triangle with flow angle $\phi$: $$F_{t} = L \sin\phi - D \cos\phi$$ $$F_{a} = L \cos\phi + D \sin\phi$$ **Torque contribution from blade element at radius $r$:** $$dT = F_{t} \cdot r = (L \sin\phi - D \cos\phi) \cdot r$$ **Total torque** (integrate over all blade elements from hub to tip): $$T = \int_{r_{hub}}^{R} N_{b}\,(L \sin\phi - D \cos\phi)\,r\,dr$$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Aerodynamic Forces on a Wind Turbine Blade ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Relative Wind V_rel] --> B[Blade Airfoil Section] B --> C[Lift Force L] B --> D[Drag Force D] C --> E[Tangential Force F_t] C --> F[Axial Thrust F_a] D --> E D --> F E --> G[Rotor Torque T] E --> H[Useful Mechanical Power] F --> I[Tower Loading Thrust] F --> J[Structural Stress on Hub] G --> K[Generator Rotor] H --> K K --> L[Electrical Power Output] ``` ## 4.2 Wind Energy Conversion Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Wind Kinetic Energy
0.5 rho A V cubed] --> P2[Rotor Aerodynamic Stage
Extraction by Betz Limit] P2 --> P3[Gearbox
Speed Multiplication] P3 --> P4[Generator
Electrical Conversion] P4 --> P5[Power Electronics
AC to AC or AC to DC to AC] P5 --> P6[Transformer
Voltage Step Up] P6 --> P7[Grid or Load] P1 -.Power Coefficient Cp.-> P2 P2 -.Cp max 0.593.-> P3 ``` ## 4.3 Power Coefficient vs Tip Speed Ratio Curve ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Tip Speed Ratio lambda] --> B[Low lambda 1 to 3] A --> C[Medium lambda 4 to 8] A --> D[High lambda greater than 10] B --> E[Low Cp below 0.2
Drag dominated] C --> F[Maximum Cp near 0.45
Optimal HAWT region] D --> G[Lower Cp
Multi blade stall type] F --> H[Design Point for Modern Turbines] ``` ## 4.4 Forces on a Blade Element — Vector Decomposition ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Relative Wind V_rel hits blade] --> B[Component along wind axis V_a] A --> C[Component perpendicular to wind omega times r] B --> D[Resultant V_rel at angle phi to rotor plane] C --> D D --> E[Lift L perpendicular to V_rel] D --> F[Drag D parallel to V_rel] E --> G[Resolve L into F_t and F_a] F --> G G --> H[F_t produces torque] G --> I[F_a produces tower thrust] ``` ## 4.5 Classification Tree of Wind Energy Systems ```mermaid flowchart TB WT[Wind Turbines] --> HAWT[Horizontal Axis HAWT] WT --> VAWT[Vertical Axis VAWT] HAWT --> H1[Upwind] HAWT --> H2[Downwind] VAWT --> V1[Savonius] VAWT --> V2[Darrieus] VAWT --> V3[Giromill] H1 --> H1a[3 Blade Modern Utility Scale] H1 --> H1b[2 Blade Moderate Scale] H1 --> H1c[Multi Blade Farm Water Pumping] ``` > [!NOTE] > The Mermaid diagrams above are block-level functional architecture flows showing how forces, power, and subsystems interact in a wind energy conversion system. These are exam-friendly schematic representations that KTU examiners expect in descriptive answers. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper] **Define Lift and Drag coefficients. State the factors on which they depend.** **Model Answer:** The **Lift coefficient** $C_{L}$ is a dimensionless number that quantifies the lift generated by a given airfoil geometry at a specified angle of attack. It is defined as: $$C_{L} = \frac{L}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V^{2}}$$ The **Drag coefficient** $C_{D}$ similarly quantifies the drag force: $$C_{D} = \frac{D}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V^{2}}$$ **Factors on which $C_{L}$ and $C_{D}$ depend:** 1. **Angle of attack (α)** — primary factor; as α increases, $C_{L}$ rises until stall, then drops sharply 2. **Airfoil shape / profile geometry** (camber, thickness ratio) 3. **Reynolds number** of the flow 4. **Surface roughness** of the blade 5. **Mach number** (for compressible flows) --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **What is the Betz limit? Why can no wind turbine exceed it?** **Model Answer:** The **Betz limit** is the maximum fraction of kinetic energy in wind that an ideal wind turbine can extract. Its value is: $$C_{p,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 = 59.3\%$$ **Why it cannot be exceeded:** The derivation (see §3.2) shows that for maximum power extraction, the wind must slow down to exactly one-third of its original speed behind the rotor. If more energy were extracted, the air would be slowed below this optimum, the mass flow rate through the disk would reduce, and net extracted power would *decrease*. The limit arises from conservation of mass and momentum, not from any technological imperfection. --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided) ### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified] **a)** Derive the expression for the power available in the wind. **(7 marks)** **b)** A wind turbine of rotor diameter 60 m operates at a wind speed of 14 m/s. Air density is 1.2 kg/m³. Calculate: (i) power in the wind, (ii) maximum power extractable (Betz limit), and (iii) power output at $C_{p} = 0.38$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation** (Refer to §3.1, complete step-by-step): - Mass flow rate: $\dot{m} = \rho A V$ - Kinetic energy: $KE = \tfrac{1}{2} m V^{2}$ - Substituting $m = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta t = \rho A V \Delta t$ - $KE = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3} \Delta t$ - Power = KE/$\Delta t$ - Final: $P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3}$ ✓ **(7 marks awarded for clear derivation with all steps)** **(b) Numerical Solution:** (i) Swept area: $A = \pi R^{2} = \pi (30)^{2} = 2827.4 \text{ m}^{2}$ $$P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.2)(2827.4)(14)^{3} = 4.66 \text{ MW}$$ (ii) Betz maximum: $$P_{Betz} = 0.593 \times 4.66 = 2.76 \text{ MW}$$ (iii) Actual output: $$P_{actual} = 0.38 \times 4.66 = 1.77 \text{ MW}$$ --- ### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper] **a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the lift and drag forces acting on a wind turbine blade. Derive the expressions for tangential and axial (thrust) force components. **(7 marks)** **b)** Explain the Betz limit with a neat derivation. State the assumptions made. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a)** Forces on a Wind Turbine Blade (Refer to §2.1 and §3.4): The relative wind velocity $V_{rel}$ strikes the blade airfoil. The aerodynamic reaction produces: - **Lift $L$** perpendicular to $V_{rel}$ - **Drag $D$** parallel to $V_{rel}$ These are resolved along the rotor plane: $$F_{t} = L \sin\phi - D \cos\phi \quad \text{(Tangential force - produces torque)}$$ $$F_{a} = L \cos\phi + D \sin\phi \quad \text{(Axial force - produces thrust)}$$ Where $\phi$ is the flow angle, $\tan\phi = V_{a}/(\omega r)$. **(b) Betz Limit Derivation** (Refer to §3.2 for full step-by-step): **Assumptions:** 1. Steady, incompressible, uniform, axial flow 2. No friction (inviscid actuator disk) 3. Infinite number of blades (uniform disk loading) 4. No wake rotation (uniform pressure across disk) 5. Static pressure far upstream and downstream equals atmospheric pressure 6. Single actuator disk extracts energy uniformly **Result:** $C_{p,\max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$, achieved at axial induction factor $a = 1/3$. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes students make in this topic — lose 1 to 3 marks each:** > 1. **Forgetting the area term:** Writing $P = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^{3}$ instead of $\tfrac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3}$. Always include the swept area $A = \pi R^{2}$. > 2. **Unit confusion:** Mixing up rotor radius $R$ with diameter $D$. Use $R = D/2$. > 3. **Betz derivation jump:** Skipping the continuity equation or momentum balance — both are essential. Marks are awarded for *each* logical step. > 4. **Air density default:** Using 1.225 kg/m³ even when the problem specifies a different value (e.g., at high altitude or warm locations). Always use the *given* value. > 5. **Confusing $C_{L}$ with $C_{D}$:** Lift coefficient is generally 5–10× larger than drag coefficient for a good airfoil at design α. Don't swap them. > 6. **Stall region ignored:** In long answers, students often write "as angle of attack increases, lift increases" — but you must mention the **stall angle** beyond which lift drops. > 7. **Tip speed ratio units:** Forgetting the factor $2\pi/60$ when converting rpm to rad/s. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Lift** is perpendicular to the relative wind; **Drag** is parallel to it. - Lift and Drag are both proportional to $V^{2}$, but **wind power** is proportional to $V^{3}$ — this cubic dependence dominates wind energy economics. - The **lift coefficient $C_{L}$** and **drag coefficient $C_{D}$** are functions of angle of attack, airfoil shape, and Reynolds number. - **Lift-to-drag ratio (L/D)** is the figure of merit for an airfoil — modern turbine blades achieve L/D ≈ 20–40 at design α. - **Power in the wind:** $P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3}$ - **Power extracted:** $P_{ext} = C_{p} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3}$, where $C_{p}$ is the power coefficient. - **Betz Limit:** $C_{p,\max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$ — the absolute theoretical ceiling. - **Optimal condition:** Wind slows to **1/3 of upstream speed** behind the rotor (axial induction factor $a = 1/3$). - **Tip Speed Ratio:** $\lambda = \omega R / V$; modern HAWTs operate at $\lambda \approx 6$–$8$ for best $C_{p}$. - **Thrust on tower:** $F_{a} = L \cos\phi + D \sin\phi$ — critical for tower structural design. - **Torque-producing force:** $F_{t} = L \sin\phi - D \cos\phi$ — this is the *useful* force. - **Real turbines** achieve $C_{p} \approx 0.35$–$0.45$ in practice (significantly below Betz limit) due to blade number finite effects, drag, wake rotation, and tip losses. - **Standard air density** at sea level, 15°C: **1.225 kg/m³**. - **Stall angle** for typical blade airfoils: 15°–20°.

topics.title: Power Coefficient: Betz Limit, Operational Parameters of Wind Turbines, Torque Coefficient, Tip Speed Ratio. Types of Wind Turbine Rotors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT631 - RENEWABLE ENERGY AND GREEN TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Power Coefficient: Betz Limit, Operational Parameters of Wind Turbines, Torque Coefficient, Tip Speed Ratio. Types of Wind Turbine Rotors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Power Coefficient ($C_P$)

The **Power Coefficient ($C_P$)** is defined as the ratio of the actual electrical/mechanical power extracted by a wind turbine to the total kinetic power available in the undisturbed wind stream passing through the swept area of the rotor.

$$C_P = \frac{P_{extracted}}{P_{wind}} = \frac{P_{extracted}}{\frac{1}{2}\,\rho\,A\,V^3}$$

Where:
- $P_{extracted}$ = Useful power captured by turbine (W)
- $\rho$ = Air density (**1.225 kg/m³** at sea level, ISA conditions)
- $A$ = Rotor swept area (m²)
- $V$ = Free-stream (upstream) wind velocity (m/s)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** $C_P$ is a dimensionless number that physically represents the **efficiency** of a wind turbine in converting the kinetic energy of wind into useful rotational mechanical energy.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Water Wheel & Net

Imagine blowing air through a window using a fan placed on a table. Now imagine a small paddle wheel in front of the fan. The paddle wheel will spin. Now imagine a net placed in the same path. The net "captures" some air but lets the rest escape.

- If the net is **too dense (a solid wall)**, it stops the air completely → no air flows through, so **0%** energy is extracted.
- If the net is **too loose (almost no net)**, air flows through freely without slowing down → again **0%** energy is extracted.
- Somewhere **in between**, the net slows the air just enough to maximize energy transfer — this optimum is the **Betz Limit**!

A wind turbine is essentially a sophisticated, rotating "net" for the wind. $C_P$ tells us how good our "net" is at catching the wind's kinetic energy.

---

## 1.2 Betz Limit ($C_{P,max}$)

The **Betz Limit** is the theoretical maximum possible value of the power coefficient for any ideal wind turbine operating in open flow. It was derived by German physicist **Albert Betz** in **1919**.

$$C_{P,Betz} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \;\; (\text{i.e., } \mathbf{59.3\%})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 59.3%?** No turbine — no matter how perfectly designed — can extract more than **59.3%** of the kinetic energy in wind. The remaining 40.7% must remain in the airstream as residual kinetic energy; otherwise, air would have to pile up behind the rotor, which violates mass continuity. This is essentially a thermodynamic/kinematic ceiling imposed by **Newton's laws** and **Bernoulli's principle**.

---

## 1.3 Tip Speed Ratio ($\lambda$ — Lambda)

The **Tip Speed Ratio (TSR)** is the ratio of the speed of the blade tip to the free-stream wind velocity.

$$\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{V} = \frac{\pi R n}{30\,V}$$

Where:
- $\omega$ = Angular velocity of rotor (rad/s)
- $R$ = Rotor radius (m)
- $n$ = Rotor rotational speed (rpm)
- $V$ = Upstream wind speed (m/s)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** $\lambda$ is one of the most important design parameters because it determines the **aerodynamic regime** in which a turbine operates and strongly influences the achievable $C_P$.

### Intuition — The Bicycle Gear Analogy

Think of cycling: when going uphill (slow), you pedal in a **low gear** (high torque, low rpm → low tip speed). When going downhill (fast), you shift to a **high gear** (high rpm). A wind turbine must similarly adjust its rotational speed to keep the blade tips moving at an "optimal ratio" relative to the wind speed for best energy harvesting.

---

## 1.4 Torque Coefficient ($C_T$)

The **Torque Coefficient ($C_T$)** is defined as the ratio of the actual shaft torque produced by the rotor to the available torque based on the dynamic pressure of the wind over the swept area.

$$C_T = \frac{T_{shaft}}{\frac{1}{2}\,\rho\,A\,R\,V^2}$$

### The Master Relationship

$$C_P = \lambda \cdot C_T \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad C_T = \frac{C_P}{\lambda}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This relationship is fundamental: since Power = Torque × Angular Velocity ($P = T\omega$), and $V^3 = V \cdot V^2$, the non-dimensionalization naturally splits power into a **speed ratio** part ($\lambda$) and a **torque part** ($C_T$).

---

## 1.5 Types of Wind Turbine Rotors — Overview

Wind turbine rotors are classified based on the **orientation of the rotational axis** relative to the ground:

| **Type** | **Axis Orientation** | **Sub-Types** |
|---|---|---|
| **HAWT** (Horizontal Axis) | Parallel to ground | Upwind, Downwind |
| **VAWT** (Vertical Axis) | Perpendicular to ground | Savonius, Darrieus, H-Rotor, Giromill |

### Conceptual Analogy — The Weathervane vs. The Spinning Top

- A **HAWT** is like a **weathervane** — its axis must align with the wind direction (needs yaw control).
- A **VAWT** is like a **spinning top on a table** — it doesn't care which way the wind blows; it accepts wind from any direction.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Actuator Disc Model — Foundation of Betz Theory

To derive the Betz Limit, we model the rotor as a **frictionless, porous disc** (an "actuator disc") placed perpendicular to the wind. This disc extracts energy uniformly across the swept area.

### Step-by-Step Physical Picture

1. **Upstream (far away):** Wind has velocity $V_1$, pressure $p_1$, cross-section $A_1$.
2. **At the rotor disc:** Wind velocity drops to $V$ (where $V_1 > V > V_2$), pressure drops to $p^-$, then jumps up to $p^+$ behind the disc.
3. **Downstream (far wake):** Wind velocity is $V_2$, pressure recovers to $p_1$, cross-section is $A_2$.

The disc experiences a **net thrust force** because $p^+ > p^-$.

---

## 2.2 Governing Equations (Three Pillars)

### (i) Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass)
For incompressible flow in a stream tube:
$$A_1 V_1 = A\,V = A_2 V_2$$

### (ii) Bernoulli's Equation (Upstream of Disc)
Applied between far upstream and just before the disc:
$$p_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2 = p^- + \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2$$

### (iii) Bernoulli's Equation (Downstream of Disc)
Applied between just after the disc and far downstream:
$$p^+ + \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 = p_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2$$

### (iv) Momentum Equation (Force on Disc)
The thrust force $F$ equals the rate of change of momentum:
$$F = \rho A V (V_1 - V_2)$$

### (v) Net Pressure Drop Across the Disc
$$\Delta p = p^+ - p^- = \frac{1}{2}\rho (V_1^2 - V_2^2)$$

---

## 2.3 Deriving the Power Extracted

The power extracted by the disc equals **Force × Velocity at the disc**:
$$P = F \cdot V = \rho A V^2 (V_1 - V_2)$$

### Substituting $V = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$ (Axial Induction Proof)

When continuity and momentum equations are combined, the disc velocity $V$ equals the **arithmetic mean** of $V_1$ and $V_2$:
$$V = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$$

Therefore:
$$P = \rho A \left(\frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}\right)^2 (V_1 - V_2)$$

### Expressing Power in Non-Dimensional Form

$$P = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V_1^3 \cdot \frac{4(V_1^2 - V_2^2)(V_1 + V_2)}{4 V_1^3} \cdot \frac{V_1+V_2}{2V_1}$$

This simplifies beautifully into:
$$C_P = \frac{P}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A V_1^3} = 4a(1-a)^2$$

Where **$a$ = Axial Induction Factor**:
$$a = 1 - \frac{V}{V_1} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; V = V_1(1-a)$$

So:
$$C_P(a) = 4a(1-a)^2$$

---

## 2.4 Maximization → The Betz Limit

To find the maximum power, differentiate $C_P$ with respect to $a$ and set to zero:
$$\frac{dC_P}{da} = 4(1-a)^2 - 8a(1-a) = 4(1-a)(1-3a) = 0$$

**Critical Points:**
- $a = 1$ → Turbine completely blocks flow → $C_P = 0$ (trivial)
- $a = \frac{1}{3}$ → **The Betz optimum**

Substituting $a = \frac{1}{3}$:
$$C_{P,max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{9} = \frac{16}{27}$$

### Physical Interpretation of $a = 1/3$
- Velocity at disc: $V = \frac{2V_1}{3}$
- Wake velocity: $V_2 = \frac{V_1}{3}$
- The disc reduces wind speed by exactly **one-third** at the optimum.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every formula** required to solve KTU numerical problems on this topic. Memorize this entire sheet.

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Boundary/Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Available Wind Power | $P_{wind}$ | $\frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3$ | W | Cubic dependence on V |
| Extracted Power | $P_{ext}$ | $C_P \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3$ | W | — |
| Power Coefficient | $C_P$ | $\frac{P_{ext}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3}$ | dimensionless | $0 \leq C_P \leq 16/27$ |
| Betz Limit | $C_{P,Betz}$ | $\frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$ | dimensionless | Theoretical max, ideal disc |
| Power Coeff. (in terms of $a$) | $C_P$ | $4a(1-a)^2$ | dimensionless | Maximum at $a = 1/3$ |
| Axial Induction Factor | $a$ | $1 - V/V_1$ | dimensionless | Optimal $a = 1/3$ |
| Tip Speed Ratio | $\lambda$ | $\omega R / V = \pi R n / (30 V)$ | dimensionless | $\omega$ in rad/s, $n$ in rpm |
| Torque Coefficient | $C_T$ | $\frac{T}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A R V^2}$ | dimensionless | — |
| Power–Torque Relation | $C_P$ | $C_P = \lambda \cdot C_T$ | dimensionless | Master identity |
| Rotor Swept Area | $A$ | $\pi R^2$ | m² | $R$ = rotor radius |
| Solidity | $\sigma$ | $\frac{N c}{2 \pi R}$ | dimensionless | $N$ = blade count, $c$ = chord |
| Cut-in / Rated / Cut-out | $V_{ci}, V_r, V_{co}$ | — | m/s | Operational thresholds |
| Wind Power Density | $P/A$ | $\frac{1}{2}\rho V^3$ | W/m² | Site selection metric |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Substitution for Exams:** When a problem gives rotor diameter $D$, remember $R = D/2$ and $A = \pi D^2 / 4$.

---

## 2.6 Operational Parameters of Wind Turbines

A real wind turbine operates across distinct regimes defined by wind speed:

| **Operating Region** | **Wind Speed Range** | **Controller Action** |
|---|---|---|
| Below Cut-in ($V < V_{ci}$) | $V < 3$–4 m/s | Turbine OFF (no power generated) |
| Partial Load (Region 2) | $V_{ci} \leq V < V_r$ | **$C_P$ maximization** (variable speed, MPPT) |
| Rated Load (Region 3) | $V_r \leq V < V_{co}$ | **Power limiting** (pitch control active) |
| Cut-out ($V \geq V_{co}$) | $V \geq 25$ m/s | Turbine SHUT DOWN (safety) |

### Operational Parameters — Definitions

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Typical Value** | **Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cut-in Speed | $V_{ci}$ | 3–4 m/s | Min. wind speed to start generating |
| Rated Wind Speed | $V_r$ | 12–14 m/s | Speed at which rated power is reached |
| Cut-out Speed | $V_{co}$ | 25 m/s | Max. safe operating wind speed |
| Rated Power | $P_r$ | 1–10 MW | Max. continuous power output |
| Rotor Diameter | $D$ | 80–220 m | Tip-to-tip blade length × 2 |
| Hub Height | $H$ | 80–150 m | Height of rotor center above ground |
| Capacity Factor | $C_F$ | 25–45% | $\frac{\text{Actual annual energy}}{P_r \times 8760}$ |

### Real-World Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these parameters matter in industry:** Wind farm developers use these thresholds to (a) estimate annual energy production (AEP), (b) size generators and gearboxes, (c) design pitch-control algorithms, and (d) optimize financial models based on capacity factor and wind-resource maps. The **Betz Limit** tells designers the upper bound — modern multi-MW turbines achieve $C_P \approx 0.45$–$0.50$, which is **75–85%** of the Betz ideal.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Numerical problems on **Betz Limit derivation** and **$C_P$ / $C_T$ calculations** are board-exam favorites. Practice the algebra until it is second nature.

---

## 3.1 EXHAUSTIVE Derivation of the Betz Limit

### Given
A wind turbine modeled as an ideal actuator disc in open flow. Free-stream wind velocity is $V_1$ upstream and $V_2$ downstream (wake). Disc velocity is $V$.

### Step 1 — Apply Continuity (Mass Conservation)

For an incompressible fluid, mass flow rate is constant through every cross-section of the stream tube:
$$\rho A_1 V_1 = \rho A V = \rho A_2 V_2$$

Since $A_1 = A_2 = A$ (the disc area equals the swept area at the disc itself, and we assume the stream tube has uniform cross-section at the disc):
$$V = \text{average velocity at disc}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation (Upstream)

Along a streamline from far upstream to just before the disc (pressure rises due to deceleration):
$$p_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2 = p^- + \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

### Step 3 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation (Downstream)

Along a streamline from just after the disc to far downstream (pressure recovers as flow expands):
$$p^+ + \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 = p_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

### Step 4 — Find Net Pressure Jump Across Disc

Subtracting (ii) from (i):
$$(p_1 - p^+) - (p_1 - p^-) = \frac{1}{2}\rho (V_2^2 - V_1^2)$$

$$\therefore\;\; \Delta p = p^+ - p^- = \frac{1}{2}\rho (V_1^2 - V_2^2) \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

### Step 5 — Thrust Force on the Disc

The thrust equals the pressure difference times the disc area:
$$F = \Delta p \cdot A = \frac{1}{2}\rho A (V_1^2 - V_2^2) \quad \text{...(iv)}$$

### Step 6 — Equate Thrust via Momentum Equation

From the momentum equation (rate of change of momentum):
$$F = \rho A V (V_1 - V_2) \quad \text{...(v)}$$

### Step 7 — Equate (iv) and (v), Solve for $V$

$$\frac{1}{2}\rho A (V_1^2 - V_2^2) = \rho A V (V_1 - V_2)$$

$$\frac{1}{2}(V_1 + V_2)(V_1 - V_2) = V (V_1 - V_2)$$

Cancel $(V_1 - V_2)$ (assuming $V_1 \neq V_2$):
$$V = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2} \quad \text{...(vi)}$$

> **This is a key result:** the disc velocity is the **arithmetic mean** of upstream and downstream velocities.

### Step 8 — Power Extracted by the Disc

Power = Force × Velocity at the disc:
$$P = F \cdot V = \rho A V (V_1 - V_2) \cdot V = \rho A V^2 (V_1 - V_2)$$

Substituting (vi):
$$P = \rho A \left(\frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}\right)^2 (V_1 - V_2)$$

### Step 9 — Compute the Power Coefficient

$$C_P = \frac{P}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A V_1^3} = \frac{\rho A \left(\frac{V_1+V_2}{2}\right)^2 (V_1-V_2)}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A V_1^3}$$

$$C_P = \frac{(V_1+V_2)^2 (V_1-V_2)}{2 V_1^3}$$

### Step 10 — Introduce the Axial Induction Factor

Let $V_2 = V_1 (1 - 2a)$ where $a = (V_1 - V)/(V_1)$ is the axial induction factor. This gives:
$$V = V_1(1 - a), \quad V_2 = V_1(1 - 2a)$$

Substitute:
$$C_P = \frac{[V_1 + V_1(1-2a)]^2 [V_1 - V_1(1-2a)]}{2 V_1^3}$$

$$C_P = \frac{[V_1(2 - 2a)]^2 [2a V_1]}{2 V_1^3} = \frac{4 V_1^2(1-a)^2 \cdot 2a V_1}{2 V_1^3}$$

$$\boxed{C_P = 4a(1-a)^2}$$

### Step 11 — Maximize $C_P$

Take the derivative with respect to $a$ and set to zero:
$$\frac{dC_P}{da} = 4\left[(1-a)^2 + a \cdot 2(1-a)(-1)\right]$$

$$= 4\left[(1-a)^2 - 2a(1-a)\right]$$

$$= 4(1-a)\left[(1-a) - 2a\right] = 4(1-a)(1-3a)$$

Setting $\frac{dC_P}{da} = 0$:
$$a = 1 \;\;(\text{rejected, gives } C_P = 0) \quad \text{or} \quad a = \frac{1}{3}$$

### Step 12 — Compute Maximum Power Coefficient

$$\boxed{C_{P,max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{9} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926}$$

### Step 13 — Verify with Second Derivative Test

$$\frac{d^2 C_P}{da^2}\bigg|_{a=1/3} < 0 \;\;\checkmark \text{ (confirms maximum)}$$

> **Conclusion:** No wind energy conversion device can capture more than **59.3%** of the kinetic energy in wind. This is the **Betz Limit**.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Exam Style

**Problem:** A wind turbine has a rotor diameter of 80 m operating in a wind of 12 m/s. Air density is 1.225 kg/m³. If $C_P = 0.40$, calculate:
(a) The rotor swept area
(b) The available wind power
(c) The actual power extracted
(d) The rotor rpm if the tip speed ratio $\lambda = 7$
(e) The torque coefficient

### Solution

**(a) Swept Area:**
$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (80)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 6400}{4} = 1600\pi$$
$$\boxed{A \approx 5026.55 \text{ m}^2}$$

**(b) Available Wind Power:**
$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3 = \frac{1}{2}(1.225)(5026.55)(12)^3$$
$$P_{wind} = 0.6125 \times 5026.55 \times 1728$$
$$\boxed{P_{wind} \approx 5.32 \text{ MW}}$$

**(c) Extracted Power:**
$$P_{ext} = C_P \cdot P_{wind} = 0.40 \times 5.32 \text{ MW}$$
$$\boxed{P_{ext} \approx 2.13 \text{ MW}}$$

**(d) Rotor Speed from TSR:**
$$R = D/2 = 40 \text{ m}$$
$$\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{V} \Rightarrow \omega = \frac{\lambda V}{R} = \frac{7 \times 12}{40} = 2.1 \text{ rad/s}$$

Convert to rpm:
$$n = \frac{\omega \times 60}{2\pi} = \frac{2.1 \times 60}{2\pi} = \frac{126}{6.283}$$
$$\boxed{n \approx 20.05 \text{ rpm}}$$

**(e) Torque Coefficient:**
$$C_T = \frac{C_P}{\lambda} = \frac{0.40}{7}$$
$$\boxed{C_T \approx 0.0571}$$

### Verification of Betz Limit

Maximum possible power = $C_{P,Betz} \times P_{wind} = 0.5926 \times 5.32 \approx 3.15$ MW.
Actual extracted = 2.13 MW → Efficiency relative to Betz = $2.13 / 3.15 \approx 67.6\%$ of theoretical max. ✓ Reasonable for a real turbine.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — HAWT vs. VAWT

| **Feature** | **HAWT (Upwind)** | **HAWT (Downwind)** | **VAWT (Darrieus)** | **VAWT (Savonius)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Axis Orientation | Horizontal | Horizontal | Vertical | Vertical |
| Wind Direction | One side only | One side only | Omni-directional | Omni-directional |
| Yaw Control Needed | **Yes** | Yes (passive) | **No** | **No** |
| Self-Starting | **Yes** | Yes | **No** (needs push) | **Yes** |
| Tip Speed Ratio ($\lambda$) | 6–8 (high) | 6–8 | 1–3 (low) | 0.5–1 (very low) |
| Peak $C_P$ | 0.45–0.50 | 0.40–0.45 | 0.30–0.40 | 0.20–0.30 |
| Tower Shadow Effect | Low (upwind) | High (downwind) | None | None |
| Maintenance Access | Hub at top | Hub at top | Ground level | Ground level |
| Power vs. Size | High power density | Medium | Medium | Low |
| Typical Application | Utility-scale farms | Utility-scale | Rooftop, distributed | Low-power, off-grid |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Point to Remember:** HAWTs dominate commercial wind energy (>95% of installed capacity globally) because they achieve higher $C_P$ at higher TSRs. VAWTs are limited by low $\lambda$, lower $C_P$, and structural challenges at scale, but offer advantages in urban/off-grid niches.

---

## 3.4 Types of Rotors — Detailed Breakdown

### A. Horizontal Axis Wind Turbines (HAWT)

#### (i) Upwind HAWT
- Rotor is positioned **upwind of the tower** (wind hits blades before tower).
- Requires **active yaw motor** to keep rotor facing the wind.
- Avoids tower shadow → smoother power output.
- **Industry standard** for modern utility-scale turbines.

#### (ii) Downwind HAWT
- Rotor is positioned **downwind of the tower** (wind passes tower before blades).
- **Passive yaw** — the rotor naturally aligns itself due to aerodynamic forces.
- Suffers from "tower shadow" — cyclic power fluctuations every revolution.
- Rare in modern designs (e.g., some legacy Enercon E-series).

### B. Vertical Axis Wind Turbines (VAWT)

#### (i) Savonius Rotor
- **Drag-based** device — uses two half-cylindrical scoops.
- Self-starting at very low wind speeds.
- Very low $\lambda$ (~0.5–1) → low $C_P$ (~0.2).
- Used for anemometers, water pumping, ventilation.

#### (ii) Darrieus Rotor ("Eggbeater")
- **Lift-based** device — uses curved airfoil blades.
- **Not self-starting** (requires motor or Savonius assist).
- $\lambda \approx 1$–3, $C_P \approx 0.3$–0.4.
- Rotational speed independent of wind direction.

#### (iii) H-Rotor (Giromill)
- Straight-bladed Darrieus variant.
- Simpler manufacturing than curved Darrieus.
- Pitchable blades for variable control.
