# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. Define *Equivalence Ratio (ER)*. State the typical ER ranges for combustion, gasification, and pyrolysis processes. Why is gasification called an "oxygen-lean" process?

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks, 50–70 words expected):**

The **Equivalence Ratio (ER)** is the ratio of the actual air–fuel ratio to the stoichiometric (theoretical) air–fuel ratio:

$$ER = \frac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoichiometric}}$$

- **Combustion:** ER > 1 (excess O₂, full oxidation)
- **Gasification:** ER ≈ 0.20 – 0.40 (sub-stoichiometric, O₂ limited)
- **Pyrolysis:** ER = 0 (no O₂, heat-only)

Gasification is called oxygen-lean because only **20–40%** of the stoichiometric oxygen is supplied. The fuel is *not* allowed to burn completely; it partially oxidises and the remaining char reduces CO₂ and H₂O into CO and H₂, yielding combustible producer gas.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for ranges, 1 mark for "why oxygen-lean" explanation.)*

---

### Question 2. List the **four sequential stages** of anaerobic digestion with the dominant microbial group and the end-products of each stage.

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Stage | Dominant Microbes | End Products |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Hydrolysis** | Cellulolytic, proteolytic, lipolytic bacteria | Monomers (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids) |
| 2 | **Acidogenesis** | Acidogenic bacteria (e.g., *Clostridium*) | VFAs (acetic, propionic, butyric), alcohols, H₂, CO₂ |
| 3 | **Acetogenesis** | Acetogenic bacteria (e.g., *Syntrophomonas*) | Acetic acid (CH₃COOH), H₂, CO₂ |
| 4 | **Methanogenesis** | Methanogenic archaea (*Methanosaeta*, *Methanosarcina*) | CH₄ + CO₂ (biogas) |

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for naming all four, 1 mark for microbes, 1 mark for end-products.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Question (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Module 3 Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Style]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **downdraft biomass gasifier**. List the chemical reactions occurring in each zone and state the typical composition and calorific value of the producer gas.

**(b) [7 marks]** A rice husk sample (ultimate analysis: C = 38%, H = 5%, O = 36%, N = 0.5%, S = 0.1%, Ash = 20.4%) is to be combusted in a furnace with 30% excess air. Calculate (i) theoretical air required, (ii) actual air required, (iii) equivalence ratio, and (iv) flue-gas O₂ percentage (on dry basis, assuming complete combustion).

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — Working of Downdraft Gasifier

**Block diagram (already covered in §4.5; here in text form):**

A downdraft gasifier has **four vertically-stacked zones** that the biomass traverses top-to-bottom in the same direction as the air flow (hence "down-draft"):

1. **Drying Zone (100–200 °C):** Moisture evaporates; latent heat absorbed.
2. **Pyrolysis / Devolatilization Zone (200–600 °C):** Biomass decomposes into **char + volatiles (CO, H₂, CH₄, tars, light HC)**. Endothermic.
3. **Combustion / Oxidation Zone (800–1200 °C):** Air introduced here. Char and some volatiles burn:
   $$C + O_2 \to CO_2 \quad ; \quad H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \to H_2O$$
   Exothermic — supplies heat to neighbouring zones.
4. **Reduction / Gasification Zone (600–900 °C):** Hot CO₂ and H₂O from combustion pass through hot char and are reduced:
   $$C + CO_2 \to 2\,CO \quad ; \quad C + H_2O \to CO + H_2 \quad ; \quad C + 2H_2 \to CH_4$$
   Endothermic — gas is the desired producer gas. Exits at the bottom, leaving ash.

**Typical Producer Gas Composition (downdraft, wood feedstock):**

| Component | % (vol) |
|---|---|
| CO | 18 – 22 |
| H₂ | 12 – 18 |
| CH₄ | 2 – 5 |
| CO₂ | 9 – 12 |
| N₂ | 45 – 55 |
| **Calorific Value** | **4.5 – 6.0 MJ/Nm³** |

**Key features of downdraft gasifier:**
- Low tar content (tar cracks in the hot reduction zone) — **suitable for direct engine use**
- Small footprint, high thermal efficiency (70–80%)
- Requires low-moisture feedstock (< 20%)

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — Stoichiometric Calculation

**Given:** Ultimate analysis (mass %, dry): C=38, H=5, O=36, N=0.5, S=0.1, Ash=20.4; EA = 30%.

**(i) Theoretical O₂ per kg fuel:**

$$O_{2,\min} = \tfrac{8}{3}\cdot\tfrac{38}{100} + 8\cdot\tfrac{5 - 36/8}{100} + \tfrac{0.1}{100}$$

$$= \tfrac{8}{3}\cdot 0.38 + 8\cdot \tfrac{5 - 4.5}{100} + 0.001$$

$$= 1.0133 + 0.040 + 0.001 = 1.0543 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

**Theoretical air:**

$$A_{th} = 1.0543/0.232 = \boxed{4.544 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}}$$

**(ii) Actual air (30% excess):**

$$A_{act} = 4.544 \times 1.30 = \boxed{5.907 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}}$$

**(iii) Equivalence Ratio:**

$$ER = \frac{A_{act}}{A_{th}} = 1.30 \quad \text{(dimensionless)}$$

Or in the *gasification* convention (EA as fractional shortfall): $ER = 1/(1+EA) = 1/1.30 = 0.77$ — but here we are combusting, so **ER = 1.30** is the correct KTU answer.

**(iv) Flue-gas O₂% (dry basis):**

Excess O₂ supplied = $EA \times O_{2,\min} = 0.30 \times 1.0543 = 0.316$ kg O₂/kg fuel (after subtracting the O₂ used in combustion).

Total dry flue gas = (CO₂ produced) + (N₂ from air) + (O₂ from EA) + (SO₂ from S).
- CO₂: $44/12 \times 0.38 = 1.393$ kg
- N₂: $0.768 \times 5.907 = 4.537$ kg (N₂ = 76.8% of air by mass)
- Excess O₂: 0.316 kg
- SO₂: $64/32 \times 0.001 = 0.002$ kg (negligible)
- Total dry flue gas: 1.393 + 4.537 + 0.316 + 0.002 = **6.248 kg**

$$\%\,O_{2,flue} = \frac{0.316}{6.248} \times 100 = \boxed{5.06\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Do **not** confuse "**Equivalence Ratio**" (ER) with "**Excess Air**" (EA). ER > 1 always means combustion; ER < 1 always means gasification. Students who write ER = 0.30 for a combustor with 30% EA will lose 1 full mark.
> 2. The $H - O/8$ term in the O₂ formula is **not** $H - O$. The 1/8 factor comes from the mass ratio of oxygen in H₂O to hydrogen (16/2 = 8).
> 3. The flue-gas O₂ should be calculated on a **dry** basis by excluding water vapour. Common mistake: including the water mass in the denominator.

---

### Question B — Module 3 Choice 2 (Alternative)

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the construction and working of a **fixed-dome (Deenbandhu) biogas plant**. Discuss its merits and demerits compared to the floating-drum (KVIC) design.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **biochemical stages of anaerobic digestion** in detail. A household owns 6 cattle producing 12 kg dung/cattle/day. With VS fraction = 0.8, slurry TS = 10%, HRT = 40 days, methane yield = 0.20 m³/kg VS, CH₄ fraction = 60%, calculate (i) digester volume, (ii) daily methane volume, (iii) daily biogas volume, and (iv) daily energy output.

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — Fixed-Dome Biogas Plant

**Construction (refer to §4.3 diagram):**
- **Inlet tank:** where dung is mixed with water to ~10% TS slurry.
- **Inlet pipe:** carries slurry into the digester.
- **Digester chamber (underground masonry):** airtight, holds slurry for HRT.
- **Displacement/expansion chamber (the dome):** where gas accumulates.
- **Outlet/compensation tank:** overflow slurry is collected for use as fertilizer.
- **Gas outlet pipe:** carries gas to the kitchen stove.

**Working principle:**
1. Slurry is fed daily into the inlet tank; gravity moves it into the digester.
2. Anaerobic microbes digest the organic matter over **HRT ≈ 40 days**.
3. Biogas (CH₄ + CO₂) rises to the dome.
4. The increasing gas pressure pushes slurry up into the compensation tank.
5. When a tap is opened at the stove, gas flows out by displacement.
6. Used slurry in the compensation tank is drained and used as **enriched bio-fertilizer** (N, P, K content is preserved, pathogens are killed).

**Comparison: Fixed-Dome vs Floating-Drum (KVIC)**

| Feature | Fixed Dome (Deenbandhu) | Floating Drum (KVIC) |
|---|---|---|
| Construction | Underground masonry | Above-ground metal drum |
| Capital cost | Low (no steel) | Higher (steel drum) |
| Gas pressure | Variable (depends on slurry level) | **Constant** (drum weight) |
| Durability | **20+ years** | 5–10 years (drum rusts) |
| Corrosion risk | None | High |
| Gas storage capacity | Limited (dome) | Adjustable (drum size) |
| Scum removal | Difficult | Easy (drum removable) |
| Winter performance | Better (insulated underground) | Poor |
| Maintenance | Low | High |
| Suitability for India | **Excellent** — low-cost, robust | Moderate |

**Merits of Fixed-Dome:**
- No moving parts, no rust, long life, low cost (uses local materials), underground → space-saving and aesthetically invisible.
- High gas yield, low maintenance.

**Demerits:**
- Variable gas pressure (problem for some burners).
- Dome construction is technically demanding — needs skilled mason.
- Difficult to inspect and clean internally.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — Anaerobic Digestion Stages + Numerical

**Biochemical stages (covered in §2.3.1; expanded):**

**Stage 1 — Hydrolysis (rate-limiting for cellulosic feeds):** Polymers (cellulose, proteins, lipids) → monomers (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids). Catalysed by extracellular enzymes (cellulases, amylases, proteases, lipases) secreted by hydrolytic bacteria.

**Stage 2 — Acidogenesis:** Monomers fermented to volatile fatty acids (VFAs: acetic, propionic, butyric), alcohols (ethanol), H₂, and CO₂. pH drops to 5.0–6.0. Key organisms: *Clostridium*, *Bacteroides*.

**Stage 3 — Acetogenesis:** VFAs and longer alcohols converted by acetogenic bacteria to **acetic acid**, **H₂**, and **CO₂**. Syntrophic with methanogens (low H₂ partial pressure required).

**Stage 4 — Methanogenesis:** Strict-anaerobic archaea produce CH₄ via two pathways:
- **Acetoclastic (≈70%):** $CH_3COOH \to CH_4 + CO_2$
- **Hydrogenotrophic (≈30%):** $CO_2 + 4H_2 \to CH_4 + 2H_2O$

**Numerical:**

**(i) Daily VS input:**
$$M = 6 \times 12 = 72 \text{ kg dung/day} \quad ; \quad M_{VS} = 72 \times 0.8 = 57.6 \text{ kg VS/day}$$

**Digester volume:**
$$V = \frac{M \cdot f \cdot HRT}{\rho \cdot VS_{frac}} = \frac{72 \times 0.8 \times 40}{1000 \times 0.08} = \frac{2304}{80} = 28.8 \text{ m}^3$$

$$\boxed{V_{digester} = 28.8 \text{ m}^3}$$

**(ii) Daily methane:**
$$G_{CH_4} = 57.6 \times 0.20 = 11.52 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

**(iii) Daily biogas:**
$$G_{biogas} = 11.52 / 0.60 = 19.2 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

**(iv) Daily energy output:**
$$E = 19.2 \times 22.5 = 432 \text{ MJ/day}$$

Equivalent to **~8.6 kg LPG/day** (LPG CV ≈ 50 MJ/kg) or **~9 kWh of electricity** (with a 30%-efficient biogas genset).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q-B):**
> 1. Do **not** forget the **syntrophic** nature of acetogenesis and methanogenesis — many students treat them as independent. The textbook "acetate cleavage" reaction $CH_3COOH \to CH_4 + CO_2$ is the acetoclastic pathway; missing the word "acetoclastic" loses a mark.
> 2. The digester volume formula has a **factor** that distinguishes **slurry volume** from **gas-holder volume**. A full-mark answer should mention that the gas-holder is sized separately (≈ 50% of the daily gas production in m³).
> 3. For the energy conversion, remember to use **biogas CV (≈ 22.5 MJ/m³)**, not the CH₄ CV (37.5 MJ/m³) — students routinely use the wrong CV and lose 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Burn-These-Into-Your-Brain Bullet Points:**

- **Biomass** is the only renewable, carbon-containing solid fuel; carbon-neutral over short cycle.
- **Combustion** = excess O₂, full oxidation, heat; stoichiometric O₂ formula: $\frac{8}{3}C/100 + 8(H - O/8)/100 + S/100$.
- **Theoretical air** = O₂_min / 0.232; **Excess air** typically 25–40% for biomass furnaces.
- **Gasification** = ER 0.2–0.4; main reactions: Boudouard, Water–Gas, Methanation, Water–Gas Shift.
- **Producer gas** CV ≈ **5 MJ/Nm³**; main combustible components CO, H₂, CH₄.
- **Downdraft gasifier** = low tar, suitable for IC engines; **updraft** = high tar, suitable for burners.
- **Anaerobic digestion** has **4 stages**: Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis.
- **Methanogens are the slowest, most sensitive microbes** — they control the whole process.
- **Biogas** ≈ 60% CH₄ + 40% CO₂ + traces; CV ≈ **22.5 MJ/m³**.
- **Mesophilic** (35 °C) digestion is more common; thermophilic (55 °C) is faster but less stable.
- **Cattle dung** is the KTU reference feedstock: C/N ≈ 25, biogas yield 0.20–0.40 m³/kg VS.
- **Fixed-dome (Deenbandhu)** plants are the Indian standard for low-cost durability.
- **Optimal parameters:** pH 6.8–7.4, C/N 20–30, HRT 20–40 days, TS 8–12%, temperature 35 °C.
- **Failure modes** to remember: pH crash, ammonia toxicity, hydraulic washout, foam, scum.
- **Bioethanol** = fermentation of sugars by yeast; theoretical yield **0.51 g/g glucose = 0.65 L/kg**.
- **Biodiesel** = transesterification of vegetable oil with methanol (6:1 molar) + NaOH catalyst; 1 mol TG → 3 mol FAME + 1 mol glycerol.
- **Direct use of vegetable oil is impractical** — high viscosity (10–17× diesel) clogs engines; transesterification fixes this.
- **Biodiesel CV** ≈ 40 MJ/kg; **ethanol CV** ≈ 30 MJ/kg — both lower than petrodiesel (45 MJ/kg) and petrol (47 MJ/kg), so fuel economy is ≈ 5–8% lower in biodiesel vehicles.
- **Sankey-style memory map:** Biomass → (Combustion, Gasification, AD, Fermentation, Transesterification) → (Heat, Producer gas, Biogas, Ethanol, Biodiesel).

---

> **End of Module 3 Notes — PEAGT631 (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> *Total content covered: Biomass Combustion, Gasification, Anaerobic Digestion, Biogas Plant Types & Design, Biogas Operating Parameters, Liquid Biofuels (Bioethanol & Biodiesel), Vegetable Oils as Fuel.*

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🟢 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biodiesel** is defined as a **mono-alkyl ester of long-chain fatty acids** derived from renewable biological feedstocks such as **vegetable oils, animal fats, or used cooking oils**. It is produced primarily through a chemical reaction called **transesterification**, in which triglycerides (the main constituents of oils/fats) react with a short-chain alcohol (commonly **methanol** or **ethanol**) in the presence of a catalyst (typically a base such as **NaOH** or **KOH**) to yield **Fatty Acid Methyl Esters (FAME)** — which IS biodiesel — and a co-product called **glycerol**.

According to the **American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM)**, pure biodiesel must conform to the standard specification **ASTM D6751** (USA) or **EN 14214** (European Union), which govern critical fuel-quality parameters such as cetane number, flash point, viscosity, density, sulfur content, and oxidation stability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology for KTU Exams:**
> - **B100** = 100 % pure biodiesel (neat biodiesel)
> - **B20** = 20 % biodiesel blended with 80 % petroleum diesel
> - **FAME** = Fatty Acid Methyl Ester (the chemical name of biodiesel)
> - **Transesterification** = The core chemical process for biodiesel synthesis

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you have a thick, syrupy substance (vegetable oil) that is too **viscous (sticky and slow-flowing)** to burn efficiently inside a diesel engine. The oil molecules are like three long ropes tied together at one end — this bulky structure (a **triglyceride**) cannot flow through fuel injectors properly, causing incomplete combustion, engine deposits, and injector choking.

Now imagine you take a pair of scissors (the catalyst + methanol) and **snip those ropes apart**, attaching a small, light "handle" (a methyl group from methanol) to each loose end. The three ropes, now freed and carrying small handles, become biodiesel (FAME). The bulky "knot" left behind is **glycerol** — a valuable by-product used in cosmetics, soaps, and pharmaceuticals.

**Analogy Summary:**
| Item | Chemical Equivalent | Role |
|---|---|---|
| Syrupy oil | Triglyceride | Too thick to burn |
| Pair of scissors | Catalyst + Methanol | Breaks the oil |
| Three freed ropes | FAME (Biodiesel) | The usable fuel |
| Leftover knot | Glycerol | By-product |

> [!TIP]
> **Why methanol, not water?** Methanol is the lightest, cheapest, and most reactive short-chain alcohol, and it produces the lowest-viscosity biodiesel. Water would not react this way and would ruin the fuel.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following benchmark values are **must-know** for KTU numericals and viva voce:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Density of biodiesel:** ≈ **880 kg/m³** (at 15 °C)
> - **Density of petro-diesel:** ≈ **850 kg/m³**
> - **Cetane number of biodiesel:** ≈ **45–60** (higher than petro-diesel)
> - **Cetane number of petro-diesel:** ≈ **40–55**
> - **Flash point of biodiesel:** ≈ **130–170 °C** (much safer than petro-diesel ≈ 55–65 °C)
> - **Kinematic viscosity of biodiesel:** ≈ **1.9–6.0 mm²/s** at 40 °C
> - **Stoichiometric molar ratio** (alcohol : oil) = **3 : 1**
> - **Practical molar ratio used** = **6 : 1 to 12 : 1**
> - **Higher Heating Value (HHV) of biodiesel:** ≈ **37–40 MJ/kg**
> - **Oxygen content of biodiesel:** ≈ **10–12 %** (by mass)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🟢 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Chemistry of Biodiesel — Step-by-Step Logic

Biodiesel is manufactured via the **transesterification** reaction. Let us dissect it systematically:

### Step 1 — The Raw Material (Triglyceride)
A **triglyceride** is one glycerol molecule (a 3-carbon "backbone") esterified with three **fatty acid chains**. Each fatty acid chain typically contains **14 to 22 carbon atoms**. Common feedstocks in India include:
- **Edible oils:** Soybean, sunflower, palm, coconut, groundnut
- **Non-edible oils:** Jatropha, Karanja (Pongamia), Mahua, Neem
- **Waste sources:** Used cooking oil, animal tallow, yellow grease

### Step 2 — The Alcohol
The alcohol acts as the **acyl-acceptor**. The two industrial choices are:
- **Methanol (CH₃OH)** — most common because it is cheap, reacts quickly, and produces the lowest-viscosity ester.
- **Ethanol (C₂H₅OH)** — renewable but forms an azeotrope with water, complicating separation.

### Step 3 — The Catalyst
The catalyst dramatically accelerates the reaction. Two classes exist:
- **Homogeneous catalysts:** NaOH, KOH, NaOCH₃ (most common in industry)
- **Heterogeneous catalysts:** CaO, MgO, zeolites, solid superacids

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** *"Why is NaOH preferred over H₂SO₄ for biodiesel production?"* — NaOH is **faster**, requires **lower temperature**, and gives **higher yields** for oils with low free fatty acid (FFA) content. H₂SO₄ is used only for high-FFA oils (preceded by an esterification step).

### Step 4 — The Reaction Mechanism (in three reversible steps)
Each of the three fatty acid chains is released sequentially. In every step, one alkyl ester (FAME) molecule is liberated and a **diglyceride → monoglyceride → glycerol** intermediate is formed.

### Step 5 — Separation and Purification
After reaction, the mixture separates by gravity into two layers:
- **Top (lighter) layer:** Biodiesel (FAME) — washed with water to remove residual catalyst, methanol, and soap.
- **Bottom (denser) layer:** Crude glycerol (~50 % purity) — refined for pharmaceutical/cosmetic use.

---

## 2.2 Governing Stoichiometric Equation

$$
\text{Triglyceride} \;+\; 3\,\text{R-OH} \;\xrightleftharpoons[\text{Catalyst}]{\text{Heat}}\; 3\,\text{R'-COO-R} \;+\; \text{Glycerol}
$$

Where:
- **R-OH** = alcohol (methanol or ethanol)
- **R'-COO-R** = fatty acid alkyl ester (biodiesel)
- **Catalyst** = base or acid

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relationship | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{n_{\text{alcohol}}}{n_{\text{oil}}} = 3 : 1$ | Stoichiometric molar ratio | Theoretical minimum methanol requirement |
| 2 | $\text{Yield (\%)} = \dfrac{m_{\text{FAME,actual}}}{m_{\text{FAME,theoretical}}} \times 100$ | Mass-based yield calculation | Plant performance evaluation |
| 3 | $m_{\text{methanol}} = 3 \times M_{\text{MeOH}} \times n_{\text{oil}} = 96\,n_{\text{oil}}$ | Methanol demand (g) per mol oil | Reagent sizing for batch reactors |
| 4 | $m_{\text{FAME}} = 3 \times M_{\text{FAME}} \times n_{\text{oil}}$ | Biodiesel mass produced (g) per mol oil | Production capacity planning |
| 5 | $m_{\text{glycerol}} = M_{\text{glycerol}} \times n_{\text{oil}} = 92\,n_{\text{oil}}$ | Glycerol co-product mass (g) | By-product revenue estimation |
| 6 | $\text{Conversion (\%)} = \dfrac{n_{\text{oil,reacted}}}{n_{\text{oil,initial}}} \times 100$ | Reaction completeness | Reactor efficiency check |
| 7 | $\text{FFA correction: } m_{\text{NaOH}} = m_{\text{oil}} \times \dfrac{\text{FFA\%}}{100} \times \dfrac{40}{282}$ | Catalyst neutralization for FFA | High-FFA feedstock handling |
| 8 | $\eta_{\text{combustion}} = \dfrac{\text{Energy recovered}}{\text{Energy input}} \times 100$ | Combustion efficiency | Engine performance assessment |
| 9 | $\rho_{\text{biodiesel}} \approx 0.88\;\text{g/cm}^3$ | Density at 15 °C | Volumetric energy calculations |
| 10 | $\text{LHV} \approx 37\;\text{MJ/kg}$ | Lower Heating Value | Energy content comparison |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Molar masses used in KTU numericals:**
> - Methanol (CH₃OH) = **32 g/mol**
> - Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) = **46 g/mol**
> - Glycerol (C₃H₈O₃) = **92 g/mol**
> - Typical biodiesel molecule (methyl oleate, C₁₉H₃₆O₂) ≈ **296 g/mol**
> - NaOH = **40 g/mol**, KOH = **56 g/mol**

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Transportation:** B20 blend is approved by most OEMs (Cummins, Bosch, Mercedes) for unmodified diesel engines.
- **Rural electrification:** India’s **National Biodiesel Mission** targets jatropha cultivation on wastelands.
- **Carbon accounting:** Biodiesel life-cycle CO₂ emissions are **~78 % lower** than petro-diesel because the source plants re-absorb the CO₂ during growth (closed carbon loop).
- **Lubricity additive:** Even 1–2 % biodiesel dramatically improves diesel-engine lubricity, extending injector life.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🟢 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem Context

A small-scale biodiesel plant processes **1 metric tonne (1000 kg)** of soybean oil (average molecular weight = **885 g/mol**) with **methanol** in a 6 : 1 molar ratio using NaOH catalyst. The reaction achieves **95 % conversion** of the triglyceride.

Calculate:
- (a) Mass of methanol required
- (b) Theoretical mass of FAME produced
- (c) Actual mass of FAME obtained at 95 % conversion
- (d) Mass of glycerol co-product
- (e) Verify mass balance (closure)

---

## 3.2 Step (a) — Mass of Methanol Required

**Step 1:** Determine moles of triglyceride (oil) available.

$$
n_{\text{oil}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{oil}}}{M_{\text{oil}}} = \dfrac{1{,}000{,}000\;\text{g}}{885\;\text{g/mol}}
$$

$$
n_{\text{oil}} = 1129.94\;\text{mol}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the stoichiometric ratio. Although the **stoichiometric** ratio is 3 : 1, a **practical** ratio of 6 : 1 is used to drive equilibrium forward (Le Chatelier’s principle).

$$
n_{\text{methanol}} = 6 \times n_{\text{oil}} = 6 \times 1129.94
$$

$$
n_{\text{methanol}} = 6779.66\;\text{mol}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert moles to mass using molar mass of methanol (32 g/mol).

$$
m_{\text{methanol}} = n_{\text{methanol}} \times M_{\text{MeOH}} = 6779.66 \times 32
$$

$$
\boxed{m_{\text{methanol}} = 216{,}949\;\text{g} \;\approx\; 216.95\;\text{kg}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **total methanol feed**. In industry, excess methanol is recovered and recycled (typically 90 % recovery), so fresh make-up methanol is only ~22 kg.

---

## 3.3 Step (b) — Theoretical Mass of FAME Produced

**Step 1:** Per the stoichiometry, 1 mole of triglyceride yields **3 moles of FAME**.

$$
n_{\text{FAME,theoretical}} = 3 \times n_{\text{oil}} = 3 \times 1129.94
$$

$$
n_{\text{FAME,theoretical}} = 3389.83\;\text{mol}
$$

**Step 2:** Convert to mass using $M_{\text{FAME}} = 296\;\text{g/mol}$ (methyl oleate as representative).

$$
m_{\text{FAME,theoretical}} = 3389.83 \times 296
$$

$$
\boxed{m_{\text{FAME,theoretical}} = 1{,}003{,}389\;\text{g} \;\approx\; 1003.39\;\text{kg}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Step (c) — Actual Mass of FAME at 95 % Conversion

$$
m_{\text{FAME,actual}} = m_{\text{FAME,theoretical}} \times \dfrac{\text{Conversion}}{100}
$$

$$
m_{\text{FAME,actual}} = 1003.39 \times 0.95
$$

$$
\boxed{m_{\text{FAME,actual}} = 953.22\;\text{kg}}
$$

---

## 3.5 Step (d) — Mass of Glycerol Co-product

**Step 1:** Moles of oil reacted = 95 % of initial.

$$
n_{\text{oil,reacted}} = 1129.94 \times 0.95 = 1073.44\;\text{mol}
$$

**Step 2:** Each mole of oil gives exactly 1 mole of glycerol.

$$
n_{\text{glycerol}} = n_{\text{oil,reacted}} = 1073.44\;\text{mol}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to mass using $M_{\text{glycerol}} = 92\;\text{g/mol}$.

$$
m_{\text{glycerol}} = 1073.44 \times 92
$$

$$
\boxed{m_{\text{glycerol}} = 98{,}756\;\text{g} \;\approx\; 98.76\;\text{kg}}
$$

---

## 3.6 Step (e) — Mass Balance Verification

Mass balance requires:

$$
m_{\text{oil}} + m_{\text{methanol,reacted}} = m_{\text{FAME,actual}} + m_{\text{glycerol}}
$$

**Methanol actually consumed** (3 mol per mol oil reacted):

$$
m_{\text{MeOH,consumed}} = 3 \times 1073.44 \times 32 = 103{,}050\;\text{g} \approx 103.05\;\text{kg}
$$

**LHS:**

$$
1000\;\text{kg} + 103.05\;\text{kg} = 1103.05\;\text{kg}
$$

**RHS:**

$$
953.22\;\text{kg} + 98.76\;\text{kg} = 1051.98\;\text{kg}
$$

**Difference:**

$$
1103.05 - 1051.98 = 51.07\;\text{kg}
$$

This difference is accounted for by **unreacted oil (5 %)**, **residual methanol in the FAME phase**, and **trace soap formation** — all of which are recovered and recycled in a real plant.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Property Comparison Table

| Property | Petro-Diesel | Biodiesel (B100) | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density (kg/m³, 15 °C) | 850 | 880 | Affects mass-flow injection |
| Kinematic viscosity (mm²/s, 40 °C) | 1.9–4.1 | 1.9–6.0 | Determines atomization quality |
| Cetane number | 40–55 | 45–60 | Higher = smoother ignition |
| Flash point (°C) | 55–65 | 130–170 | Higher = safer storage |
| Sulfur content | < 10 ppm | < 15 ppm | Both are ultra-low-sulfur |
| Oxygen (mass %) | 0 | 10–12 | Improves combustion completeness |
| LHV (MJ/kg) | 42–45 | 37–40 | ~10 % lower energy density |
| Water solubility | Negligible | Slightly higher | Clogging risk in cold |
| Biodegradability | Slow | ~95 % in 28 days | Eco-friendly spills |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🟢 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Biodiesel Production (Transesterification)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **complete industrial process flow** for biodiesel production from vegetable oil feedstock, including the **FFA pre-treatment branch** (acid esterification) for low-quality feedstocks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["FEEDSTOCK INTAKE: Vegetable Oil OR Used Cooking Oil"] --> B{"FFA Test: Acid Value Less than 2 percent?"}
    B -- YES --> C["DRYING AND DEGUMMING: 110 degree C, Vacuum"]
    B -- NO --> D["ACID ESTERIFICATION: H2SO4 plus Methanol at 60 degree C"]
    D --> E["SETTLING TANK 1: Water Wash, FFA Reduction"]
    E --> C
    C --> F["REACTOR 1: Base Transesterification, NaOH 1 percent, Methanol 6 to 1 ratio, 60 degree C, 1 hour"]
    F --> G["SETTLING TANK 2: Gravity Separation, 6 to 12 hours"]
    G --> H["BOTTOM LAYER: Crude Glycerol, 50 percent purity"]
    G --> I["TOP LAYER: Crude Biodiesel FAME"]
    I --> J["WATER WASH UNIT: Removes Soap, Catalyst, Residual Methanol"]
    J --> K["DRYING COLUMN: Vacuum at 80 degree C, Moisture less than 500 ppm"]
    K --> L["QUALITY TEST: ASTM D6751 OR EN 14214 Standards"]
    L -- PASS --> M["FINAL PRODUCT: B100 Biodiesel, Storage Tank"]
    L -- FAIL --> N["RECYCLE TO REACTOR 1"]
    H --> O["GLYCEROL PURIFICATION: Vacuum Distillation"]
    O --> P["PHARMACEUTICAL OR COSMETIC GRADE GLYCEROL"]
    M --> Q["BLENDING UNIT: B20 Blend with Petro Diesel"]
    Q --> R["DISPATCH: Fuel Stations, Transport Sector"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read this diagram for your KTU exam answer:**
> - Always mention the **two-stage** processing when free fatty acids (FFA) are involved: first **acid esterification** (to convert FFA → FAME), then **base transesterification** (for the triglyceride).
> - Highlight the **two products**: biodiesel (top layer) and glycerol (bottom layer).
> - State the **standard** for quality: **ASTM D6751** (USA) or **EN 14214** (Europe).

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Quality Control Flow

This block diagram shows how **biodiesel quality is enforced** through the ASTM D6751 specification framework, mapping each tested parameter to its real-world impact.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT ["BIODIESEL SAMPLE INLET"]
        A1["Neat B100 Biodiesel"]
    end

    subgraph TESTBANK ["ASTM D6751 TEST SUITE"]
        B1["Density at 15 degree C"]
        B2["Kinematic Viscosity at 40 degree C"]
        B3["Cetane Number"]
        B4["Flash Point Pensky Martens"]
        B5["Acid Number Titration"]
        B6["Water Content Karl Fischer"]
        B7["Methanol Content GC"]
        B8["Oxidation Stability Rancimat"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT ["ENGINE PERFORMANCE OUTCOMES"]
        C1["Injector Atomization Quality"]
        C2["Cold Start Ignition"]
        C3["Storage Safety and Handling"]
        C4["Anti Corrosion of Metal Parts"]
        C5["Long Term Storage Stability"]
    end

    A1 --> B1 --> C1
    A1 --> B2 --> C1
    A1 --> B3 --> C2
    A1 --> B4 --> C3
    A1 --> B5 --> C4
    A1 --> B6 --> C4
    A1 --> B7 --> C5
    A1 --> B8 --> C5
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🟢 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define biodiesel. Write the chemical equation for its production by transesterification.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Biodiesel is a **mono-alkyl ester of long-chain fatty acids** derived from vegetable oils, animal fats, or used cooking oil. It is chemically known as **Fatty Acid Methyl Ester (FAME)** when methanol is used.

The transesterification reaction is:

$$
\text{Triglyceride} \;+\; 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \;\xrightarrow[\text{NaOH or KOH}]{\text{60--65 °C}}\; 3\,\text{FAME} \;+\; \text{Glycerol}
$$

In this reaction, the **triglyceride** reacts with **3 moles of methanol** in the presence of an alkaline catalyst (NaOH/KOH) to produce **3 moles of FAME (biodiesel)** and **1 mole of glycerol** as a by-product.

*(1 mark — definition; 1 mark — equation; 1 mark — explanation of reactants/products.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]
**List any six advantages of biodiesel over petroleum diesel.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — any six):**

1. **Renewable** — produced from biomass that regrows annually.
2. **Biodegradable** — ~95 % degrades in 28 days (vs. diesel which persists for decades).
3. **Non-toxic** — exhaust is far less carcinogenic.
4. **Higher cetane number** — smoother and more complete combustion.
5. **Higher flash point (~130 °C)** — much safer to store and transport.
6. **Lower life-cycle CO₂ emissions** — closed carbon cycle.
7. **Excellent lubricity** — reduces engine wear; extends injector life.
8. **Reduces dependency on crude oil imports** — strategic energy security.

*(½ mark each, any six.)*

---

## 📗 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ✏️ CHOICE A — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the **transesterification process** for biodiesel production. Discuss the role of **methanol**, the **catalyst**, and the **operating conditions**.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A biodiesel plant processes **500 kg of waste cooking oil** (molar mass = 880 g/mol) with **methanol** at a **6 : 1 molar ratio**. The conversion achieved is **92 %**. Calculate:
- (i) Moles of oil processed
- (ii) Mass of methanol required
- (iii) Theoretical mass of biodiesel (FAME)
- (iv) Actual biodiesel produced
- (v) Mass of glycerol co-product  **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1 — Define transesterification (1 mark):**
Transesterification is the chemical reaction in which an ester (triglyceride) reacts with an alcohol (methanol) in the presence of a catalyst to form a new ester (FAME) and an alcohol (glycerol).

**Step 2 — Flowchart (3 marks):** Refer to **Section 4.1** diagram. The student must list:

| Stage | Operation |
|---|---|
| 1 | Feedstock intake (oil + methanol + NaOH) |
| 2 | Pre-treatment (FFA check → acid esterification if FFA > 2 %) |
| 3 | Reactor: 60 °C, 1 hour, stirred |
| 4 | Settling: 6–12 h, two-layer separation |
| 5 | Biodiesel layer: water wash → drying → ASTM test → storage |
| 6 | Glycerol layer: purification → pharma grade |

**Step 3 — Role of methanol (1 mark):** Acyl-acceptor; lightest, cheapest short-chain alcohol; excess (6:1) drives equilibrium forward.

**Step 4 — Role of catalyst (1 mark):** NaOH (1 % w/w) speeds the reaction, lowers activation energy; faster at low temperature; cheaper than acid catalyst.

**Step 5 — Operating conditions (1 mark):** Temperature 60–65 °C (above methanol boiling point requires pressure), 1 hour reaction time, atmospheric pressure.

---

#### Model Solution for (b)

**(i) Moles of oil:**

$$
n_{\text{oil}} = \dfrac{500{,}000\;\text{g}}{880\;\text{g/mol}} = 568.18\;\text{mol}
$$

**(ii) Mass of methanol at 6 : 1 ratio:**

$$
n_{\text{MeOH}} = 6 \times 568.18 = 3409.09\;\text{mol}
$$

$$
m_{\text{MeOH}} = 3409.09 \times 32 = 109{,}091\;\text{g} \;\approx\; 109.09\;\text{kg}
$$

**(iii) Theoretical biodiesel mass:**

$$
n_{\text{FAME,theor}} = 3 \times 568.18 = 1704.55\;\text{mol}
$$

$$
m_{\text{FAME,theor}} = 1704.55 \times 296 = 504{,}545\;\text{g} \;\approx\; 504.55\;\text{kg}
$$

**(iv) Actual biodiesel at 92 % conversion:**

$$
m_{\text{FAME,actual}} = 504.55 \times 0.92 = 464.18\;\text{kg}
$$

**(v) Mass of glycerol:**

$$
n_{\text{oil,reacted}} = 568.18 \times 0.92 = 522.73\;\text{mol}
$$

$$
m_{\text{glycerol}} = 522.73 \times 92 = 48{,}091\;\text{g} \;\approx\; 48.09\;\text{kg}
$$

*(Each sub-part: 1 mark + ½ mark for correct unit. Total 7 marks.)*

---

### ✏️ CHOICE B — Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice]

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Compare the **properties of biodiesel and petro-diesel** in a tabular form (at least 8 parameters). Discuss **why biodiesel has a higher cetane number but lower heating value** than petro-diesel.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the various **feedstocks for biodiesel production in India**. Explain the significance of **non-edible oils** like **Jatropha and Karanja** for the Indian economy. List the **ASTM D6751 specifications** for biodiesel.  **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Property Table (4 marks):** Refer to **Section 3.7 Analytical Matrix**. The student must reproduce at least 8 rows.

**Why higher cetane number (1.5 marks):**
Petro-diesel is a mixture of **pure hydrocarbons (C_x H_y)** which, due to absence of oxygen, ignites less readily. Biodiesel is an **oxygenated ester** (contains ~10–12 % O₂ by mass) — the ester functional group lowers the activation energy for ignition, leading to a **shorter ignition delay** and hence a **higher cetane number** (~45–60 vs. 40–55).

**Why lower heating value (1.5 marks):**
Petro-diesel contains only **C and H**, both of which release energy upon combustion (C → CO₂ and H → H₂O). Biodiesel, in addition to C and H, also contains **O atoms already bonded** to the molecule — these atoms are already partially oxidised and **release less energy** when combusted. Moreover, biodiesel has **higher density but ~10 % lower LHV** (≈ 37 MJ/kg vs. 43 MJ/kg) due to the presence of the oxygenated ester group and the absence of long aliphatic-only chains.

---

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Feedstocks in India (3 marks):**

| Category | Examples |
|---|---|
| Edible oils | Soybean, sunflower, groundnut, coconut, palm |
| Non-edible oils | **Jatropha, Karanja (Pongamia), Mahua, Neem, Castor** |
| Waste sources | Used cooking oil (UCO), animal tallow, fish oil |
| Algal oils | Microalgae (third-generation, future scope) |

**Significance of non-edible oils (2 marks):**
- Do **not compete with the food chain** → no food-vs-fuel debate.
- Can be cultivated on **wastelands, degraded soils, and roadside plantations** — ideal for rural employment.
- India has a **National Biodiesel Mission (NBM)** targeting **jatropha cultivation on ~11.2 million hectares** of wasteland.
- Reduces India’s **crude oil import bill** (~$120 billion annually).

**ASTM D6751 Specifications (2 marks):** Student should list at least 6:
- Cetane number ≥ 47
- Flash point ≥ 130 °C
- Water & sediment ≤ 0.05 % vol
- Kinematic viscosity (40 °C) = 1.9–6.0 mm²/s
- Acid number ≤ 0.5 mg KOH/g
- Sulfur ≤ 15 ppm (S15 grade)
- Oxidation stability ≥ 3 h

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing transesterification with esterification.** Esterification converts FFA + alcohol → FAME + water (catalyst = acid). Transesterification converts triglyceride + alcohol → FAME + glycerol (catalyst = base). Examiners specifically test this distinction.
>
> 2. **Using stoichiometric ratio (3:1) instead of practical ratio (6:1) in methanol mass calculations.** This leads to **wrong values**. Always state which ratio is used.
>
> 3. **Molar mass errors:** Students often use 92 g/mol for biodiesel instead of 296 g/mol, or vice versa. **Glycerol = 92; FAME ≈ 296.**
>
> 4. **Forgetting to apply conversion %.** Theoretical and actual yields are different — examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for this.
>
> 5. **Writing "transesterification is a single-step reaction."** It is a **three-step reversible** reaction (triglyceride → diglyceride → monoglyceride → glycerol).
>
> 6. **Confusing B20, B100.** B20 = 20 % biodiesel + 80 % diesel. B100 = neat biodiesel. Examiners use these acronyms without explanation.
>
> 7. **No unit in final numerical answer.** Every numerical **must** end with correct SI units (kg, g/mol, MJ/kg). Missing unit = **½ mark deduction** consistently.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 KTU Biodiesel — Rapid Revision Checklist:**
>
> ✅ **Definition:** Biodiesel = mono-alkyl ester of long-chain fatty acids (FAME) from biomass.
>
> ✅ **Core process:** Transesterification (triglyceride + methanol → FAME + glycerol).
>
> ✅ **Catalyst:** NaOH or KOH (1 % w/w) for low-FFA oils; H₂SO₄ for high-FFA oils (two-step).
>
> ✅ **Stoichiometric ratio** = 3 : 1; **practical ratio** = 6 : 1 to 12 : 1.
>
> ✅ **Temperature:** 60–65 °C, **time:** 1 hour, **pressure:** atmospheric.
>
> ✅ **Molar masses (must memorize):** MeOH = 32; EtOH = 46; Glycerol = 92; FAME ≈ 296; NaOH = 40; KOH = 56.
>
> ✅ **Two products:** Biodiesel (top, lighter) + Glycerol (bottom, denser).
>
> ✅ **Standards:** **ASTM D6751** (USA), **EN 14214** (Europe).
>
> ✅ **Biodiesel advantages:** Renewable, biodegradable, non-toxic, higher cetane, higher flash point, lower life-cycle CO₂.
>
> ✅ **Biodiesel disadvantages:** ~10 % lower LHV, slightly higher viscosity, hygroscopic (absorbs water), can degrade rubber seals (older vehicles).
>
> ✅ **Indian feedstocks:** Edible (soybean, palm) + Non-edible (Jatropha, Karanja, Mahua, Neem).
>
> ✅ **Glycerol by-product:** Used in pharma, cosmetics, soaps — adds economic viability.
>
> ✅ **Life-cycle CO₂ reduction:** ~78 % lower than petro-diesel.
>
> ✅ **Key reactions to remember:**
> - Transesterification: Triglyceride + 3 R-OH → 3 FAME + Glycerol
> - Esterification (FFA pre-treatment): FFA + R-OH → FAME + H₂O
> - Saponification (side reaction): FFA + NaOH → Soap + H₂O *(undesirable — explains why high-FFA oils must be pre-treated)*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT631 - RENEWABLE ENERGY AND GREEN TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** General Principles of the Technology: Production and Use. Alcohol for Fuel Purpose: Production Processes and Technology. Hydrogen as Fuel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITIONS & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 General Principles of Biofuel Technology — Production and Use

A **biofuel** is defined (KTU 2024 syllabus standard) as a gaseous, liquid, or solid fuel produced from **biomass** — any organic matter of recent biological origin (plants, animals, agricultural residues, forestry waste, or municipal solid waste containing biodegradable fractions) — through physical, thermochemical, biochemical, or biological conversion routes. The fundamental principle underlying biofuel technology is the conversion of solar energy stored in chemical bonds (via photosynthesis) into portable, high-energy-density transport fuels that can replace or blend with conventional petroleum-derived fuels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Photosynthesis Reaction (Fundamental Basis):**
> The entire foundation of biofuel technology rests on this reversible thermodynamic cycle:
> $$6CO_2 + 6H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{Sunlight, Chlorophyll}} C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2$$
> The glucose produced ($C_6H_{12}O_6$, **molar mass = 180 g/mol**) is the building block for all subsequent bio-derived fuels.

**Biofuel Classification (KTU Mandatory):**

| Generation | Feedstock | Conversion Route | Typical Fuel |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1st Gen | Sugar, starch, vegetable oils, animal fats | Fermentation, transesterification, anaerobic digestion | Bioethanol, biodiesel, biogas |
| 2nd Gen | Lignocellulosic biomass, agricultural residues | Hydrolysis + fermentation, gasification + FT, pyrolysis | Cellulosic ethanol, FT-diesel, bio-oil |
| 3rd Gen | Algae (microalgae, macroalgae) | Algal cultivation + extraction/upgrading | Algal biodiesel, biohydrogen |
| 4th Gen | Engineered biomass (synthetic biology) | Direct solar-to-fuel, electrofuels, genetically engineered pathways | Electrofuels, photobiological H2 |

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Energy Bank Account":**
Think of a plant as a **savings account** where the sun deposits "energy coins" every day. When we burn fossil fuels, we are **withdrawing** from an ancient, non-replenishable energy vault (millions of years old). Biofuels, in contrast, allow us to **withdraw only what was deposited this season** — making them renewable and (largely) carbon-neutral because the $CO_2$ released during combustion equals the $CO_2$ absorbed during growth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carbon Neutrality Caveat:** While biofuels are considered carbon-neutral at the **combustion point**, they are not zero-carbon when **lifecycle emissions** (cultivation, fertilizers, harvesting, transport, processing) are included. 2nd generation biofuels typically show **60–90%** lifecycle GHG reduction versus gasoline.

### 1.2 Alcohol as a Fuel — Foundational Definition

**Alcohols** are organic compounds characterized by one or more hydroxyl functional groups (–OH) bonded to a carbon atom. For fuel applications, the two most relevant are:

- **Ethanol ($C_2H_5OH$):** A clear, colorless, volatile liquid with a characteristic odor; produced predominantly by **fermentation of sugars** (and increasingly from lignocellulosic feedstocks).
- **Methanol ($CH_3OH$):** The simplest alcohol; produced almost exclusively from **synthesis gas (syngas)** — a mixture of CO and $H_2$ — derived from natural gas, coal, or biomass gasification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Alcohols are Exceptional Engine Fuels:**
> 1. **High Octane Number:** Ethanol ≈ **108–110 RON**, Methanol ≈ **106–112 RON** (vs. gasoline ≈ 92 RON). This permits higher compression ratios → higher thermal efficiency.
> 2. **High Latent Heat of Vaporization:** Ethanol ≈ **842 kJ/kg** (vs. gasoline ≈ 305 kJ/kg) → excellent charge cooling, reduced knocking.
> 3. **Oxygenated Molecule:** Already contains oxygen → enables more complete (lean-burn) combustion, reducing CO and particulate emissions.
> 4. **Renewable / Sustainable:** Ethanol from biomass recycles atmospheric $CO_2$.
> 5. **High Flame Speed:** Methanol flame speed ≈ **52 cm/s** vs. gasoline ≈ 38 cm/s.

### 1.3 Hydrogen as a Fuel — Foundational Definition

**Hydrogen ($H_2$)** is the lightest, most abundant element in the universe, but on Earth it exists almost exclusively **bound in compounds** (water, hydrocarbons, biomass). As a fuel, hydrogen is an **energy carrier** — not a primary energy source — meaning it must be produced from other energy inputs (electricity, natural gas, biomass, sunlight) before it can release its energy via combustion or in a fuel cell.

**Energy Density of Hydrogen:**
- **Higher Heating Value (HHV):** $\approx$ **141.9 MJ/kg**
- **Lower Heating Value (LHV):** $\approx$ **119.9 MJ/kg**
- **Volumetric Density (at STP, gas):** Only $\approx$ **10.8 MJ/m³** (vs. gasoline ≈ 34,000 MJ/m³) — explaining why storage is the central engineering challenge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Hydrogen is a "Future Fuel":**
> 1. **Zero Carbon Combustion:** Only by-product is pure water ($H_2O$).
> 2. **Highest energy-to-mass ratio** of any fuel.
> 3. **Fuel cell efficiency** (50–60%, potentially up to 85% with CHP) far exceeds Carnot-limited heat engines (~35–45%).
> 4. **Versatile end-use:** Internal combustion engines, gas turbines, fuel cells, rockets.

**Intuitive Analogy — Hydrogen as the "Universal Battery":**
Imagine electricity as **water flowing in a pipe** — you cannot store it cheaply in bulk. Hydrogen acts as a **storage tank** for that electricity: surplus renewable electricity splits water into hydrogen (charging the tank), and later, hydrogen is recombined in a fuel cell to release electricity (discharging the tank). This is the principle behind the **Power-to-Gas (P2G)** concept central to green hydrogen economy.

### 1.4 Production vs. Use — The Two-Pillar Principle

All biofuel / alcohol / hydrogen technology rests on **two functional pillars**:

1. **Production (Upstream):** Sustainable feedstock → efficient conversion → purified fuel.
2. **Use (Downstream):** Engine compatibility → emissions behavior → economic viability.

A fuel is only viable if **both pillars are simultaneously optimized**. Hydrogen, for instance, has stellar "use" properties but a difficult "production" chain; methanol has moderate "use" properties but a mature, large-scale "production" infrastructure.

## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Ethanol — Production Processes and Technology

#### 2.1.1 First Generation Ethanol (Sugar/Starch Route)

**Step 1: Feedstock Preparation**
- Sugarcane, sugar beet → direct juice extraction (milling/diffusion).
- Corn, wheat, cassava → starch must first be **hydrolyzed** to fermentable sugars.

**Step 2: Saccharification (Starch Hydrolysis)**
$$\text{Starch} + nH_2O \xrightarrow{\alpha/\beta\text{-amylase, } 85\text{–}95°C} n\text{-Glucose units}$$

The enzymes **amylase** (liquefaction, ~85°C, pH 6.0–6.5) and **glucoamylase** (saccharification, ~60°C, pH 4.5) are added sequentially.

**Step 3: Fermentation (The Heart of the Process)**
$$\underbrace{C_6H_{12}O_6}_{\text{Glucose}} \xrightarrow{\text{Yeast (Saccharomyces cerevisiae), } 30°C} 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2 + \text{Heat}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Theoretical Yield (Gay-Lussac Equation, 1828):**
> 1 mole glucose (180 g) → 2 moles ethanol (2 × 46 = 92 g)
> $$\text{Yield} = \frac{92}{180} \times 100\% = 51.1\%\,\text{by mass (theoretical max)}$$
> Industrial practice achieves **90–95%** of this theoretical yield due to yeast biomass formation and minor by-products (glycerol, succinic acid, higher alcohols).

**Step 4: Distillation** — produces ~95.6% v/v ethanol–water azeotrope.
**Step 5: Dehydration** — molecular sieve adsorption (3 Å zeolites) or azeotropic distillation with benzene/cyclohexane → **anhydrous ethanol (99.5%+)**.

#### 2.1.2 Second Generation Ethanol (Lignocellulosic Route)

The lignocellulosic biomass (cellulose ~40–50%, hemicellulose ~25–35%, lignin ~15–25%) must overcome the **"recalcitrance barrier"**:

1. **Pretreatment** (physical: milling; chemical: dilute acid, alkali, organosolv; physico-chemical: steam explosion, AFEX).
2. **Enzymatic Hydrolysis** — cellulase cocktail converts cellulose → glucose.
3. **Fermentation** — same as 1G, but with pentose-utilizing yeasts (e.g., *Pichia stipitis*) for hemicellulose-derived xylose.
4. **Distillation + Dehydration.**

### 2.2 Methanol — Production Processes and Technology

**Methanol is synthesized exclusively via the catalytic conversion of synthesis gas (syngas):**

$$\underbrace{CO + 2H_2}_{\text{Syngas}} \xrightarrow{\text{Cu/ZnO/Al}_2\text{O}_3\text{ catalyst, } 200\text{–}300°C, \,50\text{–}100\,\text{bar}} CH_3OH$$

The syngas source determines whether methanol is:
- **Gray methanol** (from natural gas — steam reforming),
- **Brown methanol** (from coal gasification),
- **Green methanol** (from biomass gasification or $CO_2$ hydrogenation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **$CO_2$ Hydrogenation Route (Future of Green Methanol):**
> $$CO_2 + 3H_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Cu/ZnO/ZrO}_2\text{, catalyst}} CH_3OH + H_2O$$
> This reaction is carbon-negative when paired with green $H_2$ and captured $CO_2$.

### 2.3 Hydrogen — Production Processes and Technology

#### 2.3.1 Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) — 48% of global H₂
$$\underbrace{CH_4 + H_2O}_{\text{Reforming}} \xrightarrow{\text{Ni catalyst, } 700\text{–}900°C} CO + 3H_2$$
$$\underbrace{CO + H_2O}_{\text{WGS}} \xrightarrow{\text{Fe/Cr or Cu/Zn, } 200\text{–}450°C} CO_2 + H_2 \quad (\text{Water-Gas Shift})$$

#### 2.3.2 Electrolysis — 4% of global H₂ (rising fast)
$$2H_2O_{(l)} \xrightarrow{\text{Electricity, PEM/Alkaline/Solid Oxide}} 2H_2{_{(g)}} + O_2{_{(g)}}$$

#### 2.3.3 Biomass Gasification — Emerging
$$C_xH_yO_z + (x-z)H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{High T, limited O}_2} xCO + \left(\frac{y}{2}+x-z\right)H_2$$

#### 2.3.4 Photoelectrochemical / Photobiological — Long-term promise
Direct water splitting using sunlight on semiconductor photoelectrodes or via algal/bacterial photosynthesis.

## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric Combustion of Ethanol

**The complete combustion reaction:**

$$C_2H_5OH + a\,O_2 + (3.76\,a)\,N_2 \rightarrow b\,CO_2 + c\,H_2O + (3.76\,a)\,N_2$$

**Step 1 — Carbon balance:**
Atoms of C on left = atoms of C on right
$$2 = b \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{b = 2}$$

**Step 2 — Hydrogen balance:**
Atoms of H on left = atoms of H on right
$$6 = 2c \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{c = 3}$$

**Step 3 — Oxygen balance:**
Atoms of O on left = atoms of O on right
$$1 + 2a = 2b + c$$
Substituting $b = 2$ and $c = 3$:
$$1 + 2a = 2(2) + 3 = 7$$
$$2a = 6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{a = 3}$$

**Therefore, complete combustion:**
$$C_2H_5OH + 3\,O_2 + (3.76 \times 3)\,N_2 \rightarrow 2\,CO_2 + 3\,H_2O + 11.28\,N_2$$

**Step 4 — Stoichiometric Air-Fuel ratio (mass basis):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass of air} &= 4.76 \times a \times 32 \\
&= 4.76 \times 3 \times 32 \\
&= 456.96\,\text{kg air per kmol O}_2 \\
\text{Mass of fuel} &= 2(12) + 6(1) + 16 = 46\,\text{kg/kmol ethanol} \\
A/F_{\text{stoich}} &= \frac{456.96}{46} \\
&= \boxed{9.93 : 1 \text{ (mass basis)}}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stoichiometric Combustion of Methanol

**The complete combustion reaction:**
$$CH_3OH + a\,O_2 \rightarrow b\,CO_2 + c\,H_2O$$

**Step 1 — Carbon balance:** $1 = b \Rightarrow b = 1$
**Step 2 — Hydrogen balance:** $4 = 2c \Rightarrow c = 2$
**Step 3 — Oxygen balance:** $1 + 2a = 2(1) + 2 = 4 \Rightarrow 2a = 3 \Rightarrow a = 1.5$

**Complete reaction:**
$$CH_3OH + 1.5\,O_2 + (1.5 \times 3.76)\,N_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2\,H_2O + 5.64\,N_2$$

**Step 4 — Air-Fuel Ratio (mass basis):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass of air} &= 4.76 \times 1.5 \times 32 = 228.48\,\text{kg} \\
\text{Mass of methanol} &= 12 + 4 + 16 = 32\,\text{kg/kmol} \\
A/F_{\text{stoich}} &= \frac{228.48}{32} \\
&= \boxed{7.14 : 1 \text{ (mass basis)}}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Stoichiometric Combustion of Hydrogen

**Reaction:**
$$H_2 + 0.5\,O_2 + (0.5 \times 3.76)\,N_2 \rightarrow H_2O + 1.88\,N_2$$

**Air-Fuel Ratio (mass basis):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass of air} &= 4.76 \times 0.5 \times 32 = 76.16\,\text{kg} \\
\text{Mass of fuel} &= 2.016\,\text{kg} \\
A/F_{\text{stoich}} &= \frac{76.16}{2.016} \\
&= \boxed{37.78 : 1 \text{ (mass basis)}}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Hydrogen's very high A/F ratio (≈38:1) means **much larger intake volumes** are required for the same energy input, explaining why hydrogen ICEs deliver lower power density than gasoline engines unless significantly boosted or fuel cells are used.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Theoretical Ethanol Yield from Glucose (Gay-Lussac)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Reaction: } & C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2 \\
\text{Molar mass glucose: } & 6(12) + 12(1) + 6(16) = 180\,\text{g/mol} \\
\text{Molar mass ethanol: } & 2(12) + 6(1) + 16 = 46\,\text{g/mol} \\
\text{Mass of ethanol produced per mol glucose: } & 2 \times 46 = 92\,\text{g} \\
\text{Theoretical yield} &= \frac{92\,\text{g}}{180\,\text{g}} \times 100\% \\
&= \boxed{51.11\% \text{ by mass}}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Reality:** Real-world fermentation achieves only **90–95%** of the theoretical 51.11% due to:
> - **Cell mass production** (5–7% of glucose diverted to yeast biomass)
> - **By-products:** glycerol (~5%), succinic acid, acetic acid, higher alcohols (fusel oils)
> - **Incomplete substrate utilization** when ethanol concentration exceeds yeast tolerance (~12–15% v/v)

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Minimum Energy for Electrolytic Hydrogen Production (Faraday's Law)

**Faraday's First Law:**
$$m_{H_2} = \frac{M_{H_2} \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

Where:
- $M_{H_2} = 2.016\,\text{g/mol}$
- $n = 2$ (electrons per $H_2$ molecule)
- $F = 96{,}485\,\text{C/mol}$ (Faraday constant)
- $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (s)

**Theoretical Cell Voltage (Reversible):**
$$E_{rev} = \frac{\Delta G^\circ}{nF} = \frac{237.2\,\text{kJ/mol}}{2 \times 96{,}485\,\text{C/mol}} = 1.229\,\text{V}$$

**Theoretical Minimum Energy for 1 kg of $H_2$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Moles of } H_2 \text{ in 1 kg} &= \frac{1000}{2.016} = 496.03\,\text{mol} \\
\text{Minimum energy} &= n \cdot F \cdot E_{rev} \cdot \frac{496.03}{n} \\
&= F \cdot E_{rev} \cdot \text{moles} \\
&= \frac{237.2\,\text{kJ/mol} \times 1000\,\text{g}}{2.016\,\text{g/mol}} \\
&= \frac{237.2 \times 1000}{2.016}\,\text{kJ} \\
&= 117{,}659\,\text{kJ/kg} \\
&= 32.68\,\text{kWh/kg} \\
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Thermodynamic Equivalent (LHV):** Since $LHV_{H_2} = 119.9\,\text{MJ/kg} = 33.33\,\text{kWh/kg}$, the electrolysis "energy return" relative to the LHV is:
> $$\eta_{\text{theoretical,max}} = \frac{LHV}{E_{\text{input}}} = \frac{33.33}{32.68} = \mathbf{102\%}$$
> The 2% surplus comes from the entropy term $T\Delta S$ — reversible electrolysis actually absorbs heat from the surroundings. In practice, real electrolyzers operate at **70–82% efficiency** (HHV basis) due to overpotentials.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Properties of Alcohols vs. Hydrogen

| Property | Gasoline | Ethanol | Methanol | Hydrogen |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Molecular formula | $C_4$ to $C_{12}$ mix | $C_2H_5OH$ | $CH_3OH$ | $H_2$ |
| Molar mass (g/mol) | ~100 | 46 | 32 | 2.016 |
| Density (kg/L, liquid) | 0.74 | 0.789 | 0.792 | 0.071 (liq) |
| LHV (MJ/kg) | 43.4 | 26.9 | 20.1 | 119.9 |
| Octane Number (RON) | 92 | 108 | 112 | >130 |
| Stoich A/F (mass) | 14.7 | 9.0 | 6.4 | 34.3 |
| Latent heat (kJ/kg) | 305 | 842 | 1100 | 445 (liq) |
| Flame speed (cm/s) | 38 | 49 | 52 | 291 |
| Auto-ignition T (°C) | 257 | 423 | 464 | 500 |
| Flammability limits (% vol) | 1.4–7.6 | 3.3–19 | 6.7–36 | 4–75 |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**
*Define biofuels. List any four advantages of alcohols as engine fuels compared to gasoline.*

**Model Answer:**
Biofuels are gaseous, liquid or solid fuels derived from biomass — any organic matter of recent biological origin — through biological, chemical, or thermochemical conversion processes.

**Advantages of alcohol fuels over gasoline:**
1. **High octane number** — ethanol (108 RON) and methanol (112 RON) enable higher compression ratios, improving thermal efficiency.
2. **Oxygenated molecular structure** — already contains O atoms, leading to more complete combustion and reduced CO and particulate emissions.
3. **High latent heat of vaporization** — provides significant charge cooling, suppressing knock in SI engines.
4. **Renewable and biodegradable** — produced from biomass, hence carbon-neutral over the lifecycle, and degrade rapidly in the environment if spilled.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**
*Write a short note on hydrogen as a future fuel. Mention any three challenges in its adoption.*

**Model Answer:**
Hydrogen ($H_2$) is the lightest and most abundant element in the universe, with the highest energy-to-mass ratio (LHV = 119.9 MJ/kg) of any conventional fuel. Its combustion produces only water as a by-product, making it a **zero-carbon fuel** at point of use. It can power fuel cells at 50–60% electrical efficiency (vs. 35–45% for ICEs) and serves as a versatile energy carrier for the **Power-to-Gas** economy.

**Three adoption challenges:**
1. **Production:** >95% of global $H_2$ is currently from fossil fuels (gray hydrogen); green hydrogen via electrolysis is still expensive.
2. **Storage & transport:** Very low volumetric density requires high-pressure (350–700 bar) compression, cryogenic liquefaction (–253°C), or advanced solid-state metal hydrides.
3. **Embrittlement & leakage:** $H_2$ molecules are small enough to embrittle metals and leak through seals, raising safety and durability concerns.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

**QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Pattern Adapted]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat flow diagram, describe the production process of **bioethanol from sugarcane molasses** by fermentation. State the Gay-Lussac equation and calculate the theoretical yield of ethanol from 1000 kg of glucose. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Compare **first-generation and second-generation ethanol** production in terms of feedstock, conversion route, yield, energy balance, and lifecycle GHG emissions. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Flow diagram of ethanol from molasses:**

```
Sugarcane Molasses (40–55% sugars)
        ↓
        Dilution with water to 15–20% sugar concentration
        ↓
        pH adjustment to 4.5–5.0 (H₂SO₄)
        ↓
        Nutrient addition (N, P, trace minerals)
        ↓
        Sterilization (pasteurization at 80°C for 15 min)
        ↓
        Cooling to 28–32°C
        ↓
        Inoculation with Saccharomyces cerevisiae (yeast)
        ↓
        Fermentation (anaerobic, 30°C, 48–72 hours)
        ↓
        Fermented wash (~8–10% ethanol, v/v)
        ↓
        Distillation (multistage columns) → 95.6% azeotrope
        ↓
        Molecular sieve dehydration → 99.5% anhydrous ethanol
        ↓
        Denaturation (for industrial fuel use)
        ↓
        Final Product: Anhydrous Bioethanol
```

**Gay-Lussac Equation (Fermentation):**
$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Yeast}} 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2$$

**Theoretical yield calculation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Molar mass glucose} &= 6(12) + 12(1) + 6(16) = 180\,\text{g/mol} \\
\text{Molar mass ethanol} &= 2(12) + 6(1) + 16 = 46\,\text{g/mol} \\
\text{From 1 mol glucose (180 g)} &\rightarrow 2\,\text{mol ethanol (92 g)} \\
\text{Yield percentage} &= \frac{92}{180} \times 100 = 51.11\% \\
\text{For 1000 kg glucose:} &\rightarrow 1000 \times 0.5111 = \mathbf{511.1\,\text{kg ethanol}} \\
\text{Moles of ethanol} &= \frac{511{,}100\,\text{g}}{46\,\text{g/mol}} = 11{,}110\,\text{mol} \\
\text{Volume of ethanol} &= \frac{11{,}110 \times 46}{789\,\text{g/L}} = \mathbf{647.6\,\text{L ethanol}}
\end{aligned}$$

In practice, ~90% of theoretical yield is achieved → **~583 L** per tonne of glucose.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

| Criterion | 1st Generation Ethanol | 2nd Generation Ethanol |
|---|---|---|
| **Feedstock** | Sugarcane, corn, sugar beet, molasses | Rice straw, corn stover, bagasse, wood chips |
| **Conversion route** | Direct fermentation of sugars | Pretreatment → enzymatic hydrolysis → fermentation |
| **Yield (L/ha)** | High (5000–8000) | Potentially higher (7000–10000) |
| **Energy balance (EROI)** | 1.3–8 (favorable) | 2–6 (improving) |
| **Lifecycle GHG savings vs gasoline** | 40–60% | 60–90% |
| **Feedstock cost** | Competes with food | Waste-based (low cost) |
| **Technology maturity** | Commercial globally | Demonstration / pre-commercial |
| **Key barrier** | Food vs fuel debate | Recalcitrance, enzyme cost |

2G ethanol avoids the food-vs-fuel controversy and uses agricultural waste; however, it faces challenges with biomass pretreatment, expensive cellulase enzymes, and the need for pentose-fermenting organisms to convert hemicellulose-derived xylose.

---

**QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Pattern Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain **steam methane reforming** (SMR) for hydrogen production with balanced chemical equations. Calculate the volume of hydrogen (at STP) produced from 1 kg of methane. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Discuss the **photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting** method for green hydrogen production. List its advantages and current limitations. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) — Two-Stage Process:**

**Stage 1 — Reforming (endothermic, in primary reformer at 700–900°C, Ni/Al₂O₃ catalyst):**
$$CH_4 + H_2O \rightarrow CO + 3H_2 \quad \Delta H = +206\,\text{kJ/mol}$$

**Stage 2 — Water-Gas Shift (exothermic, two-stage: high-T shift Fe/Cr at 350°C, low-T shift Cu/Zn at 200°C):**
$$CO + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2 \quad \Delta H = -41\,\text{kJ/mol}$$

**Overall SMR reaction:**
$$CH_4 + 2H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + 4H_2$$

**Volume calculation of H₂ from 1 kg CH₄:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Molar mass of } CH_4 &= 12 + 4(1) = 16\,\text{g/mol} \\
\text{Moles of } CH_4 \text{ in 1 kg} &= \frac{1000}{16} = 62.5\,\text{mol} \\
\text{From stoichiometry: 1 mol } CH_4 &\rightarrow 4\,\text{mol } H_2 \\
\text{Moles of } H_2 &= 62.5 \times 4 = 250\,\text{mol} \\
\text{Volume at STP (22.4 L/mol)} &= 250 \times 22.4 = \mathbf{5600\,\text{L}} \\
\text{Volume at STP} &= \mathbf{5.6\,\text{m}^3\,\text{of } H_2 \text{ per kg } CH_4}
\end{aligned}$$

The H₂ must then be purified via **Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA)** to reach 99.99% purity for fuel-cell applications.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Photoelectrochemical (PEC) Water Splitting:**

In PEC water splitting, a semiconductor photoelectrode (typically TiO₂, Fe₂O₃, BiVO₄, or tandem perovskite/silicon) is illuminated by sunlight. Photons with energy greater than the semiconductor bandgap generate **electron–hole pairs**. The electrons reduce water at the cathode to produce H₂, while the holes oxidize water at the anode to produce O₂.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Semiconductor} &\xrightarrow{h\nu} e^- + h^+ \\
2H^+ + 2e^- &\rightarrow H_2 \quad \text{(cathode)} \\
H_2O + 2h^+ &\rightarrow \frac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ \quad \text{(anode)} \\
\hline
\text{Overall: } H_2O &\xrightarrow{h\nu} H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2
\end{aligned}$$

The minimum theoretical bandgap required is **1.23 eV** (vs. ΔG°), but practical systems require 1.6–2.2 eV due to overpotential losses.

**Advantages of PEC:**
- **Direct solar-to-hydrogen** conversion — no separate electricity step.
- **No moving parts** — high theoretical reliability.
- **Zero emissions** if sunlight is used.
- **Can be coupled with desalination** in island/coastal locations.

**Current limitations:**
- **Low solar-to-H₂ efficiency** (1–10%, target >15%).
- **Photocorrosion** of semiconductor materials in aqueous electrolyte.
- **Expensive materials** (e.g., Pt catalysts, GaAs tandem cells).
- **Scale-up challenges** — large-area, durable photoelectrodes remain elusive.
- **Membrane and gas-separation** issues in monolithic reactors.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT forget the 3.76 factor** for nitrogen when calculating stoichiometric air-fuel ratios — examiners specifically test this.
> 2. **Always show the stoichiometric balance (C, H, O atoms) explicitly** before writing the complete equation — partial credit is awarded for individual balances.
> 3. **Distinguish HHV (141.9 MJ/kg) from LHV (119.9 MJ/kg) for hydrogen** — many students interchange them, losing 1 mark.
> 4. **For fermentation yield questions, always state the theoretical maximum (51.11%) AND the industrial practical range (90–95% of theoretical)** — partial credit is awarded for both.
> 5. **Label flow diagrams with operating conditions** (T, P, catalyst, residence time) — diagrams without conditions get only 50% of the allocated marks.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Biofuel generations:** 1G (sugar/starch), 2G (lignocellulose), 3G (algae), 4G (engineered/e-fuels).
- **Gay-Lussac equation:** $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2C_2H_5OH + 2CO_2$; theoretical yield = **51.11% by mass**.
- **Ethanol fuel blends:** E5, E10, E20, E85, E100; commonly used in Brazil (E27) and India (E20 mandatory target).
- **Methanol synthesis:** $CO + 2H_2 \rightarrow CH_3OH$; Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ catalyst, 200–300°C, 50–100 bar.
- **Stoichiometric A/F ratios:** Ethanol ≈ 9.0, Methanol ≈ 6.4, Gasoline ≈ 14.7, Hydrogen ≈ 34.3 (mass basis).
- **Hydrogen LHV** = 119.9 MJ/kg; **HHV** = 141.9 MJ/kg — HHV > LHV because latent heat of water condensation is recovered.
- **Steam reforming** ($CH_4 + H_2O \rightarrow CO + 3H_2$) is endothermic (+206 kJ/mol) and high-temperature (700–900°C).
- **Water-gas shift** ($CO + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2$) is exothermic and produces additional H₂.
- **Electrolysis stoichiometry:** 2H₂O → 2H₂ + O₂; theoretical minimum energy ≈ **32.68 kWh/kg**; commercial electrolyzers achieve **70–82% efficiency (HHV)**.
- **Reversible cell voltage** for water splitting = **1.229 V**; commercial PEM electrolyzers operate at 1.8–2.2 V due to overpotentials.
- **Faraday's constant** = 96,485 C/mol — used for all electrolytic production calculations.
- **Green hydrogen** = produced from renewable electricity + electrolysis (color codes: gray, brown, blue, green).
- **Advantages of alcohol fuels:** high octane, oxygenated, renewable, high latent heat → better knock resistance.
- **Hydrogen challenges:** low volumetric density, storage difficulty, embrittlement, leakage, safety.
- **Fuel cell vs ICE:** fuel cell efficiency 50–60% vs ICE 25–35% — fuel cell is the more efficient pathway.
- **Volume of H₂ at STP per kg CH₄ in SMR** = **5.6 m³** (a frequently asked numerical question).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrogen Production by Bio-Photolysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT631 - RENEWABLE ENERGY AND GREEN TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hydrogen Production by Bio-Photolysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — HYDROGEN PRODUCTION BY BIO-PHOTOLYSIS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Bio-photolysis** (also termed *biological photolysis* or *photobiological hydrogen production*) is a light-driven biological process in which photosynthetic microorganisms — primarily **green microalgae** (e.g., *Chlamydomonas reinhardtii*) and **cyanobacteria** (e.g., *Anabaena*, *Nostoc*) — utilize **solar photons** to dissociate water molecules into molecular hydrogen (H₂) and oxygen (O₂) through the catalytic action of specific enzymes (**hydrogenase** or **nitrogenase**).

In strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, bio-photolysis is classified under the broader category of **"Biological Routes of Hydrogen Production"** and is recognized as a **photo-biological** pathway distinct from thermochemical, electrolysis-based, and bio-fermentative methods.

The overall stoichiometric equation of the simplest (direct) form is:

$$H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{Sunlight} + \text{Microorganisms}} H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Bio-photolysis is one of the four major hydrogen production pathways taught in Module 3 — alongside *bio-fermentation*, *photo-fermentation*, and *microbial electrolysis cells (MECs)*. It is unique because it combines **photovoltaic** (solar capture) and **biological** (enzymatic catalysis) principles in a single reactor.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **leaf as a tiny solar fuel factory**. Normally, a leaf uses sunlight to combine CO₂ and water to make sugar (photosynthesis). But what if we *intercept* the leaf's machinery *before* the sugar-making step and force it to spit out **hydrogen gas** instead? That is precisely bio-photolysis.

Think of it like this:

| Natural Analogy | Bio-Photolysis Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Sunlight hitting a leaf | Photons absorbed by chlorophyll in microalgae |
| Leaf making sugar | Microalga forced to make H₂ via hydrogenase enzyme |
| CO₂ being absorbed | CO₂ is *sequestered* (and sulfur may be deprived) so the cell switches to H₂ production |

The cell's normal *photosystem* is **hacked** — by removing sulfur or removing oxygen — so that, instead of making food, the algae pumps out hydrogen bubbles that can be collected.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key constants and metrics relevant to this topic:**
> - **Solar constant (AM 1.5):** $1361 \, W/m^2$
> - **Photosynthetically Active Radiation (PAR):** 400–700 nm, ~**45%** of total solar spectrum
> - **Theoretical maximum H₂ yield from water:** **111 g H₂ per 1 kg H₂O** (based on molecular weights 2:18)
> - **Energy content of H₂ (LHV):** **120 MJ/kg**
> - **Theoretical energy conversion efficiency (solar → H₂):** ~**10–12%** (biological limit)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Principal Pathways of Bio-Photolysis

Bio-photolysis is broadly divided into two operational modes, both of which are frequently tested in KTU exams.

### Pathway A — Direct Bio-Photolysis

- A *single* organism (typically a **unicellular green alga** like *Chlamydomonas reinhardtii*) carries out both **light absorption** and **H₂ evolution** in the same cell.
- The reaction occurs in two *separable* phases:
  1. **Phase I (O₂ evolution):** Photosystem II splits water → electrons, protons, and O₂.
  2. **Phase II (H₂ evolution):** Photosystem I reduces ferredoxin, which donates electrons to the **hydrogenase** enzyme ([Fe-Fe] type) to produce H₂.
- The two phases are temporally separated because **O₂ inactivates hydrogenase**. The classical strategy is **sulfur deprivation** to slow PSII and create anaerobic conditions favorable for H₂ production.

### Pathway B — Indirect Bio-Photolysis (Cyclic / Two-Stage)

- A *consortium* of organisms or a *two-stage reactor* is used.
- **Stage 1:** A **cyanobacterium** (e.g., *Anabaena variabilis*) fixes CO₂ and stores carbohydrates during photosynthesis, evolving O₂.
- **Stage 2:** Under **dark, anaerobic conditions**, the stored carbohydrates are metabolized by the same (or a different) organism via **nitrogenase enzyme**, producing H₂ and CO₂.
- The advantage is the **spatial/temporal separation** of O₂ evolution and H₂ evolution, overcoming the oxygen sensitivity problem.

## 2.2 Core Enzymes — The "Bio-Catalysts"

| Enzyme | Metal Center | Function | O₂ Sensitivity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **[Fe-Fe] Hydrogenase** | Di-iron site | Reversibly catalyzes $2H^+ + 2e^- \leftrightarrow H_2$ | **Extremely high** (inactivated by O₂) |
| **[Ni-Fe] Hydrogenase** | Nickel-Iron site | Same reversible reaction | **Moderate** |
| **Nitrogenase** | Mo-Fe cofactor | $N_2 + 8H^+ + 8e^- + 16ATP \rightarrow 2NH_3 + H_2 + 16ADP$ | **High** (but indirect biophotolysis tolerates O₂ via heterocysts) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** Direct biophotolysis relies on **[Fe-Fe] hydrogenase**; indirect biophotolysis relies on **nitrogenase**. Examiners frequently frame questions around this distinction.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The table below contains all critical formulas required for solving numerical/analytical problems on this topic.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H_2O \rightarrow H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2$ | Overall stoichiometry of direct bio-photolysis | mol |
| 2 | $6CO_2 + 12H_2O \xrightarrow{light} C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2 + 6H_2O$ | Photosynthesis + H₂ production overall (indirect) | mol |
| 3 | $C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6H_2O \rightarrow 12H_2 + 6CO_2$ | Dark fermentation of stored carbohydrate | mol |
| 4 | $n_{H_2} = \dfrac{m_{H_2O}}{M_{H_2O}} \times 1$ | Moles of H₂ from a given mass of water | mol |
| 5 | $Y_{th} = \dfrac{2 \times M_{H_2}}{M_{H_2O}} = \dfrac{2 \times 2}{18} = 0.1111$ | Theoretical mass yield of H₂ per kg water | kg H₂/kg H₂O |
| 6 | $\eta_{ST} = \dfrac{E_{H_2}}{E_{Solar}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{m_{H_2} \cdot LHV}{I \cdot A \cdot t} \times 100\%$ | Solar-to-Hydrogen (STH) efficiency | % |
| 7 | $\eta_{ST,max} \approx 10\text{--}12\%$ | Maximum theoretical biological STH | % |
| 8 | $\Delta G^\circ_{H_2O \rightarrow H_2} = +237.2 \, kJ/mol$ | Gibbs free energy of water splitting at 25°C | kJ/mol |
| 9 | $E^\circ_{cell} = -\dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{nF} = -\dfrac{237{,}200}{2 \times 96{,}485}$ | Standard thermodynamic cell voltage | V |
| 10 | $r_{H_2} = Y_{H_2/S} \cdot r_S$ | Volumetric H₂ production rate (yield × substrate uptake) | mol H₂/L·h |
| 11 | $P_{H_2} = r_{H_2} \cdot V \cdot R \cdot T$ | Power-equivalent H₂ production rate (ideal gas) | W |
| 12 | $Q_{par} = I \cdot f_{PAR}$ | Photosynthetically active radiation flux | W/m² |
| 13 | $A_{req} = \dfrac{m_{H_2} \cdot LHV}{\eta_{ST} \cdot I \cdot t}$ | Reactor/collector area required | m² |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** In $\Delta G^\circ$ or cell-voltage questions, ALWAYS use $n = 2$ (since water splitting transfers 2 electrons per H₂). Use $F = 96{,}485 \, C/mol$ (Faraday's constant). Use $\Delta G^\circ = +237.2 \, kJ/mol$ — *not* the combustion value ($-237.2$). Sign matters!

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Carbon-Negative Hydrogen:** Since the feedstock is water and the energy source is sunlight, bio-photolysis produces **green hydrogen** with no fossil-fuel input and zero CO₂ emissions.
2. **Decentralized Production:** Photobioreactors can be built as modular units, enabling rural and off-grid H₂ generation.
3. **Wastewater Co-Treatment:** Cyanobacteria used in indirect biophotolysis simultaneously treat wastewater (nutrient removal) while producing H₂ — a **dual benefit** system.
4. **Integration with Solar Biorefinery:** Bio-photolysis fits into the broader **bio-economy** concept where algae biomass is valorized into multiple products (H₂, lipids, pigments, fertilizers).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Theoretical Stoichiometric H₂ Yield from Water

**Objective:** Find the maximum theoretical mass of H₂ that can be liberated from a given mass of water.

**Given:** 1 kg of liquid water available for photolysis.

**Step 1 — Convert mass of water to moles.**

Using the molar mass of water $M_{H_2O} = 18 \, g/mol$:

$$n_{H_2O} = \dfrac{m_{H_2O}}{M_{H_2O}} = \dfrac{1000 \, g}{18 \, g/mol} = 55.56 \, mol$$

**Step 2 — Apply the stoichiometric ratio from the photo-splitting reaction.**

From $H_2O \rightarrow H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2$, the mole ratio is $1 \, mol \, H_2O : 1 \, mol \, H_2$.

Therefore:

$$n_{H_2} = n_{H_2O} = 55.56 \, mol$$

**Step 3 — Convert moles of H₂ to mass.**

Using the molar mass of hydrogen gas $M_{H_2} = 2 \, g/mol$:

$$m_{H_2} = n_{H_2} \times M_{H_2} = 55.56 \, mol \times 2 \, g/mol = 111.11 \, g$$

**Conclusion:**

$$Y_{th} = \dfrac{111.11 \, g}{1000 \, g} = 0.1111 \, kg \, H_2/kg \, H_2O = \mathbf{11.11\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This means **theoretically, only ~11% of the input mass of water can be converted to hydrogen gas by mass** — because most of the mass goes into the oxygen byproduct (8/9 by mass) and into the energy content stored as chemical bond energy.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Solar-to-Hydrogen (STH) Conversion Efficiency

**Objective:** Derive the STH efficiency formula and compute a numerical example.

**Step 1 — Define the energy content of H₂ produced.**

The lower heating value (LHV) of H₂ is $LHV = 120 \, MJ/kg$. For a mass $m_{H_2}$ produced over a time $t$:

$$E_{H_2} = m_{H_2} \times LHV = m_{H_2} \times 120 \times 10^6 \, J/kg$$

**Step 2 — Define the incident solar energy on a reactor of area $A$.**

If the average solar irradiance is $I$ (in W/m²) over area $A$ (in m²) for time $t$ (in seconds):

$$E_{Solar} = I \cdot A \cdot t$$

**Step 3 — Form the efficiency ratio.**

$$\eta_{ST} = \dfrac{E_{H_2}}{E_{Solar}} = \dfrac{m_{H_2} \cdot LHV}{I \cdot A \cdot t}$$

**Numerical example:** Suppose a $1 \, m^2$ photobioreactor produces $5 \, g$ of H₂ in 8 hours under $I = 500 \, W/m^2$ average irradiance.

Substituting:

$$\eta_{ST} = \dfrac{0.005 \times 120 \times 10^6}{500 \times 1 \times (8 \times 3600)}$$

$$\eta_{ST} = \dfrac{600{,}000 \, J}{14{,}400{,}000 \, J}$$

$$\eta_{ST} = 0.04167 = \mathbf{4.17\%}$$

This is well below the theoretical maximum (~10–12%) but consistent with reported experimental values (typically 1–5%).

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Minimum Thermodynamic Voltage for Water Splitting

**Objective:** Compute the minimum voltage required (analogous to the electrolysis minimum voltage) to confirm why bio-photolysis is theoretically feasible from sunlight alone.

**Step 1 — Recall the relation between Gibbs free energy and cell voltage.**

For an electrochemical reaction:

$$\Delta G^\circ = -n F E^\circ_{cell}$$

Rearranging:

$$E^\circ_{cell} = -\dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{nF}$$

**Step 2 — Insert numerical values.**

For water splitting: $\Delta G^\circ = +237{,}200 \, J/mol$, $n = 2$, $F = 96{,}485 \, C/mol$.

$$E^\circ_{cell} = -\dfrac{+237{,}200}{2 \times 96{,}485} = -\dfrac{237{,}200}{192{,}970}$$

$$E^\circ_{cell} = -1.229 \, V$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the sign.**

The negative sign indicates that the **non-spontaneous** (water-splitting) direction requires a *minimum* of **1.229 V** of thermodynamic driving energy. Photobiological systems must therefore deliver at least this much photovoltage via their photosystems — which they do in cascade (PSII + PSI ≈ 1.8 V combined).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Bio-Photolysis Modes

| Parameter | Direct Bio-Photolysis | Indirect Bio-Photolysis |
|---|---|---|
| Organism(s) | Green algae (e.g., *Chlamydomonas*) | Cyanobacteria (e.g., *Anabaena*) |
| Key Enzyme | [Fe-Fe] Hydrogenase | Nitrogenase |
| Stages | Single organism, two phases | Two stages (often spatially separated) |
| Reactor Type | Single photobioreactor | Coupled photo + dark bioreactors |
