| O₂ Sensitivity Problem | **Severe** (requires S-deprivation) | **Mitigated** by spatial/temporal separation |
| Light Conversion Efficiency | ~1–3% experimental | ~1–5% experimental |
| Theoretical STH Limit | ~10% | ~10–12% |
| ATP Requirement | Low | **High** (16 ATP per H₂ from nitrogenase) |
| H₂ Production Rate | Low (transient) | Higher (sustained via heterocysts) |

---

## 3.5 Derivation IV — Energy Balance for Indirect Bio-Photolysis

**Objective:** Verify energy balance for the two-stage indirect process.

**Step 1 — Write the overall photosynthesis reaction (Stage 1).**

$$6CO_2 + 12H_2O \xrightarrow{light} C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2 + 6H_2O$$

Simplifying (6 H₂O cancel on both sides):

$$6CO_2 + 6H_2O \rightarrow C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2$$

**Step 2 — Write the dark carbohydrate fermentation (Stage 2).**

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6H_2O \rightarrow 12H_2 + 6CO_2$$

**Step 3 — Add the two reactions and cancel intermediates.**

Adding the equations directly:

$$\cancel{6CO_2} + \cancel{6H_2O} + 6H_2O \rightarrow \cancel{C_6H_{12}O_6} + \cancel{6O_2} + 12H_2 + \cancel{6CO_2}$$

Simplifying (6 CO₂ cancel, 1 glucose cancels, 6 H₂O cancel leaving only those from stage 2):

$$\boxed{6H_2O \xrightarrow{net} 12H_2 + 6O_2}$$

Dividing by 6:

$$H_2O \rightarrow H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2$$

> [!NOTE]
> The *net* stoichiometry is identical to direct biophotolysis — confirming the **thermodynamic consistency** of the indirect pathway. The intermediate glucose merely serves as a temporary energy/electron carrier.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Process Flow — Direct Bio-Photolysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sunlight Photons 400-700 nm] --> B[Photobioreactor PBR]
    W[Liquid Water Feed] --> B
    B --> C[PSII Water Splitting Site]
    C --> D[O2 Evolution Phase 1]
    D --> E[O2 Vent Outlet]
    C --> F[Electron Transport Chain]
    F --> G[PSI Ferredoxin Reduction]
    G --> H[FeFe Hydrogenase Enzyme]
    H --> I[H2 Gas Bubbles]
    S[Sulfur Deprivation Trigger] --> B
    I --> J[Gas Collection Header]
    J --> K[Purification and Storage]
    E --> L[Atmosphere]
```

### Explanation of the Flowchart
- **Sunlight photons** enter the photobioreactor (PBR).
- **Water molecules** are fed into the PBR.
- Photosystem II (PSII) splits water → O₂ is vented out (Phase 1).
- Electrons flow through the electron transport chain to Photosystem I (PSI).
- PSI reduces ferredoxin, which then donates electrons to the **[Fe-Fe] hydrogenase**.
- Hydrogenase evolves **H₂ gas bubbles**.
- A **sulfur-deprivation trigger** is applied to suppress PSII and create anaerobic conditions.
- H₂ is collected, purified, and stored.

## 4.2 Block-Level Process Flow — Indirect Bio-Photolysis (Two-Stage)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STAGE1[STAGE 1 PHOTOSYNTHESIS AEROBIC]
        A1[Sunlight] --> B1[Cyanobacterial Culture]
        W1[Water and CO2] --> B1
        B1 --> C1[O2 Released]
        B1 --> D1[Carbohydrate Accumulated Glycogen]
    end
    subgraph STAGE2[STAGE 2 DARK FERMENTATION ANAEROBIC]
        D1 --> E2[Dark Bioreactor Anoxic]
        E2 --> F2[Nitrogenase Enzyme Activated]
        F2 --> G2[H2 Evolution]
        F2 --> H2[CO2 Released]
    end
    G2 --> Z[Gas Separation and Purification]
    H2 --> Z
    Z --> OUT[Pure H2 to Storage]
```

### Explanation of the Flowchart
- **Stage 1 (Aerobic):** Cyanobacteria in light fix CO₂ and produce carbohydrates (glycogen) while evolving O₂.
- **Stage 2 (Anaerobic):** The biomass is transferred to a dark, oxygen-free bioreactor. The nitrogenase enzyme metabolizes the stored carbohydrates to produce H₂ and CO₂.
- **Gas separation** yields pure H₂ for storage or downstream use.

## 4.3 Schematic of a Tubular Photobioreactor (PBR)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PBR[Tubular Photobioreactor System]
        T1[Transparent Tubular Glass Tubes] --> T2
        T2[T2 Tube Array Laid Horizontally] --> T3
        T3[Culture Recirculation Pump] --> T4
        T4[Gas Liquid Separator] --> T5
        T5[Harvested H2 to Storage Tank] --> T6
        T6[Culture Returned to Tubes]
    end
    SUN[Sunlight Strikes Tube Surface] -.-> T1
```

### Explanation
- Transparent glass tubes maximize solar capture.
- Algal/cyanobacterial culture is pumped through the tubes.
- H₂ bubbles rise to a **gas-liquid separator**.
- Separated H₂ is sent to storage; liquid culture is recirculated.

## 4.4 Comparative Architecture — Direct vs. Indirect

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DR[Direct Biophotolysis]
        DR1[Single Organism Algae] --> DR2[FeFe Hydrogenase]
        DR2 --> DR3[H2 Plus O2 Mixed]
    end
    subgraph IR[Indirect Biophotolysis]
        IR1[Stage 1 Cyanobacteria Light] --> IR2[Stored Carbohydrate]
        IR2 --> IR3[Stage 2 Dark Anaerobic]
        IR3 --> IR4[Nitrogenase]
        IR4 --> IR5[H2 Separated from O2]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The key architectural distinction is that **direct biophotolysis** has O₂ and H₂ evolving in the *same* reactor (separation problem), while **indirect biophotolysis** separates them *spatially* across two stages — making gas separation much easier at industrial scale.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper KTU 2024]`
**Define bio-photolysis. Name the two major enzymes involved in biological hydrogen production.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Bio-photolysis is a photobiological process in which photosynthetic microorganisms (green algae or cyanobacteria) utilize solar energy to dissociate water into molecular hydrogen (H₂) and oxygen (O₂) through the catalytic action of specific enzymes.

The two major enzymes are:
1. **[Fe-Fe] Hydrogenase** — used in direct biophotolysis; reversibly catalyzes $2H^+ + 2e^- \leftrightarrow H_2$.
2. **Nitrogenase** — used in indirect biophotolysis; primarily fixes N₂ but also evolves H₂ as a byproduct, requiring 16 ATP per H₂ evolved.

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024, Sessional Model]`
**Differentiate between direct and indirect bio-photolysis in three points.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| S.No. | Direct Bio-Photolysis | Indirect Bio-Photolysis |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Single organism (green alga) carries out both light absorption and H₂ evolution. | Two stages: photosynthesis by cyanobacteria followed by dark fermentation. |
| 2 | Enzyme used is [Fe-Fe] hydrogenase. | Enzyme used is nitrogenase. |
| 3 | Major challenge is O₂ sensitivity of hydrogenase; overcome by sulfur deprivation. | O₂ and H₂ phases are separated temporally/spatially, avoiding inhibition. |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

### Question B-A. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**(a)** Explain the mechanism of **direct bio-photolysis** with a neat schematic and the role of **[Fe-Fe] hydrogenase**. Discuss why **sulfur deprivation** is necessary. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A photobioreactor of area **2 m²** receives an average solar irradiance of **600 W/m²** for **10 hours** per day. It produces **12 g of H₂** per day. Calculate: **(i)** the **solar-to-hydrogen (STH) efficiency**, and **(ii)** the **collector area required** to produce **1 kg of H₂ per day** under the same irradiance. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Mechanism of Direct Bio-Photolysis (7 marks)**

Direct bio-photolysis occurs in unicellular green algae such as *Chlamydomonas reinhardtii*. The mechanism has two phases:

**Phase 1 — Light reactions & O₂ evolution:**
- Photons are absorbed by chlorophyll in **Photosystem II (PSII)**.
- Water is oxidized: $2H_2O \rightarrow O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^-$
- Electrons travel through the electron transport chain (plastoquinone → cytochrome b₆f → plastocyanin).

**Phase 2 — H₂ evolution:**
- **Photosystem I (PSI)** absorbs additional photons and reduces ferredoxin.
- Reduced ferredoxin donates electrons to **[Fe-Fe] hydrogenase**.
- The enzyme catalyzes: $2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2$
- The overall reaction: $2H_2O \xrightarrow{light} 2H_2 + O_2$

**Role of Sulfur Deprivation:**

The [Fe-Fe] hydrogenase is **irreversibly inactivated** by O₂. To enable H₂ evolution, PSII activity must be reduced so that O₂ production drops below respiration rate, creating a **net anaerobic environment**. Sulfur deprivation:
- Inhibits the synthesis of new D1 protein (a PSII component).
- Reduces PSII repair → slows O₂ production.
- Allows the algae to switch to a **fermentative metabolism**, sustaining H₂ production for several days.

> (Marks distribution: 2 marks mechanism, 2 marks enzyme, 2 marks sulfur role, 1 mark schematic)

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

**Given:** $A = 2 \, m^2$, $I = 600 \, W/m^2$, $t = 10 \, h = 36{,}000 \, s$, $m_{H_2} = 12 \, g = 0.012 \, kg$.

**(i) STH Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{ST} = \dfrac{m_{H_2} \times LHV}{I \times A \times t}$$

$$\eta_{ST} = \dfrac{0.012 \times 120 \times 10^6}{600 \times 2 \times 36{,}000}$$

$$\eta_{ST} = \dfrac{1{,}440{,}000 \, J}{43{,}200{,}000 \, J}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{ST} = 0.0333 = 3.33\%}$$

**(ii) Required Collector Area for 1 kg H₂/day:**

$$A_{req} = \dfrac{m_{H_2} \times LHV}{\eta_{ST} \times I \times t}$$

$$A_{req} = \dfrac{1.0 \times 120 \times 10^6}{0.0333 \times 600 \times 36{,}000}$$

$$A_{req} = \dfrac{120{,}000{,}000}{719{,}280}$$

$$\boxed{A_{req} \approx 166.85 \, m^2}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):**
> - Many students forget to convert hours to **seconds** (multiply by 3600).
> - Some students mistakenly use **HHV (142 MJ/kg)** instead of LHV (120 MJ/kg). KTU board typically accepts LHV, but always state which you used.
> - Do NOT round intermediate values. Carry at least 4 significant figures until the final answer.

---

### Question B-B. `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024, Module 3 Alternate]`

**(a)** Describe the **indirect bio-photolysis** process with the help of a **two-stage reactor schematic**. Explain the role of **nitrogenase** and the advantage of using **heterocystous cyanobacteria**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **minimum thermodynamic voltage** required for water splitting. Given that the Gibbs free energy is $\Delta G^\circ = +237.2 \, kJ/mol$, $n = 2$, $F = 96{,}485 \, C/mol$, determine how many **kJ of solar energy per mole** is required and what **percentage of incident solar PAR energy** this represents if 1 mole of water absorbs photons totaling **1500 kJ** under PAR. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Indirect Bio-Photolysis (7 marks)**

**Stage 1 — Aerobic Photosynthesis:**
- Cyanobacteria (e.g., *Anabaena variabilis*) in light absorb CO₂ and water.
- Photosynthesis stores energy as **carbohydrates** (glycogen) inside the cell.
- O₂ is released to the atmosphere via the **vegetative cells**.

**Stage 2 — Anaerobic Dark Fermentation:**
- Cells are transferred to a sealed, dark, O₂-free bioreactor.
- Stored carbohydrates are catabolized:
$$C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6H_2O \rightarrow 12H_2 + 6CO_2$$
- **Nitrogenase enzyme** catalyzes H₂ evolution: $2H^+ + 2e^- + 4ATP \rightarrow H_2$.

**Role of Heterocystous Cyanobacteria:**

*Heterocysts* are specialized, thick-walled cells in certain cyanobacteria that:
- Lack Photosystem II (so no O₂ is produced inside).
- Maintain an **anaerobic microenvironment** even in light.
- Provide a safe niche for **nitrogenase** (which is O₂-sensitive).
- Thus, cyanobacteria like *Anabaena* can produce H₂ *continuously* in light — a key advantage over green algae.

**Advantage Summary:**
- Spatially separates O₂ and H₂ evolution.
- Enables **continuous** (not transient) H₂ production.
- Avoids the need for sulfur deprivation.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

**Given:** $\Delta G^\circ = +237{,}200 \, J/mol$, $n = 2$, $F = 96{,}485 \, C/mol$, $E_{solar,PAR} = 1500 \, kJ/mol$.

**(i) Minimum thermodynamic voltage:**

$$E^\circ_{cell} = -\dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{nF} = -\dfrac{237{,}200}{2 \times 96{,}485} = -\dfrac{237{,}200}{192{,}970}$$

$$\boxed{E^\circ_{cell} = -1.229 \, V}$$

The magnitude **1.229 V** is the minimum reversible voltage required for water splitting.

**(ii) Percentage of PAR energy required:**

$$\%_{PAR} = \dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{E_{solar,PAR}} \times 100 = \dfrac{237.2}{1500} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\%_{PAR} = 15.81\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):**
> - Students often forget the **negative sign** in the Gibbs-energy-to-voltage conversion. The thermodynamic voltage magnitude is **1.229 V**, but the cell EMF is reported as -1.229 V (since it is non-spontaneous).
> - Always convert kJ → J when using Faraday's constant in C/mol (units must be consistent).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Hydrogen Production by Bio-Photolysis**

- **Definition:** Light-driven H₂ production using photosynthetic microorganisms (algae/cyanobacteria).
- **Two pathways:** *Direct* (single organism, [Fe-Fe] hydrogenase) and *Indirect* (two-stage, nitrogenase).
- **Overall reaction:** $H_2O \rightarrow H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2$ (identical for both pathways at net level).
- **Theoretical H₂ yield from water:** **0.111 kg H₂ per kg H₂O** (or **111 g/kg**).
- **LHV of H₂:** **120 MJ/kg** (use this, not HHV, unless explicitly asked).
- **Solar-to-Hydrogen (STH) efficiency formula:** $\eta_{ST} = \dfrac{m_{H_2} \times LHV}{I \times A \times t}$.
- **Maximum biological STH:** ~**10–12%** theoretical; ~**1–5%** experimental.
- **Thermodynamic minimum voltage:** **1.229 V** (from $\Delta G^\circ = +237.2 \, kJ/mol$).
- **Key enzymes:** [Fe-Fe] Hydrogenase (direct, O₂-sensitive) and Nitrogenase (indirect, ATP-intensive).
- **Sulfur deprivation** is the classical strategy to enable direct biophotolysis by suppressing PSII.
- **Heterocysts** in cyanobacteria provide a natural anaerobic niche for nitrogenase in light.
- **Advantages:** Carbon-negative, solar-powered, low operating cost, integrates with wastewater treatment.
- **Disadvantages:** Low rates, large land area, O₂ sensitivity, light-conversion efficiency limited.
- **Constants to memorize:** $M_{H_2} = 2$, $M_{H_2O} = 18$, $F = 96{,}485 \, C/mol$, $\Delta G^\circ = +237.2 \, kJ/mol$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Board Examiner Warning:**
> - ALWAYS show the unit conversions explicitly in numerical answers.
> - ALWAYS state assumptions (e.g., "assuming LHV = 120 MJ/kg" or "assuming AM 1.5 irradiance").
> - Diagrams fetch **at least 1–2 marks** — never skip them in long answers.
> - Use the keyword **"biophotolysis"** correctly in definitions; do NOT confuse with "biophotonics" (a different field entirely).
> - KTU examiners award marks for *clarity of concept* and *correct chemical equations* — partial marks are generous if the equations are right even if the explanation is incomplete.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biogas Fuel Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT631 - RENEWABLE ENERGY AND GREEN TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Biogas Fuel Cells # ⚡ Biogas Fuel Cells — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Biogas Fuel Cell** is an integrated electrochemical energy conversion system that directly converts the chemical energy stored in **biogas** (a renewable gaseous fuel produced by anaerobic digestion of organic biomass) into electrical energy and heat using the **galvanic principles of a fuel cell**, bypassing the thermodynamic limitations of the Carnot cycle that constrain conventional heat engines. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A Biogas Fuel Cell is a hybrid renewable energy system where biogas (predominantly CH₄ and CO₂) is fed — either directly (in high-temperature cells) or after external reforming (in low-temperature cells) — to a fuel cell stack, producing DC electricity, water, and CO₂ with electrical efficiencies typically ranging between **40% and 60%**. The KTU 2024 scheme specifies the following typical composition of raw biogas: | Component | Chemical Formula | Typical Volume % | |---|---|---| | Methane | CH₄ | **50 – 70** | | Carbon Dioxide | CO₂ | **30 – 40** | | Hydrogen Sulfide | H₂S | 0 – 1000 ppm | | Water Vapor | H₂O | Saturated | | Nitrogen | N₂ | 0 – 5 | | Ammonia | NH₃ | < 100 ppm | | Siloxanes / Halides | Trace | < 50 mg/Nm³ | ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Bread Factory vs. The Wheat Mill" > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition):** > Imagine you have wheat (raw biomass) and want bread (electricity). > - **Traditional method (Internal Combustion Engine):** You burn the bread to heat a boiler, the boiler boils water, steam turns a turbine, the turbine spins a magnet. *You lose heat at every step (Carnot limitation).* > - **Biogas Fuel Cell method:** Anaerobic bacteria act like bakers turning wheat into bread (biogas). The fuel cell then acts like a "molecular wire" — it doesn't burn the bread, it lets the chemical bonds release their electrons *directly* into a circuit. The result is a much higher conversion efficiency with only water and CO₂ as exhaust. ## 1.3 Why Biogas Fuel Cells are a KTU High-Yield Topic - **Decentralized Power Generation:** Perfect for rural Kerala (coconut, rice straw, dairy waste). - **Dual Environmental Benefit:** Converts *waste* into *clean power*. - **High Efficiency:** Unlike the 30–35% of diesel gensets, fuel cells reach **45–60% electrical efficiency** and up to **85% total (CHP)** efficiency. - **Scalable:** From a few kW (home-scale digester + PEMFC) to multi-MW (industrial SOFC). ## 1.4 Standard Metrics to Memorize > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Benchmark Constants (Bold for memory):** > - Faraday's Constant: **F = 96,485 C mol⁻¹** > - Universal Gas Constant: **R = 8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹** > - Standard Gibbs Free Energy of H₂ Oxidation: **ΔG° = −237.2 kJ mol⁻¹** > - Standard Enthalpy of H₂ Oxidation: **ΔH° = −285.8 kJ mol⁻¹** > - Theoretical H₂-O₂ Cell Voltage: **E° = 1.229 V** > - Theoretical CH₄-O₂ Cell Voltage: **E° = 1.06 V** # 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic A biogas fuel cell system is **not a single device**; it is an engineered **chain of four sub-systems**: ### Sub-system 1: Biogas Production (Upstream) - Organic feedstock (cattle dung, kitchen waste, poultry litter, municipal solid waste) is fed into an **anaerobic digester**. - In the absence of oxygen, methanogenic bacteria (e.g., *Methanobacterium*, *Methanococcus*) break down complex carbohydrates via four stages: **Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis**. - Output: Raw biogas at ~35 °C, saturated with water and contaminated with H₂S, NH₃, siloxanes. ### Sub-system 2: Biogas Cleanup & Conditioning (Critical) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why cleanup is non-negotiable:** H₂S concentrations as low as **1 ppm** permanently poison the nickel catalyst in SOFC anodes and platinum catalyst in PAFC/PEMFC anodes. The KTU syllabus emphasizes that **gas cleanup is the single most important determinant of fuel cell lifetime**. The cleanup train typically includes: 1. **H₂S Removal** — Iron sponge, activated carbon, or biological desulfurization (target: < 1 ppm). 2. **Water Removal** — Condenser + silica gel drier (target: dew point < −40 °C). 3. **CO₂ Separation (Optional)** — Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA), water scrubbing, or membrane separation. Removing CO₂ raises the calorific value and reduces fuel dilution. 4. **Siloxane & Halide Removal** — Activated carbon polishing filters. ### Sub-system 3: Fuel Cell Stack (Core) Depending on the operating temperature and electrolyte, biogas is consumed in one of the following: | Fuel Cell Type | Electrolyte | Operating Temperature | Biogas Feed Mode | Electrical Efficiency | |---|---|---|---|---| | **PEMFC** | Polymer Membrane | 60 – 100 °C | External reformer required | 40 – 50% | | **PAFC** | Liquid H₃PO₄ | 150 – 200 °C | External reformer required | 40 – 45% | | **MCFC** | Molten (Li/K)₂CO₃ | 600 – 700 °C | Direct internal reforming | 45 – 55% | | **SOFC** | Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia (YSZ) | 600 – 1000 °C | Direct internal reforming | 50 – 60% | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Preferred Answer Pattern:** For exam answers, the examiner expects students to identify **SOFC and MCFC** as the two types ideally suited for direct biogas utilization because their high operating temperature enables **internal steam reforming of methane**. ### Sub-system 4: Power Conditioning (Downstream) - The fuel cell stack produces **DC electricity** at a low voltage (~0.6–0.8 V per cell). - A **DC-DC boost converter** steps up the voltage. - An **inverter** converts DC to AC for grid synchronization or local load. ## 2.2 Electrochemical Reactions — The "Why & How" ### For a Solid Oxide Fuel Cell (SOFC) running on CH₄ (most exam-relevant): **Step 1 — Internal Steam Reforming (endothermic, on Ni-YSZ anode):** $$CH_4 + H_2O \rightarrow CO + 3H_2$$ **Step 2 — Water-Gas Shift Reaction (mildly exothermic):** $$CO + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2$$ **Step 3 — Electrochemical Oxidation (anode, releases electrons):** $$H_2 + O^{2-} \rightarrow H_2O + 2e^-$$ **Step 4 — Oxygen Reduction (cathode, consumes electrons):** $$\frac{1}{2}O_2 + 2e^- \rightarrow O^{2-}$$ **Net Cell Reaction:** $$CH_4 + 2O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O \quad \Delta H^\circ = -890.4 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$ The electrons released in Step 3 travel through the **external circuit** (producing usable current) and reach the cathode, where they complete the circuit in Step 4. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Use `\vert` (not pipe `|`) when writing absolute values in tables to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.** | # | Formula | Description | Units / Typical Value | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E^\circ_{cell} = \dfrac{-\Delta G^\circ}{nF}$ | Standard reversible cell voltage (Gibbs–Helmholtz relation) | **E°(H₂) = 1.229 V ; E°(CH₄) = 1.06 V** | | 2 | $E = E^\circ + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \dfrac{P_{H_2} \cdot \sqrt{P_{O_2}}}{P_{H_2O}}$ | Nernst Equation (general form for H₂-O₂ cell) | E in volts | | 3 | $\eta_{thermo} = \dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{\Delta H^\circ}$ | Thermodynamic (ideal) efficiency of fuel cell | **0.83 for H₂ ; 0.99 for CH₄** | | 4 | $\eta_{electrical} = \dfrac{V_{cell}}{E_{thermo}}$ | Practical electrical efficiency (voltage ratio) | 0.50 – 0.65 typical | | 5 | $\eta_{CHP} = \dfrac{W_{elec} + Q_{thermal}}{Q_{in}}$ | Combined Heat & Power efficiency | Up to **85%** | | 6 | $I = n \cdot F \cdot \dot{n}_{fuel}$ | Faraday's Law (current from fuel flow) | I in Amperes, $\dot{n}$ in mol/s | | 7 | $P_{stack} = n_{cells} \cdot V_{cell} \cdot I$ | Stack power output | W | | 8 | $V_{stack} = n_{cells} \cdot V_{cell}$ | Stack voltage | V | | 9 | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_c}{T_h}$ | Carnot efficiency (for comparison only) | n/a for fuel cell | | 10 | $\dot{V}_{biogas,min} = \dfrac{P_{elec}}{\eta \cdot LHV_{biogas}}$ | Minimum biogas flow rate required | Nm³/s | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Combined Heat, Power & Cooling (C-HPC) for Hospitals/Hotels:** SOFC + absorption chiller for tri-generation. - **Industrial Waste-to-Power:** Distilleries, dairy plants, and paper mills in Kerala can deploy MCFC units. - **Microgrid Backbone:** Stable DC source complements solar PV intermittency. - **Carbon-Negative Pathway:** When biogas comes from manure, CO₂ released equals what the animal consumed, giving a near-zero carbon footprint. # 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Standard Cell Voltage from Gibbs Free Energy **Problem Setup:** Show that the open-circuit voltage of an ideal fuel cell is given by $E^\circ = -\Delta G^\circ / (nF)$ for the reaction H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O(ℓ). **Given:** - $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C mol}^{-1}$ - $\Delta G^\circ_{H_2O(l)} = -237.2 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ - $n = 2$ (two electrons transferred per H₂) **Step 1 — Maximum Work Theorem:** The maximum non-PV (pressure-volume) electrical work obtainable from a constant-T, constant-P electrochemical cell equals the decrease in Gibbs free energy: $$W_{max} = -\Delta G^\circ$$ **Step 2 — Charge Transferred per Mole of Fuel:** Each mole of H₂ releases $n = 2$ electrons. Total charge per mole: $$Q = nF = 2 \times 96{,}485 = 192{,}970 \text{ C mol}^{-1}$$ **Step 3 — Voltage from Work and Charge:** By definition, electrical work = charge × potential difference: $$W_{max} = Q \cdot E^\circ = nFE^\circ$$ **Step 4 — Equate and Solve:** $$nFE^\circ = -\Delta G^\circ$$ $$\boxed{E^\circ = \frac{-\Delta G^\circ}{nF} = \frac{237{,}200 \text{ J mol}^{-1}}{2 \times 96{,}485 \text{ C mol}^{-1}} \approx 1.229 \text{ V}}$$ This is the **famous 1.229 V theoretical potential of an ideal H₂-O₂ cell at 25 °C and 1 atm**. **Step 5 — Same Method for CH₄ Fuel Cell:** For $CH_4 + 2O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O$: - $\Delta G^\circ = -817.97 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ - $n = 8$ electrons transferred (CH₄ → CO₂ loses 8 e⁻) $$E^\circ_{CH_4} = \frac{817{,}970}{8 \times 96{,}485} \approx 1.06 \text{ V}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Practical Cell Voltage via Nernst Equation **Problem Setup:** Derive the Nernst equation for the cell reaction H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O at non-standard partial pressures. **Step 1 — General Thermodynamic Identity:** For a cell reaction with Gibbs free energy change at any condition: $$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$$ where $Q$ is the reaction quotient. **Step 2 — Substitute Energy and Voltage:** Using $\Delta G = -nFE$ and $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ$: $$-nFE = -nFE^\circ + RT \ln Q$$ **Step 3 — Solve for E:** $$E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$ For the H₂-O₂ cell, $Q = \dfrac{P_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}$: $$\boxed{E = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{P_{H_2} \cdot \sqrt{P_{O_2}}}{P_{H_2O}}}$$ **Step 4 — Numerical Example (typical SOFC operating at 800 °C = 1073 K):** | Variable | Value | |---|---| | $P_{H_2}$ | 0.7 atm | | $P_{O_2}$ | 0.21 atm | | $P_{H_2O}$ | 0.3 atm | | T | 1073 K | | n | 2 | | E° (at 800 °C, corrected) | 0.978 V | Plugging in: $$E = 0.978 + \frac{8.314 \times 1073}{2 \times 96{,}485} \ln \left[ \frac{0.7 \times \sqrt{0.21}}{0.3} \right]$$ $$E = 0.978 + (0.0462) \ln(1.069)$$ $$E = 0.978 + (0.0462)(0.0666)$$ $$\boxed{E \approx 0.981 \text{ V}}$$ This ~0.98 V open-circuit voltage then drops under load to a working voltage of **0.6 – 0.7 V per cell** due to **activation, ohmic, and concentration losses**. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Biogas Flow Rate for a Given Power Output **Problem Setup:** A 50 kW SOFC stack runs on cleaned biogas. The LHV of cleaned biogas is 18 MJ/Nm³ and the stack electrical efficiency is 55%. Calculate the volumetric biogas flow required. **Step 1 — Energy in Fuel per Second:** $$P_{in,thermal} = \frac{P_{elec}}{\eta_{el}} = \frac{50{,}000}{0.55} = 90{,}909 \text{ W} = 90.91 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 2 — Volumetric Flow:** $$\dot{V}_{biogas} = \frac{P_{in,thermal}}{LHV} = \frac{90.91 \text{ kJ/s}}{18{,}000 \text{ kJ/Nm}^3}$$ $$\boxed{\dot{V}_{biogas} \approx 5.05 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Nm}^3/\text{s} = 18.18 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h}}$$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Why SOFC Dominates for Biogas | Criterion | PEMFC | PAFC | MCFC | **SOFC** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Operating Temperature | 80 °C | 200 °C | 650 °C | **800 – 1000 °C** | | Internal CH₄ Reforming | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | **✓✓ (Best)** | | CO₂ Tolerance | None | Low | High | **High** | | H₂S Tolerance (ppm) | < 0.1 | < 10 | < 10 | **< 1 (with cleanup)** | | Precious Metal Catalyst | Pt (high cost) | Pt | Ni | **Ni (low cost)** | | Power Density (W/cm²) | 0.5 – 0.8 | 0.2 – 0.3 | 0.15 – 0.2 | **0.3 – 0.5** | | Startup Time | Seconds | Minutes | Hours | **Hours** | | Best for Biogas | Poor (needs reformer) | Fair | Good | **Excellent (CHP)** | # 🏗️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram — Biogas Fuel Cell Power Plant ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph A1[FEEDSTOCK UNIT] A11[Biomass Waste
Cattle Dung / Kitchen Waste] end subgraph A2[ANAEROBIC DIGESTER] A21[H2S Removal
Iron Sponge] A22[Water Removal
Condenser + Silica Gel] A23[CO2 Separation
PSA / Membrane] A24[Siloxane Removal
Activated Carbon Filter] end subgraph A3[FUEL CELL STACK] A31[MCFC / SOFC Stack
800 to 1000 C] A32[DC to DC Boost
Converter] A33[DC to AC Inverter] end subgraph A4[OUTPUT] A41[AC Load
3 Phase 415 V 50 Hz] A42[Heat Recovery Unit
Boiler / Absorption Chiller] end A11 --> A2 A2 --> A31 A31 --> A32 A32 --> A33 A33 --> A41 A31 --> A42 ``` ## 4.2 Internal Block Diagram — Single SOFC Cell with Biogas ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph B1[ANODE CHAMBER] B11[CH4 Feed In] B12[Steam H2O In] B13[Internal Reforming
CH4 plus H2O yields CO plus 3H2] B14[Water Gas Shift
CO plus H2O yields CO2 plus H2] B15[Electrochemical Oxidation
H2 plus O2 minus yields H2O plus 2e minus] end subgraph B2[CATHODE CHAMBER] B21[Air In O2 plus N2] B22[Oxygen Reduction
0.5 O2 plus 2e minus yields O2 minus] end subgraph B3[CELL HARDWARE] B31[Anode Ni YSZ Cermet] B32[Electrolyte YSZ Solid Oxide] B33[Cathode LSM Lanthanum Strontium Manganite] B34[External Circuit Load] end B11 --> B13 B12 --> B13 B13 --> B14 B14 --> B15 B15 --> B31 B22 --> B33 B33 --> B22 B31 --> B32 B32 --> B33 B31 --> B34 B34 --> B22 B15 --> B12 ``` ## 4.3 Polarization Curve — Voltage vs. Current Density ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph C1[POLARIZATION ZONES] C11[Zone 1 Activation Loss
V drops sharply at low current density
Sluggish electrode kinetics] C12[Zone 2 Ohmic Loss
Linear drop in middle
Electrolyte ionic resistance] C13[Zone 3 Concentration Loss
Sharp drop at high current
Mass transport limit] end subgraph C2[KEY VOLTAGE POINTS] C21[Open Circuit Voltage E_OCV = 0.98 V] C22[Nominal Operating Point V = 0.7 V at 0.5 A per cm2] C23[Limiting Current Density] end C11 --> C12 --> C13 C21 --> C11 C22 --> C12 C23 --> C13 ``` ## 4.4 Energy Flow Sankey-Style Diagram (Textual Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph D1[ENERGY INPUT] D11[Biogas Chemical Energy
100 percent basis] end subgraph D2[CONVERSION] D21[Fuel Cell Stack
55 percent to Electricity] D22[Heat Recovery
30 percent to Useful Heat] D23[Losses
15 percent to Stack Cooling and Reformer Heat] end subgraph D3[USEFUL OUTPUT] D31[Electrical Output AC
55 percent] D32[Thermal Output Hot Water or Steam
30 percent] end D11 --> D21 --> D31 D11 --> D22 --> D32 D11 --> D23 ``` # 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]** *(3 Marks)* **Define a Biogas Fuel Cell. Why is the high-temperature Solid Oxide Fuel Cell (SOFC) considered the most suitable for direct biogas utilization?** **Model Answer:** A **Biogas Fuel Cell** is an electrochemical device that converts the chemical energy of biogas (a mixture of CH₄ and CO₂ produced via anaerobic digestion) into DC electricity, water, and heat, without combustion. The typical composition is 50–70% CH₄, 30–40% CO₂, plus trace H₂S and moisture. The **Solid Oxide Fuel Cell (SOFC)** is the most suitable for direct biogas utilization because: 1. **High operating temperature (600 – 1000 °C)** enables direct **internal steam reforming** of methane on the Ni-YSZ cermet anode: $$CH_4 + H_2O \rightarrow CO + 3H_2$$ No external reformer is needed, simplifying the system and reducing capital cost. 2. The CO produced can be utilized via the water-gas shift reaction: $$CO + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2$$ 3. SOFC has **high CO₂ and H₂S tolerance** (after basic cleanup) and uses cheap nickel catalysts rather than expensive platinum. 4. Electrical efficiencies of **50 – 60%** are achievable, with up to **85% total CHP efficiency** when heat is recovered. > **[End of Answer — 3 Marks]** --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]** *(3 Marks)* **List any three impurities commonly found in raw biogas. Why must each be removed before feeding biogas into a fuel cell?** **Model Answer:** | # | Impurity | Effect on Fuel Cell | Removal Method | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Hydrogen Sulfide (H₂S)** | Even **1 ppm** permanently poisons the Ni or Pt anode catalyst, reducing cell voltage and lifetime. | Iron sponge, activated carbon, or biological desulfurization. | | 2 | **Water Vapor (H₂O)** | Dilutes the fuel, lowers partial pressure of H₂, and can flood the electrode pores. | Condenser + silica-gel drier (target: dew point < −40 °C). | | 3 | **Siloxanes & Halides** | At SOFC operating temperatures, they form silicate deposits on electrodes and corrode metal components. | Activated carbon polishing filter. | > **[End of Answer — 3 Marks]** --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### **Question Choice A (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model Paper 1]* **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Solid Oxide Fuel Cell (SOFC) power plant running on biogas**. Describe the role of each sub-system: anaerobic digester, gas cleanup train, fuel cell stack, and power conditioning unit. *(7 Marks)* **(b)** A 100 kW SOFC stack operates on cleaned biogas (LHV = 20 MJ/Nm³) at an electrical efficiency of 52%. The stack consists of 200 cells connected in series, each with an active area of 100 cm², and delivers a current density of 0.4 A/cm². Calculate: 1. The volumetric flow rate of biogas required (Nm³/h). 2. The operating voltage of each cell. 3. The total open-circuit voltage of the stack (assume E°(CH₄) = 1.06 V at standard conditions and neglect the Nernst correction). *(7 Marks)* --- ### **Model Solution for Question Choice A** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** A Biogas-SOFC power plant consists of **four sequential sub-systems**: **1. Anaerobic Digester:** Organic biomass (cattle dung, food waste, agricultural residue) is fed into a sealed, oxygen-free tank. Methanogenic bacteria convert it through **hydrolysis → acidogenesis → acetogenesis → methanogenesis**, producing raw biogas at ~35 °C. **2. Gas Cleanup Train:** - *H₂S removal:* iron-sponge / activated-carbon scrubber. - *Water removal:* condenser followed by silica-gel adsorption drier. - *CO₂ separation:* Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA) — optional but raises calorific value. - *Siloxane trap:* activated carbon polishing filter. **3. SOFC Stack:** Cleaned biogas enters the anode. At 800–1000 °C, internal steam reforming converts CH₄ to H₂ and CO, which then electrochemically react with O²⁻ ions conducted through the YSZ electrolyte. Electrons flow through the external circuit producing DC current. **4. Power Conditioning:** - DC-DC boost converter raises the low stack voltage to a usable DC bus level. - DC-AC inverter converts to 3-phase 415 V, 50 Hz AC for grid or local load. - Heat Recovery Boiler captures the high-temperature exhaust for hot water or steam, achieving **CHP efficiency up to 85%**. > *[7 Marks Awarded: 1 mark each for sub-system role, 1 mark for reactions, 1 mark for the diagram description, 1 mark for CHP highlight, and 1 mark for citing the relevant operating temperature]* --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Given Data:** - $P_{stack} = 100 \text{ kW}$ - $\eta_{el} = 52\% = 0.52$ - $LHV_{biogas} = 20 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3 = 20{,}000 \text{ kJ/Nm}^3$ - $n_{cells} = 200$ - Active area per cell, $A = 100 \text{ cm}^2$ - Current density, $j = 0.4 \text{ A/cm}^2$ **Step 1 — Thermal Energy Input Required:** $$P_{in,thermal} = \frac{P_{elec}}{\eta_{el}} = \frac{100}{0.52} = 192.31 \text{ kW} = 192.31 \text{ kJ/s}$$ **Step 2 — Volumetric Flow Rate of Biogas:** $$\dot{V}_{biogas} = \frac{P_{in,thermal}}{LHV} = \frac{192.31 \text{ kJ/s}}{20{,}000 \text{ kJ/Nm}^3}$$ $$\dot{V}_{biogas} = 9.615 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Nm}^3/\text{s}$$ $$\boxed{\dot{V}_{biogas} = 9.615 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 = 34.62 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h}}$$ **Step 3 — Current per Cell:** $$I_{cell} = j \times A = 0.4 \text{ A/cm}^2 \times 100 \text{ cm}^2 = 40 \text{ A}$$ **Step 4 — Stack Current and Voltage Relationship:** Stack power = stack voltage × stack current. Assuming the same current flows through each cell in series: $$P_{stack} = V_{stack} \cdot I_{stack} = (n_{cells} \cdot V_{cell}) \cdot I_{cell}$$ $$V_{cell} = \frac{P_{stack}}{n_{cells} \cdot I_{cell}} = \frac{100{,}000 \text{ W}}{200 \times 40 \text{ A}}$$ $$\boxed{V_{cell} = 12.5 \text{ V per cell}}$$ **Step 5 — Open-Circuit Voltage of Stack (Neglecting Nernst Correction):** $$V_{OCV,stack} = n_{cells} \cdot E^\circ_{CH_4} = 200 \times 1.06$$ $$\boxed{V_{OCV,stack} = 212 \text{ V}}$$ > *[7 Marks Awarded: 1 mark for setup, 2 marks for flow rate, 2 marks for cell voltage, 1 mark for OCV, and 1 mark for units]* --- ### **Question Choice B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE)** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Regular]* **(a)** Derive the **Nernst equation** for an H₂-O₂ fuel cell and explain the physical significance of each term. Show that the standard open-circuit voltage of an ideal H₂-O₂ cell is **1.229 V** at 25 °C. *(7 Marks)* **(b)** Compare the suitability of **PEMFC, PAFC, MCFC, and SOFC** for direct biogas utilization. Identify which is the most preferred for a Combined Heat and Power (CHP) system of 250 kW capacity installed at a dairy plant in Kerala, justifying your answer with reference to operating temperature, internal reforming capability, catalyst cost, and tolerance to biogas impurities. *(7 Marks)* --- ### **Model Solution for Question Choice B** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Step 1 — Free Energy Identity:** For any electrochemical reaction: $$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$$ **Step 2 — Substitute $\Delta G = -nFE$:** $$-nFE = -nFE^\circ + RT \ln Q$$ **Step 3 — Solve for E:** $$\boxed{E = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{P_{H_2} \cdot \sqrt{P_{O_2}}}{P_{H_2O}}}$$ **Physical Significance of Each Term:** - **E°:** Standard cell voltage at 1 atm, 25 °C. - **RT/nF:** Temperature-dependent Nernst term (thermal voltage). - **Numerator logarithm:** Drives voltage up when H₂ and O₂ partial pressures increase. - **Denominator P_H₂O:** Voltage drops with more product water. **Step 4 — Standard Voltage Calculation:** For $H_2 + \frac{1}{2} O_2 \rightarrow H_2O_{(l)}$: - $\Delta G^\circ = -237.2 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ - $n = 2$, $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C mol}^{-1}$ $$E^\circ = \frac{-\Delta G^\circ}{nF} = \frac{237{,}200}{2 \times 96{,}485}$$ $$\boxed{E^\circ = 1.229 \text{ V}}$$ > *[7 Marks Awarded: 2 marks for derivation, 2 marks for physical significance, 2 marks for numerical value, 1 mark for unit consistency]* --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Comparative Analysis:** | Criterion | PEMFC | PAFC | MCFC | **SOFC** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Operating Temp | 80 °C | 200 °C | 650 °C | **800 – 1000 °C** | | Internal CH₄ Reforming | ✗ (External reformer mandatory) | ✗ | ✓ | **✓✓ (Highly efficient)** | | CO₂ Tolerance | Very Low | Low | High | **Very High** | | H₂S Tolerance | < 0.1 ppm | < 10 ppm | < 10 ppm | **< 1 ppm (with cleanup)** | | Catalyst | Pt (₹₹₹) | Pt (₹₹₹) | Ni (₹) | **Ni (₹)** | | CHP Quality Heat | Low (80 °C) | Medium (200 °C) | Good (650 °C) | **Excellent (800 °C → high-grade steam)** | | Electrical Efficiency | 40 – 50% | 40 – 45% | 45 – 55% | **50 – 60%** | | Startup Time | Seconds | Minutes | Hours | **Hours** | | Cost (₹/kW) | High | Medium-High | Medium | **Medium (decreasing)** | **Recommendation for 250 kW Dairy Plant CHP in Kerala:** The **Solid Oxide Fuel Cell (SOFC)** is the most preferred choice for the following reasons: 1. **Direct Internal Reforming:** Eliminates the need for a costly external reformer, reducing system footprint and capital expenditure. 2. **High-Grade Waste Heat:** At 800 °C, exhaust heat can generate **high-pressure steam for milk pasteurization and CIP (clean-in-place) processes** at the dairy — directly replacing the diesel-fired boiler. 3. **Ni Catalyst:** Avoids platinum, which is critical for cost-sensitive rural industrial deployment. 4. **Robust to CO₂:** Biogas from Kerala's dairy farms typically contains 35–40% CO₂; SOFC tolerates this internally. 5. **Continuous Operation:** Dairy plants operate 24/7, suiting SOFC's slow-startup characteristic. 6. **Net CHP Efficiency:** A SOFC + heat recovery boiler can achieve **80 – 85% total efficiency**, drastically reducing electricity and process-heat costs. > *[7 Marks Awarded: 1 mark for table, 1 mark each for the 4 justification criteria, 1 mark for Kerala context, 1 mark for CHP efficiency]* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > 1. **Forgetting the Nernst Sign Convention:** Always write $E = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$, **not** minus. The minus sign is a classic 1-mark trap. > 2. **Confusing Electrical vs. Thermodynamic Efficiency:** $\eta_{thermo} = \Delta G^\circ/\Delta H^\circ$ is a theoretical limit. Real efficiency is the *voltage ratio* $V_{cell}/E_{thermo}$. Examiners deduct marks for using the wrong formula. > 3. **Ignoring Gas Cleanup:** Answers that describe a fuel cell but **omit H₂S and water removal** typically lose 2 full marks — the cleanup train is mandatory. > 4. **Mixing Up PEMFC and SOFC temperatures:** PEMFC is **60–100 °C**; SOFC is **600–1000 °C**. Confusing them is a guaranteed 1-mark penalty. > 5. **Unit Mismatch in Biogas Flow:** Biogas flow must always be reported in **Nm³/h** (normal cubic meters per hour), not m³/h. Failure to write "Nm" costs 1 mark. > 6. **Forgetting Heat Recovery:** Always mention **CHP** when discussing SOFC/MCFC. Examiners award bonus credit for linking the high operating temperature to a downstream thermal load. --- ## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Biogas Fuel Cell = Anaerobic Digester + Cleanup Train + Fuel Cell Stack + Power Conditioner.** - **Raw Biogas Composition:** CH₄ (50–70%), CO₂ (30–40%), H₂S (0–1000 ppm), saturated H₂O. - **Why Cleanup is Critical:** H₂S > 1 ppm **permanently poisons** anodes; water dilutes fuel. - **SOFC is the gold standard** for direct biogas use because of **internal steam reforming** at 600–1000 °C. - **MCFC** is a close second; operates at 600–700 °C and uses **molten carbonate** electrolyte. - **PEMFC and PAFC** require **external reformers** for biogas → not preferred. - **E°(H₂-O₂) = 1.229 V ; E°(CH₄-O₂) = 1.06 V** (theoretical open-circuit voltages at 25 °C). - **Nernst Equation:** $E = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{P_{H_2} \sqrt{P_{O_2}}}{P_{H_2O}}$. - **Faraday's Law:** $I = nF\dot{n}_{fuel}$. - **Electrical Efficiency of FCs (40–60%) >> Internal Combustion Engines (30–35%)** because fuel cells bypass the Carnot limit. - **Total CHP Efficiency can reach 80–85%** with proper heat recovery. - **Stack Power Formula:** $P_{stack} = n_{cells} \cdot V_{cell} \cdot I$. - **Biogas Flow Formula:** $\dot{V}_{biogas} = P_{elec} / (\eta_{el} \cdot LHV_{biogas})$ — always report in **Nm³/h**. - **Working voltage per cell is 0.6–0.8 V**, much lower than 1.229 V OCV due to activation, ohmic, and concentration losses. - **Applications in Kerala:** Dairy plants (MCFC/SOFC CHP), coconut coir industry (PEMFC + biogas), municipal waste treatment plants (MW-scale SOFC). - **Environmental Edge:** When run on manure-based biogas, the system is **carbon-neutral**, supporting India's net-zero 2070 commitment.

topics.title: Combustion, Gasification and Pyrolysis of Biomass: Basic Principles. Technology and Equipment for Biomass Combustion, Chemistry of Gasification, Different Types of Gasifiers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT631 - RENEWABLE ENERGY AND GREEN TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Combustion, Gasification and Pyrolysis of Biomass: Basic Principles. Technology and Equipment for Biomass Combustion, Chemistry of Gasification, Different Types of Gasifiers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Introduction to Biomass Thermochemical Conversion

Biomass can be converted into useful forms of energy (heat, electricity, fuels) through three principal thermochemical pathways: **Combustion**, **Gasification**, and **Pyrolysis**. These processes differ primarily in the amount of oxygen supplied to the reaction zone and the operating temperature, which collectively determine the final product distribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The three thermochemical conversion processes form the core of Module 4. Students must clearly differentiate the **oxygen equivalence ratio (ER)**, **operating temperature window**, and **end-products** for each process.

### 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Aligned)

**1. Biomass Combustion:**
Combustion is the complete oxidation of biomass fuel in the presence of **excess air** (oxygen) at temperatures typically between **800 °C and 1200 °C**, producing primarily **carbon dioxide (CO₂)**, **water vapor (H₂O)**, **heat energy**, and **ash**.

**2. Biomass Gasification:**
Gasification is the **incomplete combustion** (partial oxidation) of biomass using a controlled, sub-stoichiometric supply of air, oxygen, or steam at elevated temperatures (**700 °C to 1200 °C**), producing a combustible gas mixture called **producer gas** or **syngas** — predominantly composed of **CO, H₂, CH₄, CO₂, N₂**, and trace hydrocarbons.

**3. Biomass Pyrolysis:**
Pyrolysis is the **thermal decomposition** of biomass in the **complete absence of oxygen** (or with negligible oxygen) at moderate temperatures (**300 °C to 800 °C**), yielding three products: **bio-char (solid)**, **bio-oil (liquid)**, and **non-condensable gases**.

### 1.3 The Oxygen Equivalence Ratio (ER) — The Master Variable

The single most important parameter distinguishing these three processes is the **Equivalence Ratio (ER)**, defined as:

$$
ER = \frac{\text{Actual Air-Fuel Ratio Supplied}}{\text{Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio Required for Complete Combustion}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> - **ER ≈ 1.0** → Complete Combustion
> - **ER = 0.2 to 0.4** → Gasification Zone
> - **ER = 0 (no oxygen)** → Pure Pyrolysis

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Campfire Science" Intuition

Imagine you are lighting a large campfire with dry wood:

- **If you blow plenty of air (full oxygen):** The wood burns bright, hot, and clean — leaving only ash and smoke. This is **Combustion**. You get maximum heat directly.

- **If you restrict the air** (using a Bunsen burner mode or a charcoal starter), the wood doesn't burn fully — instead, it smolders and gives off a glowing flame that produces a flammable gas. This is **Gasification**. You are deliberately "starving" the fire to harvest a gas fuel.

- **If you seal the wood in an airtight container and heat it externally** (like an old charcoal kiln), no flame appears. The wood thermally decomposes into charcoal, tar vapors, and gases. This is **Pyrolysis**. You are harvesting three different products depending on the temperature.

This simple analogy is the cornerstone of understanding biomass thermochemistry.

### 1.5 Comparative Overview Table

| Parameter | Combustion | Gasification | Pyrolysis |
|---|---|---|---|
| Oxygen Supply | Excess (ER ≈ 1.0–1.2) | Limited (ER ≈ 0.2–0.4) | None (ER = 0) |
| Temperature | 800–1200 °C | 700–1200 °C | 300–800 °C |
| Primary Product | Heat (flame) | Producer Gas (Syngas) | Bio-char + Bio-oil + Gas |
| Key Output State | Thermal Energy | Chemical Energy (Gas) | Solid + Liquid + Gas |
| Air Requirement | High | Moderate (30% of stoichiometric) | Zero |
| Typical Efficiency | 60–95% (thermal) | 70–85% (cold gas) | 60–80% (mass yield varies) |

### 1.6 Standard Metrics to Remember

- **Stoichiometric O₂ for Carbon:** **2.667 kg O₂ / kg C**
- **Stoichiometric O₂ for complete biomass (CH₁.₄₄O₀.₆₆):** ≈ **1.07 kg O₂ / kg biomass**
- **Stoichiometric Air (with N₂):** ≈ **5.0 kg air / kg dry biomass** (for typical CH₁.₄₄O₀.₆₆)
- **Lower Heating Value (LHV) of typical producer gas:** **4.5 – 6.5 MJ/Nm³**
- **LHV of biomass:** **15 – 20 MJ/kg** (dry basis)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Biomass Combustion — Process Theory

Combustion is a multi-stage heterogeneous process. For a solid biomass particle, the conversion occurs in four sequential stages:

### Stage 1: Drying (Evaporation of Moisture)
- Biomass typically contains **5–60% moisture** (wet basis).
- Energy required to evaporate water: ~ **2.26 MJ/kg** of water (latent heat at 100 °C).
- Temperature range: **100 °C to 150 °C**.

### Stage 2: Devolatilization (Pyrolysis Stage)
- Volatile matter (VM) of biomass is typically **70–86%** of dry weight.
- VM ignites and burns as flames in a flaming combustion phase.
- Temperature range: **150 °C to 500 °C**.

### Stage 3: Char Combustion (Heterogeneous Solid-Gas Reaction)
- Residual solid char reacts with O₂.
- Two competing mechanisms:
  - **Chemical reaction-controlled** (low temperature)
  - **Diffusion-controlled** (high temperature)
- Temperature range: **500 °C to 1200 °C**.

### Stage 4: Fixed Carbon Burnout
- Final burnout of the char residue.
- Produces CO₂ and ash.

### The Combustion Chemistry (Simplified)
Theoretical reactions for a generic biomass component **CₓHᵧOᵤ** with oxygen:

$$
C_xH_yO_u + O_2 \rightarrow xCO_2 + \frac{y}{2}H_2O + \text{Heat}
$$

For a representative biomass formula **CH₁.₄₄O₀.₆₆** (typical of wood):

$$
CH_{1.44}O_{0.66} + 1.03\,O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 0.72\,H_2O
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Combustion is the most mature and widely deployed biomass conversion technology. It is used in industrial boilers, domestic cookstoves, district heating systems, and biomass-based Combined Heat and Power (CHP) plants up to **100 MWe** scale.

## 2.2 Biomass Gasification — Process Theory

Gasification converts solid biomass into a combustible gas through **partial oxidation**. The process involves four key sub-reactions in a gasifier:

| Reaction | Equation | ΔH (kJ/mol) | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| Partial Oxidation | $C + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO$ | **−111** | Exothermic |
| Complete Oxidation | $C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$ | **−394** | Exothermic |
| Boudouard Reaction | $C + CO_2 \leftrightarrow 2CO$ | **+173** | Endothermic |
| Water-Gas Reaction | $C + H_2O \leftrightarrow CO + H_2$ | **+131** | Endothermic |
| Methanation | $C + 2H_2 \leftrightarrow CH_4$ | **−75** | Exothermic |
| Water-Gas Shift | $CO + H_2O \leftrightarrow CO_2 + H_2$ | **−41** | Exothermic |

### The Four Reaction Zones in a Downdraft Gasifier

| Zone | Temperature | Primary Reaction |
|---|---|---|
| **Drying Zone** | 100–200 °C | Moisture evaporation |
| **Pyrolysis Zone** | 200–600 °C | Devolatilization → Char + Gas + Tar |
| **Combustion/Reduction Zone** | 600–1200 °C | Partial oxidation + Char reduction |
| **Ash Bed** | < 600 °C | Ash cooling & gas conditioning |

## 2.3 Biomass Pyrolysis — Process Theory

Pyrolysis yields three products whose distribution is **highly temperature-dependent**:

| Pyrolysis Type | Temperature | Heating Rate | Primary Product | Yield (wt%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Slow Pyrolysis** | 300–500 °C | Low (≤ 1 °C/s) | **Bio-char** | 35% char, 30% bio-oil, 35% gas |
| **Fast Pyrolysis** | 500–800 °C | High (> 100 °C/s) | **Bio-oil** | 12% char, 75% bio-oil, 13% gas |
| **Flash Pyrolysis** | 800–1000 °C | Very High (> 1000 °C/s) | **Gas/Bio-oil** | Low char, high gas |
| **Vacuum Pyrolysis** | 300–500 °C | — | **Bio-oil** | Higher bio-oil yield |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Insight:** Fast pyrolysis with rapid quenching (≤ 1 second residence time) maximizes bio-oil yield because secondary cracking of vapors to gas is suppressed.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Equivalence Ratio | $ER = \dfrac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoich}}$ | Dimensionless | Determines process type |
| 2 | Stoichiometric Air (typical biomass) | $A_{stoich} \approx 5.0$ | kg air/kg biomass | Combustion design |
| 3 | Air for Gasification | $A_{gasif} = ER \times A_{stoich}$ | kg air/kg biomass | Gasifier design |
| 4 | Energy in Drying | $Q_{dry} = m_w \times 2.26$ | MJ | Heat balance |
| 5 | Producer Gas LHV | $LHV_g = 30\,(CO) + 25.7\,(H_2) + 85.4\,(CH_4)$ | MJ/Nm³ per vol% | Gas calorific value |
| 6 | Cold Gas Efficiency | $\eta_{CG} = \dfrac{V_g \times LHV_g}{m_b \times LHV_b}$ | % | Gasifier performance |
| 7 | Carbon Conversion Efficiency | $\eta_C = \dfrac{(CO + CO_2)_{gas}}{(CO + CO_2)_{gas} + C_{char}} \times 100$ | % | Gasification performance |
| 8 | Pyrolysis Mass Balance | $Y_{char} + Y_{oil} + Y_{gas} = 100\%$ | % | Pyrolysis products |
| 9 | Combustion Efficiency | $\eta_{comb} = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Losses}}{HHV_{fuel}}$ | % | Boiler performance |
| 10 | Stoichiometric O₂ for carbon | $O_2 = \dfrac{32}{12} = 2.667$ | kg O₂/kg C | Combustion stoichiometry |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention:** In the gas LHV formula, **(CO)**, **(H₂)**, **(CH₄)** represent the **volume percentage** of each component in the producer gas.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Combustion** dominates in bagasse-fired boilers (sugar industry), rice husk furnaces, and domestic pellets stoves.
- **Gasification** is preferred for decentralized power generation (10 kW – 10 MW) using engines, turbines, or fuel cells.
- **Pyrolysis** is central to **biochar production for soil amendment**, **bio-oil refining** to transportation fuels, and **activated carbon** manufacturing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometric Air Requirement for Complete Combustion of Biomass

**Given:** A biomass with ultimate analysis: C = 45%, H = 6%, O = 38%, N = 1%, S = 0.1% (all on dry, ash-free basis). The remaining is ash.

**Find:** The theoretical (stoichiometric) air required per kg of dry biomass.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Mass of each element per kg of dry biomass**

For 1 kg of dry biomass:
- Mass of Carbon: $m_C = 0.45$ kg
- Mass of Hydrogen: $m_H = 0.06$ kg
- Mass of Oxygen: $m_O = 0.38$ kg
- Mass of Nitrogen: $m_N = 0.01$ kg
- Mass of Sulphur: $m_S = 0.001$ kg

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric O₂ for each element**

The individual combustion reactions and O₂ demands are:

$$
C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad O_2^{C} = \frac{32}{12} \times m_C = 2.667 \times m_C
$$

$$
H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O \quad \Rightarrow \quad O_2^{H} = \frac{32}{4} \times m_H = 8.0 \times m_H
$$

$$
S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad O_2^{S} = \frac{32}{32} \times m_S = 1.0 \times m_S
$$

**Step 3 — Subtract the oxygen already in the fuel**

The fuel-bound oxygen reduces the air requirement. The "effective" O₂ is:

$$
O_2^{eff} = O_2^{C} + O_2^{H} + O_2^{S} - O_2^{fuel}
$$

Where $O_2^{fuel} = m_O$ (kg of fuel oxygen per kg of biomass).

**Step 4 — Numerical substitution**

$$
O_2^{C} = 2.667 \times 0.45 = 1.200\ kg
$$

$$
O_2^{H} = 8.0 \times 0.06 = 0.480\ kg
$$

$$
O_2^{S} = 1.0 \times 0.001 = 0.001\ kg
$$

$$
O_2^{eff} = 1.200 + 0.480 + 0.001 - 0.380 = 1.301\ kg\ O_2 \text{ per kg of dry biomass}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to stoichiometric air**

Air is approximately **23.2% O₂ by mass**:

$$
A_{stoich} = \frac{O_2^{eff}}{0.232} = \frac{1.301}{0.232} = 5.608\ kg\ air\ per\ kg\ biomass
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{A_{stoich} \approx 5.61\ \text{kg air/kg biomass (for complete combustion)}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cold Gas Efficiency of a Downdraft Gasifier

**Given Data:**
- Biomass feed rate: $m_b = 100$ kg/hr
- LHV of biomass: $LHV_b = 17.0$ MJ/kg
- Producer gas yield: $V_g = 2.5$ Nm³/kg biomass
- Producer gas composition (vol%): CO = 20%, H₂ = 15%, CH₄ = 3%, CO₂ = 10%, N₂ = 50%, rest = 2% (CₙHₘ)

**Find:** The cold gas efficiency of the gasifier.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Calculate the LHV of the producer gas**

The LHV of producer gas per Nm³ is calculated using the standard correlation:

$$
LHV_g = 30\,(CO) + 25.7\,(H_2) + 85.4\,(CH_4) + 151.3\,(C_nH_m)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the volume percentages**

$$
LHV_g = 30 \times 20 + 25.7 \times 15 + 85.4 \times 3 + 151.3 \times 2
$$

$$
LHV_g = 600 + 385.5 + 256.2 + 302.6 = 1544.3\ \text{kJ/Nm}^3
$$

$$
LHV_g \approx 1.544\ \text{MJ/Nm}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the chemical energy in the producer gas stream per hour**

Total energy in producer gas per hour:

$$
E_g = V_g \times LHV_g \times m_b = 2.5 \times 1.544 \times 100 = 386.0\ \text{MJ/hr}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the chemical energy in the biomass feed per hour**

$$
E_b = m_b \times LHV_b = 100 \times 17.0 = 1700\ \text{MJ/hr}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the cold gas efficiency**

$$
\eta_{CG} = \frac{E_g}{E_b} \times 100\% = \frac{386.0}{1700} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{CG} \approx 22.71\%}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** A typical downdraft gasifier achieves cold gas efficiency in the range of **60–80%** when LHV of biomass is closer to **18 MJ/kg**. The low value here is due to the relatively low gas yield. Students should check the given data carefully.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Equivalence Ratio in a Practical Gasifier

**Given:** A gasifier is supplied with **1.8 kg of air per kg of dry biomass**. The stoichiometric air requirement is **5.6 kg air/kg biomass**.

**Find:** The equivalence ratio and identify the process type.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Apply the ER definition**

$$
ER = \frac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoich}} = \frac{1.8}{5.6} = 0.321
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the process type**

$$
ER = 0.321 \in [0.2, 0.4]
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Process: Gasification (typical operating range)}}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Air-to-Fuel ratio on a molar basis (if required)**

If the gasifier uses **pure oxygen** instead of air, then:

$$
ER_{O_2} = \frac{0.232 \times 1.8}{1.301} = \frac{0.4176}{1.301} \approx 0.321
$$

(Same numerical ER since it is a normalized ratio.)

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Frequently Asked in KTU)

| Feature | Combustion | Gasification | Pyrolysis |
|---|---|---|---|
| ER value | 1.0 – 1.5 | 0.2 – 0.4 | 0.0 |
| T (°C) | 800–1200 | 700–1200 | 300–800 |
| Dominant Product | Heat | Syngas (CO + H₂) | Bio-oil + Char |
| Gas LHV (MJ/Nm³) | N/A | 4–6 (air blown) / 10–18 (oxygen/steam) | 10–20 (pyrogas) |
| Tar Production | Low | Moderate to High | High in slow; Low in fast |
| Main Reactor | Furnace / Boiler | Gasifier | Pyrolyzer / Reactor |
| Scale Range | 1 kW – 100 MW | 10 kW – 10 MW | 1 kg/hr – 20 t/hr |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Classification of Biomass Thermochemical Conversion

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[BIOMASS FEEDSTOCK] --> B{THERMOCHEMICAL CONVERSION}
    B --> C[COMBUSTION]
    B --> D[GASIFICATION]
    B --> E[PYROLYSIS]

    C --> C1[Air: ER = 1.0 to 1.5]
    C --> C2[Temperature: 800 to 1200 C]
    C --> C3[Output: HEAT and ASH]

    D --> D1[Air/Oxygen/Steam]
    D --> D2[ER = 0.2 to 0.4]
    D --> D3[Temperature: 700 to 1200 C]
    D --> D4[Output: PRODUCER GAS]

    E --> E1[No Oxygen]
    E --> E2[Temperature: 300 to 800 C]
    E --> E3[Output: CHAR plus BIO-OIL plus GAS]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Downdraft Gasifier

```mermaid
graph TD
    feedstock[BIOMASS FEED HOPPER] --> dryer[Drying Zone 100-200 C]
    dryer --> pyro[Pyrolysis Zone 200-600 C]
    pyro --> comb[Combustion Zone 600-1200 C]
    comb --> reduct[Reduction Zone 800-1000 C]
    reduct --> ash[Ash Bed and Grate]
    ash --> disch[Ash Discharge]

    air_in[PRIMARY AIR INLET] --> comb
    air_sec[SECONDARY AIR] --> pyro

    reduct --> gasout[PRODUCER GAS OUTLET]
    gasout --> cyclone[Cyclone Separator]
    cyclone --> cooler[Gas Cooler]
    cooler --> filter[Bag Filter]
    filter --> engine[IC Engine or Turbine]

    pyro --> tars[Tar and Vapors]
    tars --> cracker[Tar Cracker]
    cracker --> reduct
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Pyrolysis Product Distribution vs Temperature

```mermaid
graph LR
    P[BIOMASS INPUT] --> PY{PYROLYSIS REACTOR}

    PY -->|Slow 300-500 C| SC[Bio-char Dominant]
    PY -->|Fast 500-800 C| FO[Bio-oil Dominant]
    PY -->|Flash 800-1000 C| FG[Pyrogas Dominant]

    SC --> SC1[Char: 35 percent]
    SC --> SC2[Bio-oil: 30 percent]
    SC --> SC3[Gas: 35 percent]

    FO --> FO1[Char: 12 percent]
    FO --> FO2[Bio-oil: 75 percent]
    FO --> FO3[Gas: 13 percent]

    FG --> FG1[Char: 5 percent]
    FG --> FG2[Bio-oil: 20 percent]
    FG --> FG3[Gas: 75 percent]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Reaction Zones in a Fixed-Bed Downdraft Gasifier

```mermaid
graph TB
    Z1[ZONE 1 DRYING: Moisture Evaporation at 100-200 C]
    Z2[ZONE 2 PYROLYSIS: Volatile Release at 200-600 C]
    Z3[ZONE 3 COMBUSTION: Char plus O2 at 600-1200 C]
    Z4[ZONE 4 REDUCTION: CO2 and H2O reduced to CO and H2 at 800-1000 C]
    Z5[ZONE 5 ASH BED: Cooling and Ash Removal at 200-400 C]

    Z1 --> Z2 --> Z3 --> Z4 --> Z5
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Schematic Interpretation Note:** In the downdraft gasifier, both air and biomass flow **downwards (co-current)**. This configuration allows the hot combustion gases to pass through the fresh biomass, drying and pyrolyzing it before entering the reduction zone. This is why downdraft gasifiers produce **the cleanest producer gas (lowest tar content)** among all gasifier types.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]:**
**Define biomass gasification. What is the role of equivalence ratio (ER) in distinguishing combustion, gasification, and pyrolysis?**

**Model Answer:**

Biomass gasification is a thermochemical process that converts solid biomass into a combustible gas (producer gas) by partial oxidation at elevated temperatures (700–1200 °C) using a sub-stoichiometric supply of air, oxygen, or steam.

**Role of Equivalence Ratio (ER):**
ER is defined as the ratio of actual air supplied to the stoichiometric air required for complete combustion.
- **ER > 1**: Excess air → Combustion regime
- **ER = 0.2 to 0.4**: Limited air → Gasification regime
- **ER = 0**: No air → Pyrolysis regime

Thus, ER is the **master control variable** that determines which thermochemical regime the reactor operates in. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]:**
**Differentiate between slow pyrolysis and fast pyrolysis. State any three differences.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Slow Pyrolysis | Fast Pyrolysis |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | 300–500 °C | 500–800 °C |
| Heating Rate | Low (≤ 1 °C/s) | High (> 100 °C/s) |
| Vapor Residence Time | Long (mins to hours) | Short (< 1 s) |
| Primary Product | **Bio-char** (35%) | **Bio-oil** (75%) |
| Char Yield | High | Low |

Any three of the above points carry full marks. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Model Paper Pattern]:**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a **downdraft gasifier**. List its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A biomass with ultimate analysis **C = 42%, H = 5%, O = 36%, N = 1%, S = 0.5%, Ash = 15.5%** is burnt in a furnace with **40% excess air**. Calculate:
  (i) The stoichiometric air required per kg of fuel. **(3 marks)**
  (ii) The actual air supplied per kg of fuel. **(2 marks)**
  (iii) The percentage composition of dry flue gases by mass. **(2 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

A **downdraft gasifier** is a fixed-bed reactor where both biomass and air flow **co-currently downward**. It consists of the following zones (from top to bottom):
1. **Drying Zone (100–200 °C):** Moisture evaporates.
2. **Pyrolysis Zone (200–600 °C):** Biomass devolatilizes to char, tar, and gases.
3. **Combustion/Oxidation Zone (600–1200 °C):** Char burns with air.
4. **Reduction Zone (800–1000 °C):** CO₂ and H₂O are reduced to CO and H₂.
5. **Ash Bed:** Ash cools and is removed.

**Equations of major reactions:**
$$
C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 \quad \text{(Exothermic)}
$$
$$
C + CO_2 \rightarrow 2CO \quad \text{(Boudouard - Endothermic)}
$$
$$
C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2 \quad \text{(Water-Gas)}
$$

**Advantages:**
- Lowest tar content in producer gas among all gasifier types
- Simple construction
- Suitable for IC engine operation
- Self-cleaning of ash

**Limitations:**
- Cannot handle high-moisture biomass (> 20%)
- Limited scale-up potential (max ~ 1 MW)
- High temperature at throat requires refractory lining

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Stoichiometric O₂ required:**

For 1 kg of dry biomass:
- $m_C = 0.42$ kg → $O_2^C = 2.667 \times 0.42 = 1.120$ kg
- $m_H = 0.05$ kg → $O_2^H = 8.0 \times 0.05 = 0.400$ kg
- $m_S = 0.005$ kg → $O_2^S = 1.0 \times 0.005 = 0.005$ kg
- $m_O = 0.36$ kg → $O_2^{fuel} = 0.360$ kg

**Net stoichiometric O₂:**

$$
O_2^{stoich} = 1.120 + 0.400 + 0.005 - 0.360 = 1.165\ \text{kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric air:**

$$
A_{stoich} = \frac{1.165}{0.232} = 5.021\ \text{kg air/kg fuel}
$$

**Step 3 — Actual air with 40% excess:**

$$
A_{actual} = 1.40 \times 5.021 = 7.030\ \text{kg air/kg fuel}
$$

**Step 4 — Dry flue gas composition by mass:**

O₂ in actual air = $7.030 \times 0.232 = 1.631$ kg
N₂ in actual air = $7.030 \times 0.768 = 5.399$ kg
Excess O₂ = $1.631 - 1.165 = 0.466$ kg

CO₂ produced = $\frac{44}{12} \times 0.42 = 1.540$ kg
SO₂ produced = $\frac{64}{32} \times 0.005 = 0.010$ kg
N₂ from fuel = 0.010 kg

**Total dry flue gas = 0.466 + 5.399 + 1.540 + 0.010 + 0.010 = 7.425 kg**

**Mass percentages:**
- CO₂: $\frac{1.540}{7.425} \times 100 = 20.74\%$
- SO₂: $\frac{0.010}{7.425} \times 100 = 0.13\%$
- O₂: $\frac{0.466}{7.425} \times 100 = 6.28\%$
- N₂: $\frac{5.409}{7.425} \times 100 = 72.85\%$

$$
\boxed{\text{Dry Flue Gas: CO}_2 = 20.74\%,\ O_2 = 6.28\%,\ N_2 = 72.85\%,\ SO_2 = 0.13\%}
$$

**(Total: 7 + 7 = 14 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Model Paper Pattern]:**

**(a)** Explain the **chemistry of gasification** with the help of all major reaction equations. Discuss the role of the **Boudouard reaction** and the **water-gas shift reaction** in determining the producer gas composition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A downdraft gasifier processes **150 kg/hr** of biomass with LHV of **16.5 MJ/kg** and produces producer gas with the following composition (vol%): CO = 18, H₂ = 12, CH₄ = 2, CO₂ = 11, N₂ = 55, C₂H₄ = 2. The gas yield is **2.2 Nm³/kg biomass**. Calculate:
  (i) The LHV of the producer gas. **(3 marks)**
  (ii) The cold gas efficiency. **(2 marks)**
  (iii) The carbon conversion efficiency, given that the unconverted carbon in char is 5% of the carbon in the feed. **(2 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The gasification chemistry is governed by a set of **heterogeneous** (gas–solid) and **homogeneous** (gas–gas) reactions. The major reactions are:

**Heterogeneous (Gas-Solid) Reactions:**

**(1) Partial Oxidation (Exothermic):**
$$
C + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO \quad \Delta H = -111\ \text{kJ/mol}
$$

**(2) Complete Oxidation (Exothermic):**
$$
C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 \quad \Delta H = -394\ \text{kJ/mol}
$$

**(3) Boudouard Reaction (Endothermic):**
$$
C + CO_2 \leftrightarrow 2CO \quad \Delta H = +173\ \text{kJ/mol}
$$

**(4) Water-Gas / Steam Gasification (Endothermic):**
$$
C + H_2O \leftrightarrow CO + H_2 \quad \Delta H = +131\ \text{kJ/mol}
$$

**(5) Hydrogasification (Methanation):**
$$
C + 2H_2 \leftrightarrow CH_4 \quad \Delta H = -75\ \text{kJ/mol}
$$

**Homogeneous (Gas-Gas) Reactions:**

**(6) Water-Gas Shift Reaction:**
$$
CO + H_2O \leftrightarrow CO_2 + H_2 \quad \Delta H = -41\ \text{kJ/mol}
$$

**Role of Boudouard Reaction:**
- **Endothermic** — favored at **high temperatures (> 700 °C)**.
- Converts CO₂ (a diluent) into **CO** (a combustible gas).
- Increases the **CO/H₂ ratio** and heating value of producer gas.
- Slows down at lower temperatures; thus, the **reduction zone temperature** is critical.

**Role of Water-Gas Shift Reaction:**
- Adjusts the **H₂/CO ratio** of the syngas.
- Mildly exothermic — favored at **lower temperatures**.
- Critical for downstream catalytic applications (e.g., Fischer–Tropsch synthesis requires a specific H₂:CO ratio).
- Determines the final equilibrium composition of the producer gas.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In an air-blown gasifier, the nitrogen dilution limits the LHV to **4–6 MJ/Nm³**. In contrast, an **oxygen-blown** or **steam-blown** gasifier can achieve LHV of **10–18 MJ/Nm³**, making it suitable for pipeline-quality gas or Fischer–Tropsch applications.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — LHV of Producer Gas:**

Using the standard correlation:
$$
LHV_g = 30(CO) + 25.7(H_2) + 85.4(CH_4) + 151.3(C_nH_m)
$$

$$
LHV_g = 30(18) + 25.7(12) + 85.4(2) + 151.3(2)
$$

$$
LHV_g = 540 + 308.4 + 170.8 + 302.6 = 1321.8\ \text{kJ/Nm}^3
$$

$$
\boxed{LHV_g \approx 1.322\ \text{MJ/Nm}^3}
$$

**Step 2 — Cold Gas Efficiency:**

Energy in producer gas per hour:
$$
E_g = 2.2 \times 1.322 \times 150 = 436.26\ \text{MJ/hr}
$$

Energy in biomass per hour:
$$
E_b = 150 \times 16.5 = 2475\ \text{MJ/hr}
$$

$$
\eta_{CG} = \frac{436.26}{2475} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{CG} \approx 17.63\%}
$$

**Step 3 — Carbon Conversion Efficiency:**

Carbon in producer gas (in CO and CO₂):
Total moles of producer gas per hour = $2.2 \times 150 = 330$ Nm³/hr.
Moles of CO = $\frac{18 \times 330}{100} = 59.4$ Nm³/hr
Moles of CO₂ = $\frac{11 \times 330}{100} = 36.3$ Nm³/hr

Mass of carbon in (CO + CO₂):
$$
C_{gas} = 59.4 \times \frac{12}{22.4} + 36.3 \times \frac{12}{22.4} = \frac{12}{22.4}(59.4 + 36.3) = 0.5357 \times 95.7 = 51.27\ \text{kg/hr}
$$

If unconverted carbon is 5% of feed carbon, then:
- Let $C_{feed} = X$ → $C_{char} = 0.05X$ → $C_{gas} = 0.95X$
- $0.95X = 51.27$ → $X = 53.97$ kg/hr

Carbon conversion efficiency:
$$
\eta_C = \frac{C_{gas}}{C_{feed}} \times 100\% = 95\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_C = 95\%}
$$

**(Total: 7 + 7 = 14 marks)**

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing ER with Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR):** ER is dimensionless; AFR has units. Many students write ER = 1.8 instead of ER = 0.32.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract fuel-bound oxygen** in stoichiometric air calculation. This alone causes 1–2 mark deduction.
> 3. **Mixing wet and dry flue gas compositions.** KTU typically asks for **dry** composition — water vapor must be excluded.
> 4. **Using 21% instead of 23.2% for O₂ mass fraction in air.** Always use **mass-based O₂ fraction (0.232)** for mass balance; use **mole-based (0.21)** only for mole fractions.
> 5. **In Producer Gas LHV formula**, students often forget the **CₙHₘ (151.3 MJ/Nm³)** term. If given any hydrocarbon content, include it.
> 6. **For downdraft gasifier sketches**, students draw the air inlet from the side without showing the **throat zone**. The throat (where combustion occurs) is the **defining feature** of a downdraft gasifier.
> 7. **Slow vs Fast Pyrolysis confusion:** Slow pyrolysis → more **char**; Fast pyrolysis → more **bio-oil**. KTU examiners strictly check this.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Equivalence Ratio (ER)** is the **master variable** that distinguishes combustion (ER ≈ 1.0), gasification (ER = 0.2–0.4), and pyrolysis (ER = 0).
- **Combustion** requires excess air, produces heat, ash, and CO₂/H₂O. Efficiency = 60–95%.
- **Gasification** uses sub-stoichiometric air, produces **producer gas** with LHV = 4–6 MJ/Nm³ (air-blown) or 10–18 MJ/Nm³ (oxygen/steam-blown).
- **Four key gasification reactions** to memorize: Partial Oxidation, Boudouard, Water-Gas, and Water-Gas Shift.
- **Downdraft gasifier** has the **lowest tar** content, making it most suitable for IC engines.
- **Updraft gasifier** has the **highest tar** content but tolerates **high-moisture biomass**.
- **Fluidized bed gasifier** is best for **large-scale (> 5 MW)** operations and uniform biomass.
- **Slow Pyrolysis → Bio-char (dominant); Fast Pyrolysis → Bio-oil (dominant); Flash Pyrolysis → Pyrogas (dominant).**
- **Stoichiometric air for typical biomass ≈ 5.0–5.6 kg air/kg dry biomass.**
- **Cold Gas Efficiency** is the standard performance metric for gasifiers (typical: 60–80% for well-designed systems).
- **Boudouard Reaction** (endothermic) is favored at **high T** and increases **CO** content.
- **Water-Gas Shift Reaction** (mildly exothermic) adjusts the **H₂/CO ratio** of syngas.
- For **air stoichiometry** in mass balance, use **O₂ mass fraction = 0.232**; for mole balance, use **0.21**.
- Always check if the question asks for **dry** or **wet** flue gas composition — KTU defaults to **dry**.
- **LHV of producer gas** is computed using the empirical formula: $LHV_g = 30(CO) + 25.7(H_2) + 85.4(CH_4) + 151.3(C_nH_m)$ in kJ/Nm³.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal Applications of Producer Gas: Use of Producer Gas in Engines. Concept of Green Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT631 - RENEWABLE ENERGY AND GREEN TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Thermal Applications of Producer Gas: Use of Producer Gas in Engines. Concept of Green Technology ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Producer Gas — Formal Definition **Producer Gas** (also called *air gas* or *suction gas*) is a combustible fuel gas mixture produced by the **partial combustion (incomplete combustion)** of solid carbonaceous fuels such as **biomass, charcoal, lignite, peat, or coke** in a specially designed reactor called a **gasifier**, using a controlled, sub-stoichiometric supply of air (or a mixture of air and steam). The endothermic reactions inside the gasifier convert a solid fuel into a low-heating-value gaseous fuel that can be burned in furnaces, boilers, kilns, and — most importantly for this Module — **internal combustion (IC) engines** for mechanical and electrical power generation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Producer gas is classified as a **secondary** or **derived** fuel. It is NOT a primary fossil fuel. It is a *bridge technology* between solid biomass and high-grade gaseous fuels like natural gas. ### 1.2 Typical Composition of Producer Gas (Wood/Biomass Derived) | Component | Chemical Symbol | Volume % (Wood) | Volume % (Coal/Coke) | |-----------|----------------|------------------|----------------------| | Carbon Monoxide | CO | 22 – 30 | 25 – 30 | | Hydrogen | H₂ | 10 – 15 | 5 – 10 | | Methane | CH₄ | 1 – 3 | 0 – 2 | | Nitrogen | N₂ | 45 – 55 | 50 – 55 | | Carbon Dioxide | CO₂ | 3 – 8 | 3 – 6 | | Oxygen | O₂ | 0.1 – 0.5 | 0.1 – 0.5 | **Calorific Value (CV):** **4.5 – 6.5 MJ/Nm³** (compared to **Natural Gas ≈ 35–38 MJ/Nm³**) ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Controlled Campfire" > [!NOTE] > **Intuition:** Imagine a normal campfire. You light wood, and it produces **flame + smoke + heat + ash**. Most of the chemical energy escapes as visible smoke (unburnt CO, H₂, hydrocarbons) and you get leftover char. > > Now imagine a *sealed* firebox where you **restrict the air supply** to about **25–35 %** of what complete combustion needs. The wood does NOT burn fully to CO₂. Instead, the carbon and steam react to form **CO and H₂** — both of which are combustible gases. > > These gases are then sucked out and piped into a furnace or an engine. *This is producer gas.* Think of it as **"capturing the would-be smoke and using it as fuel."** ### 1.4 Green Technology — Formal Definition **Green Technology** (also called *environmental technology* or *clean technology*) refers to the **development and application of products, equipment, and systems used to conserve the natural environment and resources**, curb the negative impacts of human activity, and promote sustainability across industrial, energy, agricultural, and economic sectors. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition (Standard):** Green technology is the application of one or more environmental sciences to **conserve the environment**, **minimize the negative impact of technology on nature**, and **provide sustainable solutions** to modern industrial problems. ### 1.5 Intuition for Green Technology — "The Three Rs Plus One" > [!NOTE] > **Real-world analogy:** Think of a household kitchen. Old kitchen practice: throw all vegetable peels into the bin → landfill methane. Green technology practice: turn peels into **compost → biogas → fertilizer + cooking gas + electricity**. > > Green tech simply applies this *closed-loop mindset* to **factories, power plants, vehicles, and supply chains** — turning *waste* into a *resource* and *pollution* into a *product*. ### 1.6 Core Objectives of Green Technology - **Reduction** of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, particularly **CO₂, CH₄, N₂O**. - **Replacement** of fossil fuels with **renewable energy** (solar, wind, biomass, hydro). - **Resource efficiency** — using less material and energy per unit output. - **Pollution prevention** at source rather than end-of-pipe treatment. - **Sustainable design** — products engineered for *recycle*, *reuse*, and *remanufacture*. - **Carbon capture, sequestration, and offsetting**. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Chemistry of Producer Gas Formation — Step-by-Step Reactions Inside a downdraft or updraft gasifier, four key heterogeneous and homogeneous reactions take place in distinct **reaction zones**: **Zone 1 — Drying Zone (Top, 100–150 °C):** $$ \text{Moisture in fuel} \longrightarrow \text{Steam} \uparrow $$ **Zone 2 — Pyrolysis Zone (200–600 °C):** $$ \text{Biomass (C, H, O)} \longrightarrow \text{Char} + \text{CO} + \text{CO}_2 + \text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2 + \text{Tar vapours} $$ **Zone 3 — Oxidation (Combustion) Zone (800–1400 °C):** $$ \text{C} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad \Delta H = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol} $$ $$ \text{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO} \quad \Delta H = -110.5 \text{ kJ/mol} $$ **Zone 4 — Reduction Zone (600–900 °C):** $$ \text{CO}_2 + \text{C} \longrightarrow 2\text{CO} \quad \text{(Boudouard reaction, endothermic)} $$ $$ \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{C} \longrightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2 \quad \text{(Water-gas reaction)} $$ $$ \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO} \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad \text{(Water-gas shift)} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in engines:** The **reduction zone is where the energy value of producer gas is decided**. If the gasifier temperature drops below **~700 °C**, the Boudouard reaction reverses and CO₂ rises while CO falls — *killing the calorific value*. ### 2.2 Key Parameters Governing Producer Gas Quality - **Equivalence Ratio (ER):** Ratio of actual air supplied to stoichiometric air. $$ \text{ER} = \frac{\text{Actual air supplied}}{\text{Stoichiometric air for complete combustion}} $$ *Optimal range for gasification:* **0.20 – 0.35** (i.e., 20 – 35 % of stoichiometric air). - **Gasification Efficiency (η_g):** $$ \eta_g = \frac{\text{Cold Gas Energy Output (CV of producer gas} \times \text{Volume)}}{\text{Energy Input (CV of biomass} \times \text{Mass)}} \times 100 \% $$ - **Carbon Conversion Efficiency:** $$ \eta_{CCE} = \frac{\text{Mass of carbon converted to gas}}{\text{Mass of carbon in feedstock}} \times 100 \% $$ ### 2.3 Why Producer Gas Can Be Used in IC Engines For any fuel to be usable in a spark-ignition (SI) engine, it must: 1.
