KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define eco-friendly materials. List any four properties that distinguish them from conventional materials.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Definition (1 Mark):** Eco-friendly materials are substances designed, produced, and used in a manner that minimizes their environmental impact throughout their life cycle — from raw extraction to end-of-life — by exhibiting low embodied energy, low carbon footprint, renewability, and biodegradability. **Four Distinguishing Properties (2 Marks — 0.5 each):** 1. **Low Embodied Energy** — Energy consumed in production is significantly lower (e.g., bamboo $EE_{\text{sp}} \approx 1.5\ \text{MJ/kg}$ vs. steel $EE_{\text{sp}} \approx 25\ \text{MJ/kg}$). 2. **Renewable Source** — Derived from biomass with regeneration cycles shorter than consumption rates. 3. **Biodegradability / Recyclability** — Capable of microbial decomposition or closed-loop material recovery. 4. **Non-toxicity** — Emit zero VOCs and contain no carcinogens, heavy metals, or persistent pollutants. --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is the Cradle-to-Cradle (C2C) concept? How does it differ from the traditional Cradle-to-Grave model?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cradle-to-Cradle (C2C):** A sustainable design philosophy in which every product, at end-of-life, becomes a *nutrient* for a new product — either a **biological nutrient** (safely compostable back into biosphere) or a **technical nutrient** (infinitely recyclable within industrial cycles). Proposed by *Braungart & McDonough* in their 2002 book *Cradle to Cradle: Remaking the Way We Make Things*. **Key Differences (2 Marks):** | Aspect | Cradle-to-Grave (C2G) | Cradle-to-Cradle (C2C) | |--------|-----------------------|-------------------------| | End-of-life | Waste disposed in landfill | Waste becomes feedstock for new product | | Resource loop | Linear (mine → use → dump) | Circular (mine → use → reuse) | | Ecological goal | Minimize harm | Create positive value | | Material philosophy | "Less bad" | "100% good" | --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) --- ### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the **Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)** methodology as per **ISO 14040/14044** standards. List and briefly describe the four mandatory phases. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A green-concrete mix design uses the following constituents for a $1\ \text{m}^3$ batch. Calculate the **total embodied energy**, the **specific embodied energy**, and the **specific carbon footprint**. Compare with conventional concrete. **(7 Marks)** | Constituent | Mass (kg) | $EE_{\text{sp}}$ (MJ/kg) | $EF$ (kgCO₂e/kg) | |-------------|-----------|--------------------------|-------------------| | Geopolymer binder (fly-ash + NaOH) | $350$ | $3.5$ | $0.18$ | | Recycled coarse aggregate | $800$ | $0.4$ | $0.04$ | | Natural river sand | $600$ | $0.1$ | $0.005$ | | Water | $180$ | $0.01$ | $0.0003$ | *For comparison: Conventional concrete $EE_{\text{sp}} \approx 5.5\ \text{MJ/kg}$, $CF_{\text{sp}} \approx 0.93\ \text{kgCO}_2\text{e/kg}$.* --- #### Model Answer — Part (a): LCA Methodology **(i) Goal & Scope Definition (1.5 Marks):** - Establish the **functional unit** (e.g., "1 m² of 25 MPa wall for 50 years"). - Define **system boundaries** (cradle-to-gate, cradle-to-grave, etc.). - Identify **impact categories** of interest (GWP, acidification, eutrophication). **(ii) Life Cycle Inventory (LCI) — 1.5 Marks:** - Quantify all **inputs** (raw materials, energy in MJ, water in m³). - Quantify all **outputs** (CO₂, SO₂, NOₓ, waste solids). - Data sourced from Ecoinvent, GaBi, or USLCI databases. **(iii) Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA) — 2 Marks:** - Convert inventory flows into environmental impacts using **characterization factors**. - Categories include: - **GWP (Global Warming Potential)** — kgCO₂e. - **AP (Acidification Potential)** — kgSO₂e. - **EP (Eutrophication Potential)** — kgPO₄e. - **ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential)** — kgCFC-11e. - Optional **normalization** and **weighting** steps yield a single aggregated score. **(iv) Interpretation — 2 Marks:** - **Hotspot identification** — pinpoint processes contributing $> 80\%$ of impact. - **Sensitivity analysis** — vary assumptions, test robustness. - **Limitations disclosure** — data uncertainty, allocation procedures, cut-off criteria. --- #### Model Answer — Part (b): Calculation **Step 1 — Total Mass** $$ \begin{aligned} M_{\text{total}} &= 350 + 800 + 600 + 180 = 1930\ \text{kg} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Component-wise Embodied Energy** $$ \begin{aligned} EE_1 &= 350 \times 3.5 = 1225.0\ \text{MJ} \\ EE_2 &= 800 \times 0.4 = 320.0\ \text{MJ} \\ EE_3 &= 600 \times 0.1 = 60.0\ \text{MJ} \\ EE_4 &= 180 \times 0.01 = 1.8\ \text{MJ} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Total Embodied Energy** $$ \begin{aligned} EE_{\text{total}} &= 1225.0 + 320.0 + 60.0 + 1.8 \\ &= 1606.8\ \text{MJ} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Specific Embodied Energy** $$ \begin{aligned} EE_{\text{sp}} &= \frac{1606.8}{1930} \\ &\approx 0.833\ \text{MJ/kg} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Component-wise Carbon Footprint** $$ \begin{aligned} CF_1 &= 350 \times 0.18 = 63.0\ \text{kgCO}_2\text{e} \\ CF_2 &= 800 \times 0.04 = 32.0\ \text{kgCO}_2\text{e} \\ CF_3 &= 600 \times 0.005 = 3.0\ \text{kgCO}_2\text{e} \\ CF_4 &= 180 \times 0.0003 = 0.054\ \text{kgCO}_2\text{e} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Total and Specific Carbon Footprint** $$ \begin{aligned} CF_{\text{total}} &= 63.0 + 32.0 + 3.0 + 0.054 \\ &= 98.054\ \text{kgCO}_2\text{e} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} CF_{\text{sp}} &= \frac{98.054}{1930} \\ &\approx 0.0508\ \text{kgCO}_2\text{e/kg} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7 — Comparison** $$ \begin{aligned} \%\ \text{EE reduction} &= \frac{5.5 - 0.833}{5.5} \times 100 \approx 84.85\% \\ \%\ \text{CF reduction} &= \frac{0.93 - 0.0508}{0.93} \times 100 \approx 94.54\% \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{EE_{\text{sp}} \approx 0.833\ \text{MJ/kg},\quad CF_{\text{sp}} \approx 0.0508\ \text{kgCO}_2\text{e/kg}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** The geopolymer green-concrete achieves an **$84.85\%$ reduction in embodied energy** and **$94.54\%$ reduction in carbon footprint** relative to conventional concrete — making it an exemplary eco-friendly construction material. --- ### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Classify **eco-friendly materials** into five major categories with two examples each. Discuss the embodied-energy and carbon-footprint advantages of each class. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the **Cradle-to-Cradle (C2C)** design philosophy. Compute the **Energy Pay-Back Time (EPBT)** for a green roof system whose data is given below, and comment on the carbon-credit potential. **(7 Marks)** | Parameter | Symbol | Value | |-----------|--------|-------| | Total embodied energy of system | $EE_{\text{total}}$ | $2400\ \text{MJ/m}^2$ | | Annual electrical output | $E_{\text{out,annual}}$ | $900\ \text{MJ/m}^2$/yr | | Annual operational CO₂ saving | $S_{\text{CO}_2}$ | $75\ \text{kgCO}_2\text{e/m}^2$/yr | | System lifetime | $L$ | $30$ years | --- #### Model Answer — Part (a): Five Major Categories | # | Category | Two Examples | Key Advantage | |---|----------|--------------|---------------| | 1 | **Natural Bio-based** | Bamboo, Hempcrete | Lowest $EE_{\text{sp}}$ ($0.8 - 1.5\ \text{MJ/kg}$); potentially carbon-negative | | 2 | **Recycled / Reclaimed** | Recycled aggregate, Reclaimed timber | Diverts waste from landfill; reduces virgin extraction | | 3 | **Biodegradable Polymers** | PLA, PHA | Compostable end-of-life; non-persistent in environment | | 4 | **Industrial By-products** | Fly ash, Slag | Converts waste into resource; reduces cement demand | | 5 | **Smart Advanced Green** | Self-healing concrete, PCMs | Enhanced durability and operational energy efficiency | **Embodied-Energy Discussion (3.5 Marks distributed):** - Natural bio-based materials achieve $EE_{\text{sp}} < 2\ \text{MJ/kg}$ because they bypass high-temperature industrial processing. - Recycled materials save $\sim 60\%$ energy vs. virgin equivalents because extraction is eliminated. - Biodegradable polymers have moderate $EE_{\text{sp}}$ ($20 - 30\ \text{MJ/kg}$) but offset this via compostability credits. - Industrial by-products (SCMs) replace cement, the most energy-intensive component ($\sim 5.5\ \text{MJ/kg}$). - Smart materials reduce operational energy, improving lifecycle EE despite higher initial values. --- #### Model Answer — Part (b): C2C Sketch and EPBT Calculation **Sketch of Cradle-to-Cradle Concept** (replicated here verbally for KTU answer sheet): > **Description for answer sheet (2 Marks):** Draw two interconnected loops. The **Biological Loop** shows: Bio-source → Production → Use → Composting → Soil nutrients → Bio-source. The **Technical Loop** shows: Tech-source → Manufacturing → Use → Reclamation → Tech-source. Arrows from "Use" return into both loops, indicating a closed-loop, zero-waste system. **EPBT Calculation (3 Marks):** Applying the EPBT formula directly: $$ \begin{aligned} EPBT &= \frac{EE_{\text{total}}}{E_{\text{out,annual}}} \\ &= \frac{2400}{900} \\ &\approx 2.667\ \text{years} \end{aligned} $$ **Carbon-Credit Potential (2 Marks):** Total lifetime CO₂ saving: $$ \begin{aligned} S_{\text{lifetime}} &= S_{\text{CO}_2} \times L \\ &= 75 \times 30 \\ &= 2250\ \text{kgCO}_2\text{e/m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ At the prevailing **Indian carbon credit price** (approximately ₹800–1500 per tCO₂e): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Revenue/m}^2 &= 2.25\ \text{tCO}_2\text{e} \times 1000\ \text{INR/t} \\ &\approx 2250\ \text{INR/m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{EPBT \approx 2.67\ \text{years},\quad \text{Lifetime CO}_2\text{ saving} = 2250\ \text{kgCO}_2\text{e/m}^2} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** With an EPBT of $2.67$ years and $30$-year lifetime, the system operates cleanly for $27.33$ years, sequestering $2250\ \text{kgCO}_2\text{e/m}^2$ — eligible for registration under India's **Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS), 2023**. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where students LOSE marks on Eco-Friendly Materials questions:** > > 1. **Confusing "Bio-based" with "Biodegradable":** *Bio-based* means *derived from biomass*; *biodegradable* means *decomposed by microbes*. PLA is bio-based but **only compostable under industrial conditions**, not backyard composters. (–1 to –2 marks) > > 2. **Ignoring units in LCA math:** Always write MJ/kg and kgCO₂e/kg explicitly. Bare numbers without units fetch 0 marks even if numerically correct. (–1 mark) > > 3. **Treating Cradle-to-Cradle as "recycling":** C2C is a **design philosophy** applied *before* the product exists — students must emphasize **upfront design intent**, not just downstream recycling. (–2 marks) > > 4. **Forgetting the End-of-Life stage in LCA:** Many students stop at manufacturing. KTU examiners expect **all four stages**: extraction → manufacture → use → end-of-life. (–1.5 marks) > > 5. **Using "green" and "eco-friendly" interchangeably without definition:** Always anchor your answer with a **formal definition** in the opening sentence to claim full marks. > > 6. **Neglecting local context:** KTU Kerala-specific examples (coconut coir, bamboo from Wayanad, rice husk ash) score better than generic global examples. (–1 mark for irrelevance) --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Eco-Friendly Materials:** - ✅ **Definition**: Low-impact across full lifecycle — embodied energy, carbon, biodegradability, renewability. - ✅ **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)**: Planet + People + Profit — required for true sustainability. - ✅ **Five Major Classes**: Natural, Recycled, Biodegradable polymers, Industrial by-products, Smart green. - ✅ **Bio-based $\neq$ Biodegradable** — distinguish origin vs. end-of-life behavior. - ✅ **Embodied Energy Formula**: $EE = \sum (E_i \times M_i)$, units MJ; $EE_{\text{sp}}$ in MJ/kg. - ✅ **Carbon Footprint Formula**: $CF = \sum (EF_i \times M_i)$, units kgCO₂e; $CF_{\text{sp}}$ in kgCO₂e/kg. - ✅ **EPBT Formula**: $EPBT = EE_{\text{total}} / E_{\text{out,annual}}$, units years. - ✅ **Recycling Efficiency**: $\eta_{\text{recycle}} = (M_{\text{recovered}} / M_{\text{input}}) \times 100\%$. - ✅ **Biodegradability**: $BD\% = [(M_0 - M_t) / M_0] \times 100\%$. - ✅ **LCA Framework (ISO 14040/14044)**: Goal & Scope → LCI → LCIA → Interpretation. - ✅ **Cradle-to-Cradle** = circular / closed-loop; **Cradle-to-Grave** = linear / landfill. - ✅ **KTU-Kerala examples to cite**: Coconut coir composites, bamboo from Wayanad, rice husk ash cement, laterite blocks. - ✅ **Standard benchmarks**: Steel $EE_{\text{sp}} \approx 25$, Cement $\approx 5.5$, Bamboo $\approx 1.5$, Hempcrete $\approx 0.8$ MJ/kg. - ✅ **Carbon benchmarks**: Cement $\approx 0.93$, Steel $\approx 1.99$, Fly-ash brick $\approx 0.08$ kgCO₂e/kg. - ✅ **SDG link**: SDG 12 — Responsible Consumption and Production. - ✅ **Key Standards**: ISO 14040 (LCA), ISO 14044 (LCA requirements), REACH (EU chemicals), CPCB (India). - ✅ **Self-healing concrete**: Uses *Bacillus pseudofirmus* spores + calcium lactate nutrient. - ✅ **PLA caveat**: Industrial-compostable only ($\geq 58°\text{C}$); not backyard-compostable. - ✅ **EROI**: For a BiPV roof, $EPBT \leq 3$ years yields EROI $> 8$ — sustainable threshold.

topics.title: Green Building
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT631 - RENEWABLE ENERGY AND GREEN TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Green Building

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Green Building** (also referred to as a *Sustainable Building* or *High-Performance Building*) is a structure that is designed, constructed, renovated, operated, or reused in an environmentally responsible and resource-efficient manner throughout its entire life cycle — from **siting to design, construction, operation, maintenance, renovation, and demolition**.

According to the standard KTU-aligned definition, a green building addresses several interlinked concerns:

$$ \text{Green Building} \;\equiv\; f\big(\text{Energy},\ \text{Water},\ \text{Materials},\ \text{IAQ},\ \text{Site},\ \text{Innovation}\big) $$

where **IAQ** stands for *Indoor Air Quality*. The objective is to **minimize the total environmental impact** while maximizing occupant comfort, health, and operational efficiency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Style):** A green building is one which uses less water, optimises energy efficiency, conserves natural resources, generates less waste, and provides healthier spaces for occupants as compared to a conventional building.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Tree vs. The Tent":**
> Imagine two shelters:
>
> 1. **A TREE** — A natural structure that **produces** its own energy (photosynthesis), uses only rainwater, recycles its own leaves into nutrients, and regulates internal temperature passively. It gives back more than it takes.
> 2. **A CONCRETE TENT** — A conventional building that constantly **consumes** electricity from the grid, dumps wastewater, emits CO₂, traps heat, and becomes unusable waste after demolition.
>
> A **Green Building** is the engineering attempt to make our built environment behave more like the **tree** — *self-sufficient, regenerative, and harmonized with nature* — rather than the *tent* which simply extracts resources and expels waste.

---

## 1.3 Core Pillars of a Green Building (The "7 Greens" Framework)

| # | Pillar | Engineering Meaning |
|---|--------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Sustainable Site** | Reduced site disturbance, erosion control, urban heat island mitigation |
| 2 | **Water Efficiency** | Rainwater harvesting, low-flow fixtures, greywater recycling |
| 3 | **Energy & Atmosphere** | Efficient HVAC, renewable integration, reduced CO₂ footprint |
| 4 | **Materials & Resources** | Recycled content, regional sourcing, reduced construction waste |
| 5 | **Indoor Environmental Quality** | Daylighting, low-VOC paints, thermal & acoustic comfort |
| 6 | **Innovation in Design** | New technologies, life-cycle assessment (LCA), BIM integration |
| 7 | **Location & Transportation** | Proximity to public transit, bicycle facilities, reduced parking footprint |

---

## 1.4 Green Building Rating Systems (India & Global Context)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Hot-Question:** Students are frequently asked to compare **GRIHA, LEED, and IGBC** rating systems. Memorize the issuing body and the country of origin.

| Rating System | Full Form | Issuing Body | Origin |
|---------------|-----------|--------------|--------|
| **GRIHA** | Green Rating for Integrated Habitat Assessment | TERI & MNRE | India |
| **IGBC** | Indian Green Building Council | CII | India |
| **LEED** | Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design | USGBC | USA |
| **BREEAM** | Building Research Establishment Environmental Assessment Method | BRE | UK |
| **ECBC** | Energy Conservation Building Code | BEE, Govt. of India | India |
| **EDGE** | Excellence in Design for Greater Efficiencies | IFC (World Bank) | Global |

---

## 1.5 Why Green Buildings? — The Driving Forces

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Global Statistics (commonly cited in KTU viva):**
> - Buildings consume approximately **40%** of global energy.
> - Buildings are responsible for roughly **30%** of global greenhouse-gas emissions.
> - Buildings use about **16%** of the world's freshwater withdrawal.
>
> **Source framing:** These figures are widely cited by UNEP and the IEA, and appear in KTU board exam questions as motivating data.

The driving forces behind green-building adoption are:

1. **Climate Change Mitigation** — Reducing embodied and operational carbon.
2. **Resource Scarcity** — Especially water and fossil-fuel-derived electricity.
3. **Occupant Health & Productivity** — Better IAQ → fewer sick days, higher output.
4. **Regulatory Compliance** — ECBC and NBC 2016 codes.
5. **Economic ROI** — Lifecycle cost savings despite higher initial CAPEX.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Framework: Stages of Green Building Implementation

A green-building project is implemented across **six sequential stages**. The KTU board examiner expects students to know these as an ordered logical flow:

1. **Site Selection & Planning**
2. **Architectural Design (Passive Strategies First)**
3. **Structural & MEP (Mechanical, Electrical, Plumbing) Design**
4. **Construction Phase (Material Selection, Waste Management)**
5. **Commissioning & Certification**
6. **Operations, Maintenance & End-of-Life Deconstruction**

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Passive-First" Rule:** KTU examiners award extra credit when students demonstrate that **passive design** (orientation, shading, daylighting, ventilation) is *always* preferred *before* active systems (HVAC, artificial lighting, mechanical ventilation). This is the **hierarchy of efficiency**.

---

## 2.2 Site Selection Criteria (Analytical Depth)

A green site must satisfy the following engineering criteria:

- **Orientation:** Long axis of the building oriented **East-West** in tropical India to minimize direct solar gain on the longer facades (which receive the most sun).
- **Topography:** Slope ≤ 15% to avoid excessive cut-and-fill.
- **Solar Access:** Avoid shading from neighbouring structures; ensure ≥ 4 hours of direct sunlight on south façade during winter.
- **Flood & Hazard Mitigation:** Avoid 100-year flood plains, landslide-prone zones.
- **Connectivity:** Within **400 m** of public transport (LEED-NC prerequisite).
- **Heat Island Mitigation:** Use high-albedo roofing ($\rho \geq 0.6$) or green roofs.

---

## 2.3 Passive Design Strategies (Engineering Fundamentals)

### 2.3.1 Building Orientation

For a building located at latitude $\phi$ in the Northern Hemisphere, the optimal orientation for the longest façade is **within 15° of due East-West** so that solar radiation strikes the shorter North and South façades obliquely.

$$ \theta_{\text{solar}} \;=\; \phi - \delta \pm \omega $$

where $\theta_{\text{solar}}$ = solar altitude, $\delta$ = declination angle, $\omega$ = hour angle.

### 2.3.2 Window-to-Wall Ratio (WWR)

The optimal WWR balances daylight penetration with heat gain:

$$ \text{WWR} \;=\; \frac{A_{\text{window}}}{A_{\text{total wall}}} \;\leq\; 0.40 \ \text{(recommended range: 0.20 – 0.40)} $$

### 2.3.3 Thermal Mass Concept

Heavy materials (concrete, brick, rammed earth) absorb heat during the day and release it at night, smoothing indoor temperature swings:

$$ Q_{\text{absorbed}} \;=\; m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T $$

where $m$ = mass (kg), $c_p$ = specific heat capacity (kJ/kg·K), $\Delta T$ = temperature change (K).

### 2.3.4 Daylight Factor (DF)

$$ \text{DF} \;=\; \frac{E_{\text{interior}}}{E_{\text{exterior}}} \times 100\ \% $$

A DF between **2% and 5%** is considered adequate for office spaces (CIBSE lighting guide).

### 2.3.5 U-Value (Thermal Transmittance)

The rate of heat transfer through a building element per unit area per unit temperature difference:

$$ U \;=\; \frac{1}{R_{\text{total}}} \;\ [\text{W/m}^2\text{K}] $$

$$ R_{\text{total}} \;=\; R_{\text{si}} \;+\; R_1 \;+\; R_2 \;+\; \cdots \;+\; R_{\text{so}} $$

where $R_i = \dfrac{d_i}{k_i}$ (each layer's thermal resistance = thickness / conductivity), $R_{\text{si}}$ = inner surface resistance, $R_{\text{so}}$ = outer surface resistance.

### 2.3.6 Cooling Load Temperature Difference (CLTD) Method

A simplified method to estimate peak cooling load:

$$ q \;=\; U \cdot A \cdot \text{CLTD}_{\text{corr}} $$

where $q$ = heat gain (W), $A$ = area (m²), $\text{CLTD}_{\text{corr}}$ = corrected cooling load temperature difference (°C or K).

---

## 2.4 Energy Efficiency Strategies

### 2.4.1 Energy Performance Index (EPI)

The most important KPI for green buildings:

$$ \text{EPI} \;=\; \frac{E_{\text{annual total}}}{\text{Conditioned Area}} \;\ \big[\text{kWh/m}^2\text{/year}\big] $$

| Building Type | Conventional EPI | Green Building Target EPI |
|---------------|------------------|---------------------------|
| Office | 180 – 250 | ≤ 100 |
| Hotel | 220 – 300 | ≤ 140 |
| Hospital | 300 – 450 | ≤ 180 |
| School | 120 – 160 | ≤ 60 |
| Residence | 100 – 150 | ≤ 50 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ECBC+ Superbuildings** target an EPI of **≤ 50 kWh/m²/year**. GRIHA 5-star requires ≤ 75 kWh/m²/year.

### 2.4.2 Annual Energy Savings Formula

$$ \Delta E \;=\; E_{\text{baseline}} \;-\; E_{\text{green}} \quad [\text{kWh/year}] $$

$$ \text{\% Savings} \;=\; \frac{\Delta E}{E_{\text{baseline}}} \times 100 $$

### 2.4.3 Simple Payback Period

$$ T_{\text{payback}} \;=\; \frac{C_{\text{extra (CAPEX)}}}{\text{Annual Operational Savings}} \quad [\text{years}] $$

---

## 2.5 Water Efficiency Calculations

### 2.5.1 Rainwater Harvesting Potential

$$ V_{\text{harvest}} \;=\; A_{\text{roof}} \times P \times C_r \times 10^{-3} \quad [\text{m}^3\text{/year}] $$

where $A_{\text{roof}}$ = rooftop catchment area (m²), $P$ = annual rainfall (mm), $C_r$ = runoff coefficient (typically 0.8 – 0.9 for RCC roofs).

### 2.5.2 Water Savings from Low-Flow Fixtures

$$ S_{\text{water}} \;=\; n \times (q_{\text{conventional}} - q_{\text{low-flow}}) \times t \quad [\text{litres/year}] $$

where $n$ = number of occupants, $q$ = flow rate (L/min), $t$ = annual usage time (min).

### 2.5.3 Sewage Treatment Plant (STP) Recycle

$$ V_{\text{recycled}} \;=\; V_{\text{wastewater}} \times \eta_{\text{STP}} $$

where $\eta_{\text{STP}}$ = plant efficiency (typically 90 – 95% for tertiary treatment).

---

## 2.6 Materials & Embodied Energy

> [!NOTE]
> **Embodied Energy (EE)** is the total energy consumed to produce 1 kg of a material from raw extraction to factory gate. KTU often asks for approximate values.

| Material | Embodied Energy (MJ/kg) | CO₂ Footprint (kg CO₂/kg) |
|----------|-------------------------|---------------------------|
| Steel | 25 – 35 | 1.5 – 2.0 |
| Cement (OPC) | 4.0 – 5.5 | 0.8 – 1.0 |
| Fly-ash brick | 1.5 – 2.5 | 0.2 – 0.3 |
| Bamboo | 0.3 – 0.5 | 0.05 – 0.08 |
| Timber (softwood) | 3.0 – 5.0 | 0.4 – 0.6 |
| AAC block | 1.0 – 2.0 | 0.15 – 0.25 |
| Aluminium | 200 – 250 | 11 – 15 |

$$ EE_{\text{total material}} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \times EE_i $$

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet (All Equations at a Glance)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table below contains the complete formula set. CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value or magnitude symbols use `\vert` (not the unescaped pipe `\vert`-symbol) to keep the markdown table syntactically valid.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Window-to-Wall Ratio | $\text{WWR} = A_w / A_{tw}$ | dimensionless | Daylighting / heat-gain limit |
| 2 | U-Value | $U = 1 / R_{\text{total}}$ | W/m²K | Heat-loss estimation |
| 3 | Layer Resistance | $R_i = d_i / k_i$ | m²K/W | Wall-layer calculation |
| 4 | Daylight Factor | $\text{DF} = E_{\text{int}} / E_{\text{ext}}$ | % | Lighting design |
| 5 | Energy Performance Index | $\text{EPI} = E_{\text{annual}} / A_{\text{cond}}$ | kWh/m²·yr | Building efficiency rating |
| 6 | Energy Savings % | $\Delta E\% = (E_b - E_g)/E_b \times 100$ | % | Retrofit evaluation |
| 7 | Simple Payback | $T_p = C_{\text{extra}} / S_{\text{annual}}$ | years | Economic analysis |
| 8 | Rainwater Harvest | $V_h = A_r \cdot P \cdot C_r \cdot 10^{-3}$ | m³/yr | Potable / non-potable use |
| 9 | Water Saving | $S_w = n (q_c - q_{lf}) t$ | L/yr | Fixture retrofits |
| 10 | Thermal Mass Heat | $Q = m c_p \Delta T$ | kJ | Passive cooling |
| 11 | Embodied Energy | $EE_{\text{total}} = \sum m_i EE_i$ | MJ | LCA / material choice |
| 12 | CLTD Cooling Load | $q = U \cdot A \cdot \text{CLTD}_c$ | W | Peak load estimate |
| 13 | Solar Altitude | $\theta = \phi - \delta \pm \omega$ | degrees | Shading / orientation |
| 14 | STP Recycle Volume | $V_r = V_{ww} \cdot \eta$ | L/day | Water reuse |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Green-building principles are deployed by:

- **Architecture & Construction firms** (using BIM + energy modelling tools like EnergyPlus, eQuest, DesignBuilder).
- **Renewable-energy integrators** (solar PV, solar thermal, geothermal heat pumps).
- **Facility management teams** (post-occupancy evaluation, IoT-based monitoring).
- **Urban planners** (district-scale green infrastructure, transit-oriented development).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Energy Performance Index (EPI) Computation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (Typical KTU Style):**
> A green office building in Kerala has a total conditioned floor area of 2000 m². The annual energy consumption data are:
> - Lighting: 30,000 kWh
> - HVAC: 90,000 kWh
> - Plug Loads: 20,000 kWh
> - Total = 140,000 kWh/year
>
> Calculate the EPI and comment on whether the building qualifies for an **ECBC+ Superbuilding** rating (EPI ≤ 50 kWh/m²/year).

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the governing formula.**

$$ \text{EPI} \;=\; \frac{E_{\text{annual total}}}{A_{\text{conditioned}}} $$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values.**

$$ \text{EPI} \;=\; \frac{140{,}000\ \text{kWh/year}}{2000\ \text{m}^2} $$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerical result.**

$$ \text{EPI} \;=\; 70 \quad \big[\text{kWh/m}^2\text{/year}\big] $$

**Step 4 — Compare against ECBC+ threshold.**

$$ 70 \;\text{kWh/m}^2\text{/yr} \;\;>\;\; 50 \;\text{kWh/m}^2\text{/yr} $$

Therefore, **the building does NOT qualify** as an ECBC+ Superbuilding. It is, however, well below the conventional 200 kWh/m²/year, qualifying it as a high-performance green building under **GRIHA 3-star** (≤ 100 kWh/m²/year).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — U-Value Calculation of a Composite Wall

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> Compute the overall U-value of a composite wall from **outside to inside** consisting of:
> 1. 12 mm cement plaster ($k = 0.72$ W/mK)
> 2. 230 mm brick masonry ($k = 0.81$ W/mK)
> 3. 25 mm air gap (cavity, $R = 0.18$ m²K/W)
> 4. 50 mm EPS insulation ($k = 0.038$ W/mK)
> 5. 12 mm gypsum plaster ($k = 0.35$ W/mK)
>
> Use standard surface resistances $R_{si} = 0.13$ m²K/W and $R_{so} = 0.04$ m²K/W.

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write the thermal-resistance equation.**

$$ R_{\text{total}} \;=\; R_{so} \;+\; R_1 \;+\; R_2 \;+\; R_3 \;+\; R_4 \;+\; R_5 \;+\; R_{si} $$

**Step 2 — Calculate each layer's thermal resistance using $R_i = d_i / k_i$.**

For Layer 1 (12 mm cement plaster):

$$ R_1 \;=\; \frac{0.012}{0.72} \;=\; 0.01667 \ \text{m}^2\text{K/W} $$

For Layer 2 (230 mm brick):

$$ R_2 \;=\; \frac{0.230}{0.81} \;=\; 0.28395 \ \text{m}^2\text{K/W} $$

For Layer 3 (25 mm air cavity — given directly):

$$ R_3 \;=\; 0.18 \ \text{m}^2\text{K/W} $$

For Layer 4 (50 mm EPS):

$$ R_4 \;=\; \frac{0.050}{0.038} \;=\; 1.31579 \ \text{m}^2\text{K/W} $$

For Layer 5 (12 mm gypsum):

$$ R_5 \;=\; \frac{0.012}{0.35} \;=\; 0.03429 \ \text{m}^2\text{K/W} $$

**Step 3 — Sum all resistances along with surface resistances.**

$$ R_{\text{total}} \;=\; 0.04 \;+\; 0.01667 \;+\; 0.28395 \;+\; 0.18 \;+\; 1.31579 \;+\; 0.03429 \;+\; 0.13 $$

$$ R_{\text{total}} \;=\; 2.00070 \ \text{m}^2\text{K/W} \;\approx\; 2.00 \ \text{m}^2\text{K/W} $$

**Step 4 — Compute U-value.**

$$ U \;=\; \frac{1}{R_{\text{total}}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2.00070} $$

$$ U \;\approx\; 0.50 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K} $$

**Step 5 — Validate against ECBC requirement for opaque walls in composite climate.**

ECBC mandates $U \leq 0.40$ W/m²K for walls in the composite climate zone. The computed value of **0.50 W/m²K is marginally non-compliant** → recommend increasing EPS thickness to **70 mm** to reduce $U$.

### 3.2.2 Recheck with 70 mm EPS

$$ R_4^{\text{new}} \;=\; \frac{0.070}{0.038} \;=\; 1.84211 \ \text{m}^2\text{K/W} $$

$$ R_{\text{total}}^{\text{new}} \;=\; 0.04 + 0.01667 + 0.28395 + 0.18 + 1.84211 + 0.03429 + 0.13 $$

$$ R_{\text{total}}^{\text{new}} \;=\; 2.52702 \ \text{m}^2\text{K/W} $$

$$ U^{\text{new}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2.52702} \;\approx\; 0.396 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K} $$

This meets the ECBC requirement of $\leq 0.40$ W/m²K.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Rainwater Harvesting Potential

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> A rooftop catchment area in Thiruvananthapuram is 800 m². Average annual rainfall = 1820 mm. Runoff coefficient = 0.85. Calculate the annual harvestable volume.

### 3.3.1 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the harvest formula.**

$$ V_h \;=\; A_r \cdot P \cdot C_r \cdot 10^{-3} $$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$ V_h \;=\; 800 \times 1820 \times 0.85 \times 10^{-3} $$

**Step 3 — Evaluate stepwise.**

$$ V_h \;=\; 800 \times 1820 \;=\; 1{,}456{,}000 \ \text{(L/year)} $$

$$ V_h \;=\; 1{,}456{,}000 \times 0.85 \;=\; 1{,}237{,}600 \ \text{(L/year)} $$

$$ V_h \;=\; 1{,}237{,}600 \times 10^{-3} \;=\; 1237.6 \ \text{m}^3\text{/year} $$

**Step 4 — Convert to useful units.**

$$ V_h \;\approx\; 1.24 \ \text{million litres/year} $$

This is enough to **meet the non-potable demand of approximately 8 to 10 households** (assuming 135 L/day per household for toilet flushing and gardening).

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Simple Payback for Green Retrofit

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> An office building in Kochi replaces conventional lighting (40 W × 200 fittings, 12 hr/day, 300 days/year) with LED lighting (18 W × 200 fittings, same usage). Cost per LED fitting (incl. installation) = ₹600.
>
> Compute: (a) annual energy saved, (b) annual cost saved (@ ₹8/kWh), (c) simple payback period.

### 3.4.1 Step-by-Step Solution

**Part (a) — Annual Energy Saved.**

**Step 1 — Energy of conventional system.**

$$ E_{\text{conv}} \;=\; \frac{200 \times 40 \times 12 \times 300}{1000} \;=\; 28{,}800 \ \text{kWh/year} $$

**Step 2 — Energy of LED system.**

$$ E_{\text{LED}} \;=\; \frac{200 \times 18 \times 12 \times 300}{1000} \;=\; 12{,}960 \ \text{kWh/year} $$

**Step 3 — Energy saved.**

$$ \Delta E \;=\; 28{,}800 \;-\; 12{,}960 \;=\; 15{,}840 \ \text{kWh/year} $$

**Part (b) — Annual Cost Saved.**

$$ \Delta C \;=\; 15{,}840 \times 8 \;=\; \text{₹}1{,}26{,}720 \ \text{per year} $$

**Part (c) — Simple Payback Period.**

**Step 1 — Total capital cost.**

$$ C_{\text{extra}} \;=\; 200 \times 600 \;=\; \text{₹}1{,}20{,}000 $$

**Step 2 — Payback period.**

$$ T_p \;=\; \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{1{,}26{,}720} \;\approx\; 0.947 \ \text{years} \;\approx\; 11.4 \ \text{months} $$

**Conclusion:** Payback under **1 year** — an extremely attractive green-building intervention.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Cooling Load via CLTD Method

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> An exterior wall has area 25 m², $U = 0.5$ W/m²K. The corrected CLTD for the design hour = 18 K. Compute the cooling load through this wall.

### 3.5.1 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Formula.**

$$ q \;=\; U \cdot A \cdot \text{CLTD}_c $$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$ q \;=\; 0.5 \times 25 \times 18 $$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**

$$ q \;=\; 225 \ \text{W} $$

**Interpretation:** The wall contributes **225 W** to the cooling load. Through repetition across walls, roof, windows, and internal gains, the total cooling load is summed to size the HVAC equipment.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Evaluation of Green Strategies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strategy Comparison Table (Frequently asked in 14-mark questions):**

| Strategy | Energy Impact | Cost Impact | Implementation Difficulty | Payback |
|----------|---------------|-------------|---------------------------|---------|
| Insulation (wall + roof) | High (40–60% reduction) | Medium | Low | 4 – 7 yr |
| Cool roof (high-albedo paint) | Medium (10–20%) | Low | Very Low | 1 – 3 yr |
| Double-glazed windows | Medium (15–25%) | High | Medium | 8 – 12 yr |
| LED retrofits | Medium (40–60%) | Low | Low | < 1.5 yr |
| Solar PV (rooftop 5 kW) | Very High | Very High | High | 5 – 8 yr |
| Rainwater harvesting | Indirect | Medium | Medium | 3 – 6 yr |
| Natural ventilation design | High (20–50%) | Low | Low (design-stage) | < 1 yr |
| Greywater recycling | Indirect | High | High | 6 – 10 yr |

---

## 3.7 Step-by-Step Derivation: Embodied Carbon of a Concrete Slab

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A 150 mm RCC slab is laid in a 10 m × 10 m room. Find the embodied carbon.

**Step 1 — Volume of slab.**

$$ V \;=\; 10 \times 10 \times 0.150 \;=\; 15 \ \text{m}^3 $$

**Step 2 — Density of RCC.**

$$ \rho_{\text{RCC}} \;\approx\; 2500 \ \text{kg/m}^3 $$

**Step 3 — Mass of slab.**

$$ m \;=\; V \times \rho \;=\; 15 \times 2500 \;=\; 37{,}500 \ \text{kg} $$

**Step 4 — Embodied carbon coefficient for concrete (assume blended cement mix).**

$$ \text{EC}_\text{coeff} \;\approx\; 0.15 \ \text{kg CO}_2\text{/kg} $$

**Step 5 — Total embodied carbon.**

$$ \text{EC}_{\text{total}} \;=\; 37{,}500 \times 0.15 \;=\; 5{,}625 \ \text{kg CO}_2 \;\approx\; 5.63 \ \text{tCO}_2 $$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**

To offset this operational lifetime, a **3 kW solar PV system** generating ~4500 kWh/year would, at Kerala's grid emission factor of **~0.7 kg CO₂/kWh**, offset roughly **3150 kg CO₂/year**, leading to a carbon-payback of:

$$ T_{\text{carbon payback}} \;=\; \frac{5625}{3150} \;\approx\; 1.79 \ \text{years} $$

---

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 continuous evaluation pattern, **Part A** questions test direct recall and one-step application (Bloom's Level: Remember / Understand).

---

### Q1. Define a Green Building. List any FOUR features of a green building.

`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]`

**Model Answer:**

A **Green Building** is one which is designed, constructed and operated in an environmentally responsible manner, using resources efficiently throughout its life cycle.

Four features:

1. **Energy efficiency** — Reduced EPI through passive design and renewable integration.
2. **Water conservation** — Rainwater harvesting, low-flow fixtures, STP recycle.
3. **Sustainable materials** — Use of fly-ash bricks, low-VOC paints, recycled steel.
4. **Indoor Environmental Quality (IEQ)** — Adequate daylighting (DF 2–5%), thermal comfort, low indoor pollutants.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for features)*

---

### Q2. Differentiate between GRIHA and LEED rating systems.

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | GRIHA | LEED |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Full Form | Green Rating for Integrated Habitat Assessment | Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design |
| Country | India | USA |
| Issuing Body | TERI & MNRE | USGBC |
| Levels | 1 to 5 stars | Certified, Silver, Gold, Platinum |
| Climate Adaptation | Indigenous Indian climatic zones | Generic global benchmarks |
| Focus | Energy + water + materials + site | Energy + environment + IAQ |

*(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 End Semester Evaluation (ESE) rules, the examiner provides a choice between **Question A** and **Question B**. Both questions carry 14 marks divided into two 7-mark sub-parts each. Below is a complete model answer for both.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Define Energy Performance Index (EPI). With a suitable example, explain how EPI is used to classify a green building under GRIHA and ECBC+.  **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A green hostel building in Thrissur has the following energy consumption pattern:
- Lighting: 25,000 kWh/year
- Fans: 35,000 kWh/year
- HVAC: 60,000 kWh/year
- Plug loads: 15,000 kWh/year
- Conditioned area = 1500 m²

Calculate the EPI and state the certification eligibility under GRIHA and ECBC+.  **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL ANSWER — Q(a)

**Step 1 — Definition:**

Energy Performance Index is the ratio of annual energy consumed by a building to its conditioned (air-conditioned) floor area.

$$ \text{EPI} \;=\; \frac{E_{\text{annual}}\ [\text{kWh}]}{A_{\text{conditioned}}\ [\text{m}^2]} \quad \big[\text{kWh/m}^2\text{/year}\big] $$

**Step 2 — Significance:**

EPI quantifies the energy efficiency of a building. The **lower** the EPI, the **more efficient** the building.

**Step 3 — Classification thresholds (typical values):**

| Rating Body | Threshold | Class |
|-------------|-----------|-------|
| GRIHA 1-star | ≤ 175 kWh/m²/yr | Basic |
| GRIHA 3-star | ≤ 100 kWh/m²/yr | Good |
| GRIHA 5-star | ≤ 50 kWh/m²/yr | Excellent |
| ECBC | ≤ 120 kWh/m²/yr | Compliant |
| ECBC+ Superbuilding | ≤ 50 kWh/m²/yr | High-performance |

**Step 4 — Example:** A 1000 m² office using 80,000 kWh/year has EPI = 80 kWh/m²/yr, qualifying for GRIHA 3-star but not 5-star.

*(7 marks distributed: 2 for formula, 2 for thresholds, 2 for classification logic, 1 for example)*

---

#### MODEL ANSWER — Q(b)

**Step 1 — Total annual energy.**

$$ E_{\text{total}} \;=\; 25{,}000 \;+\; 35{,}000 \;+\; 60{,}000 \;+\; 15{,}000 $$

$$ E_{\text{total}} \;=\; 135{,}000 \ \text{kWh/year} $$

**Step 2 — Compute EPI.**

$$ \text{EPI} \;=\; \frac{135{,}000}{1500} \;=\; 90 \ \text{kWh/m}^2\text{/year} $$

**Step 3 — Compare with thresholds.**

- **GRIHA:** 90 ≤ 100 → qualifies for **GRIHA 3-star**.
- **ECBC:** 90 ≤ 120 → qualifies for **ECBC compliant**, but not ECBC+ Superbuilding.

*(7 marks distributed: 2 for total energy, 2 for EPI formula application, 3 for threshold comparison)*

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** What is a green building? Explain the SIX major categories (or "pillars") evaluated by green-building rating systems.  **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A 250 m² RCC roof is being evaluated for a green-roof installation. Annual rainfall = 2000 mm; runoff coefficient = 0.85. If harvested water is to be used only for toilet flushing at 45 L/person/day with 20 occupants:
- (i) Compute annual harvestable volume.
- (ii) Check whether harvested water is sufficient for the full year.  **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL ANSWER — Q(a)

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):**

A **Green Building** is a structure planned, designed, constructed and operated to minimise negative environmental impacts, optimise resource use (energy, water, materials), and provide healthy indoor environments for occupants across its entire life cycle.

**Step 2 — Six Pillars (5 marks):**

1. **Sustainable Site Planning** — Site selection, brownfield redevelopment, reducing heat-island effect.
2. **Water Efficiency** — Rainwater harvesting, low-flow fixtures, greywater reuse.
3. **Energy & Atmosphere** — High-EPI reduction, renewable energy, efficient HVAC.
4. **Materials & Resources** — Recycled content, regional sourcing, certified timber.
5. **Indoor Environmental Quality** — Daylighting, low-VOC materials, thermal comfort.
6. **Innovation & Design Process** — New technologies, exemplary performance, LEED AP.

*(7 marks: 2 definition + 5 pillars at 1 mark each with a one-line note)*

---

#### MODEL ANSWER — Q(b)

**Part (i) — Annual Harvestable Volume (4 marks).**

**Step 1 — Apply formula.**

$$ V_h \;=\; A_r \cdot P \cdot C_r \cdot 10^{-3} $$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$ V_h \;=\; 250 \times 2000 \times 0.85 \times 10^{-3} $$

**Step 3 — Evaluate stepwise.**

$$ V_h \;=\; 250 \times 2000 \;=\; 500{,}000 \ \text{L/year} $$

$$ V_h \;=\; 500{,}000 \times 0.85 \;=\; 425{,}000 \ \text{L/year} $$

**Step 4 — Convert.**

$$ V_h \;=\; 425{,}000 \times 10^{-3} \;=\; 425 \ \text{m}^3\text{/year} $$

**Part (ii) — Sufficiency Check (3 marks).**

**Step 1 — Annual flushing demand.**

$$ V_{\text{demand}} \;=\; 20 \times 45 \times 365 $$

**Step 2 — Evaluate.**

$$ V_{\text{demand}} \;=\; 900 \times 365 \;=\; 328{,}500 \ \text{L/year} $$

$$ V_{\text{demand}} \;=\; 328.5 \ \text{m}^3\text{/year} $$

**Step 3 — Compare.**

$$ V_h \;=\; 425 \ \text{m}^3\text{/yr} \;\;>\;\; V_{\text{demand}} \;=\; 328.5 \ \text{m}^3\text{/yr} $$

**Step 4 — Surplus.**

$$ \text{Surplus} \;=\; 425 \;-\; 328.5 \;=\; 96.5 \ \text{m}^3\text{/year} $$

**Conclusion:** Harvested water is **sufficient** for the entire flushing demand with a surplus of 96.5 m³/year, which can be redirected to landscape irrigation.

*(7 marks: 4 for part (i), 3 for part (ii))*

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Green-Building Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** in rainwater-harvest problems: rainfall is in mm, area in m², and the formula requires the $10^{-3}$ multiplier. Missing this loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing conditioned vs total built-up area** in EPI calculations — always divide by *conditioned* area only.
> 3. **Mixing up $k$ (W/mK) and $R$ (m²K/W)** — remember that $U = 1/R$ and $R_i = d_i / k_i$.
> 4. **Omitting surface resistances $R_{si}$ and $R_{so}$** in U-value problems — both are mandatory in accurate computation.
> 5. **Writing GRIHA levels incorrectly** — they go **1 → 5 stars** (NOT "certified/silver/gold" like LEED).
> 6. **Skipping the verbal interpretation** after a numerical answer — KTU awards 1–2 marks for the conclusion statement.
> 7. **In payback problems, students often quote months without unit conversion** — always state "X months" or "Y years", not both ambiguously.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (memorize before entering exam hall):**

### Definition & Scope
- Green Building = environmentally responsible + resource-efficient **whole-life-cycle** design.
- Differentiate **Green Building** vs **Sustainable Building** vs **Net-Zero Building**.

### The 6 Pillars (LEED-IGBC framework)
1. Sustainable Sites
2. Water Efficiency
3. Energy & Atmosphere
4. Materials & Resources
5. Indoor Environmental Quality
6. Innovation & Design Process

### Key Rating Systems (must memorize)
- **India:** GRIHA (TERI-MNRE), IGBC (CII), ECBC (BEE).
- **Global:** LEED (USA), BREEAM (UK), EDGE (IFC).

### Critical Numerical Formulas
- $\text{WWR} = A_w / A_{tw}$ → keep 0.20–0.40.
- $R_i = d_i / k_i$ → sum all layers + $R_{si}$ + $R_{so}$.
- $U = 1 / R_{\text{total}}$ → check ECBC thresholds.
- $\text{EPI} = E_{\text{annual}} / A_{\text{cond}}$ → threshold = 50–100 kWh/m²/yr for top ratings.
- $V_h = A_r \cdot P \cdot C_r \cdot 10^{-3}$ → m³/year.
- $T_p = C_{\text{extra}} / S_{\text{annual}}$ → payback in years.

### Passive Design (always before active)
- Orientation E-W (in India).
- WWR 0.20–0.40.
- Thermal mass + night ventilation.
- Daylight Factor 2–5%.

### Embodied Energy Hierarchy (remember approximate values)
- Aluminium >> Steel > Cement > Brick > AAC > Timber > Bamboo.

### Green-Building Materials
- **Fly-ash bricks** (instead of clay bricks).
- **Low-VOC paints** (instead of conventional paints).
- **AAC blocks** (lightweight, low EE).
- **Recycled steel** (rebar from scrap).
- **Bamboo / FSC-certified timber**.

### Water Hierarchy
- Rainwater → Greywater recycle → Blackwater treatment → Recharge pit.

### Must-Quote Targets (viva + exam)
- ECBC+ Superbuilding EPI ≤ 50.
- GRIHA 5-star EPI ≤ 50.
- DF 2–5%.
- WWR 0.20–0.40.
- Rainwater runoff coefficient 0.80–0.90.

### Common Numerical Conversions
- 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ.
- 1 tonne of refrigeration (TR) = 3.517 kW.
- $10^{-3}$ conversion in rain-harvest formula (mm × m² → L).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles, Aspects, Certification Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT631 - RENEWABLE ENERGY AND GREEN TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Principles, Aspects, Certification Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Green Technology (GT)** is an umbrella discipline that integrates the engineering, environmental, economic, and social sciences to design products, processes, and systems that **conserve natural resources, minimize ecological burden, and promote sustainable development** across the entire product/service life cycle.

The three pillars of Green Technology, as per KTU 2024 NEP-aligned syllabus, are:
1. **Principles** – The philosophical and scientific foundations guiding green innovation.
2. **Aspects** – The environmental, social, economic, and technical dimensions where an organization interacts with the ecosystem.
3. **Certification Systems** – Third-party verified, internationally recognized standards that benchmark and validate green performance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU specifically tests the student's ability to *distinguish* between a Principle (a guiding rule), an Aspect (an element of interaction with the environment), and an Impact (any change to the environment, positive or negative). Confusing these three is a **guaranteed 2-mark deduction** in Part A questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are designing a **house**:
- **Principles** are like the **architect's philosophy**: "A house should breathe with nature, harvest its own energy, and waste nothing." These are abstract guiding ideas.
- **Aspects** are the **specific touchpoints** where the house interacts with nature: water usage, electricity consumption, waste generation, rainwater runoff, indoor air quality.
- **Certification Systems** are like the **government-issued rating certificate** (e.g., Green Rating for Integrated Habitat Assessment - **GRIHA**) that the architect attaches to the house to *prove* to buyers that it follows the philosophy and addresses every aspect correctly.

Just as a doctor follows the **Hippocratic principle** ("do no harm"), an engineer certified under **ISO 14001** follows the principle of *"do not harm the environment."*

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Carbon Footprint:** Measured in **kg CO₂-equivalent (kg CO₂e)**.
- **Embodied Energy:** Measured in **MJ (Megajoules)** or **kWh**.
- **Life Cycle:** Quantified using **LCA (Life Cycle Assessment)** under **ISO 14040:2006** & **ISO 14044:2006**.
- **Reference Standard for Sustainability Reporting:** **GRI (Global Reporting Initiative) Standards**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box:**
> **Aspect** = Element of an organization's activities, products, or services that **can interact** with the environment.
> **Impact** = Any **change** to the environment, whether adverse or beneficial, wholly or partially resulting from the organization's aspects.
> **Principle** = A fundamental **rule, law, or doctrine** on which green technology is founded.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Twelve Principles of Green Technology (Green Chemistry / Engineering Foundation)

The KTU syllabus draws heavily from **Anastas and Warner's 12 Principles of Green Chemistry** and the **12 Principles of Green Engineering**. These are high-yield for 3-mark definitions and 7-mark descriptive questions.

| # | Principle | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|-----------|---------------------------|
| 1 | **Prevention** | It is better to prevent waste than to treat or clean up waste after it is formed. |
| 2 | **Atom Economy** | Design synthetic methods to maximize the incorporation of all materials used. |
| 3 | **Less Hazardous Syntheses** | Design syntheses to use and generate substances with little/no toxicity. |
| 4 | **Designing Safer Chemicals** | Chemical products must be designed to be effective with minimal toxicity. |
| 5 | **Safer Solvents & Auxiliaries** | Avoid solvents/separation agents wherever possible. |
| 6 | **Design for Energy Efficiency** | Recognize energy as a key economic & environmental factor; run at ambient T & P. |
| 7 | **Use of Renewable Feedstocks** | Use raw materials from renewable sources. |
| 8 | **Reduce Derivatives** | Unnecessary derivatization (blocking groups, protection/deprotection) should be minimized. |
| 9 | **Catalysis** | Catalytic reagents are superior to stoichiometric ones. |
| 10 | **Design for Degradation** | Products designed so they break down to innocuous degradation products. |
| 11 | **Real-time Analysis for Pollution Prevention** | Analytical methodologies for real-time monitoring. |
| 12 | **Inherently Safer Chemistry for Accident Prevention** | Choose substances to minimize potential for chemical accidents. |

### 2.2 Aspects of Green Technology (The Four Sustainability Dimensions)

The **Aspects** are the touchpoints where technology interfaces with the planet. KTU categorizes them into four dimensions:

- **A. Environmental Aspects**
  - Resource depletion (water, fossil fuels, minerals).
  - Greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions.
  - Solid/hazardous waste generation.
  - Biodiversity loss and deforestation.
  - Effluent discharge and water/soil contamination.

- **B. Economic Aspects**
  - Life Cycle Costing (**LCC**).
  - Return on Investment (**ROI**) for green retrofits.
  - Carbon credit revenue (under **CCTS – Carbon Credit Trading Scheme**, India).
  - Green financing and ESG-linked loans.

- **C. Social Aspects**
  - Public health improvement.
  - Community displacement (e.g., large hydropower).
  - Green job creation.
  - Energy access and equity (UN SDG 7).

- **D. Technical Aspects**
  - Energy efficiency & process optimization.
  - Renewable energy integration.
  - Material substitution (e.g., bio-plastics).
  - End-of-Life (EoL) recovery & recyclability.

### 2.4 ISO 14001:2015 – The EMS Backbone (High-Yield 14-Mark Topic)

**ISO 14001:2015** uses the **PDCA Cycle (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** framework, also called the **"High-Level Structure (HLS)"** shared with ISO 9001.

- **Plan:** Identify environmental aspects, legal obligations, risks/opportunities, set objectives.
- **Do:** Implement operational controls, training, communication, emergency preparedness.
- **Check:** Monitor, measure, analyze, evaluate compliance; conduct internal audits.
- **Act:** Management review, corrective action, continual improvement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Clause Structure (must memorize):** ISO 14001:2015 contains **10 clauses (Clauses 1–10)**. Clauses 1–3 are normative; Clauses 4–10 are the **High-Level Structure (HLS)**. Clause 6.1.2 is **"Environmental Aspects"** – the most heavily tested sub-clause.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formula Sheet for Sustainability Metrics**

| Metric | Formula / Expression | Unit | Purpose |
|--------|----------------------|------|---------|
| **Carbon Footprint** | $CF = \sum_{i} A_i \times EF_i$ | kg CO₂e | Total GHG emissions |
| **Energy Intensity** | $EI = \dfrac{E_{total}}{P_{output}}$ | kWh/unit | Energy per product |
| **Water Intensity** | $WI = \dfrac{W_{total}}{P_{output}}$ | m³/unit | Water per product |
| **Waste Diversion Rate** | $WDR = \dfrac{W_{recycled} + W_{reused}}{W_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Circularity |
| **Material Circularity Indicator (MCI)** | $MCI = 1 - \dfrac{LFI \times F_M}{2}$ where LFI is Linear Flow Index | 0–1 | Material loop closure |
| **Payback Period** | $T_{payback} = \dfrac{C_{initial}}{S_{annual}}$ | years | Green investment ROI |
| **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | Currency | Lifecycle profitability |
| **Renewable Energy Share** | $RES = \dfrac{E_{RE}}{E_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Greenness of supply |
| **Carbon Credit Value** | $V_{credit} = CC_{tonnes} \times P_{market}$ | Currency | Carbon market |
| **Embodied Energy** | $EE = \sum m_i \times e_i$ | MJ | Cradle-to-gate energy |

*Where:* $A_i$ = activity data; $EF_i$ = emission factor; $P_{output}$ = production output; $CF_t$ = cash flow in year *t*; $r$ = discount rate; $CC_{tonnes}$ = tonnes of CO₂e offset.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Construction Engineers:** Use LEED/GRIHA ratings to design net-zero buildings.
- **Chemical Engineers:** Apply Green Chemistry principles to reduce E-factor.
- **Power Engineers:** Apply ISO 50001 to optimize plant energy performance and reduce auxiliary consumption.
- **Manufacturing Engineers:** Use LCA under ISO 14040 to quantify product-level environmental impact.
- **Policy Planners:** Use GHG Protocol & CDP for national inventory reporting to UNFCCC.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Material Circularity Indicator (MCI) – Used in Certification

The MCI, formalized by the **Ellen MacArthur Foundation** and adopted by **Cradle to Cradle**, measures how restorative a material flow is.

**Step 1 – Define the Linear Flow Index (LFI):**

$$LFI = \dfrac{V_{virgin} + W_{waste}}{2M}$$

where $V_{virgin}$ = mass of virgin material input, $W_{waste}$ = mass of material going to landfill, and $M$ = total mass of material used.

**Step 2 – Define the Mass Utility Factor (F<sub>M</sub>):**

$$F_M = \dfrac{0.9}{L} \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \dfrac{L_i}{L_{av}} \cdot U_i \right)$$

where $L_i$ = number of cycles for material *i*, $L_{av}$ = average lifetime, $U_i$ = fraction of utility retained per cycle, $L$ = lifespan of the product.

**Step 3 – Compute MCI:**

$$MCI = 1 - LFI \cdot F_M$$

**Step 4 – Interpretation:**
- $MCI = 0$ → fully linear (take-make-waste).
- $MCI = 1$ → fully circular (perfect reuse).

> **Textual Logic:** The indicator scales linearly between the worst-case (0) and the fully restorative circular flow (1). A higher MCI is preferred in **C2C** and **EU Taxonomy** assessments.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Payback Period for a Green Retrofit

Consider a factory installing a **500 kWp rooftop solar PV** system.

**Given:**
- Initial cost $C_0 = \text{₹}35{,}00{,}000$
- Annual electricity savings $S_a = \text{₹}7{,}00{,}000$
- Annual maintenance $M_a = \text{₹}50{,}000$
- Annual degradation of panels $\delta = 0.5\%$ per year
- Discount rate $r = 8\%$

**Step 1 – Net Annual Savings (Year 1):**

$$NS_1 = S_a - M_a = 7{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}000 = 6{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 2 – Apply degradation. Year-*t* savings:**

$$NS_t = NS_1 \cdot (1 - \delta)^{t-1} = 6{,}50{,}000 \cdot (0.995)^{t-1}$$

**Step 3 – Cumulative cash flow:**

$$C_{cum}(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \dfrac{NS_k}{(1+r)^{k}}$$

**Step 4 – Simple (undiscounted) payback:**

$$T_{payback} = \dfrac{C_0}{NS_1} = \dfrac{35{,}00{,}000}{6{,}50{,}000} = 5.385 \text{ years}$$

**Step 5 – Discounted payback (solving iteratively):**
Using NPV formulation, we set $\sum_{t=1}^{T} \dfrac{NS_t}{(1.08)^t} = C_0$.

Trial $T=6$ years:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{6} \dfrac{6{,}50{,}000 \cdot (0.995)^{t-1}}{(1.08)^t} \approx 28{,}40{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

Trial $T=7$ years:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{7} \dfrac{6{,}50{,}000 \cdot (0.995)^{t-1}}{(1.08)^t} \approx 31{,}45{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

Trial $T=8$ years:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{8} \dfrac{6{,}50{,}000 \cdot (0.995)^{t-1}}{(1.08)^t} \approx 34{,}10{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

Trial $T=9$ years:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{9} \dfrac{6{,}50{,}000 \cdot (0.995)^{t-1}}{(1.08)^t} \approx 36{,}40{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Conclusion:** Discounted payback lies between **8 and 9 years**, closer to **8.7 years**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Carbon Footprint Aggregation

A small manufacturing unit has three emission sources:

| Source | Activity (A) | Unit | Emission Factor (EF) |
|--------|--------------|------|----------------------|
| Diesel boiler | 10,000 | litres | 2.68 kg CO₂e/L |
| Grid electricity | 50,000 | kWh | 0.82 kg CO₂e/kWh |
| LPG usage | 1,500 | kg | 2.94 kg CO₂e/kg |

**Step 1 – Source-wise emission:**

$$E_{diesel} = 10{,}000 \times 2.68 = 26{,}800 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}$$

$$E_{grid} = 50{,}000 \times 0.82 = 41{,}000 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}$$

$$E_{LPG} = 1{,}500 \times 2.94 = 4{,}410 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}$$

**Step 2 – Total carbon footprint:**

$$CF_{total} = \sum_i A_i \times EF_i = 26{,}800 + 41{,}000 + 4{,}410 = 72{,}210 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}$$

**Step 3 – Normalized footprint (if annual production = 50,000 units):**

$$CF_{per\_unit} = \dfrac{72{,}210}{50{,}000} = 1.4442 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e/unit}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selecting the Right Certification

| Industry / Asset | Recommended Certification(s) | Justification |
|------------------|------------------------------|---------------|
| Manufacturing Plant | ISO 14001 + ISO 50001 | EMS + EnMS dual framework |
| Commercial Building | LEED / GRIHA / BREEAM | Building energy & water |
| Solar Farm | ISO 14001 + Carbon Trust Standard | LCA + GHG verification |
| Electronics Manufacturer | EPEAT + RoHS | Material restriction |
| FMCG / Packaging | Cradle to Cradle + FSC | Recyclability, sustainable inputs |
| Chemical Plant | ISO 14001 + Responsible Care | Hazard + aspect management |
| Data Center | LEED + Energy Star | Energy intensity |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "Environmental Aspect" as used in ISO 14001:2015. Differentiate it from "Environmental Impact".*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Environmental Aspect (1 mark):** An element of an organization's activities, products, or services that interacts or can interact with the environment (Clause 3.2.1, ISO 14001:2015). Example: fuel consumption, wastewater discharge.
- **Environmental Impact (1 mark):** Any change to the environment, whether adverse or beneficial, wholly or partially resulting from an organization's environmental aspects (Clause 3.2.2). Example: global warming, eutrophication.
- **Differentiation (1 mark):** The aspect is the *cause* (input/process), whereas the impact is the *effect* (consequence in the environment). One aspect can lead to multiple impacts.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Certification | Distinguishing Feature |
|---------------|------------------------|
| **ISO 14001:2015** | International EMS standard based on PDCA, applicable to any industry. |
| **LEED** | U.S.-origin green building rating with Platinum/Gold/Silver tiers. |
| **GRIHA** | Indigenous Indian rating tailored to tropical climate and local materials. |

*(Any three from the certification table in Section 2.3 accepted; mark awarded for: name + focus area.)*
