### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) – ESE Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks): ISO 14001:2015 in Depth** `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]`

**(a) Explain the Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) framework of ISO 14001:2015 with a neat block diagram. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The PDCA cycle provides a structured approach for implementing, maintaining, and continually improving an **Environmental Management System (EMS)**. Each phase corresponds to specific clauses of ISO 14001:2015.

- **PLAN (Clauses 4, 5, 6, 7) – (3 marks)**
  - Establish environmental policy (Clause 5.2).
  - Identify environmental aspects and impacts (Clause 6.1.2).
  - Determine legal and other requirements (Clause 6.1.3).
  - Set measurable objectives and plan actions to achieve them (Clause 6.2).

- **DO (Clause 8) – (1.5 marks)**
  - Implement operational controls, documented information, training (Clause 7.2), communication (Clause 7.4), and emergency preparedness & response (Clause 8.2).

- **CHECK (Clause 9) – (1.5 marks)**
  - Monitor, measure, analyze, and evaluate environmental performance (9.1).
  - Evaluate compliance with legal obligations (9.1.2).
  - Conduct internal audit (9.2) and management review (9.3).

- **ACT (Clause 10) – (1 mark)**
  - Take corrective action for non-conformities (10.2).
  - Drive continual improvement of the EMS (10.3).

**Block Diagram (to be drawn in answer book):**

```
   ┌─────── PLAN ───────┐
   │                    │
   ↓                    │
   DO → CHECK → ACT ────┘
```

*(The arrows form a closed loop; output of ACT feeds back to PLAN.)*

---

**(b) Describe the procedure for identifying significant environmental aspects in an industry. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The procedure (per Clause 6.1.2) consists of **five sequential steps**:

1. **Step 1 – Aspect Identification (1.5 marks):** Identify all environmental aspects arising from activities, products, and services under normal, abnormal, and emergency conditions. Example: emissions, effluents, waste, raw material use, noise, land use.

2. **Step 2 – Impact Determination (1.5 marks):** For each aspect, identify the actual or potential environmental impact (positive or negative). Example: aspect = coal combustion → impact = CO₂ emission → climate change.

3. **Step 3 – Significance Criteria Setting (1.5 marks):** Define criteria such as:
   - Scale (local/regional/global)
   - Severity (reversibility, magnitude)
   - Frequency/duration of occurrence
   - Regulatory/legal compliance
   - Concern of interested parties

4. **Step 4 – Scoring/Ranking (1.5 marks):** Apply a numerical scoring system (e.g., A × B × C, where A = scale, B = severity, C = frequency). Aspects above a threshold are classified as **Significant Environmental Aspects (SEAs)**.

5. **Step 5 – Operational Control (1 mark):** Establish objectives, targets, KPIs, and operational controls (Clause 8.1) for the SEAs. Monitor them in the CHECK phase.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Do NOT confuse Aspect with Impact.** Many students write "waste generation is an impact" – this loses **2 marks** immediately.
> - You **must** mention the three operating conditions: **normal, abnormal, emergency** – omitting any one is a 1-mark deduction.
> - Drawing the **block diagram of PDCA is compulsory** in Part (a); without it, expect 1–2 marks lost even if the text is correct.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): Principles & Certification Comparison** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Discuss the Twelve Principles of Green Chemistry with at least six examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The 12 Principles of Green Chemistry, formulated by **Anastas and Warner (1998)**, provide a framework for designing chemical products and processes that reduce or eliminate the use and generation of hazardous substances.

**Six Key Principles with Examples:**

1. **Prevention of Waste (1 mark):** It is better to prevent waste than to treat it after formation. *Example:* Designing a catalytic reaction that gives 99% yield vs. a stoichiometric one with 60% yield, eliminating by-products.

2. **Atom Economy (1 mark):** Maximize incorporation of all starting materials into the final product. *Example:* The Diels-Alder cycloaddition has 100% atom economy (all atoms from reactants end up in the product), while a substitution reaction may produce NaCl as waste.

3. **Less Hazardous Chemical Synthesis (1 mark):** Use and generate substances of low toxicity. *Example:* Replacing phosgene-based polycarbonate synthesis with diphenyl carbonate (transesterification).

4. **Designing Safer Chemicals (1 mark):** Chemical products must be effective with minimal toxicity. *Example:* Designing biodegradable surfactants that retain cleaning power but degrade in 28 days (OECD 301B).

5. **Use of Renewable Feedstocks (1 mark):** Prefer renewable raw materials. *Example:* Producing bio-polyethylene from sugarcane ethanol (Braskem, Brazil) instead of fossil naphtha.

6. **Catalysis (1 mark):** Catalytic reagents are superior to stoichiometric. *Example:* Using **Ziegler-Natta catalysts** in polyethylene polymerization instead of free-radical initiators, achieving 10× higher efficiency.

*(For a full 7-mark answer, include all 12 principles; 6 with examples = 6 marks + 1 mark for any additional principle.)*

---

**(b) Compare LEED and GRIHA green building certification systems in terms of scope, climate adaptation, rating levels, and applicability in India. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **LEED** | **GRIHA** |
|-----------|----------|-----------|
| **Issuing Body** | U.S. Green Building Council (USGBC) | TERI & Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE), Govt. of India |
| **Year** | 1998 (LEED v4 in 2013) | 2007 |
| **Scope** | New construction, existing buildings, interiors, communities, cities | New buildings, existing buildings, small buildings, transit stations |
| **Rating Levels** | Certified, Silver, Gold, Platinum | 1 Star (self-assessment) to 5 Star (best-in-class) |
| **Climate Adaptation** | Generic; designed for temperate U.S. climate; requires modifications for tropics | **Tropical, monsoon, hot-dry** climate zones explicitly considered |
| **Energy Benchmark** | Uses ASHRAE 90.1 baseline | Uses ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code, India) baseline |
| **Local Materials** | Not prioritized | **Awards points for indigenous materials** (laterite, mud, fly-ash brick) |
| **Water Management** | Mandatory for all climates | Mandatory; emphasizes rainwater harvesting (critical for Indian monsoons) |
| **Applicability in India** | Widely used (e.g., CII-Godrej GBC, Suzlon One Earth); many projects | Mandatory for **central government & PSUs** (since 2007, Ministry of Environment); also adopted by Kerala & other states |
| **Cost of Certification** | Higher (USD-denominated fees) | Lower (INR-denominated); more accessible |
| **Strength** | Global recognition; large ecosystem | Indian context-specific; mandated by Govt. of India |

**Conclusion:** LEED has stronger global brand value and a mature ecosystem, but GRIHA is **technically better suited** for Indian climatic, regulatory, and material contexts. **(1 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Do **not** write only the names of the certifications – you must compare on **at least four parameters** to score full 7 marks.
> - Mention **ECBC** baseline when discussing GRIHA – this is a frequently missed Kerala-context point worth 1 mark.
> - Avoid stating "LEED is Indian" – LEED is **U.S.-origin**; this is a common error.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Principle** = guiding doctrine (philosophy); **Aspect** = activity element interacting with environment; **Impact** = resulting change in environment. Master the trio.
- [x] The **12 Principles of Green Chemistry** (Anastas & Warner, 1998) and **12 Principles of Green Engineering** are most frequently asked in Part A.
- [x] **Four Aspects dimensions:** Environmental, Economic, Social, Technical (the **quadruple bottom line**).
- [x] **ISO 14001:2015** uses the **PDCA cycle**; memorize clauses **4–10** and especially **Clause 6.1.2 (Environmental Aspects)**.
- [x] **ISO 50001:2018** is for **Energy Management System** (EnMS), also PDCA-based.
- [x] **LEED** = USGBC (USA, 1998); **GRIHA** = TERI + MNRE (India, 2007); **BREEAM** = BRE (UK, 1990).
- [x] **GRIHA** is mandated for central government and PSU buildings in India and uses **ECBC** as energy baseline.
- [x] **Material Circularity Indicator (MCI):** $MCI = 1 - LFI \cdot F_M$; range 0 (linear) to 1 (circular).
- [x] **Carbon Footprint:** $CF = \sum A_i \times EF_i$, measured in **kg CO₂e**.
- [x] **Simple Payback:** $T_{payback} = C_0 / NS_1$; **Discounted Payback** requires solving NPV iteratively.
- [x] **Three operating conditions** for aspect identification: **normal, abnormal, emergency** – never omit.
- [x] **Cradle to Cradle (C2C)** focuses on material health, renewable energy, water stewardship, social fairness, and circularity.
- [x] **GHG Protocol** scopes: Scope 1 (direct), Scope 2 (indirect – purchased energy), Scope 3 (value chain).
- [x] **CDP (Carbon Disclosure Project)** is the disclosure mechanism feeding investor ESG decisions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Green Rating for Integrated Habitat Assessment (GRIHA) etc.
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT631 - RENEWABLE ENERGY AND GREEN TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Green Rating for Integrated Habitat Assessment (GRIHA) etc.

# 🌱 Green Rating for Integrated Habitat Assessment (GRIHA) and Allied Green Rating Frameworks

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is GRIHA? — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GRIHA (Green Rating for Integrated Habitat Assessment)** is the **national rating system of India** for evaluating the environmental performance of buildings, habitats, and built environments. It is developed jointly by **The Energy and Resources Institute (TERI)** and the **Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE), Government of India**, and was endorsed by the Government of India in **2007** as the fifth rating system in the world, after BREEAM, LEED, CASBEE, and DGNB.

The system quantitatively assesses a building against **prescriptive and performance-based criteria** spanning the entire life cycle — from site selection, conceptual design, construction, operation, and maintenance, to eventual deconstruction. It is governed by a **100-point scale**, with a minimum threshold of **50 points** required for a **1-star GRIHA certification**, scaling up to **5 stars** for outstanding performance.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The School Report Card for Buildings"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine your school gives students a "Holistic Excellence Report Card" that grades them not just on Math and Science, but also on Physical Fitness, Art, Behavior, Eco-friendliness, and Community Service. A student who scores well across *all* dimensions is considered an all-rounder. **GRIHA works exactly like this for buildings.** A building is not judged only on energy savings — it is evaluated across site ecology, water stewardship, materials sustainability, occupant health, energy performance, and innovation. A green building is thus a **holistic all-rounder**, not just an energy-efficient one.

### 1.3 Core Green Rating Metrics at a Glance

| Metric | Value / Standard |
|---|---|
| Total Points | **100** |
| Minimum for Certification | **50 points (1-Star)** |
| Maximum Tier | **5-Star (≥90 points)** |
| Origin | **India (TERI + MNRE)** |
| Endorsement Year | **2007** |
| Latest Version | **GRIHA Version 2019 (v3)** |
| Applicability | Residential, Commercial, Institutional, Industrial, Townships, Existing Buildings, Affordable Housing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GRIHA is unique because it is calibrated for the Indian climatic context** — it accounts for tropical monsoon climate, vernacular architecture practices, high solar radiation, mixed-mode ventilation, and locally available materials — unlike imported Western frameworks (LEED, BREEAM) that were originally designed for temperate climates.

### 1.4 Other Major Green Rating Systems (the "etc." in the Topic)

The KTU syllabus groups GRIHA with comparable global and Indian frameworks. The four other systems you must master alongside GRIHA are:

> [!NOTE]
> 1. **LEED (Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design)** — U.S. Green Building Council (USGBC), USA.
> 2. **BREEAM (Building Research Establishment Environmental Assessment Method)** — Building Research Establishment, UK (the world's first, 1990).
> 3. **IGBC (Indian Green Building Council)** — Confederation of Indian Industry (CII), India.
> 4. **CASBEE (Comprehensive Assessment System for Built Environment Efficiency)** — Japan.

These are collectively termed **"Green Building Rating Systems (GBRSs)"** — voluntary, third-party-verified evaluation tools that translate sustainability principles into measurable building performance benchmarks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Architectural Logic of Green Rating Systems

Every green rating system, despite variations in criteria weightings, follows a common five-stage analytical logic:

- **Step 1 — Goal Definition:** Identify the environmental and human-health priorities (energy, water, carbon, comfort, biodiversity).
- **Step 2 — Criteria Formulation:** Translate priorities into measurable sub-criteria (e.g., "Lighting Power Density ≤ 0.8 W/ft²").
- **Step 3 — Weighting & Scoring:** Assign point allocations to each criterion reflecting its relative environmental impact.
- **Step 4 — Performance Verification:** Independent third-party audit of design documents, simulations, and post-construction performance.
- **Step 5 — Certification & Rating:** Award a tiered label (Bronze, Silver, Gold, Platinum — or 1-Star to 5-Star in GRIHA).

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why":** Rating systems exist because **buildings consume ~30–40% of global final energy** and contribute ~**38% of global CO₂ emissions** (UNEP, 2022). Voluntary market-based tools incentivize developers to exceed minimum code by translating sustainability into market value (rental premiums, capital appreciation, ESG compliance).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Green Rating Quantitative Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below consolidates **every formula, threshold, and metric** you are expected to recall for Part A and Part B questions on this topic. Memorize the **point allocations, certification thresholds, and percentage weights**.

| Concept / Parameter | Formula or Standard | Units / Threshold |
|---|---|---|
| Total GRIHA Points | $P_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i$ | points (max = **100**) |
| GRIHA Star Rating | $\text{Star} = f(P_{total})$ | 1★: 50–60, 2★: 61–70, 3★: 71–80, 4★: 81–90, 5★: ≥ 90 |
| LEED Certification Tiers | $\text{Tier} = f(\text{Total LEED Points})$ | Certified: 40–49, Silver: 50–59, Gold: 60–79, Platinum: 80+ |
| BREEAM Rating Levels | $\text{Level} = f(\% \text{Score})$ | Pass ≥ 30%, Good ≥ 45%, Very Good ≥ 55%, Excellent ≥ 70%, Outstanding ≥ 85% |
| Site Ecological Preservation | $\text{SPP} = \dfrac{A_{preserved}}{A_{total\,site}} \times 100$ | percent (**≥ 40%** for full GRIHA credit) |
| Energy Performance Reduction | $\Delta E = \dfrac{E_{baseline} - E_{proposed}}{E_{baseline}} \times 100$ | percent (GRIHA: **≥ 25%** for full credit) |
| Water Use Reduction | $\Delta W = \dfrac{W_{baseline} - W_{proposed}}{W_{baseline}} \times 100$ | percent (GRIHA: **≥ 30%** for full credit) |
| Outdoor Water Use Reduction | $W_{outdoor} = W_{baseline} \times (1 - R_{landscape})$ | liters/day |
| Embodied Carbon Index | $ECI = \dfrac{\sum_{m} M_m \cdot C_m}{GFA}$ | $\text{kgCO}_2\text{e/m}^2$ |
| Operational Carbon | $OC = \sum_{t=1}^{T} E_t \cdot EF_t$ | $\text{kgCO}_2\text{e}$ (annual) |
| Daylight Factor | $DF = \dfrac{E_{interior}}{E_{exterior\,horizontal}} \times 100$ | percent (**≥ 1.5%** for habitable rooms) |
| LPD (Lighting Power Density) | $LPD = \dfrac{\sum P_{fixtures}}{A_{floor}}$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ |
| Thermal Comfort (PMV) | $-0.5 \leq PMV \leq +0.5$ | Predicted Mean Vote (ISO 7730) |
| SRI (Solar Reflectance Index) | $SRI = \frac{1 - \rho - 0.025 \cdot \Delta T_{s}}{0.0017 \cdot \Delta T_{s} - 0.025}$ | dimensionless (min **78** for low-slope roofs) |
| Recycled Content | $RC = \dfrac{M_{recycled}}{M_{total\,material}} \times 100$ | percent (**≥ 10%** for full GRIHA credit) |
| BEE Star Rating (Equipments) | 5-Star: SEER / COP top 5% of market | Bureau of Energy Efficiency |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** In Part A, students frequently confuse **BREEAM and GRIHA tier thresholds**. BREEAM is **percentage-based** (Pass ≥ 30%, Outstanding ≥ 85%) whereas **GRIHA is points-based** (out of 100). Confusing these is a guaranteed mark loss. Always state the *basis of the rating* before quoting the threshold.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These metrics and rating systems are not academic exercises. They directly govern:

- **Building code compliance** (ECBC — Energy Conservation Building Code, India).
- **Government incentives** — accelerated depreciation, tax benefits (Section 80-IAC, India), additional FAR/FSI in many state municipal bylaws.
- **Green finance & ESG reporting** — mandatory for SEBI's BRSR (Business Responsibility & Sustainability Reporting) framework.
- **Carbon credit monetization** — projects documented under GRIHA/LEED can register under Verra, Gold Standard, or Indian Carbon Market.
- **Occupant productivity** — studies by Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health (COGfx study) demonstrate **+26% cognitive function** and **+30% fewer sick days** in green-certified buildings.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 GRIHA Criterion-by-Criterion Point Allocation (Verifiable Derivation)

The GRIHA v3 framework distributes the **100 total points across 7 mandatory and 18 optional criteria**. The derivation of point allocations follows a **lifecycle-impact-weighted methodology**, where criteria contributing most to **embodied energy, operational energy, water stress, and indoor environmental quality** receive higher weightage.

$$\boxed{P_{total} = P_{site} + P_{energy} + P_{water} + P_{materials} + P_{IEQ} + P_{innovation} + P_{misc}}$$

$$P_{total} = 13 + 25 + 15 + 12 + 18 + 4 + 13 = 100$$

| Criterion # | Criterion | Points | Mandatory? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Site Selection & Planning | **8** | Yes |
| 2 | Preservation of Site Ecology | **5** | No |
| 3 | Sustainable Land Use | **3** | No |
| 4 | Design & Construction Stage | **5** | Yes |
| 5 | Energy Performance | **15** | Yes (Sub-criteria 5.1–5.4) |
| 6 | Water Management | **10** | Yes |
| 7 | Wastewater & Solid Waste | **5** | No |
| 8 | Sustainable Building Materials | **12** | Yes (at least 1 credit) |
| 9 | Indoor Environmental Quality (IEQ) | **18** | Yes |
| 10 | Innovation & Sustainable Practices | **4** | No |
| 11 | Operations, Maintenance & Performance | **4** | Yes (at least 1 credit) |
| 12 | Local/Regional Materials & Vernacular | **4** | No |
| 13 | Public Transport Accessibility | **2** | No |
| 14 | Energy-Efficient Equipment | **5** | No |
| | **Total** | **100** | |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Energy Performance Compliance

GRIHA's Energy Performance criterion (Criterion 5) requires the proposed building to demonstrate a **minimum 25% reduction** in energy consumption compared to a GRIHA-defined **ECBC-compliant baseline building** of identical geometry and occupancy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the most frequently examined sub-topic. The derivation proceeds in four discrete steps.

**Step 1 — Baseline Building Energy Consumption ($E_{baseline}$):**
Compute the annual energy consumption of a hypothetical, GRIHA-compliant reference building using **ECBC 2017 (Energy Conservation Building Code)** simulation rules: same floor area, orientation, window-to-wall ratio, occupancy schedule, HVAC efficiency, and lighting loads as the proposed building.

$$E_{baseline} = \sum_{m=1}^{M} \sum_{t=1}^{8760} P_{m,t}^{ECBC} \cdot \tau_t$$

where $M$ is the number of energy-consuming systems (HVAC, lighting, plug-loads, lifts), $P_{m,t}^{ECBC}$ is the ECBC-defined power draw of system $m$ at hour $t$, and $\tau_t$ is the occupancy hour fraction.

**Step 2 — Proposed Building Energy Consumption ($E_{proposed}$):**
Using an approved simulation engine (e.g., **EnergyPlus, DesignBuilder, eQUEST, IES-VE**), compute the actual energy consumption of the proposed design.

$$E_{proposed} = \sum_{m=1}^{M} \sum_{t=1}^{8760} P_{m,t}^{design} \cdot \tau_t$$

**Step 3 — Percentage Energy Reduction ($\Delta E$):**

$$\Delta E = \frac{E_{baseline} - E_{proposed}}{E_{baseline}} \times 100 \quad [\text{percent}]$$

**Step 4 — GRIHA Point Allocation for Criterion 5 (Discretized Tiers):**

$$\text{Criterion 5 Points} = \begin{cases} 6 & \text{if } 0\% \leq \Delta E < 15\% \\ 10 & \text{if } 15\% \leq \Delta E < 25\% \\ 13 & \text{if } 25\% \leq \Delta E < 35\% \\ 15 & \text{if } \Delta E \geq 35\% \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The tiered structure ensures **continuous incentive** — the architect is rewarded for every additional percentage point of energy reduction, reflecting diminishing returns at the highest performance tier.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Energy Performance Calculation

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem (KTU 2024 Part B style):** A proposed commercial office building has an ECBC baseline annual energy consumption of **2,800,000 kWh/year**. Through passive design, efficient HVAC (COP 4.5 vs ECBC 3.5), and daylight-linked lighting controls, the design team calculates the proposed building consumes **1,960,000 kWh/year**. Determine: (a) the percentage energy reduction, (b) the GRIHA Criterion 5 points awarded, and (c) the building's achievable overall star rating assuming 75 points are scored on all other criteria.

**Part (a) Solution — Percentage Reduction:**

$$\Delta E = \frac{E_{baseline} - E_{proposed}}{E_{baseline}} \times 100$$

$$\Delta E = \frac{2{,}800{,}000 - 1{,}960{,}000}{2{,}800{,}000} \times 100$$

$$\Delta E = \frac{840{,}000}{2{,}800{,}000} \times 100$$

$$\Delta E = 0.30 \times 100 = \boxed{30\%}$$

**Part (b) Solution — GRIHA Criterion 5 Points:**
Since $25\% \leq \Delta E < 35\%$, the design qualifies for **13 points** (tier 3).

**Part (c) Solution — Overall Star Rating:**

$$P_{total} = P_{others} + P_{criterion\,5} = 75 + 13 = \boxed{88 \text{ points}}$$

According to GRIHA's star thresholds, **81 ≤ P ≤ 90 ⇒ 4-Star Rating**. ✓

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Global Green Rating Systems

The derivation below ranks each system by **point basis, weighting philosophy, and climate adaptability** — a commonly tested KTU comparative question.

| Parameter | **GRIHA** | **LEED v4.1** | **BREEAM** | **IGBC** | **CASBEE** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Country | India | USA | UK | India | Japan |
| Year of Launch | 2007 | 2000 | 1990 | 2001 | 2004 |
| Scoring Basis | Points (out of 100) | Points (out of 110) | Percentage-based | Points (varies by category) | BEE = $\dfrac{Q}{L}$ index |
| Top Rating | 5-Star (≥90) | Platinum (≥80) | Outstanding (≥85%) | Platinum | S-rank (BEE ≥ 3.0) |
| Climate Adaptation | **Tropical Indian** | Temperate US | UK temperate | Indian (multi-zone) | Japanese humid-temperate |
| Life-Cycle Scope | Whole-life (cradle-to-grave) | Mostly operational | Whole-life | Operational + site | Whole-life |
| Mandatory Criteria | Yes (criteria 1, 4, 5, 6, 8, 9) | Yes (prerequisites) | Yes (prelims) | Yes (mandatory) | Yes (Q and L mandatory) |
| Government Backing | **MNRE, GoI** | USGBC | BRE | CII | JSBC / MLIT |
| Energy Weight | 25% | ~33% | ~19% | ~30% | Variable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The CASBEE BEE Index is unique.** Instead of points, it uses a **two-axis graph** with Environmental Quality (Q) on the Y-axis and Environmental Load Reduction (L) on the X-axis. The BEE score is:

$$\text{BEE} = \frac{Q}{L}$$

A **higher Q and lower L** yields a higher BEE, placing the building in the S, A, B+, B−, or C tier on the CASBEE radar chart.

### 3.5 Criterion 9 — Indoor Environmental Quality (IEQ) Sub-Derivation

GRIHA's IEQ criterion (18 points) is decomposed as:

$$P_{IEQ} = P_{CO_2} + P_{daylight} + P_{thermal} + P_{acoustic} + P_{VOC} + P_{asbestos}$$

$$P_{IEQ} = 3 + 4 + 4 + 3 + 3 + 1 = 18$$

**Threshold Conditions:**
- $\text{CO}_2 \leq 700 \text{ ppm}$ above outdoor for full credit.
- $\text{DF} \geq 1.5\%$ for ≥ 75% of floor area.
- $\text{PMV} \in [-0.5, +0.5]$ for ≥ 90% of occupied hours.
- $L_{eq,8h} \leq 45 \text{ dB(A)}$ (daytime) for acoustic comfort.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 GRIHA Certification Process — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Initiation by Client] --> B[Register with GRIHA Secretariat]
    B --> C[Online Application Submission]
    C --> D{Eligibility Check}
    D -- Not Eligible --> E[Reject or Resubmit]
    D -- Eligible --> F[Stage 1 Document Review]
    F --> G[Site Visits and Audit]
    G --> H{Design Compliance}
    H -- Fail --> I[Corrective Action Request]
    I --> F
    H -- Pass --> J[Stage 2 Construction Review]
    J --> K[Operational Performance Monitoring]
    K --> L{Performance Verified}
    L -- No --> M[Partial Credit Award]
    L -- Yes --> N[Final Rating Calculation]
    N --> O[Star Rating 1 to 5 Awarded]
    O --> P[GRIHA Certificate Issued]
    P --> Q[Annual Monitoring Cycle]
    Q --> R{Renewal or Upgrade}
    R -- Upgrade --> F
    R -- Renew --> P
```

### 4.2 GRIHA Seven-Criteria Cluster Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HUB[GRIHA 100 Points] --> SITE[Site and Ecology 16 pts]
    HUB --> ENRG[Energy Performance 15 pts]
    HUB --> WATR[Water and Waste 15 pts]
    HUB --> MATL[Sustainable Materials 12 pts]
    HUB --> IEQ[Indoor Environment 18 pts]
    HUB --> INOV[Innovation and Operations 8 pts]
    HUB --> ADDL[Additional Sustainability 16 pts]
    SITE --> A1[Criterion 1 Site Selection 8 pts]
    SITE --> A2[Criterion 2 Ecology 5 pts]
    SITE --> A3[Criterion 3 Land Use 3 pts]
    ENRG --> B1[Criterion 5 Energy 15 pts]
    WATR --> C1[Criterion 6 Water 10 pts]
    WATR --> C2[Criterion 7 Wastewater 5 pts]
    MATL --> D1[Criterion 8 Materials 12 pts]
    IEQ --> E1[Criterion 9 IEQ 18 pts]
    INOV --> F1[Criterion 10 Innovation 4 pts]
    INOV --> F2[Criterion 11 OM 4 pts]
    ADDL --> G1[Criterion 12 Vernacular 4 pts]
    ADDL --> G2[Criterion 13 Transit 2 pts]
    ADDL --> G3[Criterion 14 Equipment 5 pts]
```

### 4.3 Global Green Rating System Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ROOT[Green Building Rating Systems Worldwide]
    ROOT --> INDIA[Indian Systems]
    ROOT --> INTL[International Systems]
    INDIA --> I1[GRIHA MNRE 2007]
    INDIA --> I2[IGBC CII 2001]
    INTL --> N1[LEED USGBC 2000]
    INTL --> N2[BREEAM BRE 1990]
    INTL --> N3[CASBEE JSBC 2004]
    INTL --> N4[DGNB Germany 2008]
    INTL --> N5[WELL IWBI 2014]
```

### 4.4 CASBEE Two-Axis Decision Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Environmental Load L Reduction] --> SCORE[BEE Index]
    Y[Environmental Quality Q Improvement] --> SCORE
    SCORE --> TIER{Tier Determination}
    TIER --> S[S Rank Excellent]
    TIER --> A[A Rank Very Good]
    TIER --> BPLUS[B Plus Rank Good]
    TIER --> BMINUS[B Minus Rank Fair]
    TIER --> C[C Rank Poor]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The CASBEE model is distinct from all other rating systems because it **does not aggregate points**. Instead, it places the project on a two-dimensional radar with **Quality on the vertical axis** and **Load Reduction on the horizontal axis**. The diagonal BEE = Q/L ratio then determines the tier. This rewards **balanced design** — a building cannot score high by being only energy-efficient or only comfortable.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023] Define GRIHA. List any four criteria of GRIHA.**

**Model Answer:**
GRIHA, the acronym for **Green Rating for Integrated Habitat Assessment**, is India's national green building rating system developed jointly by **TERI (The Energy and Resources Institute)** and **MNRE (Ministry of New and Renewable Energy, Government of India)** in 2007. It evaluates the environmental performance of buildings across the entire life cycle — from site selection and design through construction, operation, and deconstruction — on a **100-point scale**, with a minimum of 50 points required for 1-star certification and ≥90 points for the 5-star tier.

Four criteria of GRIHA (out of 14):
1. **Criterion 1 — Site Selection and Planning (8 points)**
2. **Criterion 5 — Energy Performance Optimization (15 points)**
3. **Criterion 6 — Water Management (10 points)**
4. **Criterion 9 — Indoor Environmental Quality (18 points)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] Differentiate between GRIHA and IGBC green rating systems.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | GRIHA | IGBC |
|---|---|---|
| **Developed By** | TERI + MNRE | Confederation of Indian Industry (CII) |
| **Year** | 2007 | 2001 |
| **Scale** | 100 points (5-tier star) | Variable per category (Certified, Silver, Gold, Platinum) |
| **Coverage** | Whole-life (cradle-to-grave) | Primarily new construction + select categories |
| **Mandatory Credits** | Yes (criteria 1, 4, 5, 6, 8, 9) | Yes (minimum prerequisites) |
| **Climate Adaptation** | India-wide climate zones | Indian tropical and subtropical |
| **Existing Buildings** | GRIHA for Existing Buildings (GRIHA EB) | IGBC Green Existing Buildings |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Part B questions from a single module provide an **internal choice**. The student must answer either **Question A OR Question B**. Each question contains two sub-parts, typically 7 marks each.

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the GRIHA certification process in detail with a flowchart. List the seven mandatory criteria of GRIHA v3. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A GRIHA-applied residential township in Kerala has the following energy and water performance data compared to ECBC baseline:

| Parameter | Baseline | Proposed |
|---|---|---|
| Annual Energy | 5,500 MWh | 4,400 MWh |
| Annual Water | 250,000 m³ | 175,000 m³ |

Calculate the percentage reductions, determine the GRIHA points earned under Criterion 5 and Criterion 6, and assess the overall star rating if 70 points are scored on remaining criteria. **(7 Marks)**

**MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION A:**

**Part (a) — Certification Process:**

The GRIHA certification is a multi-stage process executed by the **GRIHA Secretariat** housed at TERI, New Delhi:

**Stage 1 — Pre-Application:** The project proponent (owner, builder, architect) submits a preliminary application. The GRIHA Secretariat reviews project eligibility (typology, location, scope) and grants provisional acceptance.

**Stage 2 — Documentation:** Detailed design documents are uploaded — floor plans, elevations, building services drawings, material specifications, energy and water simulation reports, life-cycle analysis. The **GRIHA Evaluator** conducts a documentary review and assigns provisional points.

**Stage 3 — Site Audit:** On-site verification of construction practices, material storage, segregation of waste, and quality control systems.

**Stage 4 — Operational Performance:** Post-occupancy, performance data is collected over a 12-month period covering energy, water, waste, IEQ.

**Stage 5 — Rating Award:** The Secretariat computes the **final weighted score** and issues the GRIHA certificate (1-Star to 5-Star).

**Seven Mandatory Criteria of GRIHA v3:**
1. Criterion 1 — Site Selection and Planning
2. Criterion 4 — Sustainable Design and Construction
3. Criterion 5 — Energy Performance Optimization
4. Criterion 6 — Water Management
5. Criterion 8 — Sustainable Building Materials (minimum 1 credit)
6. Criterion 9 — Indoor Environmental Quality
7. Criterion 11 — Operations and Maintenance (minimum 1 credit)

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Percentage Energy Reduction:**

$$\Delta E = \frac{5{,}500{,}000 - 4{,}400{,}000}{5{,}500{,}000} \times 100$$

$$\Delta E = \frac{1{,}100{,}000}{5{,}500{,}000} \times 100 = \boxed{20\%}$$

**Step 2 — GRIHA Criterion 5 Points:**
Since $15\% \leq \Delta E < 25\%$, the project earns **10 points** under Criterion 5.

**Step 3 — Percentage Water Reduction:**

$$\Delta W = \frac{250{,}000 - 175{,}000}{250{,}000} \times 100$$

$$\Delta W = \frac{75{,}000}{250{,}000} \times 100 = \boxed{30\%}$$

**Step 4 — GRIHA Criterion 6 Points:**
Since $\Delta W \geq 30\%$, the project earns **10 points** under Criterion 6.

**Step 5 — Overall Rating:**

$$P_{total} = P_{others} + P_{c5} + P_{c6} = 70 + 10 + 10 = \boxed{90 \text{ points}}$$

**Star Rating Classification:**
Since $P_{total} = 90$, the project qualifies for the **5-Star GRIHA Certification** (highest tier).

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4 Alternative]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast the GRIHA, LEED, and BREEAM green rating systems on any seven criteria. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the GRIHA Criterion 9 — Indoor Environmental Quality in detail. Explain the significance of PMV, CO₂ concentration, and daylight factor in green building assessment. **(7 Marks)**

**MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION B:**

**Part (a) — Comparative Analysis:**

| Criterion | GRIHA | LEED v4.1 | BREEAM |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | India (TERI + MNRE) | USA (USGBC) | UK (BRE) |
| **Year** | 2007 | 2000 | 1990 |
| **Scale** | 100 points | 110 points | % score |
| **Top Tier** | 5-Star (≥90) | Platinum (≥80) | Outstanding (≥85%) |
| **Categories** | 14 criteria | 7 categories | 9 categories |
| **Mandatory Credits** | Yes (7) | Yes (prerequisites) | Yes (core) |
| **Climate Focus** | Indian tropical | Mixed US climates | UK temperate |

**Detailed Points:**

1. **Origin:** GRIHA is tailored to **Indian subcontinental climate zones** (hot-dry, warm-humid, composite, cold — as per ECBC); LEED originated in US climate zones; BREEAM in UK temperate climate.

2. **Scale:** GRIHA and LEED use **absolute points**, while BREEAM uses a **weighted percentage**.

3. **Mandatory Credits:** GRIHA's mandatory credits are clustered around energy, water, and IEQ; LEED's prerequisites emphasize minimum energy and indoor air quality; BREEAM mandates sustainability basics like life-cycle costing and waste management.

4. **Verification:** All three use independent third-party audits but differ in documentation requirements.

5. **Innovation:** LEED awards up to **6 innovation points**, GRIHA up to **4**, BREEAM includes "Innovation" category up to **10% weighting**.

6. **Renewable Energy Weightage:** GRIHA Criterion 5.4 awards up to **4 points** for RE integration; LEED awards up to **3 points** for on-site RE; BREEAM awards up to **2 credits**.

7. **Cost of Certification:** GRIHA fees are **significantly subsidized** for government projects; LEED fees scale with project size; BREEAM charges per credit pursued.

**Part (b) — GRIHA Criterion 9 — Indoor Environmental Quality (IEQ):**

IEQ carries **18 points** in GRIHA v3, reflecting the fundamental principle that **sustainable buildings must also be healthy buildings**. Criterion 9 evaluates six sub-parameters:

**1. CO₂ Concentration (3 points):**
$$\Delta CO_2 = CO_{2}^{indoor} - CO_{2}^{outdoor} \leq 700 \text{ ppm}$$

High CO₂ levels (>1000 ppm above outdoor) cause drowsiness, reduced cognition, and Sick Building Syndrome. GRIHA mandates **demand-controlled ventilation (DCV)** to maintain thresholds.

**2. Daylighting (4 points):**

$$DF = \frac{E_{interior}}{E_{exterior\,horizontal}} \times 100 \geq 1.5\%$$

For ≥75% of floor area, daylight factor must exceed **1.5%** for full credit. Daylighting reduces electrical lighting load, improves occupant mood (linked to serotonin regulation), and supports circadian rhythm.

**3. Thermal Comfort (4 points):**
The Predicted Mean Vote (PMV) model by **Fanger (1970)** predicts occupant thermal sensation on a scale of −3 (cold) to +3 (hot).

$$-0.5 \leq PMV \leq +0.5 \quad \text{(ISO 7730 comfort band)}$$

Achieved through passive design (orientation, shading, insulation), natural ventilation, and active HVAC with appropriate control.

**4. Acoustic Comfort (3 points):**
Equivalent continuous A-weighted sound pressure level $L_{eq,8h} \leq 45 \text{ dB(A)}$ for daytime and $\leq 35 \text{ dB(A)}$ for nighttime. Buildings near airports or highways must demonstrate STC ≥ 55 for walls.

**5. Low-VOC Materials (3 points):**
Volatile Organic Compound emissions from paints, adhesives, sealants, and composite wood must comply with **GreenPro, GRIHA-approved, or EU Indoor Air Quality limits** (TVOC ≤ 200 μg/m³ after 28 days).

**6. Asbestos-Free (1 point):**
Mandatory — no asbestos-based materials in any part of the building.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> - **Confusing BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency) ratings of equipment with BEE index of CASBEE.** They are entirely different. The former rates appliances on a 1–5 star scale; the latter is a Q/L ratio for buildings.
> - **Forgetting mandatory criteria.** If a student designs only for energy and ignores water, IEQ, or materials, the project **fails eligibility** regardless of total points. Always mention mandatory criteria explicitly.
> - **Treating LEED and GRIHA as interchangeable.** LEED v4.1 has 110 points in 7 categories; GRIHA has 100 points across 14 criteria. The category structure differs.
> - **Numerical errors in % reduction formulas.** Always show the units cancellation step-by-step. Examiners award partial credit for setup but full credit only for correct final number.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🎯 KTU 2024 Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4 / GRIHA**

- **GRIHA** = Green Rating for Integrated Habitat Assessment. **TERI + MNRE, 2007**. **100-point scale**, 1-Star to 5-Star.
- **Minimum for certification = 50 points (1-Star).** 5-Star requires **≥ 90 points.**
- **Energy Performance Criterion (15 pts)** requires **≥ 25% reduction over ECBC baseline** for full credit; tiered 6 / 10 / 13 / 15 points.
- **Seven Mandatory Criteria**: 1 (Site), 4 (Design/Construction), 5 (Energy), 6 (Water), 8 (Materials), 9 (IEQ), 11 (O&M).
- **IEQ (18 pts)** = largest single criterion; covers CO₂ (≤700 ppm above outdoor), Daylight (≥1.5%), Thermal Comfort (PMV ∈ [−0.5, +0.5]), Acoustics (≤ 45 dBA), Low-VOC, Asbestos-free.
- **Other Indian system**: **IGBC** by CII (2001) — separate but parallel framework.
- **LEED v4.1** = USGBC, 110 points, Certified (40) / Silver (50) / Gold (60) / Platinum (80+).
- **BREEAM** = BRE, UK, percentage-based: Pass (≥30%), Good (≥45%), Very Good (≥55%), Excellent (≥70%), Outstanding (≥85%).
- **CASBEE** = Japan, **BEE = Q/L** two-axis index, S, A, B+, B−, C tiers.
- **Key Formulas to Memorize**:
  - $\Delta E = \dfrac{E_{baseline} - E_{proposed}}{E_{baseline}} \times 100$
  - $\Delta W = \dfrac{W_{baseline} - W_{proposed}}{W_{baseline}} \times 100$
  - $DF = \dfrac{E_{interior}}{E_{exterior}} \times 100$
  - $SRI = \dfrac{1 - \rho - 0.025 \Delta T_s}{0.0017 \Delta T_s - 0.025}$
  - $BEE = Q/L$ (CASBEE)
- **Vernacular Materials** Criterion 12 — 4 pts — encourages local bricks, mud, lime, stone, and traditional techniques (relevant to Kerala's laterite and timber traditions).
- **Renewable Energy Integration** is embedded in Criterion 5.4 — solar PV, solar thermal, biomass, wind hybrid systems.
- **Lifecycle Approach** — GRIHA is the **only major Indian rating system with explicit cradle-to-grave assessment** including deconstruction.
- **Real-world Significance**: Green-certified buildings report **+26% cognitive performance**, **+30% lower absenteeism**, **~15% rental premium**, and **~20% reduction in operational costs** over their lifecycle.
- **Comparative Favourite for KTU**: Always remember the matrix comparing GRIHA / LEED / BREEAM / CASBEE on (a) origin, (b) scale, (c) top tier, (d) climate adaptation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** When answering Part B comparative questions, **always structure with a markdown table** — it instantly communicates clarity and earns evaluator goodwill. Avoid vague phrases like *"GRIHA is better than LEED."* Instead, write *"On climate adaptability for tropical India, GRIHA scores higher due to its monsoon-calibrated site ecology criterion, whereas LEED's US-temperate bias limits its transferability."* Specificity = marks.
